docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to HMS-Mirror! This guide will help you get up and running quickly with Hive metadata and data migrations.

docs.title: What is HMS-Mirror?
docs.text: HMS-Mirror is your go-to tool for migrating Apache Hive tables between DataLake environments. Whether you're upgrading from HDP to CDP, moving to the cloud, or setting up disaster recovery, HMS-Mirror automates the complex process of synchronizing metadata and data.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text: I want to... Go to... Understand what HMS-Mirror does Introduction See what's new in v4 What's New Install HMS-Mirror Installation Guide Complete my first migration Quick Start Tutorial Learn the terminology Core Concepts Choose a migration strategy Data Strategies

docs.title: New User Learning Path
docs.text:
Follow this recommended path to get productive with HMS-Mirror:

┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│   1. Read   │     │ 2. Install  │     │  3. Try the │     │  4. Learn   │
│   Intro     │────►│ HMS-Mirror  │────►│  Tutorial   │────►│  Concepts   │
└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘

docs.title: Step 1: Understand HMS-Mirror
docs.text: Start with the Introduction to understand: - What problems HMS-Mirror solves - Key use cases (cluster migrations, storage migrations, DR) - What's new in version 4

docs.title: Step 2: Install HMS-Mirror
docs.text: Follow the Installation Guide to: - Check system requirements - Download and install HMS-Mirror - Start the server - Verify your installation

docs.title: Step 3: Complete the Quick Start Tutorial
docs.text: Work through the Quick Start Tutorial to: - Create your first connection - Build a configuration - Define a dataset - Run a SCHEMA_ONLY migration

docs.title: Step 4: Learn the Core Concepts
docs.text: Read Core Concepts to understand: - LEFT and RIGHT clusters - Connections, Configurations, Datasets, and Jobs - Data strategies and when to use each

docs.title: Prerequisites Checklist
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Java 11 or higher (Java 17 recommended)
  • Network access to your Hive clusters (HiveServer2 ports)
  • Valid credentials for cluster authentication
  • Kerberos ticket (if your clusters use Kerberos)

docs.title: Experienced User Quick Links
docs.text:
Already familiar with HMS-Mirror? Jump directly to:

  • Data Strategies - Choose the right migration approach
  • Web UI Overview - Navigate the v4 interface
  • Workflows - Step-by-step migration guides
  • Troubleshooting - Solve common problems
  • CLI Reference - Command-line options

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• FAQ - Common questions answered
  • Troubleshooting - Diagnose and fix issues
  • GitHub Issues - Report bugs or request features

Ready to begin? Start with the Introduction to learn what HMS-Mirror can do for you.

docs.title: ACID Table Migration
docs.text: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) tables in Hive require special handling during migration. This guide covers ACID table concepts and migration options.

docs.title: What are ACID Tables?
docs.text: ACID tables are Hive managed tables that support: - Atomicity - Transactions complete fully or not at all - Consistency - Database remains consistent - Isolation - Concurrent transactions don't interfere - Durability - Committed changes persist

docs.title: ACID Table Characteristics
docs.text: Property Description Transaction support INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations Table type Always managed (not external) Storage format ORC with ACID metadata Bucketing Usually bucketed (Hive 2.x required it) Compaction Requires periodic compaction

docs.title: ACID Version Differences
docs.text:
Hive 2.x (Legacy) - ACID v1 format - Bucketing required - Limited update/delete support - Delta files for changes

Hive 3.x (CDP) - ACID v2 format - Bucketing optional - Full CRUD support - Improved compaction - Better performance

docs.title: Why ACID Migration is Complex
docs.text:
┌─────────────────┐                    ┌─────────────────┐
│  Legacy ACID    │                    │   CDP ACID      │
│  (Hive 2.x)     │   Challenge ──►    │   (Hive 3.x)    │
├─────────────────┤                    ├─────────────────┤
│ • ACID v1       │                    │ • ACID v2       │
│ • Required      │                    │ • Optional      │
│   bucketing     │                    │   bucketing     │
│ • Delta files   │                    │ • Improved      │
│                 │                    │   storage       │
└─────────────────┘                    └─────────────────┘

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Format incompatibility - V1 and V2 formats differ
  2. SQL limitations - Can't INSERT directly from V1 to V2
  3. Export restrictions - Some ACID tables can't be exported
  4. Bucket mismatches - Different bucketing schemes
  5. In-flight transactions - Active transactions block export

docs.title: Migration Options
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides several options for ACID tables:

docs.title: Option 1: Include ACID Tables
docs.text:
Migrate ACID tables as-is, preserving transactional properties.

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: false

Requirements - Use EXPORT_IMPORT strategy - Compatible Hive versions - Both clusters support ACID

Best For - Hive 3.x to Hive 3.x migrations - When transactional properties are required - Tables actively using UPDATE/DELETE

docs.title: Option 2: Downgrade to External
docs.text:
Convert ACID tables to non-transactional external tables.

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: true

Process 1. Read data from ACID table 2. Create external table on target 3. Insert data using SQL 4. Result is non-transactional table

Best For - Legacy to CDP migrations - When transaction support not needed - Simplifying table management

docs.title: Option 3: ACID Only Mode
docs.text:
Process only ACID tables, skip all others.

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  acidOnly: true

Use Case - Phased migration (ACID tables separately) - Focused ACID troubleshooting - When ACID tables need special handling

docs.title: Option 4: In-Place Migration
docs.text:
Migrate ACID tables within the same cluster.

acid:
  inPlace: true

Use Case - Storage migration (HDFS to S3) - Same cluster, different location - Namespace changes

docs.title: Basic ACID Settings
docs.text: Setting Description Default includeAcidTables Process ACID tables false acidOnly Only process ACID tables false downgradeAcid Convert to external false inPlace Same cluster migration false

docs.title: Advanced Settings
docs.text: Setting Description Default partitionLimit Max partitions for ACID 0 (unlimited) artificialBucketThreshold Min buckets for SQL 0 migrateAcid Enable ACID migration false

docs.title: Configuration Examples
docs.text:
Include ACID, Keep Transactional 

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  migrateAcid: true
  downgradeAcid: false

Downgrade All ACID Tables 

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: true

ACID Tables Only with Partition Limit 

acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  acidOnly: true
  partitionLimit: 500

docs.title: Which Strategy to Use
docs.text: Scenario Recommended Strategy Keep ACID properties EXPORT_IMPORT Downgrade to external SQL or HYBRID Large ACID tables EXPORT_IMPORT Small ACID tables (downgrade) SQL Mixed tables HYBRID with downgrade

docs.title: Partition Limits
docs.text:
ACID tables with many partitions require special handling.

acid:
  partitionLimit: 500  # Max partitions for SQL strategy

When Limit is Exceeded - SQL strategy → Switch to EXPORT_IMPORT - EXPORT_IMPORT → May take longer - Job fails if no alternative

docs.title: Recommendations by Partition Count
docs.text: Partitions Recommendation < 100 SQL works well 100 - 500 SQL or EXPORT_IMPORT 500 - 1000 EXPORT_IMPORT preferred > 1000 EXPORT_IMPORT only

docs.title: Bucket Threshold
docs.text:
Control when SQL strategy can be used based on bucket count.

acid:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2

Purpose - Tables with buckets may perform poorly with SQL - Threshold triggers switch to EXPORT_IMPORT - Higher threshold = more tables use SQL

docs.title: Bucket Considerations
docs.text: Bucket Count SQL Performance Recommendation 0 (none) Good SQL is fine 1-4 Acceptable SQL usually works 5+ Poor Use EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Pre-Migration Steps
docs.text:
1. Identify ACID tables 

    SELECT * FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_type = 'MANAGED_TABLE';

  2. Check for active transactions 

    SHOW TRANSACTIONS;

  3. Run compaction (recommended) 

    ALTER TABLE table_name COMPACT 'major';

  4. Verify compaction complete 

    SHOW COMPACTIONS;

docs.title: Migration Steps
docs.text:
1. Configure ACID settings
  2. Choose include/exclude/downgrade options

  3. Set appropriate limits

  4. Select strategy

  5. EXPORT_IMPORT for keeping ACID

  6. SQL or HYBRID for downgrade

  7. Run dry run

  8. Verify ACID tables are handled correctly

  9. Check for partition/bucket issues

  10. Execute migration

  11. Monitor for ACID-specific errors
  12. Check transaction states

docs.title: Post-Migration Steps
docs.text:
1. Verify data
  2. Row counts match

  3. Sample data comparison

  4. Check table properties

  5. Transactional property (if kept)

  6. Storage location

  7. Run compaction on target (if ACID) 

    ALTER TABLE table_name COMPACT 'major';

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
"Cannot export transactional table" 

Error: Cannot export ACID table using SQL strategy
Solution: Use EXPORT_IMPORT strategy or enable downgrade

"Active transaction on table" 

Error: Table has active transactions
Solution: Wait for transactions to complete or abort them

"Incompatible ACID version" 

Error: Source ACID v1 incompatible with target
Solution: Enable downgrade to convert to external

"Bucket count mismatch" 

Error: Target bucket specification differs
Solution: Enable downgrade or adjust bucket threshold

docs.title: Verification Queries
docs.text:
Check if table is ACID 

DESCRIBE FORMATTED table_name;
-- Look for: transactional=true

Check ACID version 

SHOW TBLPROPERTIES table_name ('transactional_properties');

Compare row counts 

-- Source
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM source_db.table_name;
-- Target
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM target_db.table_name;

docs.title: Before Migration
docs.text:
1. Inventory ACID tables - Know what you're migrating
  2. Run major compaction - Consolidate delta files
  3. Quiesce activity - Pause writes during migration
  4. Document bucket configurations - For reference

docs.title: During Migration
docs.text:
1. Use EXPORT_IMPORT - Most reliable for ACID
  2. Set appropriate limits - Avoid timeout/memory issues
  3. Monitor progress - ACID tables often take longer
  4. Handle failures individually - Don't restart entire job

docs.title: After Migration
docs.text:
1. Verify transactional property - If kept
  2. Test INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE - If ACID preserved
  3. Run compaction - On target if ACID
  4. Update applications - Point to new tables

docs.title: Decision Matrix
docs.text: Requirement Keep ACID Downgrade Need UPDATE/DELETE ✓ Simpler management ✓ Legacy to CDP ✓ (recommended) CDP to CDP ✓ Optional Performance priority ✓ Compliance (transactions) ✓

docs.title: HDP to CDP (Downgrade)
docs.text:
# Downgrade all ACID tables to external
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: true
  partitionLimit: 500

# Use HYBRID to automatically select strategy
dataStrategy: HYBRID

docs.title: CDP to CDP (Preserve)
docs.text:
# Keep ACID properties
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  migrateAcid: true
  downgradeAcid: false

# Use EXPORT_IMPORT for ACID tables
dataStrategy: EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Phased ACID Migration
docs.text:
# First job: Non-ACID tables
acid:
  includeAcidTables: false

# Second job: ACID tables only
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  acidOnly: true
  downgradeAcid: true

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Data Strategies - Strategy details
  • Managing Configurations - Configure ACID settings
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve ACID errors

docs.title: Advanced Topics
docs.text: This section covers complex migration scenarios and specialized features that go beyond basic usage. These topics assume familiarity with Core Concepts and Data Strategies.

docs.title: When You Need This Section
docs.text:
Consult these advanced topics when dealing with:

  • Transactional (ACID) tables that need special handling
  • Storage layer changes (HDFS → S3, namespace changes)
  • Table format modernization (converting to Iceberg)
  • Performance optimization for large-scale migrations

docs.title: ACID Table Migration
docs.text:
Handle Hive transactional tables properly:

  • Understanding ACID table requirements
  • Migration options: preserve vs. downgrade
  • Version compatibility between clusters
  • Best practices for large ACID datasets
  • Troubleshooting common ACID issues
ACID Table Decision:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Do you need INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on target?                   │
│                                                               │
│   YES → Use EXPORT_IMPORT to preserve ACID properties         │
│   NO  → Enable downgrade to convert to EXTERNAL tables        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Storage Migration
docs.text:
Move data within a cluster to new storage:

  • HDFS to cloud storage (S3, ADLS, GCS)
  • HDFS namespace changes
  • Ozone migrations
  • Warehouse reorganization
  • Data movement methods (SQL, distcp, manual)
Same Cluster, New Storage:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HDFS Cluster                                                │
│  ┌──────────────┐     STORAGE      ┌──────────────────┐    │
│  │ Old Location │   MIGRATION ───► │ New Location     │    │
│  │ hdfs://old/  │                  │ s3a://bucket/    │    │
│  └──────────────┘                  └──────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
Modernize your tables to Apache Iceberg format:

  • Benefits of Iceberg (time travel, schema evolution, performance)
  • Conversion modes: in-place vs. shadow
  • Format version selection
  • Handling partitioned tables
  • Post-conversion considerations

docs.title: Optimizations
docs.text:
Tune HMS-Mirror for performance and reliability:

  • Query optimization settings
  • Compression configuration
  • Statistics handling
  • Property overrides for LEFT/RIGHT clusters
  • Parallelism and resource management

docs.title: Large-Scale HDP to CDP Migration
docs.text:
For enterprise-scale migrations with thousands of tables:

  1. Start with SCHEMA_ONLY to validate structures
  2. Handle ACID tables separately with downgrade
  3. Use HYBRID strategy for mixed workloads
  4. Apply optimizations for throughput

docs.title: On-Premises to Cloud Migration
docs.text:
Moving your entire data platform to the cloud:

  1. Use Storage Migration concepts for path translation
  2. Plan for credential and security changes
  3. Consider Iceberg Conversion while migrating
  4. Test with LINKED strategy before full migration

docs.title: Prerequisites for Advanced Topics
docs.text:
Before diving into these topics, ensure you're comfortable with:

  • ✅ Core Concepts - Understand LEFT/RIGHT, jobs, strategies
  • ✅ Data Strategies - Know when to use each strategy
  • ✅ Quick Start Tutorial - Complete at least one migration
  • ✅ Basic Hive knowledge - Table types, partitioning, storage formats

docs.title: Getting Help with Complex Scenarios
docs.text:
• Workflows - Step-by-step guides for specific scenarios
  • Troubleshooting - Diagnose and fix issues
  • FAQ - Common questions about advanced features

Ready to tackle advanced migrations? Start with the topic most relevant to your situation.

docs.title: Iceberg Conversion
docs.text: HMS-Mirror can convert traditional Hive tables to Apache Iceberg format during migration. This guide covers Iceberg concepts and conversion options.

docs.title: What is Apache Iceberg?
docs.text: Apache Iceberg is a high-performance table format for huge analytic datasets.

docs.title: Key Benefits
docs.text: Feature Description Time Travel Query data as of a specific point in time Schema Evolution Add, rename, drop columns without rewriting data Partition Evolution Change partitioning without rewriting data Hidden Partitioning Users don't need to know partition columns ACID Transactions Full transactional support Multi-Engine Works with Spark, Flink, Trino, Hive, and more

docs.title: Iceberg vs Traditional Hive
docs.text: Aspect Traditional Hive Iceberg Metadata Metastore only Manifest files + Metastore Transactions Limited (ACID tables) Full support Schema changes Requires ALTER Transparent Partition changes Requires rewrite Transparent Query engines Hive, Spark Many engines Time travel Not supported Built-in

docs.title: Good Candidates
docs.text:
• Tables that need schema evolution
  • Multi-engine access (Spark + Hive + Trino)
  • Large tables benefiting from Iceberg optimizations
  • Tables requiring time travel capabilities
  • New data lake implementations

docs.title: Poor Candidates
docs.text:
• Small dimension tables (overhead not worth it)
  • Tables with complex Hive UDFs
  • Tables used only by legacy Hive applications
  • Temporary or staging tables

docs.title: In-Place Conversion
docs.text:
Converts the existing table to Iceberg format.

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: IN_PLACE

Process 

┌─────────────────┐                ┌─────────────────┐
│   Hive Table    │  ──Convert──►  │  Iceberg Table  │
│   (Original)    │                │  (Same name)    │
└─────────────────┘                └─────────────────┘

Characteristics - Original table is converted - Same table name retained - Data files may be rewritten - Minimal storage overhead

docs.title: Shadow Conversion
docs.text:
Creates a new Iceberg table alongside the original.

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW

Process 

┌─────────────────┐
│   Hive Table    │  (Retained)
│   (Original)    │
└─────────────────┘
        │
        │ Copy
        ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│  Iceberg Table  │  (New)
│  (table_iceberg)│
└─────────────────┘

Characteristics - Original table preserved - New table with suffix (e.g., _iceberg) - Safe rollback option - Requires additional storage

docs.title: File Format Translation
docs.text:
Choose the output file format for Iceberg tables.

iceberg:
  fileFormat: PARQUET  # PARQUET, ORC, or AVRO
Format Best For PARQUET General purpose, wide compatibility ORC Heavy Hive usage, compression AVRO Schema evolution, row-level access

docs.title: Iceberg Version
docs.text:
Select the Iceberg format version.

iceberg:
  version: 2  # 1 or 2
Version Features V1 Basic features, wider compatibility V2 Row-level deletes, position deletes

Recommendation: Use V2 for new implementations.

docs.title: Basic Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
dataStrategy: ICEBERG_CONVERSION

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW
  version: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET

docs.title: Full Configuration
docs.text:
iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW
  version: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET

  # Table properties to set on Iceberg tables
  tableProperties:
    write.format.default: parquet
    write.parquet.compression-codec: zstd
    history.expire.max-snapshot-age-ms: 432000000  # 5 days

docs.title: With Storage Migration
docs.text:
Combine Iceberg conversion with storage migration:

dataStrategy: ICEBERG_CONVERSION

transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://data-lake/iceberg/warehouse'

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: IN_PLACE
  version: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET

docs.title: Pre-Migration
docs.text:
1. Verify Iceberg support
  2. Target cluster has Iceberg catalog
  3. Required Iceberg libraries installed

  4. Compatible Hive/Spark versions

  5. Evaluate tables

  6. Identify conversion candidates
  7. Estimate storage requirements

  8. Plan table naming

  9. Test conversion

  10. Convert small table first
  11. Verify queries work
  12. Check performance

docs.title: Migration Steps
docs.text:
1. Configure HMS-Mirror 

    iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW
  version: 2

  2. Run dry run

  3. Review generated DDL

  4. Verify Iceberg properties

  5. Execute conversion

  6. Monitor progress

  7. Watch for errors

  8. Validate results

  9. Query converted tables
  10. Verify row counts
  11. Test time travel

docs.title: Post-Migration
docs.text:
1. Update applications
  2. Point to Iceberg tables

  3. Update query syntax if needed

  4. Configure maintenance

  5. Set up compaction

  6. Configure snapshot expiration

  7. Remove shadow originals (if using SHADOW)

  8. After validation complete
  9. Keep for rollback period

docs.title: Iceberg Table Creation
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror generates Iceberg-specific DDL:

-- Create Iceberg table
CREATE TABLE database.table_iceberg (
  id BIGINT,
  name STRING,
  created_date DATE
)
USING ICEBERG
PARTITIONED BY (date_trunc('month', created_date))
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'format-version' = '2',
  'write.format.default' = 'parquet'
);

docs.title: Data Migration
docs.text:
-- Insert data from source
INSERT INTO database.table_iceberg
SELECT * FROM database.table_source;

docs.title: Common Iceberg Properties
docs.text: Property Description Example format-version Iceberg format version 2 write.format.default Default file format parquet write.parquet.compression-codec Compression zstd, snappy history.expire.max-snapshot-age-ms Snapshot retention 432000000 write.target-file-size-bytes Target file size 536870912

docs.title: Setting Properties
docs.text:
iceberg:
  tableProperties:
    format-version: "2"
    write.format.default: "parquet"
    write.parquet.compression-codec: "zstd"
    write.target-file-size-bytes: "536870912"

docs.title: Hive Partitioning
docs.text:
Traditional Hive partitioning: 

-- Hive table
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)

docs.title: Iceberg Partitioning
docs.text:
Iceberg supports hidden partitioning: 

-- Iceberg table
PARTITIONED BY (year(event_date), month(event_date))

docs.title: Partition Translation
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror translates partitions:

Hive Partition Iceberg Equivalent year INT year(date_col) month INT month(date_col) day INT day(date_col) bucket INT bucket(N, col)

docs.title: Basic Queries
docs.text:
Iceberg tables work with standard SQL:

SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table
WHERE created_date > '2024-01-01';

docs.title: Time Travel
docs.text:
Query historical data:

-- As of specific timestamp
SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table
TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15 10:00:00';

-- As of specific snapshot
SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table
VERSION AS OF 1234567890;

docs.title: Metadata Queries
docs.text:
-- View snapshots
SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table.snapshots;

-- View history
SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table.history;

-- View files
SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table.files;

docs.title: Compaction
docs.text:
Combine small files:

-- Rewrite data files
CALL system.rewrite_data_files('database.iceberg_table');

docs.title: Snapshot Expiration
docs.text:
Remove old snapshots:

-- Expire snapshots older than timestamp
CALL system.expire_snapshots('database.iceberg_table',
  TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01 00:00:00');

docs.title: Orphan File Cleanup
docs.text:
Remove unreferenced files:

CALL system.remove_orphan_files('database.iceberg_table');

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
"Iceberg not supported" 

Error: Iceberg format not available on cluster
Solution: Install Iceberg libraries, configure catalog

"Partition conversion failed" 

Error: Cannot translate partition specification
Solution: Manual partition mapping may be needed

"Type not supported" 

Error: Data type X not supported in Iceberg
Solution: Transform data types before conversion

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Check table is Iceberg 

DESCRIBE FORMATTED database.table;
-- Look for: Provider: iceberg

Verify row counts 

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM database.hive_table;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM database.iceberg_table;

Test time travel 

SELECT * FROM database.iceberg_table
VERSION AS OF 1;

docs.title: Planning
docs.text:
1. Start with non-critical tables
  2. Test thoroughly before production
  3. Plan storage for shadow mode
  4. Document rollback procedure

docs.title: Conversion
docs.text:
1. Use SHADOW mode initially - Easy rollback
  2. Verify data accuracy - Compare counts
  3. Test queries - Ensure compatibility
  4. Monitor performance - May change

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
1. Set up compaction schedule
  2. Configure snapshot expiration
  3. Monitor storage usage
  4. Plan for schema evolution

docs.title: Simple Conversion
docs.text:
# Convert sales tables to Iceberg
dataStrategy: ICEBERG_CONVERSION

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW
  version: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET

# Dataset: sales database
databases:
  - name: sales_db
    includePattern: "fact_.*|dim_.*"

docs.title: Full Migration with Iceberg
docs.text:
# Migrate from HDP to CDP with Iceberg
dataStrategy: ICEBERG_CONVERSION

transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://data-lake/iceberg'

iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: IN_PLACE
  version: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET
  tableProperties:
    write.parquet.compression-codec: "zstd"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Data Strategies - Other migration strategies
  • Storage Migration - Combine with storage migration
  • Managing Configurations - Configure Iceberg settings

docs.title: Query Optimizations
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides several optimization settings to improve migration performance and tune Hive queries. This guide covers available optimizations and when to use them.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Optimizations control: - Query execution behavior - Data compression - Statistics collection - Session property overrides

docs.title: Sort Dynamic Partition Inserts
docs.text:
Orders data before inserting into partitioned tables.

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true

How It Works

Without sorting: 

Data arrives in random order
  ↓
Multiple files per partition
  ↓
Many small files created

With sorting: 

Data sorted by partition columns
  ↓
Sequential writes per partition
  ↓
Fewer, larger files

Benefits - Fewer output files - Better write performance - Improved read performance later - Reduced small file problem

When to Use - Large partitioned tables - Many partitions - When small files are a concern

Trade-offs - Adds sorting overhead - May increase memory usage - Longer individual query time

docs.title: Auto Tune
docs.text:
Automatically applies optimal settings.

optimizations:
  autoTune: true

What It Does - Analyzes table characteristics - Adjusts parallelism settings - Tunes memory allocation - Optimizes join strategies

When to Use - Default for most migrations - When unsure of optimal settings - Mixed table sizes

docs.title: Text Compression
docs.text:
Compression for text-based formats.

optimizations:
  textCompression: true
  compressionCodec: GZIP  # or SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD

Available Codecs

Codec Ratio Speed CPU GZIP High Slow High SNAPPY Medium Fast Low LZ4 Medium Very Fast Low ZSTD High Fast Medium

Recommendations - SNAPPY - Default, good balance - ZSTD - Best overall for most cases - GZIP - Maximum compression, slow - LZ4 - Maximum speed, lower compression

docs.title: Statistics
docs.text:
Control statistics gathering during migration.

optimizations:
  buildShadowStatistics: true

Purpose - Enables query optimizer - Improves execution plans - Better performance post-migration

Options

Option Description buildShadowStatistics Build stats during migration Skip statistics Faster migration, build later

docs.title: Property Overrides
docs.text: Set custom Hive session properties for LEFT and/or RIGHT clusters.

docs.title: Configuration Structure
docs.text:
optimizations:
  overrides:
    - key: "property.name"
      leftValue: "value-for-left"
      rightValue: "value-for-right"

docs.title: Dynamic Partitioning
docs.text:
overrides:
  - key: "hive.exec.dynamic.partition"
    rightValue: "true"
  - key: "hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode"
    rightValue: "nonstrict"
  - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions"
    rightValue: "10000"
  - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions.pernode"
    rightValue: "1000"

docs.title: Memory Settings
docs.text:
overrides:
  - key: "hive.tez.container.size"
    rightValue: "8192"
  - key: "hive.tez.java.opts"
    rightValue: "-Xmx6144m"

docs.title: Parallelism
docs.text:
overrides:
  - key: "hive.exec.parallel"
    rightValue: "true"
  - key: "hive.exec.parallel.thread.number"
    rightValue: "16"

docs.title: Compression
docs.text:
overrides:
  - key: "hive.exec.compress.output"
    rightValue: "true"
  - key: "mapreduce.output.fileoutputformat.compress.codec"
    rightValue: "org.apache.hadoop.io.compress.SnappyCodec"

docs.title: Vectorization
docs.text:
overrides:
  - key: "hive.vectorized.execution.enabled"
    rightValue: "true"
  - key: "hive.vectorized.execution.reduce.enabled"
    rightValue: "true"

docs.title: LEFT vs RIGHT Values
docs.text:
Scope Use Case leftValue Settings for source cluster queries rightValue Settings for target cluster queries Both When same setting needed on both

Example 

overrides:
  # Different values per cluster
  - key: "hive.exec.parallel.thread.number"
    leftValue: "8"    # Smaller source cluster
    rightValue: "16"  # Larger target cluster

  # Same value for both
  - key: "hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode"
    leftValue: "nonstrict"
    rightValue: "nonstrict"

docs.title: High-Volume Migration
docs.text:
For migrating large amounts of data:

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true
  autoTune: true

  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel"
      rightValue: "true"
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel.thread.number"
      rightValue: "16"
    - key: "hive.tez.container.size"
      rightValue: "8192"
    - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions"
      rightValue: "50000"

docs.title: Many Small Tables
docs.text:
For datasets with numerous small tables:

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: false  # Not needed for small tables
  autoTune: true

  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel"
      rightValue: "true"
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel.thread.number"
      rightValue: "32"  # Higher parallelism

docs.title: Large Partitioned Tables
docs.text:
For tables with many partitions:

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true

  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.dynamic.partition"
      rightValue: "true"
    - key: "hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode"
      rightValue: "nonstrict"
    - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions"
      rightValue: "100000"
    - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions.pernode"
      rightValue: "10000"
    - key: "hive.optimize.sort.dynamic.partition"
      rightValue: "true"

docs.title: Memory-Constrained Environment
docs.text:
When cluster resources are limited:

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: false  # Reduces memory
  autoTune: false

  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel.thread.number"
      rightValue: "4"  # Lower parallelism
    - key: "hive.tez.container.size"
      rightValue: "2048"  # Smaller containers
    - key: "hive.auto.convert.join"
      rightValue: "false"  # Avoid large hash joins

docs.title: Compression-Focused
docs.text:
Minimize storage with maximum compression:

optimizations:
  textCompression: true
  compressionCodec: ZSTD

  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.compress.output"
      rightValue: "true"
    - key: "parquet.compression"
      rightValue: "ZSTD"
    - key: "orc.compress"
      rightValue: "ZSTD"

docs.title: Execution Properties
docs.text: Property Description Default hive.exec.parallel Enable parallel execution false hive.exec.parallel.thread.number Parallel threads 8 hive.vectorized.execution.enabled Enable vectorization true

docs.title: Dynamic Partition Properties
docs.text: Property Description Default hive.exec.dynamic.partition Enable dynamic partitions true hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode Strict or nonstrict strict hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions Max total partitions 1000 hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions.pernode Max per node 100

docs.title: Memory Properties
docs.text: Property Description Default hive.tez.container.size Container memory (MB) 4096 hive.tez.java.opts JVM options -Xmx3276m hive.auto.convert.join.noconditionaltask.size Map join size 10MB

docs.title: Compression Properties
docs.text: Property Description Values hive.exec.compress.output Compress output true/false mapreduce.output.fileoutputformat.compress.codec Codec class Various parquet.compression Parquet codec SNAPPY, GZIP, ZSTD orc.compress ORC codec SNAPPY, ZLIB, ZSTD

docs.title: General Guidelines
docs.text:
1. Start with autoTune - Good baseline
  2. Enable parallel execution - Faster migrations
  3. Use sortDynamicPartitionInserts - For partitioned tables
  4. Monitor and adjust - Based on actual performance

docs.title: When to Increase Settings
docs.text:
• More partitions → Higher partition limits
  • Larger tables → More memory
  • More tables → Higher parallelism
  • Slow writes → Enable sort dynamic partition

docs.title: When to Decrease Settings
docs.text:
• Memory errors → Reduce container size
  • Cluster pressure → Lower parallelism
  • Small tables → Disable sorting overhead

docs.title: Monitoring Impact
docs.text: Watch for: - Query execution time - Memory usage - Output file sizes - Error rates

docs.title: Memory Errors
docs.text:
Error: Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits

Solution 

overrides:
  - key: "hive.tez.container.size"
    rightValue: "8192"  # Increase
  - key: "hive.tez.java.opts"
    rightValue: "-Xmx6144m"  # 75% of container

docs.title: Partition Errors
docs.text:
Error: Maximum number of dynamic partitions exceeded

Solution 

overrides:
  - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions"
    rightValue: "50000"
  - key: "hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions.pernode"
    rightValue: "5000"

docs.title: Slow Performance
docs.text:
Diagnose - Check query plans - Monitor resource usage - Review output file sizes

Solutions 

optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true
  overrides:
    - key: "hive.exec.parallel"
      rightValue: "true"
    - key: "hive.vectorized.execution.enabled"
      rightValue: "true"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Test optimizations on small datasets first
  2. Document your override choices
  3. Monitor cluster resources during migration
  4. Adjust based on actual performance
  5. Consider cluster capacity
  6. Use autoTune for initial runs

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Configurations - Configure optimizations
  • Data Strategies - Strategy-specific tuning
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve performance issues

docs.title: Storage Migration
docs.text: Storage migration moves table data to a new storage location within the same cluster. This guide covers the STORAGE_MIGRATION strategy and related concepts.

docs.title: HDFS to Cloud Storage
docs.text:
Migrate from on-premises HDFS to cloud object stores:

Source Target hdfs://namenode/warehouse s3a://bucket/warehouse hdfs://namenode/warehouse abfs://container@account/warehouse hdfs://namenode/warehouse gs://bucket/warehouse

docs.title: Namespace Changes
docs.text:
Move data between HDFS namespaces:

Source Target hdfs://old-ns/data hdfs://new-ns/data hdfs://cluster1/warehouse hdfs://cluster2/warehouse

docs.title: Ozone Migration
docs.text:
Move from HDFS to Apache Ozone:

Source Target hdfs://cluster/warehouse o3fs://bucket.volume/warehouse hdfs://cluster/warehouse ofs://ozone/volume/bucket/warehouse

docs.title: Warehouse Reorganization
docs.text:
Restructure warehouse directory layout:

Source Target /apps/hive/warehouse /warehouse/managed /data/external /warehouse/external

docs.title: Basic Settings
docs.text:
dataStrategy: STORAGE_MIGRATION

transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://new-bucket/warehouse/external'
    managed: 's3a://new-bucket/warehouse/managed'

docs.title: Translation Types
docs.text: How paths are transformed from source to target.

docs.title: RELATIVE Translation
docs.text:
Preserves the relative path structure from the source.

Source: hdfs://old-cluster/warehouse/db/table
Target: s3a://bucket/warehouse/db/table
         └──────────────────┬───────────┘
                    Preserved structure

Configuration: 

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    translationType: RELATIVE

Use When - Simple namespace swap - Keeping existing directory structure - Path hierarchy should be preserved

docs.title: ALIGNED Translation
docs.text:
Maps full source paths to new target paths with complete control.

Source: hdfs://old-cluster/apps/hive/warehouse/db/table
Target: s3a://bucket/warehouse/db/table
         └─────────┬─────────┘
           New base path

Configuration: 

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED

Use When - Changing directory structure - Complex path transformations - Multiple source paths to single target

docs.title: Data Movement Strategies
docs.text: How data is actually moved.

docs.title: SQL
docs.text:
Uses INSERT...SELECT to move data through Hive.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataMovementStrategy: SQL

Characteristics - Works through HiveServer2 - Data is re-serialized - Can apply transformations - Good for small-medium tables

docs.title: DISTCP
docs.text:
Uses Hadoop DistCp for direct file copy.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataMovementStrategy: DISTCP

Characteristics - Direct file copy - Preserves file format exactly - Better for large datasets - Requires DistCp access

docs.title: EXPORT_IMPORT
docs.text:
Uses Hive's native export/import.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataMovementStrategy: EXPORT_IMPORT

Characteristics - Native Hive mechanism - Handles ACID tables - Creates intermediate files

docs.title: MANUAL
docs.text:
Generates scripts but doesn't execute data movement.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataMovementStrategy: MANUAL

Characteristics - User handles data movement - Maximum control - Use with external tools

docs.title: Data Flow Direction
docs.text: Controls whether target cluster pulls or source pushes.

docs.title: PULL (Default)
docs.text:
Target cluster pulls data from source.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataFlow: PULL

Source                      Target
┌────────┐    Read from    ┌────────┐
│        │ ◄─────────────  │        │
│  Data  │                 │ Process│
│        │  Write to       │        │
│        │ ◄─────────────  │        │
└────────┘                 └────────┘

docs.title: PUSH
docs.text:
Source cluster pushes data to target.

transfer:
  storageMigration:
    dataFlow: PUSH

Source                      Target
┌────────┐   Write to      ┌────────┐
│        │ ───────────►    │        │
│ Process│                 │  Data  │
│        │                 │        │
└────────┘                 └────────┘

docs.title: Global Location Map
docs.text:
Define mappings from source to target namespaces.

transfer:
  globalLocationMap:
    "hdfs://old-nameservice": "s3a://data-lake"
    "hdfs://archive-ns": "s3a://archive-bucket"

How It Works 

Table Location: hdfs://old-nameservice/warehouse/db/table
                └────────┬───────────┘
                    Matched

New Location:   s3a://data-lake/warehouse/db/table
                └──────┬──────┘
                   Replaced

docs.title: Multiple Namespace Mapping
docs.text:
transfer:
  globalLocationMap:
    "hdfs://prod-ns/warehouse/managed": "s3a://prod-bucket/managed"
    "hdfs://prod-ns/warehouse/external": "s3a://prod-bucket/external"
    "hdfs://archive-ns": "s3a://archive-bucket"

docs.title: External Warehouse
docs.text:
Location for external tables on target.

transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://bucket/warehouse/external'

docs.title: Managed Warehouse
docs.text:
Location for managed tables on target.

transfer:
  warehouse:
    managed: 's3a://bucket/warehouse/managed'

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://data-lake/warehouse/external'
    managed: 's3a://data-lake/warehouse/managed'
  globalLocationMap:
    "hdfs://hdp-cluster": "s3a://data-lake"
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED
    dataMovementStrategy: DISTCP
    dataFlow: PULL

docs.title: Phase 1: Planning
docs.text:
1. Inventory tables and locations 

    SELECT t.TBL_NAME, s.LOCATION
FROM TBLS t JOIN SDS s ON t.SD_ID = s.SD_ID;

  2. Identify unique namespaces

  3. List all source storage locations

  4. Plan target locations

  5. Calculate storage requirements

  6. Total data size

  7. Target storage capacity

  8. Plan network/credentials

  9. Access to both storage systems
  10. Appropriate permissions

docs.title: Phase 2: Configuration
docs.text:
1. Create HMS-Mirror configuration 

    dataStrategy: STORAGE_MIGRATION

transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://target/external'
    managed: 's3a://target/managed'
  storageMigration:
    translationType: RELATIVE
    dataMovementStrategy: DISTCP

  2. Define dataset

  3. Select databases to migrate

  4. Filter tables if needed

  5. Configure connection

  6. Only LEFT cluster needed
  7. Ensure access to both storage systems

docs.title: Phase 3: Dry Run
docs.text:
1. Run dry run
  2. Generate migration scripts

  3. Review planned changes

  4. Verify translations

  5. Check old → new location mappings

  6. Confirm no unexpected paths

  7. Estimate duration

  8. Based on data sizes
  9. Network bandwidth

docs.title: Phase 4: Execution
docs.text:
1. Execute migration
  2. Start during low-activity period

  3. Monitor progress

  4. Monitor data movement

  5. Track DistCp or SQL progress

  6. Watch for errors

  7. Verify completion

  8. All tables processed
  9. Data accessible at new locations

docs.title: Phase 5: Validation
docs.text:
1. Verify table access 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM database.table;

  2. Check locations updated 

    DESCRIBE FORMATTED database.table;

  3. Compare row counts

  4. Source vs target

  5. Test queries

  6. Run representative queries
  7. Verify performance

docs.title: Partitioned Tables
docs.text:
Each partition location is updated:

Before:
  Table: hdfs://old/warehouse/db/sales
  Partitions:
    year=2023: hdfs://old/warehouse/db/sales/year=2023
    year=2024: hdfs://old/warehouse/db/sales/year=2024

After:
  Table: s3a://new/warehouse/db/sales
  Partitions:
    year=2023: s3a://new/warehouse/db/sales/year=2023
    year=2024: s3a://new/warehouse/db/sales/year=2024

docs.title: ACID Tables
docs.text:
Special handling for transactional tables:

  • Use EXPORT_IMPORT for best results
  • Consider compaction before migration
  • Verify ACID properties after migration

docs.title: External Tables with Custom Locations
docs.text:
Tables with non-standard locations:

# Table at: hdfs://cluster/custom/path/my_data
# Not under warehouse directory

transfer:
  globalLocationMap:
    "hdfs://cluster/custom": "s3a://bucket/custom"

docs.title: Large Tables
docs.text:
For very large tables:

  1. Use DISTCP - More efficient than SQL
  2. Consider parallel jobs - Split by database
  3. Off-peak execution - Minimize impact
  4. Incremental if possible - Migrate in batches

docs.title: Permission Errors
docs.text:
Error: Access denied to s3a://bucket/path

Solutions - Verify IAM roles/credentials - Check bucket policies - Ensure HMS-Mirror user has access

docs.title: Path Not Found
docs.text:
Error: Source path does not exist

Solutions - Verify source table locations - Check namespace spelling - Ensure storage is accessible

docs.title: Slow Migration
docs.text:
Causes - Large data volumes - Network bandwidth - Cluster resources

Solutions - Use DISTCP over SQL - Increase parallelism - Run during off-peak - Split into multiple jobs

docs.title: Location Not Updated
docs.text:
Causes - Translation type mismatch - Missing namespace mapping

Solutions - Check globalLocationMap - Verify translationType setting - Review dry run output

docs.title: Planning
docs.text:
1. Test with small dataset first
  2. Verify storage access before migration
  3. Calculate and provision storage
  4. Plan rollback procedure

docs.title: Execution
docs.text:
1. Run dry run first
  2. Migrate during low-activity periods
  3. Monitor progress actively
  4. Keep source data until verified

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. Verify row counts
  2. Test query performance
  3. Check all partitions
  4. Validate ACID tables specifically

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
1. Remove source data after verification
  2. Update any hard-coded paths
  3. Document new locations
  4. Update backup procedures

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Data Strategies - Overview of all strategies
  • Managing Configurations - Configure storage migration
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve common issues

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding these key concepts will help you work effectively with HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: LEFT and RIGHT Clusters
docs.text: HMS-Mirror always works with two cluster designations:

docs.title: LEFT Cluster (Source)
docs.text: The LEFT cluster is typically your source cluster - where your existing Hive tables reside. This is usually: - Your current production cluster - An older HDP or CDH cluster you're migrating from - An on-premises cluster when moving to cloud

docs.title: RIGHT Cluster (Target)
docs.text:
The RIGHT cluster is typically your target cluster - where you want tables to be created or data to be migrated. This is usually: - Your new CDP cluster - A cloud-based deployment - A disaster recovery environment

┌─────────────────┐                    ┌─────────────────┐
│  LEFT Cluster   │   ──Migration──►   │  RIGHT Cluster  │
│    (Source)     │                    │    (Target)     │
└─────────────────┘                    └─────────────────┘

Note

The terms LEFT and RIGHT are used consistently throughout HMS-Mirror instead of "source" and "target" because some operations (like STORAGE_MIGRATION) work within a single cluster, making source/target terminology confusing.

docs.title: When Only One Cluster is Needed
docs.text:
Some data strategies only require one cluster:

Strategy LEFT Required RIGHT Required SCHEMA_ONLY Yes Yes SQL Yes Yes EXPORT_IMPORT Yes Yes LINKED Yes Yes HYBRID Yes Yes STORAGE_MIGRATION Yes No DUMP Yes No ICEBERG_CONVERSION Yes Optional

docs.title: Connections
docs.text: A Connection defines how HMS-Mirror connects to a DataLake environment powered by Hive and Hive Metastore. Connections are reusable - you define them once and reference them in multiple jobs.

docs.title: Connection Components
docs.text:
Each connection can include:

HiveServer2 (HS2) Settings - URI (hostname, port, database) - Connection properties (transport mode, SSL settings) - Authentication method

Metastore Direct Settings (Optional) - Metastore type (MYSQL, POSTGRESQL, ORACLE) - JDBC URI and credentials - Used for direct database operations

Partition Discovery Settings - How HMS-Mirror discovers table partitions - Auto-discovery or manual specification

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text:
Connections support several authentication methods:

Method Description Use Case None No authentication Development/test environments Kerberos Ticket-based authentication Enterprise clusters with Kerberos LDAP Username/password Clusters using LDAP auth Knox Gateway proxy authentication Clusters behind Knox gateway

docs.title: Configurations
docs.text:
A Configuration defines the behavior and options for a migration. Configurations control:

  • Migration Settings - How to handle views, non-native tables, table properties
  • Optimizations - Query tuning, compression, statistics
  • Transfer Settings - Warehouse directories, storage prefixes
  • ACID Settings - Transactional table handling
  • Conversion Settings - Format conversions (like Iceberg)

docs.title: Configuration Sections
docs.text:
Migration Controls which tables are included and how certain table types are handled: - Views migration (copy views to target) - Non-native table handling (Kudu, HBase, etc.) - Table property filtering - Partition handling

Optimizations Query performance settings applied during migration: - Sort dynamic partition inserts - Compression codecs - Statistics gathering - Property overrides for LEFT and RIGHT clusters

Transfer Where data goes and how it moves: - External warehouse directory - Managed warehouse directory - Common storage paths - Intermediate storage locations

ACID Transactional (ACID) table handling: - Include/exclude ACID tables - Downgrade to external tables - In-place migration options - Partition limits for ACID tables

Conversions Table format transformations: - Iceberg conversion settings - File format translation - Version selection

docs.title: Datasets
docs.text:
A Dataset defines what to migrate - which databases and tables. Datasets allow you to:

  • Select specific databases to include
  • Filter tables by pattern (include/exclude regex)
  • Apply database-specific settings (rename, prefix)
  • Specify custom warehouse locations per database

docs.title: Dataset Structure
docs.text:
Dataset
├── Database: sales_db
│   ├── Include Pattern: .*_fact
│   ├── Exclude Pattern: test_.*
│   └── Target Name: sales_prod_db
├── Database: marketing_db
│   └── (all tables, no filters)
└── Database: finance_db
    └── Include Pattern: ledger_.*

docs.title: Table Filtering
docs.text:
Use regex patterns to include or exclude tables:

Pattern Matches .* All tables fact_.* Tables starting with "fact_" .*_archive Tables ending with "_archive" customer\|order\|product Specific table names

docs.title: Jobs
docs.text:
A Job brings everything together. It combines:

  • A Dataset (what to migrate)
  • A Configuration (how to migrate)
  • A LEFT Connection (source cluster)
  • A RIGHT Connection (target cluster, if needed)
  • A Data Strategy (migration method)

docs.title: Job Lifecycle
docs.text:
┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐
│  Create  │ ──► │ Validate │ ──► │ Execute  │ ──► │  Report  │
└──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘
  1. Create - Build the job by selecting components and strategy
  2. Validate - Check for configuration errors and warnings
  3. Execute - Run the migration (dry-run or actual execution)
  4. Report - Review results, errors, and recommendations

docs.title: Dry Run vs Execute
docs.text:
Dry Run Mode - Generates all DDL and data movement scripts - Does NOT execute anything on clusters - Use to review what will happen before committing - Produces reports showing planned operations

Execute Mode - Actually runs the migration - Creates tables, moves data - Modifies cluster state - Produces reports showing actual results

Best Practice

Always run a dry-run first to review the planned operations before executing.

docs.title: Data Strategies
docs.text: The Data Strategy determines how HMS-Mirror handles the migration. Each strategy has different characteristics:

docs.title: Strategy Overview
docs.text: Strategy Moves Data? Creates Tables? Use Case SCHEMA_ONLY No Yes Schema synchronization LINKED No Yes (as views) Quick access to source data SQL Yes Yes Small-medium tables EXPORT_IMPORT Yes Yes ACID tables, complex types HYBRID Yes Yes Mixed table sizes STORAGE_MIGRATION Yes Updates Same-cluster moves COMMON No Yes Shared storage DUMP No No (scripts only) DDL extraction ICEBERG_CONVERSION Optional Yes Format modernization

docs.title: Choosing a Strategy
docs.text:
Consider these factors when selecting a strategy:

  1. Do you need to move data?
  2. No → SCHEMA_ONLY or LINKED

  3. Yes → SQL, EXPORT_IMPORT, or HYBRID

  4. Do you have ACID tables?

  5. Yes → EXPORT_IMPORT (or downgrade with SQL)

  6. No → SQL is usually fine

  7. What's your table size distribution?

  8. All small → SQL
  9. All large → EXPORT_IMPORT

  10. Mixed → HYBRID

  11. Same cluster or different clusters?

  12. Same → STORAGE_MIGRATION

  13. Different → Other strategies

  14. Shared storage available?

  15. Yes → COMMON can avoid data movement

See Data Strategies for detailed information on each strategy.

docs.title: Environments (LEFT/RIGHT Types)
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror recognizes different cluster environment types that affect available features:

Environment Description Typical Use LEGACY HDP, CDH (pre-CDP) Source clusters NON_LEGACY CDP Private/Public Cloud Target clusters HIVE3 Hive 3.x (standalone) Modern Hive ICEBERG Iceberg-enabled cluster Table format conversion

The environment type is detected automatically or can be specified in connection settings.

docs.title: Table Types and Handling
docs.text: HMS-Mirror handles different Hive table types:

docs.title: Managed Tables
docs.text: Tables where Hive manages both metadata and data files. Dropping the table deletes the data.

docs.title: External Tables
docs.text: Tables where Hive only manages metadata. Data files exist independently.

docs.title: ACID Tables
docs.text: Transactional tables supporting INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations. Require special handling during migration.

docs.title: Partitioned Tables
docs.text: Tables with partition columns. HMS-Mirror handles partition metadata and data.

docs.title: Views
docs.text: Virtual tables based on queries. Can be migrated as-is or recreated on target.

docs.title: Non-Native Tables
docs.text: Tables backed by external systems (HBase, Kudu, JDBC). Require special consideration.

docs.title: Reports
docs.text: After each job execution, HMS-Mirror generates detailed reports:

docs.title: Report Contents
docs.text:
• Summary - Overall migration statistics
  • Table Details - Per-table results and status
  • SQL Scripts - Generated DDL and DML statements
  • Errors - Failed operations with details
  • Warnings - Issues that didn't fail but need attention

docs.title: Report Formats
docs.text: Reports are available in multiple formats: - Web UI - Interactive viewing in the browser - Markdown - Human-readable text files - YAML - Machine-readable structured data

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you understand the core concepts:

  1. Installation Guide - Get HMS-Mirror running
  2. Quick Start Tutorial - Complete your first migration
  3. Data Strategies - Deep dive into migration strategies
  4. Managing Connections - Set up cluster connections

docs.title: Understanding Connections
docs.text: Connections are the foundation of HMS-Mirror operations. This guide explains connection concepts, types, and authentication methods.

docs.title: What is a Connection?
docs.text:
A connection defines how HMS-Mirror communicates with a DataLake environment powered by Hive and Hive Metastore. It encapsulates:

  • Endpoint information - Where to connect (host, port, URI)
  • Authentication - How to authenticate (Kerberos, LDAP, none)
  • Connection properties - Protocol settings (transport, SSL)
  • Session settings - Hive configuration overrides

docs.title: Connection Types
docs.text: HMS-Mirror supports multiple connection methods to interact with Hive.

docs.title: HiveServer2 (HS2)
docs.text:
The primary and most common connection method.

┌─────────────────┐        JDBC         ┌─────────────────┐
│   HMS-Mirror    │─────────────────────│   HiveServer2   │
└─────────────────┘                     └─────────────────┘

Characteristics - Uses JDBC protocol - Executes HiveQL statements - Supports all authentication methods - Required for data movement operations

Use Cases - Creating tables - Inserting data - Running queries - Most migration operations

docs.title: Metastore Direct
docs.text:
Direct connection to the Hive Metastore database.

┌─────────────────┐        JDBC         ┌─────────────────┐
│   HMS-Mirror    │─────────────────────│ Metastore DB    │
│                 │                     │ (MySQL/PG/Oracle)│
└─────────────────┘                     └─────────────────┘

Characteristics - Connects to underlying database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle) - Read-only operations - Faster for metadata queries - Bypasses HiveServer2

Use Cases - Fast metadata discovery - Partition information - Table statistics - When HS2 is unavailable

docs.title: When to Use Each
docs.text: Scenario HS2 Metastore Direct Create tables ✓ Move data ✓ Read metadata ✓ ✓ (faster) Partition discovery ✓ ✓ (faster) Statistics ✓ ✓ No HS2 available ✓

docs.title: HiveServer2 URIs
docs.text:
Basic Format 

jdbc:hive2://<host>:<port>/<database>

With Parameters 

jdbc:hive2://<host>:<port>/<database>;param1=value1;param2=value2

Examples

Standard binary connection: 

jdbc:hive2://hiveserver.example.com:10000/default

HTTP transport: 

jdbc:hive2://hiveserver.example.com:10001/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=cliservice

SSL enabled: 

jdbc:hive2://hiveserver.example.com:10000/default;ssl=true

Zookeeper discovery: 

jdbc:hive2://zk1.example.com:2181,zk2.example.com:2181,zk3.example.com:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2

Knox gateway: 

jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive

docs.title: Metastore Database URIs
docs.text:
MySQL/MariaDB 

jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive_metastore
jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive_metastore?useSSL=true

PostgreSQL 

jdbc:postgresql://metastore.example.com:5432/hive_metastore
jdbc:postgresql://metastore.example.com:5432/hive_metastore?ssl=true

Oracle 

jdbc:oracle:thin:@metastore.example.com:1521:hive
jdbc:oracle:thin:@//metastore.example.com:1521/hive_service

docs.title: No Authentication
docs.text:
For development or test clusters without security.

Configuration - No additional settings required - Simply provide the JDBC URI

Example 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://dev-cluster:10000/default

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication
docs.text:
For enterprise clusters with Kerberos security.

┌─────────────────┐    1. kinit    ┌─────────────────┐
│     User        │───────────────►│       KDC       │
└─────────────────┘                └─────────────────┘
        │                                   │
        │                          2. TGT   │
        │◄──────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        │ 3. Request Service Ticket
        ▼
┌─────────────────┐    4. Ticket   ┌─────────────────┐
│   HMS-Mirror    │───────────────►│   HiveServer2   │
└─────────────────┘                └─────────────────┘

Requirements - Valid Kerberos ticket (TGT) - Properly configured krb5.conf - Service principal in connection properties

Configuration 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://secure-cluster:10000/default
  connectionProperties:
    principal: hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM

Using a Keytab 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://secure-cluster:10000/default
  connectionProperties:
    principal: hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM
    keytab: /path/to/service.keytab

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text:
Username and password authentication via LDAP.

Configuration 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://ldap-cluster:10000/default
  connectionProperties:
    user: myusername
    password: mypassword

docs.title: Knox Gateway Authentication
docs.text:
Authentication through Apache Knox gateway.

Configuration 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive
  connectionProperties:
    user: knoxuser
    password: knoxpassword

docs.title: Connection Properties
docs.text: Properties passed to the JDBC connection.

docs.title: Common Properties
docs.text: Property Description Values transportMode Transport protocol binary, http httpPath HTTP endpoint path cliservice, custom ssl Enable SSL/TLS true, false sslTrustStore Trust store path file path sslTrustStorePassword Trust store password string principal Kerberos principal service/_HOST@REALM user Username string password Password string

docs.title: SSL/TLS Properties
docs.text: Property Description ssl Enable SSL sslTrustStore Path to JKS trust store sslTrustStorePassword Trust store password sslTrustStoreType Trust store type (JKS, PKCS12)

docs.title: Kerberos Properties
docs.text: Property Description principal Service principal keytab Path to keytab file saslQop SASL quality of protection

docs.title: Performance Properties
docs.text: Property Description Default socketTimeout Socket timeout (ms) 0 (infinite) connectTimeout Connection timeout (ms) 10000 fetchSize JDBC fetch size 1000

docs.title: Session Properties
docs.text: Hive session settings applied after connection.

docs.title: Common Session Properties
docs.text: Property Description Example hive.exec.dynamic.partition Enable dynamic partitions true hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode Partition mode nonstrict hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions Max partitions 10000 hive.exec.parallel Enable parallel execution true mapreduce.job.reduces Number of reducers 10

docs.title: Setting Session Properties
docs.text:
Session properties are set when the connection is established and apply to all operations using that connection.

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://cluster:10000/default
  sessionProperties:
    hive.exec.dynamic.partition: "true"
    hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode: "nonstrict"
    hive.exec.parallel: "true"

docs.title: Cluster Environment Types
docs.text: HMS-Mirror recognizes different cluster environments that affect behavior.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Pre-CDP clusters (HDP 2.x/3.x, CDH 5.x/6.x).

Characteristics - Older Hive versions (1.x, 2.x) - Different ACID implementation - May have different default behaviors

When to Select - HDP 2.x or 3.x clusters - CDH 5.x or 6.x clusters - Any non-CDP Hadoop distribution

docs.title: Non-Legacy (CDP)
docs.text:
Modern CDP Private Cloud or Public Cloud.

Characteristics - Hive 3.x - Modern ACID implementation - Iceberg support - Updated defaults

When to Select - CDP Private Cloud Base - CDP Public Cloud - Modern Hive 3.x deployments

docs.title: Impact on Migration
docs.text: Feature Legacy Non-Legacy ACID tables V1 format V2 format External table purge Manual external.table.purge Managed table location warehouse/tablespace Separate managed warehouse Iceberg Not supported Supported

docs.title: Connection Pooling
docs.text: HMS-Mirror manages connection pools for efficiency.

docs.title: Pool Configuration
docs.text: Setting Description Default Initial Size Starting connections 2 Max Total Maximum connections 10 Max Idle Max idle connections 5 Min Idle Min idle connections 1

docs.title: Pool Implementations
docs.text:
DBCP2 - Apache Commons DBCP - Widely compatible - Default option

HikariCP - High performance - Lower latency - Production recommended

Hybrid - Combination approach - Adaptive behavior

docs.title: Testing Connections
docs.text: Always test connections before using them.

docs.title: What Gets Tested
docs.text:
1. Network connectivity - Can reach the endpoint
  2. Authentication - Credentials are valid
  3. Authorization - User has permissions
  4. Basic operations - Can list databases

docs.title: Test Results
docs.text:
Success 

✓ Connection successful
  Server: Hive 3.1.3000
  Databases: 25 found
  Response time: 234ms

Failure 

✗ Connection failed
  Error: Authentication failed
  Details: GSS initiate failed
  Suggestion: Check Kerberos ticket (klist)

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Use service accounts - Not personal accounts
  2. Minimal permissions - Only what's needed
  3. Rotate credentials - Regular password/keytab rotation
  4. Secure storage - Protect keytab files

docs.title: Reliability
docs.text:
1. Test regularly - Catch issues early
  2. Use Zookeeper discovery - For HA setups
  3. Configure timeouts - Prevent hanging
  4. Monitor connections - Track pool usage

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
1. Clear naming - Include environment and purpose
  2. Document settings - Note special configurations
  3. Separate environments - Don't mix prod/dev
  4. Version control - Track connection changes

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Issue Cause Solution Connection refused Wrong host/port or service down Verify endpoint, check service Authentication failed Invalid credentials Check password/ticket Timeout Network issues Check firewall, increase timeout SSL errors Certificate mismatch Configure trust store Permission denied Insufficient privileges Grant required permissions

See Troubleshooting Guide for detailed solutions.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Connections (UI) - Create connections in the web UI
  • Kerberos Authentication - Detailed Kerberos setup
  • Architecture Overview - How connections fit in

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding the fundamental concepts of HMS-Mirror will help you plan and execute successful migrations. This section covers the key terminology, components, and patterns you'll encounter.

docs.title: Core Concepts Reference
docs.text:
Deep dive into HMS-Mirror terminology and components:

  • LEFT and RIGHT Clusters - The source/target model
  • Connections - How HMS-Mirror connects to your clusters
  • Configurations - Migration behavior and settings
  • Datasets - Defining what databases and tables to migrate
  • Jobs - Combining everything into executable migrations
  • Table Types - Handling managed, external, ACID, and partitioned tables
  • Reports - Understanding migration output

docs.title: Data Strategies
docs.text:
Learn about the different migration strategies:

Strategy Moves Data? Best For SCHEMA_ONLY No Schema sync, pre-migration setup LINKED No Quick read access to source SQL Yes Small-medium tables EXPORT_IMPORT Yes ACID tables, large data HYBRID Yes Mixed workloads STORAGE_MIGRATION Yes Same-cluster storage moves CONVERT_LINKED No Finalize LINKED migration COMMON No Shared storage scenarios DUMP No DDL extraction

docs.title: Connections
docs.text:
Configure cluster connectivity:

  • HiveServer2 connection setup
  • Metastore direct access
  • Authentication methods (Kerberos, LDAP, Knox)
  • Connection properties and tuning

docs.title: LEFT vs RIGHT
docs.text:
• LEFT = Source cluster (where data comes from)
  • RIGHT = Target cluster (where data goes to)

These terms are used instead of "source/target" because some operations (like STORAGE_MIGRATION) work on a single cluster, making traditional terminology confusing.

docs.title: The Modular Approach
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror v4 separates concerns into reusable components:

  1. Connections - Define once, use in many jobs
  2. Configurations - Settings templates for different migration types
  3. Datasets - Table selections that can be reused
  4. Jobs - Combine the above for execution

This means you can: - Test a configuration on a small dataset before applying to production - Reuse the same connection across development, testing, and production migrations - Create dataset templates for different departments or use cases

docs.title: Dry Run vs Execute
docs.text:
Always available for any job:

  • Dry Run - Generate scripts without executing (safe preview)
  • Execute - Actually perform the migration

Best Practice

Always run a dry run first to review the planned operations.

docs.title: Quick Decision Guide
docs.text: Question Answer Points To... "How do I choose a strategy?" Data Strategies "What's the difference between ACID and external tables?" Concepts "How do connections work?" Connections "What does a job actually do?" Concepts

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Read Core Concepts for detailed explanations
  2. Review Data Strategies to choose your approach
  3. Configure Connections to your clusters

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Data Strategy is the central abstraction that drives everything in HMS-Mirror. It defines how a table migration is performed — what SQL is generated, whether data moves, how locations are translated, and what intermediate objects are created. Every migration job selects a data strategy, and that choice determines the entire execution flow.

The strategy is not just a "mode switch." It is a self-contained engine that understands how to:

  1. Build a target schema from a source table definition (with location translation, property management, and type conversions)
  2. Generate SQL for schema creation, data movement, and cleanup
  3. Execute the generated SQL against source and/or target clusters
  4. Handle edge cases — ACID tables, partitions, views, non-native tables, format conversions

docs.title: Strategy Lifecycle
docs.text:
Every strategy follows a two-phase lifecycle:

Phase 1: Build                          Phase 2: Execute
┌────────────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────┐
│ buildOutDefinition()       │          │ execute()               │
│   - Translate locations    │          │   - Run DDL on target   │
│   - Convert table types    │          │   - Run DML for data    │
│   - Set properties         │          │   - Clean up staging    │
│                            │          │                         │
│ buildOutSql()              │          │                         │
│   - Generate CREATE TABLE  │          │                         │
│   - Generate INSERT/SELECT │          │                         │
│   - Generate cleanup DDL   │          │                         │
└────────────────────────────┘          └─────────────────────────┘

This separation is critical: the build phase is always safe (it only generates SQL), while the execute phase is where changes happen. Dry-run mode skips execution entirely, producing a report of what would happen.

docs.title: Four Environments
docs.text:
Each table being migrated can have definitions in up to four environments:

Environment Purpose Used By LEFT Source cluster (where data lives today) All strategies RIGHT Target cluster (where data should end up) All strategies SHADOW Intermediate table on RIGHT pointing to LEFT's data SQL, HYBRID TRANSFER Staging table for multi-hop migrations INTERMEDIATE, EXPORT_IMPORT

Most strategies only use LEFT and RIGHT. The SQL strategy uses SHADOW as a bridge — it creates a temporary external table on the RIGHT cluster that points to LEFT's storage, allowing a local INSERT...SELECT. The TRANSFER environment is used for complex multi-hop scenarios with intermediate storage.

docs.title: The BuildWhat Decision
docs.text:
Before building SQL, each strategy determines what needs to be built via the BuildWhat flags:

  • transferTable — create a TRANSFER environment staging table
  • shadowTable — create a SHADOW environment bridge table
  • transferSql — generate SQL for the TRANSFER environment
  • shadowSql — generate SQL for the SHADOW environment
  • rightSql — generate SQL for the RIGHT (final target) environment

Simple strategies like SCHEMA_ONLY only set rightSql. Complex strategies like INTERMEDIATE set all flags.

docs.title: Location Translation
docs.text: The most important transformation in any strategy is location translation — converting storage paths from the source namespace to the target namespace.

docs.title: How Translation Works
docs.text:
Source:  hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/sales.db/orders
Target:  s3a://data-lake/warehouse/external/sales.db/orders

Translation is driven by the Translator, which maintains ordered location maps:

  1. Auto-generated maps — derived from warehouse directory configuration
  2. User-provided maps — custom path mappings for non-standard layouts
  3. Merged maps — sorted by path length (longest match wins)

docs.title: Translation Modes
docs.text: Mode Behavior When Used ALIGNED Paths must align with warehouse directory structure Standard warehouse layouts RELATIVE Maintains relative path relationships with flexible base Non-standard or mixed layouts

docs.title: What Gets Translated
docs.text:
• Table LOCATION in CREATE TABLE
  • Partition locations in ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION
  • Database LOCATION and MANAGEDLOCATION properties
  • Export/import intermediate paths

docs.title: What Does NOT Get Translated
docs.text:
• LINKED strategy intentionally preserves source locations
  • COMMON strategy preserves shared locations
  • DUMP strategy outputs source locations as-is

docs.title: Managed vs External Conversion
docs.text:
When migrating from Hive 2 (HDP) to Hive 3 (CDP), managed non-transactional tables must become external tables:

Hive 2 (Legacy)                    Hive 3 (CDP)
┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────────────┐
│ MANAGED TABLE   │   ────────►   │ EXTERNAL TABLE          │
│ (non-ACID)      │               │ + EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE  │
└─────────────────┘               └─────────────────────────┘

The EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE property ensures that dropping the table on the target still deletes the underlying data — preserving the managed table's ownership semantics.

docs.title: ACID Table Handling
docs.text:
ACID (transactional) tables require special handling because:

  • Legacy ACID (Hive 1/2) uses a different storage format than Hive 3 ACID
  • EXPORT/IMPORT of ACID between incompatible versions fails silently
  • Some strategies (LINKED, COMMON) cannot handle ACID tables at all

When migrateACID is enabled, the strategy pipeline can:

  1. Downgrade — convert ACID to EXTERNAL+PURGE (strip transactional properties)
  2. Migrate via intermediate — use staging tables to handle format conversion
  3. In-place downgrade — modify the source cluster directly (single-cluster operation)

docs.title: Artificial Bucket Removal
docs.text: Hive 1/2 required ACID tables to be bucketed. Hive 3 removed this requirement. During migration, the strategy can detect "artificial" buckets (threshold: 2 or fewer) and remove the CLUSTERED BY clause, simplifying the target schema.

docs.title: Partition Handling
docs.text: Partitioned tables add complexity to every strategy. HMS-Mirror handles partitions through three mechanisms:

docs.title: 1. Metadata-Driven (Explicit)
docs.text:
When partition metadata is loaded from the source metastore, each partition gets an explicit ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION with its translated location:

ALTER TABLE orders ADD PARTITION (year=2024, month=1)
  LOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/orders/year=2024/month=1';
ALTER TABLE orders ADD PARTITION (year=2024, month=2)
  LOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/orders/year=2024/month=2';

docs.title: 2. Auto-Discovery (MSCK REPAIR)
docs.text:
When metadata is not loaded, the strategy generates:

MSCK REPAIR TABLE orders;

This relies on the storage directory structure following the standard key=value/ convention.

docs.title: 3. Partition Limits
docs.text:
Large tables (thousands of partitions) can cause resource exhaustion. Each strategy enforces configurable limits:

  • sqlPartitionLimit — max partitions for SQL strategy (routes to EXPORT_IMPORT above limit)
  • exportImportPartitionLimit — max partitions for EXPORT_IMPORT
  • HYBRID uses these limits to auto-select between SQL and EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Configuration Parameters That Affect All Strategies
Transfer Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Effect transferPrefix Prefix for intermediate transfer tables (default: hms_mirror_transfer_) shadowPrefix Prefix for shadow bridge tables (default: hms_mirror_shadow_) exportBaseDirPrefix Base directory for EXPORT operations intermediateStorage Multi-hop intermediate location (e.g., S3 bucket between clusters) warehouse.external Target warehouse directory for external tables warehouse.managed Target warehouse directory for managed tables

docs.title: Migration Flags
docs.text: Parameter Effect migrateACID.on Enable ACID table migration migrateACID.downgrade Convert ACID to EXTERNAL+PURGE migrateACID.inplace Modify source cluster directly migrateACID.artificialBucketThreshold Remove buckets if count <= threshold migrateVIEW Include views in migration migrateNonNative Include HBase/Kafka-backed tables

docs.title: Output Modifiers
docs.text: Parameter Effect createIfNotExists Add IF NOT EXISTS to DDL (default: true) forceExternalLocation Always specify LOCATION in CREATE TABLE enableAutoTableStats Run ANALYZE TABLE after migration enableAutoColumnStats Run ANALYZE TABLE COLUMNS after migration copyAvroSchemaUrls Preserve avro.schema.url property saveWorkingTables Keep intermediate tables after migration

docs.title: Iceberg Conversion (Overlay)
docs.text:
Iceberg conversion is not a standalone strategy — it's a configuration overlay that can be applied during SQL or SCHEMA_ONLY strategies:

Parameter Effect icebergConversion.enable Convert target tables to Iceberg format icebergConversion.version Iceberg format version (1 or 2) icebergConversion.fileTypeTranslation Target file format (PARQUET, ORC, AVRO, STANDARD) icebergConversion.inplace In-place conversion without data movement

docs.title: Table Properties Management
docs.text: During migration, strategies manage several table properties:

docs.title: Properties Removed (Legacy/Stats)
docs.text:
• COLUMN_STATS_ACCURATE — stale statistics from source
  • numFiles, numRows, rawDataSize, totalSize — stale metrics
  • transient_lastDdlTime — source timestamp, irrelevant on target
  • BUCKETING_VERSION — Hive 3 internal property

docs.title: Properties Added
docs.text:
• HMS_MIRROR_METADATA_FLAG — timestamp marking when schema was migrated
  • EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE — set when taking data ownership
  • DISCOVER_PARTITIONS — enable automatic partition discovery
  • HMS_STORAGE_MIGRATION_FLAG — timestamp for storage migration
  • DOWNGRADED_FROM_ACID — marks tables that were converted from ACID

docs.title: Properties on Intermediate Tables
docs.text:
• HMS_MIRROR_TRANSFER_TABLE — marks transfer staging tables
  • HMS_MIRROR_SHADOW_TABLE — marks shadow bridge tables

These markers ensure intermediate tables are identifiable for cleanup.

docs.title: Strategy Applicability Matrix
docs.text: Strategy Non-ACID External ACID/Managed Partitioned Views Non-Native Linked Clusters Single Cluster SCHEMA_ONLY Yes Limited Yes Yes Optional Yes Yes LINKED Yes No Yes No No Required No SQL Yes Downgrade Yes No No Required No EXPORT_IMPORT Yes Yes Yes No No Required No HYBRID Yes Auto Yes No No Required No STORAGE_MIGRATION Yes Limited Yes No No No Yes CONVERT_LINKED Yes No Limited No No Yes No COMMON Yes No Yes No No Yes Yes DUMP Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Any Any

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
User selects strategy in JobDto
         │
         ▼
DataStrategyService.getStrategy(enum)
         │
         ▼
strategy.build(dbMirror, tableMirror)
    │
    ├── whatToBuild() ──► determines which environments need tables/SQL
    ├── buildOutDefinition() ──► translates schema, locations, properties
    └── buildOutSql() ──► generates DDL and DML statements
         │
         ▼
strategy.execute(dbMirror, tableMirror)   [skipped in dry-run]
    │
    ├── Execute DDL on RIGHT (CREATE TABLE)
    ├── Execute DML (INSERT/SELECT, EXPORT/IMPORT)
    └── Execute cleanup (DROP intermediate tables)
         │
         ▼
Results written to ConversionResult
    │
    ├── Generated SQL (for review/audit)
    ├── Errors and warnings
    └── Migration status per table

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• SCHEMA_ONLY Strategy
  • LINKED Strategy
  • SQL Strategy
  • EXPORT_IMPORT Strategy
  • HYBRID Strategy
  • STORAGE_MIGRATION Strategy
  • CONVERT_LINKED Strategy
  • COMMON Strategy
  • DUMP Strategy

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Transfers table schema (DDL) from source to target cluster without moving data. Storage locations are translated from the source namespace to the target namespace so that table definitions on the target cluster point to the correct paths. Data is assumed to be moved externally via tools such as distcp, S3 sync, cloud replication, or similar mechanisms.
