The core operation is an ALTER TABLE SET LOCATION that swings the table's storage pointer from the source cluster's namespace to the target cluster's namespace. After conversion, the RIGHT cluster owns its local copy of the data rather than reading from the LEFT cluster remotely.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Reads the existing linked table definition from the RIGHT cluster (the table must already exist on RIGHT, having been created by a prior LINKED run)
  2. Validates the table is eligible (not ACID, AVRO schema check passes)
  3. Translates the table location from the source namespace to the target namespace using TranslatorService.translateTableLocation()
  4. Generates ALTER TABLE SET LOCATION SQL pointing to the new target path
  5. If the table was a legacy managed table (detected via HMS_MIRROR_LEGACY_MANAGED_FLAG), sets external.table.purge to true so the target takes data ownership

docs.title: Partitioned Table Handling
docs.text: When CONVERT_LINKED encounters a partitioned table, it takes a different path: it drops the existing RIGHT table and delegates to the SCHEMA_ONLY strategy to rebuild it from scratch. Before dropping, it removes EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE if set (to prevent data deletion on drop). This is necessary because partition locations cannot be individually updated via a single ALTER TABLE SET LOCATION.

docs.title: Schema Translation Details
docs.text: Aspect Behavior Location Translated from source namespace to target namespace (this is the core operation) EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE Set to true when converting legacy managed tables (detected via hms-mirror_LegacyManaged flag) Table structure Preserved from the existing linked table (non-partitioned) or rebuilt via SCHEMA_ONLY (partitioned) AVRO schema URLs Checked via AVROCheck() before proceeding Statistics Not explicitly refreshed for non-partitioned conversion

docs.title: Data Mapping
docs.text:
• NO data movement -- assumes data was copied externally (e.g., distcp) before this strategy runs
  • The translated location must match where the external copy placed the data
  • After conversion, the RIGHT cluster owns its copy of the data and no longer depends on LEFT cluster storage access
  • If data has not been copied to the target namespace, the table will point to a nonexistent location

docs.title: Configuration Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Effect warehouse.external / warehouse.managed Target directories used for location translation Translator location maps Custom path mappings for non-standard layouts

Minimal configuration is required beyond what was set for the original LINKED run. The translator must be configured so that the source locations map correctly to the target namespace.

docs.title: Where This Strategy Is NOT Applicable
docs.text:
• ACID tables -- LINKED does not support ACID, so there is nothing to convert. The strategy explicitly checks TableUtils.isACID() and rejects ACID tables.
  • Tables not previously created by LINKED -- If the RIGHT table does not exist, the strategy issues an error: "Table doesn't exist. To transfer, run 'SCHEMA_ONLY'".
  • When data has not been copied -- This strategy does not move data. If the target location is empty, queries against the converted table will return no results.
  • Views and non-native tables -- Only Hive-native external tables created by LINKED are eligible.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
1. Phase 2 of gradual migration -- After LINKED provided immediate query access and data was independently copied via distcp, CONVERT_LINKED updates the table metadata to point to the local copy. This is the designed two-phase migration workflow.
  2. Migration cutover -- Switch linked tables from remote storage to local storage during a planned maintenance window. Run LINKED for all tables, validate access, copy data at your own pace, then run CONVERT_LINKED to finalize.
  3. Hybrid migration schedules -- Different tables may be ready for cutover at different times. CONVERT_LINKED can be run iteratively against subsets of tables as their data copies complete.

docs.title: (a) Non-Partitioned Table: ALTER TABLE SET LOCATION
docs.text:
USE target_db;
ALTER TABLE orders SET LOCATION "s3a://target-bucket/warehouse/external/target_db.db/orders";

docs.title: (b) Legacy Managed Table: SET LOCATION + EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE
docs.text:
When the table was originally a legacy managed table (detected by the hms-mirror_LegacyManaged property):

USE target_db;
ALTER TABLE orders SET LOCATION "s3a://target-bucket/warehouse/external/target_db.db/orders";
ALTER TABLE orders SET TBLPROPERTIES ("external.table.purge"="true");

docs.title: (c) Partitioned Table: DROP and RECREATE via SCHEMA_ONLY
docs.text:
For partitioned tables, the strategy removes purge protection, drops the existing linked table, and delegates to SCHEMA_ONLY:

USE target_db;
ALTER TABLE orders_partitioned UNSET TBLPROPERTIES ("external.table.purge");
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders_partitioned;
-- (followed by SCHEMA_ONLY generated DDL with translated locations)

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
build()
  │
  ├── Set strategy = CONVERT_LINKED
  ├── Check RIGHT table exists ──► if not: error "run SCHEMA_ONLY"
  ├── Check not ACID ──► if ACID: error "not eligible"
  │
  ├── [Partitioned?]
  │     YES ──► Remove EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE
  │             DROP TABLE
  │             Delegate to SchemaOnlyDataStrategy.build()
  │
  │     NO ──► AVROCheck()
  │            Translate location via TranslatorService
  │            ALTER TABLE SET LOCATION
  │            Check HMS_MIRROR_LEGACY_MANAGED_FLAG
  │              └── if set: ADD EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE
  │
  └── return success/failure

execute()
  └── tableService.runTableSql(tableMirror, Environment.RIGHT)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Data Strategy Overview
  • LINKED Strategy -- the first phase that CONVERT_LINKED completes
  • SCHEMA_ONLY Strategy -- used internally for partitioned table reconstruction

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Creates table definitions on the target cluster that point to the same storage location as the source cluster. Both clusters share the same physical data. Unlike LINKED (which implies the target is borrowing temporary access to source data), COMMON implies a designed shared-storage architecture where both clusters legitimately access the same storage layer.

This strategy registers existing tables in a second metastore without moving any data or translating any paths.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Reads table definition from LEFT cluster
  2. Validates the table is Hive-native and not ACID
  3. Builds a CopySpec with replaceLocation = false (preserving source paths)
  4. If the target is CDP (non-legacy), upgrades legacy managed tables to EXTERNAL with EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE
  5. Creates the table on RIGHT using the same storage location as LEFT
  6. For partitioned tables, generates partition registration SQL (explicit ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION or MSCK REPAIR TABLE)

docs.title: Sync Mode
docs.text:
When sync is enabled, COMMON supports incremental schema management:

  • If LEFT exists and RIGHT does not: CREATE the table on RIGHT
  • If both exist and schemas match: no action (skip with message)
  • If both exist and schemas differ: REPLACE (DROP + CREATE) on RIGHT, unless RIGHT has EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE set (which blocks replacement to prevent accidental data deletion)
  • In sync mode, takeOwnership is forced to false to prevent either side from claiming purge rights over shared data

docs.title: Non-Sync Mode
docs.text:
Without sync:

  • If RIGHT already exists: no action (skip)
  • If RIGHT does not exist: CREATE on RIGHT

docs.title: Schema Translation Details
docs.text: Aspect Behavior Locations NOT translated -- same paths used on both clusters (replaceLocation = false) Table type Forced to EXTERNAL (via upgrade path if source is legacy managed) EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE Set based on takeOwnership logic: true unless readOnly or noPurge is set. Forced false in sync mode. ACID properties NOT supported -- ACID tables are rejected before build Statistics properties Stripped (standard behavior via CopySpec) HMS_MIRROR_METADATA_FLAG Added to mark the table as migrated by HMS-Mirror Partition locations Preserved as-is from source (no translation) Owner Transferred via ALTER TABLE SET OWNER when ownershipTransfer.table is enabled and target is non-legacy

docs.title: Data Mapping
docs.text:
• NO data movement -- both clusters read/write the same physical storage
  • BOTH clusters can potentially modify the data (no isolation)
  • Changes from either cluster are immediately visible to the other
  • The storage layer must support concurrent access from multiple compute engines
  • When takeOwnership is true, RIGHT can delete data on table drop (via EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE)

docs.title: Configuration Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Effect readOnly If true, disables takeOwnership (RIGHT cannot delete shared data) noPurge If true, disables takeOwnership regardless of other settings sync Enables incremental schema management (compare and update existing tables) createIfNotExists Adds IF NOT EXISTS to DDL ownershipTransfer.table If true, transfers table owner from LEFT to RIGHT loadMetadataDetails If true, generates explicit partition ADD statements; otherwise uses MSCK REPAIR Warehouse settings NOT used -- locations are not translated in COMMON

docs.title: Where This Strategy Is NOT Applicable
docs.text:
• ACID/transactional tables -- Concurrent ACID access from multiple metastores is unsafe. The strategy explicitly rejects ACID tables with the message: "Can't use COMMON for ACID tables".
  • When data isolation is required -- Both clusters share the same physical data. Writes from one cluster are immediately visible to (and can conflict with) the other.
  • When clusters use different storage systems -- COMMON requires both clusters to access the same storage namespace (e.g., both reading from the same S3 bucket or HDFS cluster).
  • When ownership semantics must be clear -- With shared data, DROP TABLE on one cluster could delete data needed by the other (when EXTERNAL_TABLE_PURGE is set).
  • Views -- Only Hive-native tables are supported.
  • Non-native tables -- Tables backed by HBase, Kafka, etc. are not handled by this strategy.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
1. Dual-compute on shared cloud storage -- Two CDP clusters both reading/writing the same S3/ABFS data lake. Metastore entries registered on both so that either cluster can query the data.
  2. Parallel Hive metastore instances -- Multiple HMS instances over the same HDFS warehouse (e.g., separate dev/prod metastores on shared storage, or a migration from one metastore version to another on the same storage).
  3. Register existing tables in a new metastore -- When standing up a new cluster with access to existing shared storage, use COMMON to populate the new metastore without copying any data.
  4. Read-only secondary cluster -- With readOnly = true, register table definitions on a secondary cluster for read access without granting purge/delete capabilities.

docs.title: (a) External Table on Shared S3 Storage
docs.text:
USE target_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id    BIGINT,
    customer_id BIGINT,
    order_date  STRING,
    total       DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/sales.db/orders'
TBLPROPERTIES (
    "external.table.purge"="true",
    "hms-mirror_Metadata_Stage1"="2026-02-16 12:00:00"
);

docs.title: (b) Shared Storage with readOnly (No Purge)
docs.text:
USE target_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id    BIGINT,
    customer_id BIGINT,
    order_date  STRING,
    total       DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/sales.db/orders'
TBLPROPERTIES (
    "hms-mirror_Metadata_Stage1"="2026-02-16 12:00:00"
);

docs.title: (c) Partitioned Table with Explicit Partition Locations
docs.text:
USE target_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events (
    event_id  BIGINT,
    event_type STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date STRING)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/analytics.db/events'
TBLPROPERTIES (
    "external.table.purge"="true",
    "hms-mirror_Metadata_Stage1"="2026-02-16 12:00:00"
);

ALTER TABLE events ADD IF NOT EXISTS
  PARTITION (event_date='2025-01-01') LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/analytics.db/events/event_date=2025-01-01'
  PARTITION (event_date='2025-02-01') LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/analytics.db/events/event_date=2025-02-01';

docs.title: (d) Sync Mode: Schema Replacement
docs.text:
When sync detects a schema mismatch:

USE target_db;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id    BIGINT,
    customer_id BIGINT,
    order_date  STRING,
    total       DECIMAL(10,2),
    status      STRING           -- new column not in previous schema
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 's3a://shared-bucket/warehouse/sales.db/orders'
TBLPROPERTIES (
    "hms-mirror_Metadata_Stage1"="2026-02-16 12:00:00"
);

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
build()
  │
  ├── Set strategy = COMMON
  ├── Check not ACID ──► if ACID: error "Can't transfer SCHEMA reference on COMMON storage"
  │
  ├── buildOutDefinition()
  │     ├── Validate table is Hive-native and not ACID
  │     ├── Create CopySpec(LEFT → RIGHT, replaceLocation=false)
  │     ├── Set upgrade=true if convertManaged
  │     ├── Set takeOwnership based on readOnly/noPurge flags
  │     ├── [Sync mode?]
  │     │     ├── Both exist, schemas match → LEAVE (no action)
  │     │     ├── Both exist, schemas differ → REPLACE (unless PURGE set)
  │     │     ├── LEFT exists, RIGHT missing → CREATE
  │     │     └── Force takeOwnership=false
  │     ├── [Non-sync mode?]
  │     │     ├── RIGHT exists → LEAVE (no action)
  │     │     └── RIGHT missing → CREATE
  │     └── buildTableSchema(copySpec)
  │
  ├── buildOutSql()
  │     ├── Generate USE database
  │     ├── [CreateStrategy]
  │     │     ├── NOTHING/LEAVE → no SQL
  │     │     ├── DROP → DROP TABLE
  │     │     ├── REPLACE → DROP TABLE + CREATE TABLE
  │     │     └── CREATE → CREATE TABLE (+ SET OWNER if configured)
  │     └── [Partitioned?]
  │           ├── loadMetadataDetails → explicit ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION
  │           └── partitionDiscoveryInitMSCK → MSCK REPAIR TABLE
  │
  └── return success/failure

execute()
  └── tableService.runTableSql(tableMirror, Environment.RIGHT)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Data Strategy Overview
  • LINKED Strategy -- similar concept but implies temporary/borrowed access
  • SCHEMA_ONLY Strategy -- similar approach but with location translation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Extracts DDL (CREATE TABLE) statements from the source cluster without executing anything on any cluster. Produces SQL scripts for review, archival, or manual execution. This is a read-only, zero-risk strategy -- it only reads metadata and writes output files.

DUMP is the simplest strategy in HMS-Mirror. It requires minimal configuration and makes no changes to either cluster.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Reads table definitions from the LEFT cluster
  2. Standardizes the definition (strips database prefix from table names)
  3. For non-legacy ACID tables, strips the LOCATION property (since ACID location is managed by the metastore)
  4. Generates CREATE TABLE DDL and writes it to the LEFT environment's SQL output
  5. For partitioned non-ACID tables, generates partition registration SQL
  6. The execute() method is a no-op -- it returns true without executing any SQL

docs.title: Key Distinction
docs.text: Unlike all other strategies, DUMP writes SQL to the LEFT environment's output (not RIGHT). There is no target cluster -- the output is purely for documentation or manual use.

docs.title: Schema Translation Details
docs.text: Aspect Behavior Locations Output as-is from the source cluster (no translation) Table properties Included as-is ACID properties Included as-is; LOCATION stripped for non-legacy ACID tables Database prefix Stripped from table names in CREATE statements Partition metadata Included if loaded (explicit ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION) or via MSCK REPAIR TABLE Views DDL included (when migrateVIEW is enabled) Non-native tables DDL included (when migrateNonNative is enabled) Owner Transferred via ALTER TABLE SET OWNER when ownershipTransfer.table is enabled and source is non-legacy

docs.title: Data Mapping
docs.text:
• NO data movement
  • NO schema changes on any cluster
  • NO SQL execution -- the execute() method is a no-op
  • Read-only metadata extraction written to output report files

docs.title: Configuration Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Effect migrateVIEW Include view DDL in output migrateNonNative Include non-native table DDL (HBase, Kafka-backed tables) ownershipTransfer.table Include ALTER TABLE SET OWNER in output loadMetadataDetails Load partition metadata for explicit partition DDL transfer.storageMigration.distcp When true, partition repair SQL is placed in cleanup SQL section

This is the lowest-configuration strategy -- it only needs a connection to the source cluster (LEFT). No RIGHT cluster, warehouse directories, or translator configuration is required.

docs.title: Where This Strategy Is NOT Applicable
docs.text:
• When you need actual migration -- DUMP does not create tables or move data on any cluster.
  • When you need location translation -- DUMP preserves source paths exactly as they are. Use SCHEMA_ONLY or other strategies for path translation.
  • When you need automated execution -- DUMP generates scripts but does not run them. It is a reporting tool, not a migration tool.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
1. Schema documentation -- Extract all table definitions for documentation, compliance audits, or governance inventories.
  2. Pre-migration analysis -- Review what tables exist, their types, locations, properties, and partition structures before planning a migration strategy.
  3. Disaster recovery planning -- Archive DDL scripts as part of a backup strategy. In case of metastore corruption, the DDL output can be used to recreate table definitions.
  4. Manual migration -- Generate scripts for teams that prefer to review, modify, and execute SQL manually rather than using automated migration.
  5. Table inventory -- Catalog all tables, their types, storage formats, locations, and properties across a cluster.
  6. Migration validation -- Compare DUMP output before and after a migration to verify that table definitions were correctly transferred.

docs.title: (a) Simple External Table
docs.text:
USE sales_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id    BIGINT,
    customer_id BIGINT,
    order_date  STRING,
    total       DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders';

docs.title: (b) ACID Table (Non-Legacy -- Location Stripped)
docs.text:
For non-legacy (Hive 3) ACID tables, the LOCATION clause is removed since it is managed by the metastore:

USE inventory_db;
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS stock_levels (
    product_id   BIGINT,
    warehouse_id BIGINT,
    quantity     INT,
    updated_at   TIMESTAMP
)
CLUSTERED BY (product_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES (
    "transactional"="true",
    "transactional_properties"="default"
);

docs.title: (c) View
docs.text:
USE sales_db;
CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS active_orders AS
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';

docs.title: (d) Partitioned Table with Explicit Partition Metadata
docs.text:
When loadMetadataDetails is enabled:

USE analytics_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events (
    event_id   BIGINT,
    event_type STRING,
    payload    STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date STRING)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/analytics_db.db/events';

ALTER TABLE events ADD IF NOT EXISTS
  PARTITION (event_date='2025-01-01') LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/analytics_db.db/events/event_date=2025-01-01'
  PARTITION (event_date='2025-02-01') LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/analytics_db.db/events/event_date=2025-02-01';

docs.title: (e) Partitioned Table with MSCK REPAIR
docs.text:
When partitionDiscoveryInitMSCK is enabled instead of loading metadata details:

USE analytics_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events (
    event_id   BIGINT,
    event_type STRING,
    payload    STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date STRING)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/analytics_db.db/events';

MSCK REPAIR TABLE events;

docs.title: (f) Table with Owner Transfer
docs.text:
When ownershipTransfer.table is enabled:

USE sales_db;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id    BIGINT,
    customer_id BIGINT,
    order_date  STRING,
    total       DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders';

ALTER TABLE orders SET OWNER USER data_engineering;

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
build()
  │
  ├── Set strategy = DUMP
  │
  ├── buildOutDefinition()
  │     ├── Strip database prefix from table name
  │     └── [Non-legacy ACID?] Strip LOCATION property
  │
  └── buildOutSql()
        ├── Generate USE database
        ├── Generate CREATE TABLE statement (written to LEFT SQL)
        ├── [ownershipTransfer?] Generate ALTER TABLE SET OWNER
        └── [Partitioned and non-ACID?]
              ├── [partitionDiscoveryInitMSCK] → MSCK REPAIR TABLE
              └── [loadMetadataDetails] → ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION statements

execute()
  └── return true (no-op)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Data Strategy Overview
  • SCHEMA_ONLY Strategy -- similar extraction but with translation and execution

docs.title: Driver Management
docs.text: HMS-Mirror requires JDBC drivers to connect to Hive clusters and metastore databases. This section explains how to obtain, install, and configure these drivers.

docs.title: Hive JDBC Drivers
docs.text:
Configure drivers for HiveServer2 connections:

  • Platform-specific drivers for CDP, HDP, CDH, and Apache Hive
  • Download and installation procedures
  • Driver selection based on cluster version
  • Connection string formats
  • Troubleshooting driver issues

docs.title: Metastore JDBC Drivers
docs.text:
Configure drivers for direct metastore database access:

  • MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and MariaDB drivers
  • Download sources and versions
  • Installation locations
  • Direct metastore connection setup
  • When to use metastore direct access

docs.title: Driver Locations
docs.text:
After installation, drivers are organized in ~/.hms-mirror/:

~/.hms-mirror/
├── drivers/              # Hive JDBC drivers (by platform)
│   ├── cdp/              # Cloudera Data Platform drivers
│   ├── hdp/              # Hortonworks Data Platform drivers
│   ├── cdh/              # Cloudera Distribution for Hadoop drivers
│   └── apache/           # Apache Hive open-source drivers
│
└── aux_libs/             # Metastore JDBC drivers
    ├── mysql-connector-j-x.x.x.jar
    ├── postgresql-x.x.x.jar
    ├── mariadb-java-client-x.x.x.jar
    └── ojdbc11-x.x.x.jar

docs.title: Download Scripts
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror includes helper scripts to download drivers:

# Download Hive JDBC drivers for your platform
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-hive-drivers.sh

# Download Metastore JDBC drivers
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-metastore-drivers.sh

docs.title: Manual Installation
docs.text:
If automatic download isn't available:

  1. Hive Drivers: Copy from your cluster's client libraries or download from vendor
  2. Metastore Drivers: Download from Maven Central or vendor sites

docs.title: Driver Compatibility Matrix
docs.text: Cluster Type Hive Driver Location Recommended Version CDP 7.x drivers/cdp/ Match CDP version HDP 3.x drivers/hdp/ HiveJDBC4 HDP 2.x drivers/hdp/ HiveJDBC41 CDH 6.x drivers/cdh/ Cloudera JDBC CDH 5.x drivers/cdh/ Cloudera JDBC Apache Hive drivers/apache/ Match Hive version Metastore DB Driver Maven Coordinates MySQL MySQL Connector/J mysql:mysql-connector-java PostgreSQL PostgreSQL JDBC org.postgresql:postgresql MariaDB MariaDB Connector org.mariadb.jdbc:mariadb-java-client Oracle Oracle JDBC com.oracle.database.jdbc:ojdbc11

docs.title: "No suitable driver found"
docs.text:
• Verify driver JAR is in the correct location
  • Check driver class name in connection configuration
  • Ensure JAR file permissions allow reading

docs.title: "Driver version mismatch"
docs.text:
• Match driver version to cluster version
  • Check for conflicting driver versions
  • Review cluster documentation for compatible versions

docs.title: "Class not found"
docs.text:
• Driver JAR may be corrupted - re-download
  • Check classpath configuration
  • Verify JAR contains expected classes

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Hive Drivers - Set up HiveServer2 connectivity
  • Metastore Drivers - Configure direct metastore access
  • Connections - Create cluster connections using drivers
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve driver issues

docs.title: Hive Driver Management
docs.text: This guide covers the automated Hive JDBC driver management system in HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides a fully automated driver management system through the web UI. Users can download and install Hive JDBC drivers for various Hadoop platforms with a single click. The application automatically selects the correct driver based on your connection's platform type.

docs.title: Accessing Driver Management
docs.text: Navigate to Utilities > Drivers in the web UI to access the driver management page. The page has two tabs: - Hive Drivers - For HiveServer2 connections - Metastore RDBMS Drivers - For direct Metastore database connections

docs.title: Available Hive Drivers
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror supports drivers for all major Hadoop platforms:

Driver Version Supported Platforms Apache Hive 2 2.3.10 Apache Hive 2.x Apache Hive 3 3.1.3 Apache Hive 3.x, EMR 6, EMR 7 Apache Hive 4 4.0.1 Apache Hive 4.0.x Apache Hive 4.1 4.1.0 Apache Hive 4.1.x CDH 5 1.1.0-cdh5.16.99 Cloudera CDH 5.x CDH 6 2.1.1-cdh6.3.4 Cloudera CDH 6.x HDP 2 1.2.1000.2.6.5.0-292 Hortonworks HDP 2.x CDP 7.1.9 3.1.3000.7.1.9.1059-4 HDP 3.x, CDP 7.0, CDP 7.1, CDP 7.1.9 SP1 CDP 7.3.1 3.1.3000.7.3.1.404-1 CDP 7.2, CDP 7.3

docs.title: From the Web UI
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Utilities > Drivers
  2. Select the Hive Drivers tab
  3. Find the driver for your platform
  4. Click Download (or Re-install if previously downloaded)
  5. Wait for the download to complete
  6. A green checkmark indicates successful installation

docs.title: What Gets Downloaded
docs.text:
When you download a Hive driver, HMS-Mirror automatically downloads: - The Hive JDBC standalone JAR - Required Hadoop dependencies (hadoop-common, hadoop-auth) - Log4j dependencies for compatibility

All files are downloaded from: - Maven Central - Cloudera Repository (for CDP/CDH drivers)

docs.title: Installation Status
docs.text: Each driver shows its current status: - Green checkmark - Driver is installed and ready to use - Download button - Driver needs to be downloaded - Installed files - Shows the JAR files in the driver directory

docs.title: Automatic Driver Selection
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror automatically selects the correct driver based on your connection's Platform Type:

Platform Type Driver Used APACHE_HIVE2 Apache Hive 2 APACHE_HIVE3, EMR6, EMR7 Apache Hive 3 APACHE_HIVE4 Apache Hive 4 CDH5 CDH 5 CDH6 CDH 6 HDP2 HDP 2 HDP3, CDP7_0, CDP7_1, CDP7_1_9_SP1 CDP 7.1.9 CDP7_2, CDP7_3 CDP 7.3.1

When you configure a connection and select a platform type, HMS-Mirror automatically uses the appropriate driver - no manual configuration needed.

docs.title: Driver Storage Location
docs.text:
Drivers are stored in: 

~/.hms-mirror/drivers/

The directory structure is organized by platform: 

~/.hms-mirror/drivers/
├── apache/
│   └── hive/
│       ├── 2/           # Apache Hive 2 driver files
│       ├── 3/           # Apache Hive 3 driver files
│       ├── 4/           # Apache Hive 4 driver files
│       └── 4_1/         # Apache Hive 4.1 driver files
├── cdh/
│   ├── 5/               # CDH 5 driver files
│   └── 6/               # CDH 6 driver files
├── cdp/
│   ├── 7_1_9/           # CDP 7.1.9 driver files
│   └── 7_3_1/           # CDP 7.3.1 driver files
└── hdp/
    └── 2/               # HDP 2 driver files

docs.title: Connection Driver Verification
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror verifies driver availability when you work with connections:

  1. When creating or editing a connection, the system checks if the required driver is installed
  2. If a driver is missing, you'll be notified and can download it from the Drivers page
  3. The connection test will fail if the required driver is not installed

docs.title: Refreshing/Re-installing Drivers
docs.text:
If you encounter issues with a driver (corrupted files, partial downloads), you can refresh it:

  1. Navigate to Utilities > Drivers
  2. Find the problematic driver
  3. Click Re-install
  4. The system will delete the existing files and download fresh copies

docs.title: Driver Download Failed
docs.text:
Causes: - Network connectivity issues - Repository temporarily unavailable - Firewall blocking access

Solutions: 1. Check your network connection 2. Verify you can reach Maven Central and Cloudera repositories 3. Try the download again 4. Check HMS-Mirror logs for detailed error messages

docs.title: Partial Download
docs.text: If only some files downloaded successfully: 1. The status will show as partial 2. Click Re-install to delete and re-download all files 3. Check logs for specific file failures

docs.title: Connection Test Fails with Driver Error
docs.text:
Error: "No suitable driver found" or "ClassNotFoundException"

Solutions: 1. Go to Utilities > Drivers 2. Verify the required driver shows a green checkmark 3. If not installed, click Download 4. Ensure the connection's Platform Type is set correctly

docs.title: Wrong Driver Being Used
docs.text: If HMS-Mirror is using the wrong driver: 1. Check your connection's Platform Type setting 2. The platform type determines which driver is loaded 3. Update the platform type to match your cluster

docs.title: Driver Isolation
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror uses ClassLoader isolation to: - Load multiple driver versions simultaneously - Prevent conflicts between different driver versions - Support connections to clusters with different Hive versions in the same session

This means you can connect to both an HDP 2.x cluster and a CDP 7.3 cluster in the same HMS-Mirror session without driver conflicts.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Download drivers before creating connections - Ensures smooth connection setup
  2. Use the correct Platform Type - This determines driver selection
  3. Keep drivers updated - Re-download if you experience issues
  4. Check driver status - Verify green checkmarks before troubleshooting connection issues

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Metastore Driver Management - Configure database drivers
  • Managing Connections - Create cluster connections
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve common issues

docs.title: Metastore Driver Management
docs.text: This guide covers the automated Metastore RDBMS driver management system in HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides a fully automated driver management system for Metastore database connections through the web UI. Users can download and install JDBC drivers for MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and MariaDB with a single click, choosing from multiple available versions.

docs.title: Accessing Driver Management
docs.text: Navigate to Utilities > Drivers in the web UI and select the Metastore RDBMS Drivers tab.

docs.title: When Metastore Direct is Needed
docs.text: Direct Metastore database access is optional but enables: - Faster metadata retrieval for large catalogs - Access to extended metadata not available through HiveServer2 - Partition discovery optimization - Schema validation

docs.title: Available Drivers
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror supports drivers for all common Metastore databases:

Database Driver Default Version MySQL MySQL Connector/J 8.2.0 PostgreSQL PostgreSQL JDBC Driver 42.7.2 Oracle Oracle JDBC (ojdbc11) 23.9.0.25.07 MariaDB MariaDB Connector/J 3.5.6

docs.title: From the Web UI
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Utilities > Drivers
  2. Select the Metastore RDBMS Drivers tab
  3. Expand the driver for your database type
  4. Browse available versions (newest first)
  5. Click Download next to your desired version
  6. A green checkmark indicates successful installation

docs.title: Multiple Versions
docs.text: Unlike Hive drivers, you can install multiple versions of the same Metastore driver: - Each version is stored in its own directory - Useful for testing compatibility - The default version is highlighted in the UI

docs.title: Version Discovery
docs.text: HMS-Mirror automatically queries Maven Central to show available versions: - Up to 50 versions displayed per driver - Versions sorted newest first - Default version from HMS-Mirror configuration highlighted

docs.title: Driver Storage Location
docs.text:
Drivers are stored in: 

~/.hms-mirror/drivers/

The directory structure is organized by database and version: 

~/.hms-mirror/drivers/
├── mysql/
│   ├── 8.2.0/
│   │   └── mysql-connector-j-8.2.0.jar
│   └── 8.1.0/
│       └── mysql-connector-j-8.1.0.jar
├── postgres/
│   └── 42.7.2/
│       └── postgresql-42.7.2.jar
├── oracle/
│   └── 23.9.0.25.07/
│       └── ojdbc11-23.9.0.25.07.jar
└── mariadb/
    └── 3.5.6/
        └── mariadb-java-client-3.5.6.jar

docs.title: Connection Configuration
docs.text:
When you configure a Metastore Direct connection, you need to specify: - Metastore Type: MYSQL, POSTGRESQL, ORACLE, or MARIADB - URI: JDBC connection string - Username: Database user - Password: Database password

HMS-Mirror automatically uses the appropriate driver based on the Metastore Type.

docs.title: JDBC Driver Classes
docs.text: Database Driver Class MySQL com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver PostgreSQL org.postgresql.Driver Oracle oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver MariaDB org.mariadb.jdbc.Driver

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text:
jdbc:mysql://hostname:3306/hive_metastore

With SSL: 

jdbc:mysql://hostname:3306/hive_metastore?useSSL=true&requireSSL=true

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
jdbc:postgresql://hostname:5432/hive_metastore

With SSL: 

jdbc:postgresql://hostname:5432/hive_metastore?ssl=true&sslmode=verify-full

docs.title: Oracle
docs.text:
Standard format: 

jdbc:oracle:thin:@hostname:1521:hive_metastore

Service name format: 

jdbc:oracle:thin:@//hostname:1521/hive_metastore

docs.title: MariaDB
docs.text: jdbc:mariadb://hostname:3306/hive_metastore

docs.title: Installation Status
docs.text: Each driver and version shows its current status: - Green checkmark - Version is installed and ready to use - Download button - Version needs to be downloaded - Default badge - Indicates the default version from HMS-Mirror configuration

docs.title: Refreshing/Re-installing Drivers
docs.text:
If you encounter issues with a driver version:

  1. Navigate to Utilities > Drivers > Metastore RDBMS Drivers
  2. Expand the driver
  3. Find the problematic version
  4. Click Re-install
  5. The system will delete the existing file and download a fresh copy

docs.title: SSL/TLS Configuration
docs.text:
For SSL connections to Metastore databases:

  1. Import the database server's certificate via Utilities > Preferences > Certificates
  2. Configure SSL parameters in your connection URL
  3. See Certificates for detailed setup

docs.title: Driver Download Failed
docs.text:
Causes: - Network connectivity issues - Maven Central temporarily unavailable - Firewall blocking access

Solutions: 1. Check your network connection 2. Verify you can reach Maven Central (repo1.maven.org) 3. Try the download again 4. Check HMS-Mirror logs for detailed error messages

docs.title: Connection Test Fails
docs.text:
Error: "No suitable driver found"

Solutions: 1. Go to Utilities > Drivers > Metastore RDBMS Drivers 2. Verify your database driver shows at least one version with a green checkmark 3. If not installed, download a version 4. Ensure the connection's Metastore Type is set correctly

docs.title: SSL Certificate Errors
docs.text:
Error: "SSL handshake failed" or "certificate validation failed"

Solutions: 1. Import the database server certificate via Utilities > Preferences > Certificates 2. Verify certificate is not expired 3. Check hostname matches certificate CN or SAN 4. See Certificates for detailed setup

docs.title: Authentication Failed
docs.text:
Error: "Access denied for user"

Solutions: 1. Verify username and password are correct 2. Check user has appropriate database permissions 3. Verify user can connect from HMS-Mirror server's IP address

docs.title: Required Database Permissions
docs.text: The database user for Metastore Direct needs read access to Hive Metastore tables:

docs.title: MySQL/MariaDB
docs.text:
GRANT SELECT ON hive_metastore.* TO 'hms_mirror'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE hive_metastore TO hms_mirror;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO hms_mirror;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO hms_mirror;

docs.title: Oracle
docs.text:
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO hms_mirror;
GRANT SELECT ON hive_metastore.TBLS TO hms_mirror;
GRANT SELECT ON hive_metastore.DBS TO hms_mirror;
-- Grant SELECT on other required tables

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Download drivers before configuring connections - Ensures smooth connection setup
  2. Use the default version - Unless you have specific compatibility requirements
  3. Use read-only accounts - Metastore Direct only needs SELECT access
  4. Use SSL for production - Encrypt connections to database servers
  5. Check driver status - Verify green checkmarks before troubleshooting connection issues

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Hive Driver Management - Configure Hive JDBC drivers
  • Managing Connections - Create cluster connections
  • Certificates - Configure SSL certificates
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve common issues

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: This document describes the technical architecture of HMS-Mirror v4, including its components, data flow, and integration points.

docs.title: Web UI (Frontend)
docs.text:
The user interface is a modern React single-page application.

Technology Stack - React 18 with TypeScript - Tailwind CSS for styling - React Router for navigation - Axios for API communication

Key Features - Wizard-driven workflows - Real-time job monitoring - Interactive reports - AI analysis integration

Directory Structure 

frontend/
├── src/
│   ├── components/      # React components
│   │   ├── common/      # Shared components
│   │   ├── config/      # Configuration UI
│   │   ├── connections/ # Connection management
│   │   ├── datasets/    # Dataset management
│   │   ├── jobs/        # Job management
│   │   └── wizards/     # Wizard components
│   ├── api/             # API client code
│   ├── types/           # TypeScript definitions
│   └── utils/           # Utility functions

docs.title: Spring Boot Application (Backend)
docs.text:
The server is a Spring Boot application providing REST APIs and business logic.

Technology Stack - Java 17+ - Spring Boot 3.x - Spring Web for REST APIs - JDBC for database connections

Package Structure 

com.cloudera.utils.hms.mirror/
├── cli/                 # Command-line interface
├── domain/              # Domain models
│   ├── HmsMirrorConfig  # Main configuration
│   ├── TableMirror      # Table migration state
│   ├── DBMirror         # Database migration state
│   └── Cluster          # Cluster connection info
├── service/             # Business services
├── datastrategy/        # Migration strategies
├── connections/         # Connection pooling
├── web/                 # Web controllers
│   └── controller/
│       └── api/v1/      # REST API endpoints
└── repository/          # Data persistence

docs.title: RocksDB Storage
docs.text:
Persistent storage for configurations, jobs, and state.

~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb/
├── connections/     # Saved connections
├── configurations/  # Migration configurations
├── datasets/        # Dataset definitions
├── jobs/            # Job configurations
└── reports/         # Migration reports

Benefits - No external database required - Fast key-value access - Embedded and portable - Automatic compaction

docs.title: Port Reference
docs.text: Service Default Port Protocol HMS-Mirror Web UI 8090 HTTP HiveServer2 (Binary) 10000 Thrift HiveServer2 (HTTP) 10001 HTTP Hive Metastore 9083 Thrift Knox Gateway 8443 HTTPS MySQL 3306 TCP PostgreSQL 5432 TCP Oracle 1521 TCP Kerberos KDC 88 UDP/TCP

docs.title: Firewall Requirements
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror server needs outbound access to:

  1. HiveServer2 on both clusters
  2. Metastore database (if using direct access)
  3. Kerberos KDC (if using Kerberos auth)
  4. Knox Gateway (if accessing via Knox)
  5. LLM endpoints (if using cloud LLM providers)

docs.title: Single Instance Deployment
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Single HMS-Mirror              │
│                                          │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │     Handles All Operations         │ │
│  │  - Web UI                          │ │
│  │  - Job Execution                   │ │
│  │  - Report Generation               │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                          │
│  Suitable for:                           │
│  - Small to medium migrations            │
│  - Development/testing                   │
│  - Single admin user                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Resource Recommendations
docs.text: Workload Memory CPU Storage Small (<100 tables) 2 GB 2 cores 500 MB Medium (<1000 tables) 4 GB 4 cores 2 GB Large (>1000 tables) 8 GB+ 8+ cores 10+ GB

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation Guide - Deploy HMS-Mirror
  • Core Concepts - Understand key concepts
  • Network Requirements - Configure connectivity

docs.title: Command-Line Reference Guide
docs.text: This guide covers installing and running HMS-Mirror components from the command line, including the Web Server, CLI, and Client modules.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Prerequisites
  • Installation
  • Download the Distribution
  • User Installation (Recommended)
  • Root Installation (System-wide)
  • Directory Structure
  • JDBC Drivers
  • Running the Server
  • Running the CLI
  • Running the Client
  • Configuration Files
  • Environment Variables

docs.title: Java Requirements
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror requires Java 17 or higher. The installation scripts will verify your Java version.

# Check your Java version
java -version

# Set JAVA_HOME if needed
export JAVA_HOME=/path/to/java17

docs.title: Network Requirements
docs.text: Ensure network access to your DataLake environments: - HiveServer2: Port 10000 (binary) or 10001 (HTTP) - Metastore: Port 9083 (if using direct access) - Knox Gateway: Port 8443 (if applicable)

docs.title: Download the Distribution
docs.text:
Download the HMS-Mirror distribution archive and extract it:

# Extract the server distribution
tar -xzf hmsm-server-<version>.tar.gz

# Or extract the CLI distribution
tar -xzf hmsm-cli-<version>.tar.gz

docs.title: User Installation (Recommended)
docs.text: User installation places files in ~/.hms-mirror/ and creates symlinks in ~/bin/. This is the recommended approach for most users.

docs.title: Server Installation
docs.text:
cd hmsm-server-<version>
./bin/setup.sh

docs.title: CLI Installation
docs.text:
cd hmsm-cli-<version>
./bin/setup.sh

Installation locations (non-root):

Component Location Binaries ~/.hms-mirror/bin/ Libraries ~/.hms-mirror/lib/ Configurations(Legacy-CLI) ~/.hms-mirror/cfg/ JDBC Drivers ~/.hms-mirror/drivers/ Auxiliary JARs ~/.hms-mirror/aux_libs/ Settings ~/.hms-mirror/settings/ Reports (Legacy-CLI) ~/.hms-mirror/reports/ RocksDB Data ~/.hms-mirror/data/ Logs ~/.hms-mirror/logs/

Add to PATH:

After this installation, symlinks are created in ~/bin/ for user installations to run the server and CLI. 

# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"

docs.title: Root Installation (System-wide)
docs.text:
Running the setup script as root installs to /usr/local/hms-mirror/ and creates symlinks in /usr/local/bin/.

sudo ./bin/setup.sh

Installation locations (root):

Component Location Binaries /usr/local/hms-mirror/bin/ Libraries /usr/local/hms-mirror/lib/ Symlinks /usr/local/bin/hmsm-server, /usr/local/bin/hmsm-cli User Data ~/.hms-mirror/ (per-user)

Note

Even with root installation, user-specific data (configurations, drivers, reports) is stored in each user's ~/.hms-mirror/ directory.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
After installation, the following directory structure is created:

~/.hms-mirror/
├── aux_libs/          # Additional JARs for classpath (e.g., cloud storage libs)
├── cfg/               # Configuration files (YAML configs)
├── drivers/           # JDBC drivers for Hive and metastore databases
│   ├── apache/hive/   # Apache Hive drivers by version
│   ├── cdh/           # CDH Hive drivers
│   ├── cdp/           # CDP Hive drivers
│   ├── hdp/           # HDP Hive drivers
│   ├── mysql/         # MySQL metastore drivers
│   ├── postgres/      # PostgreSQL metastore drivers
│   ├── oracle/        # Oracle metastore drivers
│   └── mariadb/       # MariaDB metastore drivers
├── logs/              # Application logs
├── reports/           # Migration reports and output
├── data/              # RocksDB persistent storage (server only)
├── security/          # SSL certificates and truststores
└── settings/          # Properties files
    ├── cli.properties     # CLI JVM and logging settings
    └── web.properties     # Server JVM and logging settings

docs.title: JDBC Drivers
docs.text: HMS-Mirror requires JDBC drivers to connect to Hive and metastore databases.

docs.title: Download Hive Drivers
docs.text:
For the Legacy CLI interface, use the provided script to download Hive JDBC drivers:

# Download all Hive drivers
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-hive-drivers.sh

# Download to custom location
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-hive-drivers.sh -d /custom/path/drivers

# Download specific drivers only
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-hive-drivers.sh -s cdp7_1_9,cdp7_3_1

Available Hive driver types:

Type Platform Hive Version apache2 Apache Hive 2.3.10 apache3 Apache Hive 3.1.3 apache4 Apache Hive 4.0.1 apache4_1 Apache Hive 4.1.0 cdh5 Cloudera CDH 5 1.1.0-cdh5.16.99 cdh6 Cloudera CDH 6 2.1.1-cdh6.3.4 hdp2 Hortonworks HDP 2 1.2.1000.2.6.5.0-292 cdp7_1_9 Cloudera CDP 7.1.9 3.1.3000.7.1.9.1059-4 cdp7_3_1 Cloudera CDP 7.3.1 3.1.3000.7.3.1.404-1

docs.title: Download Metastore Drivers
docs.text:
Download JDBC drivers for metastore database connections:

# Download all metastore drivers
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-metastore-drivers.sh

# Download specific drivers
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-metastore-drivers.sh -s mysql,postgres

# Download with custom version
~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-metastore-drivers.sh -s mysql -v mysql:8.0.33

Available metastore driver types:

Type Driver Default Version mysql MySQL Connector/J 8.2.0 postgres PostgreSQL JDBC 42.7.2 oracle Oracle ojdbc11 23.9.0.25.07 mariadb MariaDB Connector/J 3.5.6

docs.title: Running the Server
docs.text: The HMS-Mirror web server provides the web UI and REST API.

docs.title: Starting the Server
docs.text:
# Start the server (runs in background)
hmsm-server

# Start with custom port
hmsm-server --server.port=8091

# Start with custom output directory
hmsm-server -o /path/to/reports

docs.title: Stopping the Server
docs.text:
# Stop the running server
hmsm-server --stop

docs.title: Server Command-Line Options
docs.text: Option Description --server.port=PORT Set the HTTP port (default: 8090) -o, --output-dir DIR Set the report output directory --aux-libs DIR Set auxiliary libraries directory --show-cp Display the classpath --stop Stop the running server -DKEY=VALUE Pass JVM system properties -CP PATH Add to classpath

docs.title: Accessing the Web UI
docs.text:
After starting, access the web interface at:

http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror

docs.title: Server JVM Configuration
docs.text:
Manage JVM settings through the Web UI under Preferences / Utilities / JVM. Settings are stored in ~/.hms-mirror/settings/web.properties:

# JVM Settings
jvm.initial.heap.size=4096m
jvm.max.heap.size=8192m
jvm.garbage.collector=G1GC
jvm.prefer.ipv4.stack=true
jvm.additional.options=

# Logging
logging.threshold=INFO
logging.file.prefix=hmsm-service
logging.dir=

Note

When using the Embedded Ollama LLM, the LLM runs as a separate native process and its memory is managed by the operating system, not the JVM.

docs.title: Running the CLI (Legacy)
docs.text: The CLI provides command-line batch migration capabilities.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Run with configuration file
hmsm-cli -cfg /path/to/config.yaml

# Dry run (validate without executing)
hmsm-cli -cfg /path/to/config.yaml --dry-run

# Specify output directory
hmsm-cli -cfg /path/to/config.yaml -o /path/to/reports

docs.title: CLI Command-Line Options
docs.text: Option Description -cfg, --config FILE Configuration file path -o, --output-dir DIR Report output directory --dry-run Validate without executing -db, --database DB Database to process -tf, --table-filter REGEX Table name filter (regex) -edbf, --exclude-database-filter REGEX Exclude databases matching pattern -etf, --exclude-table-filter REGEX Exclude tables matching pattern --aux-libs DIR Auxiliary libraries directory --show-cp Display classpath -p, --password PWD Encrypt a password -dp, --decrypt-password PWD Decrypt a password -DKEY=VALUE Pass JVM system properties

docs.title: Password Encryption
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror supports encrypted passwords in configuration files:

# Encrypt a password
hmsm-cli -p 'mypassword'

# Decrypt a password
hmsm-cli -dp 'encrypted_password_string'

Use the encrypted output in your configuration file.

docs.title: CLI JVM Configuration
docs.text:
JVM settings are controlled via ~/.hms-mirror/settings/cli.properties:

# JVM Settings
jvm.initial.heap.size=4096m
jvm.max.heap.size=8192m
jvm.garbage.collector=G1GC
jvm.prefer.ipv4.stack=true
jvm.additional.options=

# Logging
logging.threshold=INFO
logging.file.prefix=hmsm-cli
logging.dir=

docs.title: CLI Example Configurations
docs.text:
High-memory for large migrations: 

jvm.initial.heap.size=8g
jvm.max.heap.size=16g
jvm.garbage.collector=G1GC

Debug mode: 

logging.threshold=DEBUG
jvm.additional.options=-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError

Kerberos: 

jvm.additional.options=-Djava.security.krb5.conf=/etc/krb5.conf -Dsun.security.krb5.debug=true

Custom SSL truststore: 

jvm.additional.options=-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStore=/path/to/truststore.jks -Djavax.net.ssl.trustStorePassword=changeit

docs.title: Running the Client
docs.text: The client module provides a Java library for programmatic access to HMS-Mirror server APIs.

docs.title: Including in Your Project
docs.text:
Add the client library to your project:

Maven: 

<dependency>
    <groupId>com.cloudera.utils.hms</groupId>
    <artifactId>hms-mirror-client</artifactId>
    <version>4.0.0.x</version>
</dependency>

docs.title: Using the Client JAR
docs.text:
The client is distributed as a fat JAR containing all dependencies:

java -cp hms-mirror-client-<version>.jar com.example.YourClass

docs.title: Configuration File Location for Legacy CLI
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror looks for configuration files in these locations:

  1. Path specified via -cfg option
  2. ~/.hms-mirror/cfg/
  3. Current working directory

docs.title: Sample Configuration
docs.text:
Create a YAML configuration file:

# Sample HMS-Mirror configuration
clusters:
  LEFT:
    hiveServer2:
      uri: "jdbc:hive2://left-hs2.example.com:10000/default"
      driverClassName: "org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver"
    partitionDiscovery:
      auto: true
      initMSCK: true

  RIGHT:
    hiveServer2:
      uri: "jdbc:hive2://right-hs2.example.com:10000/default"
      driverClassName: "org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver"
    partitionDiscovery:
      auto: true
      initMSCK: true

transfer:
  warehouse:
    managedDirectory: "/warehouse/tablespace/managed/hive"
    externalDirectory: "/warehouse/tablespace/external/hive"

databases:
  - "my_database"

docs.title: JAVA_HOME
docs.text:
Specifies the Java installation to use:

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk

docs.title: JAVA_OPTS
docs.text:
Overrides all JVM settings from properties files. Note that JVM memory settings and garbage collectors are setting in the above properties files.

export JAVA_OPTS="xxxx"

docs.title: HMS_MIRROR_AUX_LIBS
docs.text:
Specifies the auxiliary libraries directory:

export HMS_MIRROR_AUX_LIBS=/custom/path/aux_libs

docs.title: Java Version Error
docs.text:
Error: Java version "1.8.0_xxx" is too old!
Please upgrade to Java 17 or higher

Solution: Install Java 17+ and set JAVA_HOME or update your PATH.

docs.title: Driver Not Found
docs.text:
No suitable driver found for jdbc:hive2://...

Solution: Download the appropriate Hive driver: 

~/.hms-mirror/bin/download-hive-drivers.sh -s cdp7_3_1

docs.title: Permission Denied
docs.text:
Permission denied: /usr/local/hms-mirror/...

Solution: Run as root for system-wide installation, or use user installation (recommended).

docs.title: Process Already Running
docs.text:
Process is already running with PID: xxxxx

Solution: Stop the existing process first: 

hmsm-server --stop

docs.title: Connection Timeout
docs.text:
Solution: Verify network connectivity and increase timeout in connection properties: 

socketTimeout=120000

docs.title: Server Commands
docs.text:
hmsm-server                          # Start server
hmsm-server --stop                   # Stop server
hmsm-server --server.port=8091       # Custom port
hmsm-server -o /path/to/reports      # Custom output directory

docs.title: CLI Commands
docs.text:
hmsm-cli -cfg config.yaml            # Run migration
hmsm-cli -cfg config.yaml --dry-run  # Dry run
hmsm-cli -p 'password'               # Encrypt password

docs.title: Driver Download Commands
docs.text:
download-hive-drivers.sh             # All Hive drivers
download-hive-drivers.sh -s cdp7_3_1 # Specific driver
download-metastore-drivers.sh        # All metastore drivers
download-metastore-drivers.sh -s mysql,postgres  # Specific drivers

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation Guide - Detailed installation instructions
  • Quick Start Tutorial - Get started quickly
  • Connections - Configure cluster connections
  • Data Strategies - Choose the right migration strategy

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to HMS-Mirror! This guide will help you get up and running quickly with Hive metadata and data migrations.

docs.title: What is HMS-Mirror?
docs.text: HMS-Mirror is your go-to tool for migrating Apache Hive tables between DataLake environments. Whether you're upgrading from HDP to CDP, moving to the cloud, or setting up disaster recovery, HMS-Mirror automates the complex process of synchronizing metadata and data.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text: I want to... Go to... Understand what HMS-Mirror does Introduction See what's new in v4 What's New Install HMS-Mirror Installation Guide Complete my first migration Quick Start Tutorial Learn the terminology Core Concepts Choose a migration strategy Data Strategies

docs.title: New User Learning Path
docs.text:
Follow this recommended path to get productive with HMS-Mirror:

┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│   1. Read   │     │ 2. Install  │     │  3. Try the │     │  4. Learn   │
│   Intro     │────►│ HMS-Mirror  │────►│  Tutorial   │────►│  Concepts   │
└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘

docs.title: Step 1: Understand HMS-Mirror
docs.text: Start with the Introduction to understand: - What problems HMS-Mirror solves - Key use cases (cluster migrations, storage migrations, DR) - What's new in version 4

docs.title: Step 2: Install HMS-Mirror
docs.text: Follow the Installation Guide to: - Check system requirements - Download and install HMS-Mirror - Start the server - Verify your installation

docs.title: Step 3: Complete the Quick Start Tutorial
docs.text: Work through the Quick Start Tutorial to: - Create your first connection - Build a configuration - Define a dataset - Run a SCHEMA_ONLY migration

docs.title: Step 4: Learn the Core Concepts
docs.text: Read Core Concepts to understand: - LEFT and RIGHT clusters - Connections, Configurations, Datasets, and Jobs - Data strategies and when to use each

docs.title: Prerequisites Checklist
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Java 11 or higher (Java 17 recommended)
  • Network access to your Hive clusters (HiveServer2 ports)
  • Valid credentials for cluster authentication
  • Kerberos ticket (if your clusters use Kerberos)

docs.title: Experienced User Quick Links
docs.text:
Already familiar with HMS-Mirror? Jump directly to:

  • Data Strategies - Choose the right migration approach
  • Web UI Overview - Navigate the v4 interface
  • Workflows - Step-by-step migration guides
  • Troubleshooting - Solve common problems
  • CLI Reference - Command-line options

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• FAQ - Common questions answered
  • Troubleshooting - Diagnose and fix issues
  • GitHub Issues - Report bugs or request features

Ready to begin? Start with the Introduction to learn what HMS-Mirror can do for you.

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This guide walks you through installing HMS-Mirror v4 and getting the server running. Choose the deployment method that fits your environment.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Minimum Recommended Java JDK 17 JDK 17+ Memory 2 GB 4 GB+ Disk Space 500 MB 2 GB+ (for RocksDB storage) OS Linux, macOS Linux

docs.title: Network Requirements
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror needs network access to your DataLake environments:

  • HiveServer2 Port — Typically 10000 (or 10001 for HTTP transport)
  • Metastore Thrift Port — Typically 9083 (if using direct Metastore access)
  • Knox Gateway — If accessing through Knox (typically 8443)

Ensure firewalls and security groups allow connections from the HMS-Mirror server to these ports on both source and target clusters.

docs.title: Kerberos Requirements (If Applicable)
docs.text:
If your clusters use Kerberos authentication:

  • A valid /etc/krb5.conf file pointing to the correct KDC realm on the HMS-Mirror host
  • Network access to the KDC (Key Distribution Center)

HMS-Mirror manages Kerberos authentication internally — keytabs and credentials are configured through the Web UI under Preferences > Kerberos. There is no need to kinit externally or manage ticket caches outside the application.

docs.title: Deployment Options
docs.text: Method Best For Cloudera Manager Production CDP clusters with CM-managed services Standalone Edge nodes, standalone servers, development Docker Containerized environments, quick evaluation Systemd Service Production Linux servers without CM

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Deployment
docs.text: Deploy HMS-Mirror as a managed service on a CDP cluster using a CSD (Custom Service Descriptor) and Parcel. This is the recommended deployment for CDP environments.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Cloudera Manager 7.0 or later
  • CDP cluster with at least one host available for the HMS-Mirror Server role
  • Admin access to Cloudera Manager
  • JDK 17+ installed on the target host

docs.title: Step 1 — Install the CSD
docs.text:
The CSD tells Cloudera Manager how to configure and manage the HMS-Mirror service. It must be installed before the parcel.

Obtain the CSD JAR (HMS_MIRROR-<version>.jar) from the release artifacts.

  1. Copy the CSD JAR to /opt/cloudera/csd/ on the Cloudera Manager host
  2. Restart the Cloudera Manager Server: 
    sudo systemctl restart cloudera-scm-server
  3. After restart, HMS Mirror will appear as an available service type

docs.title: Step 2 — Install and Activate the Parcel
docs.text:
The parcel contains the HMS-Mirror application binaries that get distributed to cluster hosts.

Obtain the parcel (HMS_MIRROR-<version>-<distro>.parcel) and its SHA checksum file from the release artifacts. Parcel builds are available for EL7, EL8, EL9, and Ubuntu.

  1. Copy the .parcel and .parcel.sha files to /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/ on the Cloudera Manager host
  2. In Cloudera Manager, go to Parcels and click Check for New Parcels
  3. Find HMS_MIRROR in the list
  4. Click Distribute and wait for distribution to complete
  5. Click Activate to make the parcel available for use

docs.title: Step 3 — Add the Service
docs.text:
1. In Cloudera Manager, go to your cluster and click Add Service
  2. Select HMS Mirror from the service list
  3. Assign the HMS Mirror Server role to a host
  4. Configure the service (see Configuration below)
  5. Click Start to launch the service

docs.title: CM Configuration
docs.text: All settings are available in the Cloudera Manager UI under the HMS Mirror service configuration.

docs.title: Core Settings
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Server Port 8090 HTTP/HTTPS port for Web UI and REST API Working Directory /var/lib/hms-mirror Root directory for all data (RocksDB, drivers, certificates, etc.) Log Directory /var/log/hms-mirror Application log files Maximum Concurrent Threads 10 Maximum migration threads (5–100)

docs.title: JVM Settings
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Initial Heap Size 4096 MB Initial JVM heap Maximum Heap Size 8192 MB Maximum JVM heap Garbage Collector G1GC GC algorithm (G1GC, ZGC, ParallelGC) Log Level INFO Application log verbosity (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR)

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Parameter Default Description Authentication Type local local, ldap, or oidc Keystore Password — Master password for encrypting credentials (required)

LDAP and OIDC parameters are also configurable through the CM UI. See Authentication for details.

docs.title: Feature Flags
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Enable Migration Planner false Migration planning and tracking Enable Trino Connections false Trino JDBC connection support Enable LLM Features false AI-assisted analysis Create Sample Data false Create demo data on startup

docs.title: Auto-TLS
docs.text:
When Auto-TLS is enabled on the cluster, HMS-Mirror automatically discovers and uses the cluster's keystores and truststores. The control script searches for:

  • cm-auto-host_keystore.jks — Server keystore (for HTTPS)
  • cm-auto-host_key.pw — Keystore password
  • cm-auto-in_cluster_truststore.jks — In-cluster truststore (preferred)
  • cm-auto-global_truststore.jks — Global truststore (fallback)

No manual certificate configuration is needed when Auto-TLS is active.

For custom certificates, configure the Client Truststore Location and Client Truststore Password in the SSL/TLS section of the service configuration.

docs.title: Web Links
docs.text:
After the service starts, Cloudera Manager provides quick links:

Link URL HMS Mirror Web UI https://<host>:<port>/hms-mirror API Documentation (Swagger) https://<host>:<port>/hms-mirror/swagger-ui.html Health Status https://<host>:<port>/hms-mirror/actuator/health

docs.title: Service Management
docs.text:
Once deployed, manage HMS-Mirror through Cloudera Manager like any other service:

  • Start / Stop / Restart from the service Actions menu
  • View logs from the HMS Mirror Server role Logs tab
  • Monitor health via the built-in health check
  • Collect Diagnostics via the service command
  • Update configuration and restart when needed

docs.title: Upgrading via CM
docs.text:
To upgrade to a new version:

  1. Obtain the new parcel and CSD JAR from the release artifacts
  2. Replace the CSD JAR in /opt/cloudera/csd/ and restart CM Server
  3. Upload the new parcel to /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/
  4. In Cloudera Manager: Distribute and Activate the new parcel, Deactivate the old one
  5. Restart the HMS Mirror service

docs.title: CM Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Service fails to start Check stderr log in CM role instance Logs tab Parcel not visible in CM Verify .parcel and .sha files are in /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/ and click Check for New Parcels CSD not visible after install Restart the Cloudera Manager Server process SSL handshake failures Verify Auto-TLS is active or configure a custom truststore Java version error Ensure the host has JDK 17+ installed LDAP filter errors CM uses USERNAME_PLACEHOLDER instead of {0} — the control script handles the translation automatically

docs.title: Download
docs.text:
Download the latest release (look for the hms-mirror-server-x.x.x.x-dist.tar.gz file):

# Extract the archive
tar -xzf hms-mirror-server-x.x.x.x-dist.tar.gz

# Run the install
hms-mirror-server-install-x.x.x.x/setup.sh

The release package contains: 

hms-mirror-server-install-4.x.x.x/
├── bin/
│   ├── hmsm-server                    # Server startup script
│   └── hcfs-cli                       # HCFS CLI utility
├── lib/
│   └── hms-mirror-server-4.x.x.x-server.jar
└── setup.sh                           # Installation script

After running setup.sh, HMS-Mirror is installed to $HOME/.hms-mirror/:

$HOME/.hms-mirror/
├── bin/                        # Executable scripts
│   ├── hmsm-server             # Server startup script
│   └── hcfs-cli                # HCFS CLI utility
├── lib/                        # Application JARs
│   └── hms-mirror-server-4.x.x.x-server.jar
├── drivers/                    # Platform-specific Hive JDBC drivers
│   ├── cdp/                    # CDP drivers
│   ├── hdp/                    # HDP drivers
│   ├── cdh/                    # CDH drivers
│   └── apache/                 # Apache Hive drivers
├── data/                       # RocksDB persistent storage
├── certificates/               # SSL/TLS certificates and truststores
├── security/                   # PKCS12 keystore
├── settings/                   # Application settings
├── logs/                       # Application logs
├── reports/                    # Migration reports output
├── llms/                       # Local LLM models (if using Ollama)
└── hms-mirror.pid              # Server process ID file

docs.title: Running the Server
docs.text:
After installation, use the hmsm-server script (added to your PATH by setup.sh):

# Start the server
hmsm-server start

# Start with custom port
hmsm-server --server.port=8091

# Stop the server
hmsm-server stop

Info

Windows is not currently supported. HMS-Mirror runs on Linux and macOS.

docs.title: Docker Deployment
docs.text: Run HMS-Mirror in a Docker container. Persistent volume mounts are essential to preserve your data (connections, jobs, configurations, reports) across container restarts.

docs.title: Volumes
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror stores all persistent data in its working directory. You must map a host directory to avoid data loss when the container is recreated.

Container Path Purpose /opt/hms-mirror/data RocksDB database, drivers, certificates, keystore, settings, reports /var/log/hms-mirror Application logs

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Create host directories for persistent storage
mkdir -p ~/.hms-mirror/data ~/.hms-mirror/logs

# Run with persistent storage
docker run -d \
  --name hms-mirror \
  -p 8090:8090 \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/data:/opt/hms-mirror/data \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/logs:/var/log/hms-mirror \
  cloudera/hms-mirror:4.x.x

Open http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror in your browser.

Always Mount Persistent Volumes

Without the -v volume mounts, all data (connections, jobs, configurations, reports, encryption keys) is stored inside the container and will be lost when the container is removed. Always mount host directories for /opt/hms-mirror/data and optionally /var/log/hms-mirror.

docs.title: Kerberos Support
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror manages Kerberos authentication internally — keytabs are uploaded through the Web UI. The only external requirement is a valid krb5.conf pointing to the correct realm:

mkdir -p ~/.hms-mirror/data ~/.hms-mirror/logs

docker run -d \
  --name hms-mirror \
  -p 8090:8090 \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/data:/opt/hms-mirror/data \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/logs:/var/log/hms-mirror \
  -v /etc/krb5.conf:/etc/krb5.conf:ro \
  cloudera/hms-mirror:4.x.x

After starting, upload your keytab and configure the Kerberos principal through Preferences > Kerberos in the Web UI.

docs.title: Custom Truststore
docs.text:
For clusters using self-signed certificates, mount a truststore:

docker run -d \
  --name hms-mirror \
  -p 8090:8090 \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/data:/opt/hms-mirror/data \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/logs:/var/log/hms-mirror \
  -v /etc/krb5.conf:/etc/krb5.conf:ro \
  -v /path/to/truststore.jks:/etc/pki/truststore.jks:ro \
  -e JAVA_OPTS="-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStore=/etc/pki/truststore.jks -Djavax.net.ssl.trustStorePassword=changeit" \
  cloudera/hms-mirror:4.x.x

Or upload certificates through the Web UI under Preferences > Certificates after starting.

docs.title: Configuration via Environment Variables
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name hms-mirror \
  -p 8090:8090 \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/data:/opt/hms-mirror/data \
  -v ~/.hms-mirror/logs:/var/log/hms-mirror \
  -e JAVA_OPTS="-Xmx8g -XX:+UseG1GC" \
  -e HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="your-production-password" \
  cloudera/hms-mirror:4.x.x
Variable Default Description SERVER_PORT 8090 HTTP/HTTPS port JAVA_OPTS — JVM options (heap, GC, etc.) HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD hms-mirror-dev-keystore-password Master password for encrypting credentials

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
version: '3'
services:
  hms-mirror:
    image: cloudera/hms-mirror:4.x.x
    ports:
      - "8090:8090"
    volumes:
      - ./data:/opt/hms-mirror/data
      - ./logs:/var/log/hms-mirror
      - /etc/krb5.conf:/etc/krb5.conf:ro
    environment:
      - JAVA_OPTS=-Xmx8g -XX:+UseG1GC
      - HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=your-production-password
    restart: unless-stopped

docs.title: Managing the Container
docs.text:
docker start hms-mirror       # Start
docker stop hms-mirror        # Stop
docker restart hms-mirror     # Restart
docker logs -f hms-mirror     # Follow logs
docker rm hms-mirror          # Remove (data preserved in mounted volumes)

docs.title: Docker Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Container exits immediately Check logs: docker logs hms-mirror Port conflict Change the -p port mapping Data lost after restart Ensure volume mounts are configured (see Volumes) SSL connection failures Mount a truststore or upload certificates through the Web UI Out of memory Increase heap via JAVA_OPTS and Docker --memory limit

docs.title: Systemd Service
docs.text:
For production Linux deployments without Cloudera Manager:

# Create service file
sudo cat > /etc/systemd/system/hmsm-server.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=HMS-Mirror Server
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=forking
User=hms-mirror
Group=hms-mirror
WorkingDirectory=/home/hms-mirror/.hms-mirror
ExecStart=/home/hms-mirror/.hms-mirror/bin/hmsm-server start
ExecStop=/home/hms-mirror/.hms-mirror/bin/hmsm-server stop
PIDFile=/home/hms-mirror/.hms-mirror/hms-mirror.pid
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=10
Environment="HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=your-production-password"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Reload systemd
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

# Enable and start
sudo systemctl enable hmsm-server
sudo systemctl start hmsm-server

# Check status
sudo systemctl status hmsm-server

# View logs
sudo journalctl -u hmsm-server -f

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Version Java JDK 17+ Apache Maven 3.8+ Node.js 18.x (LTS) npm 9.x+ Git 2.x+

docs.title: Clone and Build
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/cloudera-labs/hms-mirror.git
cd hms-mirror

# Full build (includes frontend and documentation)
mvn clean package -DskipTests

# Build server module only
mvn clean package -pl server -am -DskipTests

# Build the CM parcel (after building server)
mvn clean package -pl parcel -am -DskipTests

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
hms-mirror/
├── pom.xml                 # Parent POM
├── common/                 # Shared domain models and DTOs
├── server/                 # Spring Boot web server
│   ├── pom.xml
│   ├── mkdocs/             # MkDocs documentation
│   └── src/main/
│       ├── java/           # Java source code
│       ├── resources/      # Configuration files
│       └── frontend/       # React web interface
├── client/                 # Java API client
├── csd/                    # Cloudera Manager CSD
│   └── HMS_MIRROR/         # Custom Service Descriptor
└── parcel/                 # Cloudera Manager Parcel
    └── HMS_MIRROR/         # Parcel metadata and scripts

docs.title: Build Artifacts
docs.text:
After a successful build:

server/target/
└── hms-mirror-server-4.x.x.x-server.jar    # Spring Boot executable JAR

csd/HMS_MIRROR/target/
└── HMS_MIRROR-4.x.x.x.jar                  # CSD JAR for Cloudera Manager

parcel/target/
├── HMS_MIRROR-4.x.x.x-el9.parcel           # Parcel for EL9
└── HMS_MIRROR-4.x.x.x-el9.parcel.sha       # SHA checksum

docs.title: Running from Source
docs.text:
# Using Maven (development)
cd server
mvn spring-boot:run

# Using the JAR directly
java -jar server/target/hms-mirror-server-4.x.x.x-server.jar

docs.title: Frontend Development
docs.text:
For active frontend development with hot-reloading:

  1. Start the backend: 

    cd server && mvn spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.arguments="--server.port=8080"

  2. In a separate terminal, start the Vite dev server: 

    cd server/src/main/frontend && npm run dev

  3. Access the application at http://localhost:3000/hms-mirror/

docs.title: First-Time Startup
docs.text:
1. Start the server using any deployment method above

  2. Watch the startup logs: 

    INFO  - Starting HmsMirrorApplication v4.x.x
INFO  - RocksDB initialized at /home/user/.hms-mirror/data
INFO  - Tomcat started on port(s): 8090
INFO  - Started HmsMirrorApplication in 5.123 seconds

  3. Verify the server is running: 

    curl http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health

  4. Open the Web UI at http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror

  5. Register your admin account — the first user registered is automatically granted the ADMIN role

  6. Set up connections — navigate to Connections and configure your source and target clusters

docs.title: Server Won't Start
docs.text:
# Check if port is in use
lsof -i :8090

# Use a different port
java -jar hms-mirror-server.jar --server.port=8091

# Check Java version (17+ required)
java -version

docs.title: Cannot Connect to Clusters
docs.text:
# Test HiveServer2 connectivity
telnet your-cluster.example.com 10000

# Test Metastore connectivity
telnet your-cluster.example.com 9083

docs.title: Kerberos Issues
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror handles Kerberos authentication internally. Ensure:

  • /etc/krb5.conf exists and points to the correct KDC realm
  • Network connectivity to the KDC is available
  • The keytab is uploaded through the Web UI (Preferences > Kerberos)

docs.title: RocksDB Errors
docs.text:
# Check directory permissions
ls -la ~/.hms-mirror/

# Fix permissions
chmod -R 755 ~/.hms-mirror/

# Backup and recreate (will lose saved configurations)
mv ~/.hms-mirror/data ~/.hms-mirror/data.backup
# Restart server to create fresh database

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Quick Start Tutorial — Complete your first migration
  2. Managing Connections — Set up cluster connections
  3. Core Concepts — Understand HMS-Mirror terminology
  4. Master Password — Change from the default keystore password for production

Tip

For production deployments, consider:

  • Changing the master password from the default
  • Enabling TLS/HTTPS for encrypted access
  • Configuring LDAP or OIDC for enterprise authentication
  • Setting up regular RocksDB backups for disaster recovery

docs.title: What is HMS-Mirror?
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror is a powerful tool for migrating and synchronizing Apache Hive metadata and data between DataLake environments powered by Hive and Hive Metastore. Whether you're upgrading from HDP to CDP, moving data to cloud storage, or setting up disaster recovery, HMS-Mirror automates the complex process of Hive table migrations.

HMS-Mirror works by connecting to the Hive Metastore on source (LEFT) and target (RIGHT) clusters, analyzing table structures, and orchestrating the migration of both schema definitions and table data using configurable strategies.

docs.title: Cluster Migrations
docs.text:
HDP to CDP Migration Migrate your entire Hive warehouse from Hortonworks Data Platform (HDP) to Cloudera Data Platform (CDP). HMS-Mirror handles the differences in Hive versions, ACID table formats, and storage configurations automatically.

CDH to CDP Migration Upgrade from Cloudera Distribution for Hadoop (CDH) to the unified Cloudera Data Platform, preserving your existing table structures and data.

On-Premises to Cloud Move your Hive workloads from on-premises clusters to cloud-based deployments (AWS, Azure, GCP) while transforming storage locations and updating table definitions.

docs.title: Storage Migrations
docs.text:
HDFS to Cloud Storage Migrate table data from HDFS to cloud object stores like Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage (ADLS), or Google Cloud Storage (GCS). HMS-Mirror updates all table locations and handles the data movement.

Namespace Changes Move data between HDFS namespaces or storage accounts while preserving table relationships and partition structures.

Warehouse Reorganization Restructure your Hive warehouse directories, consolidating or reorganizing external and managed table locations.

docs.title: Schema Synchronization
docs.text:
Development to Production Synchronize schema changes from development environments to production clusters without affecting existing data.

Multi-Cluster Consistency Keep table definitions consistent across multiple clusters in your data platform.

docs.title: Disaster Recovery
docs.text:
DR Setup Establish disaster recovery environments by replicating schemas and data to standby clusters.

Incremental Sync Perform ongoing synchronization to keep DR clusters up-to-date with production changes.

docs.title: What's New in Version 4
docs.text: HMS-Mirror v4 represents a major evolution of the tool, introducing a modern web-based interface and powerful new capabilities.

docs.title: Web-Based User Interface
docs.text:
The most visible change in v4 is the new web UI. Instead of managing complex configuration files and command-line options, you now have:

  • Visual Configuration - Create and edit configurations through intuitive forms
  • Wizard-Driven Workflows - Step-by-step guidance for creating connections, configurations, and jobs
  • Real-Time Feedback - See validation results and potential issues before running migrations
  • Progress Monitoring - Watch your migrations progress in real-time with detailed status updates

docs.title: Wizard-Driven Workflows
docs.text:
New users can get started quickly with guided wizards:

  • Connection Wizard - Configure cluster connections with guided steps for authentication, HiveServer2, and Metastore settings
  • Configuration Wizard - Build migration configurations by answering questions about your requirements
  • Job Wizard - Assemble jobs by selecting datasets, configurations, and connections
  • Strategy Selection Wizard - Interactive guide to help choose the right data strategy for your use case

docs.title: AI-Powered Analysis
docs.text:
