HMS-Mirror v4 introduces optional LLM (Large Language Model) integration for intelligent analysis:

  • Configuration Analysis - Get AI-powered review of your configuration settings with recommendations and potential issues
  • Job Analysis - Understand what a job will do before running it, with risk assessment and best practices
  • Report Analysis - After migration, get AI-generated insights into results, failures, and recommended actions

Supports multiple LLM providers: - Embedded Ollama (runs locally, no external dependencies) - Local LLM servers (Ollama, LM Studio, LocalAI) - Cloud providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock)

docs.title: Improved Job Management
docs.text:
Jobs in v4 are first-class entities that can be:

  • Saved and Reused - Create a job once, run it multiple times
  • Validated - Check for configuration errors before execution
  • Monitored - Track progress and view detailed results
  • Copied - Duplicate existing jobs as templates for new migrations

docs.title: Persistent Storage with RocksDB
docs.text:
All your configurations, connections, datasets, and jobs are now persisted in RocksDB:

  • No External Database Required - Everything stored locally
  • Fast Access - Embedded key-value store for quick retrieval
  • Portable - Easy backup and migration of your HMS-Mirror setup

docs.title: Modular Architecture
docs.text:
v4 introduces a cleaner separation of concerns:

  • Connections - Define how to connect to clusters (reusable across jobs)
  • Configurations - Define migration behavior and options (reusable across jobs)
  • Datasets - Define what databases and tables to migrate (reusable across jobs)
  • Jobs - Combine connections, configurations, and datasets for execution

This modular approach means you can: - Use the same connection for multiple jobs - Apply the same configuration to different datasets - Mix and match components as needed

docs.title: Enhanced Data Strategies
docs.text:
While the core strategies remain, v4 adds:

  • Iceberg Conversion - Convert Hive tables to Apache Iceberg format during migration
  • Improved ACID Handling - Better support for transactional table migrations
  • Smarter Hybrid Mode - More intelligent automatic strategy selection

docs.title: How HMS-Mirror Works
docs.text:
At a high level, HMS-Mirror operates in these phases:

┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│   LEFT Cluster  │     │   HMS-Mirror    │     │  RIGHT Cluster  │
│    (Source)     │◄────┤     Server      ├────►│    (Target)     │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
        │                       │                       │
        ▼                       ▼                       ▼
   ┌─────────┐           ┌───────────┐           ┌─────────┐
   │Metastore│           │  Analyze  │           │Metastore│
   │   HS2   │           │  Generate │           │   HS2   │
   └─────────┘           │  Execute  │           └─────────┘
                         └───────────┘

  1. Connect - HMS-Mirror establishes connections to source and target clusters via HiveServer2 and/or direct Metastore access

  2. Analyze - Tables in the source cluster are analyzed to determine structure, format, location, and migration requirements

  3. Generate - Based on your configuration and chosen strategy, HMS-Mirror generates the necessary DDL statements and data movement commands

  4. Execute - In execute mode, HMS-Mirror runs the generated commands to create schemas and move data. In dry-run mode, it produces scripts for review

  5. Report - Detailed reports show what was migrated, any errors encountered, and recommendations

docs.title: Source Clusters (LEFT)
docs.text:
• HDP 2.x and 3.x
  • CDH 5.x and 6.x
  • CDP Private Cloud Base
  • CDP Public Cloud

docs.title: Target Clusters (RIGHT)
docs.text:
• CDP Private Cloud Base
  • CDP Public Cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP)
  • Open-source Hive deployments

docs.title: Storage Systems
docs.text:
• HDFS
  • Amazon S3
  • Azure Data Lake Storage (ADLS Gen2)
  • Google Cloud Storage (GCS)
  • Ozone

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to get started? Here's your path forward:

  1. Installation Guide - Install HMS-Mirror and start the server
  2. Quick Start Tutorial - Complete your first migration in 15 minutes
  3. Core Concepts - Understand the key concepts and terminology
  4. Data Strategies - Learn about migration strategies to choose the right one

Note

This documentation covers HMS-Mirror v4 with the web UI. If you're using the legacy CLI-only version, some features may differ. See the CLI Reference for command-line usage.

docs.title: Release Notes
docs.text: This page documents the changes, improvements, and fixes in each release of HMS-Mirror. For upgrade instructions and compatibility information, refer to the specific version notes below.
Release Date: March 2026 Previous Version: 4.1.5.0

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text: This is a major feature release that introduces AI Agent integration with Claude Code CLI, a Cloudera Platform Framework (CPF) plugin for embedding HMS-Mirror inside Cloudera Manager, MCP (Model Context Protocol) security with per-user JWT authentication, a comprehensive CMA-aligned UI redesign, security hardening from a full threat model review, and Docker support for Claude CLI.

docs.title: AI Agent Integration (Claude Code CLI)
docs.text:
• Claude Agent Sessions — New AgentSessionManager and AgentSession provide synchronous Claude CLI interaction for per-user AI analysis
  • Claude CLI Manager — Shared ClaudeCliManager handles CLI detection, context directory deployment, MCP config creation, and API key management
  • AI Analysis for Configs, Jobs, Reports, and Connections — One-click AI analysis across all major entity pages using the agent-based analysis pattern
  • Analysis Tracker — Persistent analysisTracker service preserves AI feedback across page navigation
  • Analysis Banner — Reusable AnalysisBanner component displays AI analysis results inline on any page
  • Chat Session Export — Export Assistant conversations to Markdown with the download button on chat tabs
  • Session Expiry Handling — Chat sessions show timeout banners and disable input for expired sessions
  • Feature Flag Consolidation — Unified hms-mirror.features.llm.enabled flag replaces separate assistant.enabled and llm.enabled flags

docs.title: CPF Plugin (Cloudera Platform Framework)
docs.text:
• PF4J Plugin — HMS-Mirror runs as a headless plugin inside Cloudera Manager Application (CMA), sharing CMA's authentication and navigation
  • DispatcherServlet Router — Lightweight (~180 lines) Spring MVC router replaces the 1,482-line custom request router for dispatching REST calls within the plugin classloader
  • MCP Tool Provider — Plugin implements McpToolProvider to expose HMS-Mirror tools through CPF's native MCP integration
  • Plugin-Mode UI Filtering — Frontend adapts layout (no header, sidebar adjustments) when running inside CMA
  • CM Proxy Client — Plugin routes Cloudera Manager API calls through CPF's built-in proxy instead of direct HTTP

docs.title: MCP Security
docs.text:
• Per-User JWT Authentication — MCP endpoints authenticated via per-conversation JWT tokens with 8-hour TTL
  • MCP Authentication Filter — Dedicated McpAuthenticationFilter validates Bearer tokens on /mcp endpoints
  • WebSocket Authentication — JWT-based WebSocketAuthInterceptor secures Socket.IO connections
  • API Key Management — Self-service API key creation and management via Profile page with RocksDB storage
  • Forwarding Credentials — JwtTokenProvider generates forwarding tokens for MCP session paths, ensuring tool calls run with the originating user's identity

docs.title: Security Hardening (Threat Model)
docs.text:
• Account Lockout Protection (T08) — Rate-limiting LoginRateLimitFilter with configurable thresholds, lazy auto-unlock after cooldown, and admin unlock endpoint
  • Swagger UI Gating (T07) — Swagger UI disabled by default; when enabled, requires ROLE_ADMIN
  • Admin Account Unlock UI — Lock status badges, unlock buttons, and unlock dialog in the user management page
  • Configurable CORS Origins (T09) — HMS_MIRROR_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS environment variable and cors.allowed.origins property for production deployments
  • User Registration Toggle — HMS_MIRROR_REGISTRATION_ENABLED controls whether open self-registration is allowed

docs.title: Docker Claude CLI Support
docs.text:
• Node.js 20 in Docker Image — Rocky Linux 9 Docker image now includes Node.js 20 LTS for Claude CLI
  • Claude CLI Pre-installed — @anthropic-ai/claude-code npm package installed during Docker build
  • ANTHROPIC_API_KEY — New environment variable passthrough in Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml

docs.title: UI/UX — CMA Visual Alignment
docs.text:
• CMA Design System — Complete visual alignment with Cloudera Manager Application design tokens (colors, typography, spacing, border-radius)
  • Roboto Font — Typography updated to match CMA's Roboto font family
  • CUIX Icons — Replaced Heroicons with Cloudera CUIX icon set across all components
  • CMA Layout Components — New PageHeader, PageSubtitleBar, Breadcrumb, DashboardPanel, and FormField components matching CMA patterns
  • Dark Sidebar — Permanently dark sidebar matching CMA navigation style
  • Page Migrations — All pages (6 batches) migrated to CMA layout components

docs.title: JWT Secret Management
docs.text:
• JWT Secret UI — Admin interface for viewing, generating, and persisting JWT secrets under Preferences > Security
  • Startup Script Support — HMS_MIRROR_JWT_SECRET environment variable and jwt.secret property in web.properties
  • Graceful Fallback — Random JWT secret generated on startup if none configured (sessions lost on restart)

docs.title: CSD and Parcel
docs.text:
• CSD Sync — control.sh synced with latest application changes, JWT secret parameter added
  • TLS Default — Default TLS protocol updated to TLSv1.3

docs.title: Build System
docs.text:
• Skip Frontend/MkDocs Builds — Maven properties skip.frontend.build and skip.mkdocs.build for faster iteration
  • Decoupled CMA Plugin POM — Plugin POM decoupled from reactor parent to avoid CMA dependency conflicts

docs.title: Test Infrastructure
docs.text:
• E2E Test Framework — New v4 test case framework with ConversionResultTestFactory, migrated legacy-to-CDP test cases
  • RocksDB Repository Fix — Fixed double key-prefix bug in AbstractRocksDBRepository.deleteById() that caused incorrect return values

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed stripDatabase failing on backtick-quoted `db`.`table` format
  • Fixed database SQL not included in schema-only migration response
  • Fixed setDbRename for dbSpec — migrating tables from source to differently-named target database now works correctly (AMCM-3941)
  • Fixed Assistant chat sessions not persisting across page navigation
  • Fixed main layout dynamic left nav display
  • Fixed connection copy to correctly handle all fields
  • Fixed ZooKeeper-imported connections not picking up SSL configurations
  • Fixed PasswordEncoder interface usage instead of BCryptPasswordEncoder concrete type
  • CVE dependency updates for Netty and hcfs-browser

docs.title: Upgrade Notes
docs.text:
1. Feature Flag Change — hms-mirror.features.assistant.enabled has been removed. Use hms-mirror.features.llm.enabled to control all AI/LLM features.
  2. JWT Secret — For production deployments, configure HMS_MIRROR_JWT_SECRET or jwt.secret in web.properties to persist sessions across restarts.
  3. Docker — The Docker image now includes Node.js 20 and Claude CLI. Pass ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to enable AI features.
  4. CORS — Production deployments behind a reverse proxy should set HMS_MIRROR_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS to the actual origin URL.
  5. Swagger — Swagger UI is now disabled by default. Enable with springdoc.swagger-ui.enabled=true (requires ADMIN role).

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text: Metric Value Total Commits 182 Files Changed 1,453 Lines Added 100,827 Lines Removed 17,981
Release Date: February 2026 Previous Version: 4.0.0.6

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text: This is a major release that introduces Cloudera Manager integration for automated cluster discovery and configuration import, enterprise SSO via OIDC authentication, LDAP directory authentication, a PKCS12 keystore for PCI-DSS/NIST-compliant encryption key management, a new Job Build Wheel UI, Cloudera Manager Parcel packaging, and a complete documentation overhaul using MkDocs.

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Integration
docs.text:
• CM Connector — Discover and import Hadoop/Hive configurations directly from Cloudera Manager instances
  • Automated HCFS Profile Import — Import HDFS/OFS/S3 storage configurations with namespace and warehouse settings
  • Automated HS2 Connection Import — Import HiveServer2 endpoints with JDBC URIs, ZooKeeper discovery, and SSL/TLS settings
  • Kerberos Detection — Automatic detection of Kerberos requirements during CM import with credential assignment
  • CM Connection Management — Create, test, and manage multiple Cloudera Manager connections with encrypted credential storage
  • CM Feature Flag — hms-mirror.features.cm.enabled flag controls visibility of CM-specific UI features and hides non-applicable preferences (JVM, TLS, Authentication) in CM mode

docs.title: OIDC Authentication (OAuth2 / OpenID Connect)
docs.text:
• Single Sign-On — Connect HMS-Mirror to identity providers for enterprise SSO authentication
  • Provider Presets — Pre-configured issuer URIs for Google, Okta, Azure AD, and Keycloak
  • Group-Based Authorization — Map OIDC groups to HMS-Mirror roles (ADMIN / USER)
  • Dynamic User Provisioning — Auto-create local accounts on first OIDC login
  • OIDC Configuration UI — Full configuration interface under Preferences > Authentication

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text:
• Enterprise Directory Integration — Connect to Active Directory, FreeIPA, or OpenLDAP for centralized authentication
  • Quick Setup Presets — Auto-fill common directory configurations for AD, FreeIPA, and OpenLDAP
  • LDAP Connection Testing — Test LDAP connectivity, bind credentials, and user search configuration from the UI
  • Group-Based Authorization — Map LDAP groups to HMS-Mirror user and admin roles
  • SSL/STARTTLS Support — Secure LDAP connections with SSL (LDAPS) or STARTTLS

docs.title: Master Password & Keystore Security
docs.text:
• PKCS12 Keystore — Encryption keys stored in a password-protected PKCS12 keystore, separate from encrypted data in RocksDB
  • Key Separation Compliance — Meets PCI-DSS 3.5.2 and NIST SP 800-57 requirements for cryptographic key separation
  • Master Password UI — Admin-only interface for changing the keystore password with backup creation
  • External Password Configuration — Support via HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD environment variable or system property
  • Automatic Key Migration — Existing encryption keys in RocksDB automatically migrate to the keystore on first startup
  • CM Connection Encryption — New cm_connections_key domain for encrypting Cloudera Manager credentials

docs.title: User Management
docs.text:
• Admin User Management — Administrators can reset passwords, grant/revoke admin roles, and enable/disable user accounts
  • Self-Service Profile — All users can update their email and change their password
  • Role-Based Access — ADMIN and USER roles with appropriate feature access controls

docs.title: Job Build Wheel Interface
docs.text:
• Circular Wheel UI — New visual interface with 5 segments (Job Details, Data Strategy, References, Options, Summary)
  • Non-Linear Navigation — Click any segment to jump directly to that section
  • Visual Progress Tracking — Segments show completion status and error indicators
  • Animated Segment Cards — Smooth transitions with Framer Motion animations
  • Central Hub Display — Shows job name and overall completion percentage
  • User Preference Toggle — Choose between Wheel and Wizard interfaces via Settings

docs.title: TLS/HTTPS Configuration
docs.text:
• Self-Signed Certificate Generation — Generate server certificates directly from the Preferences UI
  • Configurable Certificate Fields — CN, hostname, organization, validity, key size, TLS protocol, SANs
  • Certificate Status Monitoring — View generation date, expiry, and color-coded validity indicators
  • TLS Enable/Disable — Toggle HTTPS access with a restart

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Parcel Packaging
docs.text:
• Parcel Build — Automated Maven build of CM-compatible parcel (.parcel file with SHA checksum)
  • CSD (Custom Service Descriptor) — Service descriptor for CM integration
  • Multi-Platform Support — Build profiles for EL7, EL8, EL9, and Ubuntu

docs.title: MkDocs Documentation
docs.text:
• Documentation Overhaul — Migrated from JetBrains Writerside to MkDocs with Material theme
  • Integrated Documentation — Docs served directly from the application at /docs/
  • In-App Help Links — Context-sensitive help icons throughout the UI linking to relevant documentation pages
  • Search — Full-text search across all documentation pages
  • Dark/Light Mode — Documentation theme matches user preference

docs.title: Application Preferences
docs.text:
• Dataset Page Preference — Choose between Builder (drag-and-drop) and Wizard (step-by-step) for dataset creation
  • Job Page Preference — Choose between Wheel (circular) and Wizard (step-by-step) for job creation
  • Persistent Preferences — User preferences stored in RocksDB across sessions

docs.title: Job Management
docs.text:
• Job Copy — Copy existing jobs as templates for new ones
  • Job Validation — Pre-run validation with error and warning dialogs
  • Dry-Run Support — Validate job configuration without executing changes
  • Grid and List Views — Toggle between display modes for job lists
  • Advanced Filtering — Filter jobs by strategy, dataset, configuration, and connections
  • Section-Based Form — Job form refactored into independent section components with shared state via useJobForm hook

docs.title: Connection Management
docs.text:
• ZooKeeper SSL Fix — Imported connections from Cloudera Manager now correctly pick up SSL configurations for ZooKeeper-based HS2 discovery
  • HCFS Profile Support — Intermediate storage and target namespace selection via HCFS profiles

docs.title: Keystore File Permissions
docs.text:
• Restrictive POSIX Permissions — Keystore file set to 0400 (owner read-only), directory set to 0700 (owner only)
  • Automatic Backup — Timestamped backup created before each password change

docs.title: Upgrade Notes
docs.text:
1. Java 17+ Required — Minimum Java version remains Java 17
  2. Master Password — The default development password should be overridden for production. See Master Password documentation.
  3. Automatic Key Migration — On first startup after upgrade, encryption keys are automatically migrated from RocksDB to the PKCS12 keystore. No action required.
  4. Authentication — If switching from Local to LDAP or OIDC authentication, configure under Preferences > Authentication and restart. See Authentication.
  5. Documentation — Documentation is now served from /docs/ within the application. The Writerside documentation has been replaced.
  6. Parcel Deployment — For Cloudera Manager deployments, use the parcel build: mvn clean package -pl parcel -am
Release Date: December 9, 2025 Previous Version: 4.0.0.5 Commits: 4 commits, 41 files changed (+4,589 / -178 lines)

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text: This release introduces the Dataset Builder with HCFS directory browsing, Application Preferences for UI customization, CLI Integration feature flags, and fixes for RocksDB backup functionality.

docs.title: Dataset Builder
docs.text:
• Interactive Dataset Builder Page - New drag-and-drop interface for building datasets with visual database and table selection
  • HCFS Directory Browser Dialog - Browse and select warehouse directories directly from HCFS (HDFS/OFS/S3) storage within the Dataset Builder
  • Create Directory Support - Ability to create new directories directly from the HCFS browser dialog
  • Dynamic Schema Tooltips - Tooltips now pull descriptions dynamically from @Schema annotations for accurate field documentation
  • Directory Validation - Warehouse directory fields validate that paths don't contain database names (automatically appended during location translations)

docs.title: Application Preferences
docs.text:
• Dataset Editor Preference - New Settings page under Utilities to choose between Builder (drag-and-drop) and Wizard (step-by-step) interfaces for dataset creation
  • Persistent Preferences - Application preferences stored in RocksDB for persistence across sessions

docs.title: RocksDB Backup System
docs.text:
• Fixed Backup Persistence - Resolved issue where backups would disappear after creation due to incorrect BackupEngine usage
  • Proper BackupEngine Usage - Now uses single persistent backup directory with RocksDB managing multiple backups internally via backup IDs
  • Backup Names with Timestamps - Backup names now include timestamps (e.g., backup_1_2025-12-09_14-30-45) for better organization
  • Enhanced Backup Logging - Added detailed logging for backup initialization, execution, and completion
  • Manual Backup Trigger API - New POST /api/v1/preferences/backup/trigger endpoint for testing backup configuration

docs.title: Dataset Management
docs.text:
• Fixed Edit Mode - Dataset Builder now properly calls update API when editing existing datasets (was incorrectly calling create)
  • Connection Copy Fix - Fixed bug when copying connections

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Fixed Connection Copy functionality not working correctly
  • Fixed Dataset edit mode throwing "Dataset exists already" error
  • Fixed RocksDB backups disappearing immediately after creation
  • Fixed backup scheduler unnecessarily rescheduling after each execution

Release Date: December 8, 2025 Previous Version: 4.0.0.3 Commits: 19 commits, 795 files changed (+25,239 / -22,748 lines)

docs.title: AI Advisor & Embedded LLM Support

docs.title: What's New in HMS-Mirror v4
docs.text: This document highlights the significant new features and improvements in HMS-Mirror v4 compared to the previous Thymeleaf-based web interface and the legacy CLI version.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror v4 represents a complete architectural redesign with a modern React-based single-page application (SPA), persistent storage with RocksDB, enterprise-grade security features, AI-powered analysis via Claude Agent integration, and a Cloudera Platform Framework (CPF) plugin for embedding inside Cloudera Manager. The application now provides a comprehensive web-based interface for managing complex Hive metadata migrations.

Check out the Release Notes for a detailed list of new features, improvements, and issues fixed in a release.

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Command-line only, required manual configuration file editing
  • Thymeleaf: Server-rendered pages with limited interactivity

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• Single Page Application (SPA) built with React and Tailwind CSS
  • Responsive Design with navigation sidebar and card-based layouts
  • Real-time UI Updates with Redux state management
  • Modern UX Patterns including wizards, modals, toast notifications, and loading states
  • Context-Aware Navigation that adapts based on loaded configuration

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Single configuration file with LEFT/RIGHT cluster definitions
  • Thymeleaf: Basic connection editing through forms

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• Multi-Connection Support: Manage unlimited named connections
  • Connection Wizard: Multi-step guided setup including:
  • Basic info (name, description, environment type)
  • HiveServer2 configuration with connection properties
  • Metastore Direct connectivity options
  • Connection pooling configuration
  • Warehouse path configuration
  • Test and validation step
  • Real-time Connection Testing: Test individual components (HCFS, HiveServer2, Metastore)
  • Driver Availability Checking: Verify required JDBC drivers before connecting
  • Partition Discovery Configuration: Auto-discovery and MSCK settings per connection
  • Bulk Operations: Test and manage multiple connections simultaneously
  • Legacy Config Import: Import connections from CLI configuration files

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Database/table filtering via command-line arguments or config file
  • Thymeleaf: Limited dataset definition capabilities

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• Dataset Wizard: Create targeted migration datasets by:
  • Selecting source and target connections
  • Choosing specific databases and tables
  • Configuring database-specific settings
  • Reviewing and saving for reuse
  • Named Datasets: Save and reuse dataset definitions
  • Dataset Listing: Browse, search, and filter datasets
  • Dataset Validation: Verify dataset definitions before use

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Single execution per invocation, no job persistence
  • Thymeleaf: Basic execution without job management

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• Persistent Job Definitions: Create, save, and reuse migration jobs
  • Job Definition Wizard: Guided job creation with:
  • Basic info (name, description)
  • Strategy selection (SQL, Distcp, Hybrid, Export/Import, etc.)
  • Configuration and dataset selection
  • Advanced execution parameters
  • Job Validation: Pre-execution validation with error and warning reporting
  • Job Execution Dialog: Run jobs with configurable execution options
  • Job Status Tracking: Monitor running jobs in real-time
  • Job History: Track execution history and results
  • Duplicate Jobs: Clone existing jobs for similar migrations
  • LLM Job Analysis: AI-powered analysis of job configurations

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Manual kinit before execution, no integrated management
  • Thymeleaf: No Kerberos management interface

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• Kerberos Preferences Panel: Full Kerberos configuration UI
  • Keytab Upload: Upload and manage keytab files through the UI
  • Principal Configuration: Configure Kerberos principal settings
  • Automatic Ticket Refresh: Cron-based scheduler for automatic ticket renewal
  • Ticket Status Monitoring: Real-time display of ticket validity
  • Manual Refresh: On-demand ticket refresh capability
  • Auto-Recovery: Automatic re-authentication on ticket expiration

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:

  • Thymeleaf: No certificate management

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:

  • Thymeleaf: No JVM configuration UI

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• JVM Settings Panel: Configure JVM parameters through the UI:
  • Initial heap size (Xms)
  • Maximum heap size (Xmx)
  • Garbage collection settings
  • Additional JVM arguments
  • Persistent Settings: JVM settings stored in web.properties
  • Reset to Defaults: One-click restore of default settings
  • Restart Guidance: Clear indication when restart is required

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: No AI/LLM features
  • Thymeleaf: No AI/LLM features

docs.title: What's New (AI-Powered Features)
docs.text:
• Claude Agent Integration: AI analysis powered by Claude Code CLI with MCP tool access to HMS-Mirror's REST API
  • One-Click AI Analysis: Available across all major pages:
  • Configuration analysis and recommendations
  • Job configuration review
  • Migration report insights
  • Connection troubleshooting and diagnostics
  • Interactive Assistant Chat: Multi-tab chat interface with conversation persistence across page navigation
  • Chat Export: Download conversations as Markdown files
  • Session Management: Automatic session expiry with timeout banners and idle cleanup
  • Analysis Persistence: Analysis results survive page navigation via the analysis tracker service
  • Model Selection: Configure the Claude model from Preferences > AI Agent
  • Feature Flag: Single hms-mirror.features.llm.enabled flag controls all AI features
  • Docker Ready: Claude CLI pre-installed in the Docker image with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY passthrough

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Standalone only
  • Thymeleaf: Standalone only

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• CMA Embedded Mode: HMS-Mirror runs as a PF4J plugin inside Cloudera Manager Application (CMA)
  • Shared Authentication: Plugin inherits CMA's authentication and user context
  • DispatcherServlet Router: Lightweight Spring MVC router dispatches REST calls within the plugin classloader
  • MCP Tool Provider: Plugin exposes HMS-Mirror tools through CPF's native MCP integration for AI assistants
  • CM Proxy Client: Cloudera Manager API calls route through CPF's built-in proxy
  • Adaptive UI: Frontend adapts layout (removes header, adjusts sidebar) when running inside CMA
  • Independent Build: Plugin POM decoupled from reactor parent to avoid dependency conflicts

docs.title: Previous Versions
docs.text:
• CLI: Relies on OS-level security
  • Thymeleaf: Basic authentication

docs.title: What's New
docs.text:
• MCP Authentication: Per-user JWT tokens for MCP tool calls with 8-hour TTL
  • WebSocket Security: JWT-based handshake authentication for Socket.IO connections
  • API Key Management: Self-service API key creation from the Profile page
  • Account Lockout Protection: Rate-limiting login filter with configurable thresholds and auto-unlock cooldown
  • Admin Account Unlock: UI for viewing locked accounts and unlocking them
  • Swagger UI Gating: Disabled by default; requires ADMIN role when enabled
  • Configurable CORS: HMS_MIRROR_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS for production deployments behind reverse proxies
  • JWT Secret Management: Admin UI for configuring persistent JWT secrets with environment variable support
  • Registration Control: Toggle open self-registration on or off

docs.title: Previous Versions

  • CUIX Icons: Replaced Heroicons with Cloudera CUIX icon set throughout all components

  • Dark Sidebar: Permanently dark navigation sidebar matching CMA style

docs.title: Summary Comparison Table
docs.text: Feature CLI Thymeleaf v4.0 Web Modern UI - Partial Full React SPA Multi-Connection - - Yes Connection Wizard - - Yes Persistent Jobs - - Yes (RocksDB) Kerberos Management - - Yes Certificate Management - - Yes JVM Configuration UI - - Yes LLM Integration - - Yes (Ollama) Driver Management - - Yes Wizard Workflows - - Yes REST API - Partial Full Report Browsing File-based Basic Full AI Analysis - - Yes

docs.title: From CLI
docs.text:
1. Start the HMS-Mirror web server
  2. Use Utilities > Import CLI Config to import existing configurations
  3. Review and adjust imported connections and settings
  4. Create jobs using imported configurations

docs.title: From Thymeleaf Version
docs.text:
1. Install the new v4.0 web application
  2. Configurations will need to be recreated (RocksDB is a new storage backend)
  3. Use the wizards to recreate connections and jobs
  4. Take advantage of new features like persistent jobs and Kerberos management

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
• Java: 17 or higher
  • Memory: 4GB minimum, 8GB+ recommended
  • Disk: 10GB+ for RocksDB storage and reports
  • Browser: Modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
  • Optional: Ollama for LLM features

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Download and extract the HMS-Mirror distribution
  2. Run bin/hmsm-server to start the web server
  3. Access the UI at http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror
  4. Use the Configuration Wizard to create your first migration configuration
  5. Download required drivers via Utilities > Drivers
  6. Create connections using the Connection Wizard
  7. Define datasets and create jobs
  8. Execute and monitor migrations through the web interface

For detailed documentation, see the HMS-Mirror Documentation.

docs.title: Authentication Configuration
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror supports three authentication methods: Local (default), LDAP, and OIDC (OAuth2/OpenID Connect). Configure these under Preferences > Authentication.

Note

Authentication settings are not available when Cloudera Manager mode is enabled. Changes require an application restart.

docs.title: Local Authentication
docs.text: The default mode. Users authenticate with locally stored credentials. No additional configuration is required.

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text: Connect HMS-Mirror to your enterprise directory (Active Directory, OpenLDAP, or FreeIPA) for centralized authentication.

docs.title: Quick Setup Presets
docs.text:
Use a preset to auto-fill common directory configurations:

Preset User Filter Username Attribute Active Directory objectClass=user sAMAccountName FreeIPA / Red Hat IdM objectClass=person uid OpenLDAP objectClass=inetOrgPerson uid

docs.title: Server Connection
docs.text: Field Description LDAP Server URL Server URL (ldap:// for plain/STARTTLS, ldaps:// for SSL) Use SSL (LDAPS) Enable SSL on port 636 Use STARTTLS Enable STARTTLS on port 389 Skip SSL Certificate Verification Allow self-signed certs (testing only) Connection Timeout Timeout in ms (default: 5000) Read Timeout Read timeout in ms (default: 10000)

docs.title: Service Account (Bind Credentials)
docs.text:
Field Description Bind DN Distinguished Name of the service account for directory queries Bind Password Service account password

Example Bind DN: cn=hms-mirror-svc,ou=ServiceAccounts,dc=example,dc=com

docs.title: User Search
docs.text: Field Description User Search Base DN Where to search for users (e.g., ou=Users,dc=example,dc=com) User Search Filter LDAP filter with {0} as username placeholder Username Attribute Which attribute contains the username

docs.title: Group-Based Authorization
docs.text:
Optionally restrict access using LDAP groups:

Field Description User Group DN Group DN for standard user access Admin Group DN Group DN for admin privileges Dynamically Create Users Auto-create local accounts on first LDAP login

docs.title: Testing the Connection
docs.text:
Click Test Connection to verify:

  • LDAP server is reachable
  • Bind credentials are valid
  • User search configuration works

docs.title: OIDC Authentication (OAuth2 / OpenID Connect)
docs.text: Connect HMS-Mirror to an identity provider for Single Sign-On.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text:
Preconfigured issuer URIs are available for:

  • Google (https://accounts.google.com)
  • Okta (https://{your-domain}.okta.com)
  • Azure AD (https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/v2.0)
  • Keycloak (https://{host}/realms/{realm})

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Field Description Issuer URI Identity provider's OpenID Connect discovery endpoint Client ID Application ID registered with the identity provider Client Secret Application secret from the identity provider Scopes OAuth2 scopes (default: openid profile email groups)

docs.title: Group-Based Authorization
docs.text: Field Description User Group Name Group name for standard user access Admin Group Name Group name for admin privileges Dynamically Create Users Auto-create local accounts on first OIDC login

docs.title: Setup Steps
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Preferences > Authentication
  2. Select the authentication method (Local, LDAP, or OIDC)
  3. Fill in the required fields for your chosen method
  4. For LDAP: click Test Connection to verify
  5. Click Save Settings
  6. Restart HMS-Mirror for changes to take effect

docs.title: Certificate Management
docs.text:
This guide covers managing SSL/TLS certificates for secure connections to DataLake environments and other services from HMS-Mirror.

Note

This is where you register certificates for systems that use self-signed certificates for TLS. When connecting to clusters or services with self-signed SSL certificates, you must import those certificates here for HMS-Mirror to trust them.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HMS-Mirror uses a centralized truststore to manage SSL certificates for all outbound connections. This includes connections to: - HiveServer2 (JDBC over SSL) - Metastore databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL over SSL) - Knox Gateway - Other HTTPS services

docs.title: When to Use Certificate Management
docs.text: You need to import certificates when: - Connecting to clusters with self-signed SSL certificates - Using internal Certificate Authorities (CAs) not in the Java default truststore - Connecting through Knox Gateway with custom certificates - SSL connections fail with certificate validation errors

docs.title: Accessing Certificate Management
docs.text: Navigate to Utilities > Preferences in the web UI and select the Certificates tab.

docs.title: Application Truststore
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror maintains a central truststore for all SSL connections. The truststore information is displayed at the top of the Certificates tab:

Field Description Path Location of the truststore file Certificates Number of certificates currently installed

Default truststore location: 

~/.hms-mirror/security/truststore.jks

All SSL connections in HMS-Mirror automatically use certificates from this truststore.

docs.title: Method 1: Upload Individual Certificate
docs.text:
For single certificate files (.crt, .cer, .pem format):

  1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences > Certificates
  2. In the Upload Certificate section:
  3. Click Choose File and select your certificate file
  4. Enter a meaningful Certificate Alias (e.g., cdp-cluster-ca)
  5. Click Import Certificate
  6. The certificate appears in the Installed Certificates table

Supported Formats: - .crt - X.509 certificate - .cer - X.509 certificate (alternative extension) - .pem - PEM-encoded certificate

docs.title: Method 2: Import from Existing Truststore
docs.text:
To import certificates from an existing JKS truststore:

  1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences > Certificates
  2. In the Import from Truststore section:
  3. Click Choose File and select the .jks truststore file
  4. Enter the Truststore Password
  5. Click List Certificates
  6. A dialog shows all certificates in the truststore
  7. Select the certificates you want to import (or click Select All)
  8. Click Import Selected

This is useful when: - Migrating from another tool with an existing truststore - Your cluster administrator provides a truststore with multiple certificates - You have a corporate CA bundle in JKS format

docs.title: Viewing Certificates
docs.text:
The Installed Certificates table shows:

Column Description Alias Unique identifier for the certificate Type Certificate type (usually X.509) File Original filename (if available) Size Certificate file size Actions Delete button

docs.title: Deleting Certificates
docs.text:
To remove a certificate:

  1. Find the certificate in the Installed Certificates table
  2. Click the Delete button
  3. Confirm the deletion

Note

Removing a certificate will cause SSL connections that rely on it to fail.

docs.title: From HiveServer2 / DataLake Environment
docs.text:
Use OpenSSL to retrieve the certificate:

# Replace hostname and port with your HiveServer2 details
openssl s_client -connect hs2.example.com:10001 -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > hs2-cert.pem

docs.title: From Knox Gateway
docs.text:
openssl s_client -connect knox.example.com:8443 -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > knox-cert.pem

docs.title: From Database Server
docs.text:
# MySQL
openssl s_client -connect mysql.example.com:3306 -starttls mysql -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > mysql-cert.pem

# PostgreSQL
openssl s_client -connect postgres.example.com:5432 -starttls postgres -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > postgres-cert.pem

docs.title: From Cluster Administrator
docs.text: Request the following from your cluster administrator: - The CA certificate used to sign cluster certificates - Any intermediate certificates in the chain - The certificate file in PEM or DER format

docs.title: Certificate Chain Handling
docs.text:
If your cluster uses a certificate chain (server cert → intermediate CA → root CA), you may need to import: 1. The root CA certificate 2. Any intermediate CA certificates

Import each certificate separately with descriptive aliases: - company-root-ca - company-intermediate-ca - cluster-server-cert

docs.title: CDP Private Cloud
docs.text:
CDP typically uses Knox Gateway with SSL. Import the Knox certificate:

# Get Knox certificate
openssl s_client -connect knox.cdp.example.com:8443 </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > cdp-knox.pem

Then upload cdp-knox.pem with alias cdp-knox.

docs.title: HDP with SSL
docs.text:
For HDP clusters with SSL enabled:

# Get HiveServer2 certificate (binary transport)
openssl s_client -connect hs2.hdp.example.com:10000 </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > hdp-hs2.pem

# Get HiveServer2 certificate (HTTP transport)
openssl s_client -connect hs2.hdp.example.com:10001 </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -outform PEM > hdp-hs2-http.pem

docs.title: Self-Signed Certificates
docs.text:
For development or test clusters with self-signed certificates:

  1. Obtain the self-signed certificate from the cluster
  2. Import it using the Upload Certificate feature
  3. Use a descriptive alias like dev-cluster-selfsigned

docs.title: SSL Handshake Failure
docs.text:
Error: 

javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: sun.security.validator.ValidatorException:
PKIX path building failed

Cause: The certificate or CA is not in the truststore.

Solution: 1. Obtain the server's certificate using openssl 2. Import it via Certificates tab 3. Test the connection

docs.title: Certificate Expired
docs.text:
Error: 

javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: NotAfter: [date]

Cause: The certificate has expired.

Solution: 1. Obtain a new certificate from the server 2. Delete the old certificate 3. Import the new certificate

docs.title: Hostname Verification Failed
docs.text:
Error: 

javax.net.ssl.SSLPeerUnverifiedException: Hostname verification failed

Cause: The hostname used doesn't match the certificate's CN or SANs.

Solution: 1. Use the exact hostname in the certificate 2. Or add the hostname to DNS/hosts file 3. Some connections support disabling hostname verification (not recommended for production)

docs.title: Certificate Not Yet Valid
docs.text:
Error: 

javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: NotBefore: [date]

Cause: The certificate's validity period hasn't started.

Solution: 1. Verify system clock is correct 2. Wait until the certificate becomes valid 3. Or obtain a currently valid certificate

docs.title: Chain of Trust Issues
docs.text:
Error: 

unable to find valid certification path to requested target

Cause: Missing intermediate or root CA certificates.

Solution: 1. Import the entire certificate chain: - Root CA certificate - Intermediate CA certificates - Server certificate 2. Use aliases to identify each certificate in the chain

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use descriptive aliases that identify the source (e.g., prod-cdp-knox, dev-hdp-hs2)
  2. Document certificate purposes for team reference
  3. Track expiration dates and renew before expiration
  4. Import CA certificates rather than individual server certs when possible
  5. Test connections after importing certificates
  6. Backup the truststore before making changes
  7. Use PEM format for easier inspection and management
  8. Verify certificates before import using: 
    openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout

docs.title: View Certificate Details
docs.text:
# View certificate information
openssl x509 -in certificate.pem -text -noout

# Check expiration date
openssl x509 -in certificate.pem -enddate -noout

# Verify certificate chain
openssl verify -CAfile ca-bundle.pem certificate.pem

docs.title: List Truststore Contents (JKS)
docs.text: keytool -list -v -keystore truststore.jks -storepass changeit

docs.title: Export Certificate from JKS
docs.text: keytool -exportcert -alias myalias -keystore truststore.jks -file cert.pem -rfc

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Kerberos Authentication - Configure Kerberos for cluster access
  • Managing Connections - Configure cluster connections
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve common issues

docs.title: JVM Settings
docs.text: This guide covers configuring Java Virtual Machine (JVM) settings for HMS-Mirror through the web UI.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: JVM settings control how HMS-Mirror uses system resources including memory allocation, garbage collection, and logging. These settings are managed through Utilities > Preferences > JVM in the web UI.

docs.title: Accessing JVM Settings
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences in the web UI
  2. Select the JVM tab
  3. Configure settings as needed
  4. Click Save Settings
  5. Restart HMS-Mirror for changes to take effect

Important

JVM settings are applied at application startup. After saving changes, you must restart HMS-Mirror for them to take effect.

docs.title: Settings File Location
docs.text:
JVM settings are persisted to a properties file displayed at the top of the JVM tab. The default location is:

~/.hms-mirror/settings/jvm.properties

This file is read by the startup script when launching HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: Initial Heap Size (-Xms)
docs.text:
The initial amount of memory allocated to the JVM when HMS-Mirror starts.

Setting Description Example Format Use 'm' for MB or 'g' for GB 4096m or 4g Recommended 25-50% of max heap 4096m

docs.title: Maximum Heap Size (-Xmx)
docs.text:
The maximum amount of memory the JVM can use.

Setting Description Example Format Use 'm' for MB or 'g' for GB 8192m or 8g Minimum At least 4GB for production 4g Recommended 50-75% of available system RAM 8g

Guidelines: - Small migrations (< 100 tables): 4GB minimum - Medium migrations (100-500 tables): 8GB recommended - Large migrations (500+ tables): 12-16GB or more

Note on Embedded LLM

When using the Embedded Ollama LLM provider, the LLM runs as a separate native process outside the JVM. LLM memory requirements are managed by the operating system, not the JVM heap. See LLM Integration for LLM memory planning.

docs.title: Garbage Collector
docs.text: Select the garbage collection algorithm based on your workload:

docs.title: G1 Garbage Collector (Recommended)
docs.text:
Best for: Most HMS-Mirror workloads, heaps > 4GB

-XX:+UseG1GC
  • Balanced throughput and latency
  • Automatic region sizing
  • Good default choice

docs.title: Z Garbage Collector (ZGC)
docs.text:
Best for: Large heaps with low latency requirements (Java 15+)

-XX:+UseZGC
  • Sub-millisecond pause times
  • Handles very large heaps (multi-terabyte)
  • Requires more CPU overhead

docs.title: Parallel Garbage Collector
docs.text:
Best for: Batch processing, throughput-oriented

-XX:+UseParallelGC
  • Maximum throughput
  • Longer pause times acceptable
  • Good for non-interactive workloads

docs.title: Serial Garbage Collector
docs.text:
Best for: Small heaps, testing, single-core systems

-XX:+UseSerialGC
  • Single-threaded
  • Minimal overhead
  • Not recommended for production

docs.title: Prefer IPv4 Stack
docs.text:
Enable this option if you experience network connectivity issues with IPv6.

-Djava.net.preferIPv4Stack=true

When to enable: - Network timeouts connecting to clusters - DNS resolution issues - Mixed IPv4/IPv6 environments - Legacy cluster configurations

docs.title: Logging Level
docs.text:
Controls the verbosity of application logs.

Level Description TRACE Most detailed, includes all events DEBUG Detailed debugging information INFO Normal operational messages (default) WARN Warning conditions ERROR Error conditions only

Recommendations: - Production: INFO or WARN - Troubleshooting: DEBUG - Deep debugging: TRACE

docs.title: Log File Prefix
docs.text:
Sets the prefix for log file names. Default: hms-mirror-service

Log files are created at: 

~/.hms-mirror/logs/{prefix}.log

docs.title: Additional JVM Options
docs.text: Enter any additional JVM options as a space-separated list. Use with caution.

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text:
Heap Dump on Out of Memory: 

-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp

GC Logging (Java 11+): 

-Xlog:gc*:file=/var/log/hms-mirror/gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags

Remote Debugging: 

-agentlib:jdwp=transport=dt_socket,server=y,suspend=n,address=5005

JMX Monitoring: 

-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote -Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.port=9010 -Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.authenticate=false -Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false

docs.title: Development Environment
docs.text:
initialHeapSize=2g
maxHeapSize=4g
garbageCollector=G1GC
loggingThreshold=DEBUG

docs.title: Production - Standard
docs.text:
initialHeapSize=4g
maxHeapSize=8g
garbageCollector=G1GC
loggingThreshold=INFO
preferIPv4Stack=true

docs.title: Production - Large Scale
docs.text:
initialHeapSize=8g
maxHeapSize=16g
garbageCollector=G1GC
loggingThreshold=INFO
preferIPv4Stack=true
additionalOptions=-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/hms-mirror

docs.title: Save Settings
docs.text: Saves the current configuration to the properties file. Requires restart to take effect.

docs.title: Reset to Defaults
docs.text: Resets all JVM settings to their default values. Useful if you've made problematic changes.

docs.title: Cancel
docs.text: Discards unsaved changes and reverts to the last saved configuration.

docs.title: Out of Memory Errors
docs.text:
Symptoms: 

java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space

Solutions: 1. Increase maxHeapSize 2. Enable heap dump for analysis 3. Check for memory leaks in job configuration

docs.title: Slow Startup
docs.text:
Causes: - Initial heap too large for system - GC initialization overhead

Solutions: 1. Reduce initialHeapSize 2. Ensure sufficient system RAM 3. Consider ZGC for large heaps

docs.title: GC Pauses
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Application freezes periodically - Slow response times

Solutions: 1. Switch to G1GC or ZGC 2. Increase heap size 3. Enable GC logging to analyze pause patterns

docs.title: Network Timeouts
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Connection timeouts to clusters - DNS resolution failures

Solutions: 1. Enable preferIPv4Stack 2. Check network configuration 3. Verify cluster hostnames resolve correctly

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Set initial and max heap to same value in production to avoid resize overhead
  2. Use G1GC as the default garbage collector
  3. Enable heap dumps in production for post-mortem analysis
  4. Start with INFO logging and only increase verbosity when needed
  5. Monitor memory usage during initial deployments
  6. Test configuration changes in a non-production environment first

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• LLM Integration - Configure embedded LLM for AI-powered analysis
  • Installation Guide - Initial setup and requirements
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve common issues

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication
docs.text: This guide covers configuring HMS-Mirror for Kerberos-secured DataLake environments.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Kerberos is the standard authentication mechanism for enterprise DataLake environments powered by Hive and Hive Metastore. HMS-Mirror supports Kerberos authentication for HiveServer2 connections. Kerberos credentials are managed centrally through the Preferences > Kerberos page in the web UI.

docs.title: On the HMS-Mirror Server
docs.text:
1. Kerberos client installed 

    # RHEL/CentOS
yum install krb5-workstation

# Ubuntu/Debian
apt-get install krb5-user

  2. krb5.conf configured

  3. Located at /etc/krb5.conf

  4. Contains realm and KDC information

  5. Network access to KDC

  6. Port 88 (UDP/TCP) to Key Distribution Center
  7. Port 749 (TCP) for kadmin (if needed)

docs.title: Kerberos Configuration File
docs.text:
Example /etc/krb5.conf:

[libdefaults]
    default_realm = EXAMPLE.COM
    dns_lookup_realm = false
    dns_lookup_kdc = false
    ticket_lifetime = 24h
    renew_lifetime = 7d
    forwardable = true

[realms]
    EXAMPLE.COM = {
        kdc = kdc.example.com
        admin_server = kdc.example.com
    }

[domain_realm]
    .example.com = EXAMPLE.COM
    example.com = EXAMPLE.COM

docs.title: Managing Kerberos in HMS-Mirror
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides a centralized interface for managing Kerberos authentication through the web UI at Utilities > Preferences > Kerberos.

docs.title: Configuring Kerberos Credentials
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences in the web UI
  2. Select the Kerberos tab
  3. Configure your authentication method:

docs.title: Using a Keytab (Recommended for Production)
docs.text:
1. Principal: Enter your Kerberos principal (e.g., hms-mirror@EXAMPLE.COM)
  2. Keytab File: Upload your keytab file through the interface
  3. Refresh Schedule (optional): Set a cron expression for automatic ticket renewal (e.g., 0 */4 * * * for every 4 hours)
  4. Auto Recovery: Enable to automatically re-initialize tickets if they expire

docs.title: Using Password
docs.text:
1. Principal: Enter your Kerberos principal
  2. Password: Enter your Kerberos password
  3. Configure refresh schedule as needed

docs.title: Monitoring Ticket Status
docs.text: The Kerberos tab displays: - Current ticket validity status - Ticket expiration time - Next scheduled refresh time - Error messages if authentication fails

docs.title: Manual Ticket Refresh
docs.text: Click the Refresh button to manually refresh the Kerberos ticket at any time.

docs.title: HiveServer2 Connection Configuration
docs.text: When connecting to a Kerberized HiveServer2, the service principal must be specified in the JDBC URL, not in the connection properties.

docs.title: Important: Principal in URL
docs.text:
The Kerberos service principal for HiveServer2 must be included in the JDBC URI:

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM

Correct - Principal in URI: 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM

Incorrect - Principal in connection properties (will not work): 

# DO NOT DO THIS
hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default
  connectionProperties:
    principal: hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM  # This won't work!

docs.title: Connection Examples
docs.text:
Binary Transport 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM
  connectionProperties:
    transportMode: binary

HTTP Transport 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10001/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=cliservice;principal=HTTP/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM

With SSL 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;ssl=true;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM
  connectionProperties:
    sslTrustStore: /path/to/truststore.jks
    sslTrustStorePassword: changeit

With Zookeeper Discovery 

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM

docs.title: Metastore Direct Connections
docs.text:
Direct Metastore connections use database authentication (username/password), not Kerberos. The Kerberos ticket managed in Preferences is used for HiveServer2 connections.

metastoreDirect:
  type: MYSQL
  uri: jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive
  username: hive_user
  password: password

docs.title: Understanding Principals
docs.text:
service/hostname@REALM
  │       │       │
  │       │       └── Kerberos realm (uppercase)
  │       └── Fully qualified hostname
  └── Service name (hive, HTTP, etc.)

docs.title: _HOST Placeholder
docs.text:
The _HOST placeholder is automatically replaced with the actual hostname:

hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM
  ↓
hive/hs2.example.com@EXAMPLE.COM

docs.title: Common Service Principals
docs.text: Service Principal Format HiveServer2 (Binary) hive/_HOST@REALM HiveServer2 (HTTP) HTTP/_HOST@REALM Knox HTTP/_HOST@REALM

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
"GSS initiate failed" 

javax.security.sasl.SaslException: GSS initiate failed

Causes - No valid ticket - Check the Kerberos tab in Preferences for ticket status - Wrong principal in URL - Clock skew between client and server

Solutions 1. Check Preferences > Kerberos for ticket status 2. Click Refresh to re-initialize the ticket 3. Verify the principal in your connection URL is correct 4. Check time synchronization

"Server not found in Kerberos database" 

KrbException: Server not found in Kerberos database

Causes - Wrong principal name in the connection URL - Service not registered in KDC

Solutions - Verify principal exists: kadmin -q "listprincs *hive*" - Check hostname resolution - Verify _HOST replacement is working

"Clock skew too great" 

KrbException: Clock skew too great

Solution 

# Sync time with NTP
sudo ntpdate -s time.nist.gov
# or
sudo systemctl restart chronyd

"Cannot find KDC" 

Cannot locate KDC for realm

Solutions - Verify /etc/krb5.conf configuration - Check DNS resolution for KDC - Verify network connectivity to port 88

docs.title: Diagnostic Commands
docs.text:
# Check Kerberos configuration
cat /etc/krb5.conf

# Test KDC connectivity
telnet kdc.example.com 88

# Verbose kinit (for testing outside application)
KRB5_TRACE=/dev/stdout kinit user@REALM

# Verify DNS
nslookup hs2.example.com
host -t SRV _kerberos._udp.EXAMPLE.COM

docs.title: Debug Logging
docs.text:
Enable Kerberos debugging:

Java System Property 

java -Dsun.security.krb5.debug=true -jar hms-mirror-server.jar

In application.properties 

logging.level.org.apache.hadoop.security=DEBUG
logging.level.sun.security.krb5=DEBUG

docs.title: Cross-Realm Authentication
docs.text:
When source and target clusters are in different realms:

# /etc/krb5.conf
[realms]
    SOURCE.COM = {
        kdc = kdc.source.com
    }
    TARGET.COM = {
        kdc = kdc.target.com
    }

[capaths]
    SOURCE.COM = {
        TARGET.COM = .
    }
    TARGET.COM = {
        SOURCE.COM = .
    }

docs.title: Configuration for Multiple Realms
docs.text:
When working with multiple realms, ensure your Kerberos principal (configured in Preferences > Kerberos) has cross-realm trust established, and specify the appropriate service principal in each connection URL:

# LEFT connection (SOURCE.COM)
leftConnection:
  hiveServer2:
    uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.source.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@SOURCE.COM

# RIGHT connection (TARGET.COM)
rightConnection:
  hiveServer2:
    uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.target.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@TARGET.COM

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Use keytabs for production - Configure via Preferences > Kerberos
  2. Restrict keytab access - Secure keytab files before uploading
  3. Use dedicated service accounts - Not personal accounts
  4. Enable auto-recovery - Ensures continuous operation

docs.title: Reliability
docs.text:
1. Configure refresh schedule - Set automatic ticket renewal in Preferences
  2. Monitor ticket status - Check the Kerberos tab regularly
  3. Enable auto-recovery - Automatically re-initialize expired tickets

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
1. Document principal names - For troubleshooting
  2. Coordinate with security team - For keytab creation
  3. Test connections - Use the connection test feature after configuring Kerberos

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Kerberos Credentials
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences > Kerberos
  2. Enter principal: hms-mirror@EXAMPLE.COM
  3. Upload keytab file
  4. Set refresh schedule: 0 */4 * * * (every 4 hours)
  5. Enable auto-recovery
  6. Save configuration

docs.title: Step 2: Create Kerberized Connection
docs.text:
In Connections, create a new connection:

name: prod-kerberized-cluster
legacyHive: false

hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM;ssl=true
  connectionProperties:
    transportMode: binary
    sslTrustStore: /opt/hms-mirror/security/truststore.jks
    sslTrustStorePassword: changeit

sessionProperties:
  hive.exec.dynamic.partition: "true"
  hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode: "nonstrict"

docs.title: Step 3: Test Connection
docs.text: Click Test Connection to verify Kerberos authentication is working correctly.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Understanding Connections - Connection concepts
  • Managing Connections (UI) - Configure in web UI
  • Troubleshooting - Resolve connection issues

docs.title: LLM Integration
docs.text: HMS-Mirror v4 includes optional AI-powered analysis using Large Language Models (LLMs). This guide covers setup, configuration, and usage of LLM features.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: LLM integration provides: - Configuration Analysis - Review settings and get recommendations - Job Analysis - Understand what a job will do before running - Report Analysis - Get insights from migration results - Strategy Suggestions - Help choosing the right approach

docs.title: Accessing LLM Settings
docs.text: Navigate to Utilities > Preferences in the web UI and select the LLM tab.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text: HMS-Mirror supports multiple LLM providers organized into three categories:

docs.title: Embedded Providers
docs.text:
Managed by HMS-Mirror, no external setup required.

Provider Description Embedded Ollama Bundled Ollama, runs on HMS-Mirror server

docs.title: Local Providers
docs.text:
Connect to LLM servers running in your environment.

Provider Description Local Ollama External Ollama instance LM Studio LM Studio local server LocalAI LocalAI server OpenAI Compatible Any OpenAI-compatible local endpoint

docs.title: Hosted Providers
docs.text:
Cloud-based services requiring API keys.

Provider Description OpenAI ChatGPT API Anthropic Claude API Google AI Gemini API Azure OpenAI Azure-hosted OpenAI AWS Bedrock AWS-hosted models Groq Fast inference API Together AI Open model hosting Fireworks Fast model inference Grok xAI's Grok models

docs.title: Embedded Ollama (Simplest)
docs.text:
No external setup required:

  1. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences > LLM
  2. Select the Embedded tab
  3. Click Download Ollama if not already installed
  4. Click Start Server to start the embedded Ollama
  5. Go to Available Models and click Download on your preferred model
  6. Click the star icon on an installed model to set it as active
  7. Enable LLM features in General Settings
  8. Click Save Settings

docs.title: Local Ollama
docs.text:
Use an existing Ollama installation:

  1. Install Ollama: https://ollama.ai
  2. Pull a model: ollama pull llama3.1
  3. In HMS-Mirror, navigate to Utilities > Preferences > LLM
  4. Select the Local tab
  5. Choose Ollama (Local)
  6. Set endpoint: http://localhost:11434
  7. Click Test to verify connectivity
  8. Click the star icon on a model to set it as active
  9. Enable LLM features and save

docs.title: Cloud Provider (OpenAI Example)
docs.text:
1. Get API key from OpenAI
  2. Navigate to Utilities > Preferences > LLM
  3. Select the Hosted tab
  4. Choose OpenAI
  5. Enter your API key
  6. Click Test to verify connectivity
  7. Select a model (e.g., GPT-4o)
  8. Enable LLM features and save

docs.title: Embedded Ollama
docs.text: Setting Description Default Model Model to use llama3.1 Auto-start Start with HMS-Mirror true

docs.title: Local Ollama
docs.text: Setting Description Example Endpoint Ollama API URL http://localhost:11434 Model Model name llama3.1, mistral, codellama

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text: Setting Description Example API Key OpenAI API key sk-... Model Model ID gpt-4o, gpt-4-turbo Organization Org ID (optional) org-...

docs.title: Anthropic
docs.text: Setting Description Example API Key Anthropic API key sk-ant-... Model Model ID claude-3-5-sonnet, claude-3-opus

docs.title: Azure OpenAI
docs.text: Setting Description Example Endpoint Azure endpoint https://xxx.openai.azure.com API Key Azure API key ... Deployment Deployment name gpt-4-deployment API Version API version 2024-02-15-preview

docs.title: AWS Bedrock
docs.text: Setting Description Example Region AWS region us-east-1 Model ID Bedrock model anthropic.claude-3-sonnet Access Key AWS access key AKIA... Secret Key AWS secret key ...

docs.title: Model Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Description Range Default Temperature Creativity level 0.0-1.0 0.7 Max Tokens Response length 100-8192 4096

Temperature Guidelines - 0.0-0.3: Focused, deterministic (good for analysis) - 0.4-0.7: Balanced (recommended) - 0.8-1.0: Creative, varied (may be inconsistent)

docs.title: Configuration Analysis
docs.text:
Get AI-powered review of your migration configuration.

Access 1. Navigate to Configurations 2. Click ✨ (sparkles) on a configuration card 3. View analysis in dialog

What It Analyzes - Configuration completeness - Potential issues - Best practices - Recommendations

Example Output 

Configuration Analysis: hdp-migration-config

Summary
───────
This configuration is set up for migrating from HDP to CDP with
ACID table downgrade enabled.

Observations
────────────
• ACID tables will be converted to external tables
• Sort dynamic partition inserts is disabled
• No warehouse directories configured

Recommendations
───────────────
1. Consider enabling sortDynamicPartitionInserts for better
   performance with partitioned tables.

2. Set explicit warehouse directories to control where
   migrated tables are stored.

3. Review the partition limit (currently 0) - unlimited
   partitions may cause memory issues.

Risk Assessment
───────────────
• Low risk: Configuration is valid
• Medium risk: Missing warehouse paths may use defaults

docs.title: Job Analysis
docs.text:
Understand what a job will do before running.

Access 1. Navigate to Jobs → Build 2. Click ✨ (sparkles) on a job card 3. View analysis in dialog

What It Analyzes - Job configuration summary - Expected operations - Potential risks - Prerequisites

Example Output 

Job Analysis: hdp-to-cdp-migration

Overview
────────
This job will migrate 45 tables from the sales_db database
using the HYBRID strategy.

Expected Operations
──────────────────
• 35 tables will use SQL strategy (< 1GB)
• 8 tables will use EXPORT_IMPORT (> 1GB)
• 2 ACID tables will be downgraded to external

Timing Estimate
───────────────
Based on table sizes and strategy selection:
• Estimated duration: 30-45 minutes
• Parallel operations: Up to 8 tables

Potential Issues
────────────────
1. Table 'large_transactions' is 50GB - consider running
   during off-peak hours.

2. 2 ACID tables will lose transactional properties.

Recommendations
───────────────
1. Run a dry run first to verify DDL generation.
2. Ensure target warehouse has 100GB+ free space.
3. Consider scheduling during maintenance window.

docs.title: Report Analysis
docs.text:
Get insights from completed migration reports.

Access 1. Navigate to Reports 2. Open a report 3. Click ✨ AI Analysis

What It Analyzes - Success/failure patterns - Error categorization - Root cause analysis - Remediation suggestions

Example Output 

Report Analysis: hdp-to-cdp-migration

Executive Summary
─────────────────
Migration completed with 95% success rate. 3 tables failed
due to partition limits, 2 were skipped per configuration.

Success Analysis
────────────────
• 42 of 45 tables migrated successfully
• Average migration time: 45 seconds per table
• SQL strategy performed well for small tables

Failure Analysis
────────────────
Failed Tables:
1. order_items: Exceeded partition limit (1500 > 500)
2. event_log: Query timeout after 1 hour
3. audit_trail: Permission denied on target

Root Causes:
• Partition limit too conservative for some tables
• Large table without EXPORT_IMPORT strategy
• Missing permissions on target cluster

Recommendations
───────────────
1. Increase partitionLimit to 2000 and re-run failed tables
2. Switch event_log to EXPORT_IMPORT strategy
3. Grant CREATE TABLE permission for audit_trail

Next Steps
──────────
1. Address the 3 failed tables with suggested changes
2. Verify data in successfully migrated tables
3. Update applications to point to new locations

docs.title: General Settings
docs.text:
The General Settings section at the top of the LLM tab controls global LLM behavior:

Setting Description Default Enable LLM Features Turns AI analysis on/off throughout the application Off Max Tokens Maximum length of LLM responses 2048 Temperature Controls response creativity (0.0-2.0) 0.7

Temperature Guidelines - 0.0-0.3: Focused, deterministic (good for analysis) - 0.4-0.7: Balanced (recommended) - 0.8-1.0: Creative, varied (may be inconsistent)

docs.title: Download Ollama Binary
docs.text: If using the Embedded provider for the first time: 1. Go to Utilities > Preferences > LLM 2. Select the Embedded tab 3. Click Download Ollama to download the Ollama binary 4. Wait for the download to complete

docs.title: Start/Stop the Server
docs.text:
• Click Start Server to start the embedded Ollama server
  • Click Stop Server to stop it
  • The server status is displayed showing whether it's running

docs.title: Download a Model
docs.text:
1. Go to Utilities > Preferences > LLM > Embedded
  2. Click Available Models tab
  3. Find the model you want and click Download
  4. Wait for download (progress bar shows status)
  5. The model will appear in Installed Models when complete

docs.title: Set Active Model
docs.text: Click the star icon (☆) on any installed model to set it as the active model for LLM features.

docs.title: Available Models
docs.text: Model Size Best For llama3.1 4.7GB General purpose mistral 4.1GB Fast, good quality codellama 3.8GB Code-focused phi 1.6GB Lightweight

docs.title: Delete a Model
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings → LLM
  2. Click Manage Models
  3. Select model to remove
  4. Click Delete

docs.title: Model Selection Tips
docs.text: Use Case Recommended Quick analysis mistral, phi Detailed analysis llama3.1, gpt-4 Code-heavy configs codellama Production accuracy gpt-4, claude-3-opus

docs.title: Built-in Prompts
docs.text: HMS-Mirror includes optimized prompts for: - Configuration analysis - Job analysis - Report analysis

docs.title: Prompt Locations
docs.text:
Prompts are stored in: 

resources/prompts/
├── configuration/
│   └── wizard/
│       └── descriptionbuilder.prompt
├── job/
│   └── wizard/
│       └── jobAnalyzer.prompt
└── report/
    └── detail/
        └── reportAnalysis.prompt

docs.title: Local Models (Ollama)
docs.text:
System Requirements - CPU: Modern multi-core (ARM or x86) - RAM: 8GB minimum, 16GB recommended - Storage: 5-10GB per model

Performance Tips - Use smaller models for quick analysis - Larger models give better results but slower - GPU acceleration improves speed significantly

docs.title: Cloud Providers
docs.text:
Considerations - Network latency affects response time - API costs per token - Rate limits may apply

Cost Management - Use smaller models for routine analysis - Reserve larger models for complex cases - Monitor token usage

docs.title: Model Won't Load
docs.text:
Error: Failed to load model

Solutions - Verify model is pulled - Check available memory - Restart Ollama service

docs.title: API Connection Failed
docs.text:
Error: Cannot connect to LLM provider

Solutions - Verify API key is correct - Check network connectivity - Confirm provider status

docs.title: Slow Responses
docs.text:
Causes - Large model on limited hardware - Network latency (cloud) - Complex analysis request

Solutions - Use smaller model - Reduce max tokens - Check network connection

docs.title: Empty or Poor Results
docs.text:
Causes - Temperature too high - Max tokens too low - Model limitations

Solutions - Lower temperature (0.3-0.5) - Increase max tokens - Try different model

docs.title: Local Models (Recommended for Sensitive Data)
docs.text:
• All processing on HMS-Mirror server
  • No data sent externally
  • Full privacy control

docs.title: Cloud Providers
docs.text:
• Data sent to provider API
  • Review provider's data policies
  • Consider for non-sensitive configs

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use local models for sensitive configurations
  2. Review provider policies before using cloud
  3. Don't include secrets in analyzed configs
  4. Consider compliance requirements

docs.title: Disabling LLM Features
docs.text:
If you don't want to use LLM features:

  1. Go to Settings → LLM
  2. Uncheck Enable LLM Features
  3. Save settings

The ✨ icons will be hidden throughout the UI.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Configurations - Use AI analysis
  • Managing Jobs - Analyze jobs before running
  • Reports - Get insights from results

docs.title: Master Password
docs.text:
The Master Password protects the PKCS12 keystore that contains all encryption keys used by HMS-Mirror. Changing the master password re-encrypts the keystore with the new password while preserving all stored encryption keys.

Note

The Master Password tab is only visible to users with the ADMIN role and is hidden when Cloudera Manager mode is enabled.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror stores encryption keys in a PKCS12 keystore file at ~/.hms-mirror/keystore/hms-mirror-keys.p12. These keys encrypt all sensitive data including:

  • Database connection passwords
  • Kerberos keytab files
  • Kerberos credential passwords
  • Trino connection passwords
  • HCFS configuration files
  • Cloudera Manager connection passwords

The master password controls access to this keystore. Without the correct password, HMS-Mirror cannot decrypt any stored credentials.

For detailed information on the encryption architecture, see the Security Architecture overview.

docs.title: Viewing Keystore Status
docs.text:
Navigate to Preferences > Master Password. The status panel displays:

Field Description Status Whether the keystore file exists on disk (Active or Not Found) Configured Whether a keystore password has been set Keystore Path File system location of the PKCS12 keystore (e.g., ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror-keys.p12)

docs.title: Providing the Password
docs.text: The master password is resolved in the following order of precedence:

docs.title: Option 1: Environment Variable (Recommended)
docs.text:
export HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="your-secure-password"
java -jar hmsm-server-*.jar

docs.title: Option 2: System Property
docs.text: java -Dhms-mirror.keystore.password="your-secure-password" -jar hmsm-server-*.jar

docs.title: Option 3: Default Development Password
docs.text: If no environment variable or system property is set, HMS-Mirror uses the default development password defined in application.yaml (hms-mirror-dev-keystore-password). This is convenient for development but must be overridden for production deployments since it is a well-known value.

docs.title: Changing the Master Password
docs.text:
There are two common scenarios for changing the master password:

  • Initial production setup — replacing the default development password with a secure production password
  • Password rotation — periodically changing an existing production password per your organization's security policy

Both follow the same process in the UI.

docs.title: Step-by-Step Process
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Preferences > Master Password
  2. Review the Encryption Keystore Status panel to confirm the keystore is active
  3. In the Change Master Password form:
    • Enter the Current Password — this is the password HMS-Mirror is currently using (the default hms-mirror-dev-keystore-password if you haven't changed it, or your current production password)
    • Enter the New Password (minimum 8 characters)
    • Enter the new password again in Confirm New Password
  4. Click Change Master Password
  5. On success, a Restart Required alert appears (see below)

Tip

Each password field has a visibility toggle (eye icon) so you can verify what you've typed.

docs.title: Validation Rules
docs.text:
The form validates the following before submitting:

Rule Description Current password required Cannot be empty Current password correct Must match the password the keystore was encrypted with New password minimum length At least 8 characters Passwords must match New password and confirmation must be identical Password must change New password must differ from the current password

docs.title: What Happens During the Change
docs.text:
When you click Change Master Password, the server performs these steps:

  1. Validates the current password by loading the existing PKCS12 keystore
  2. Creates a backup of the current keystore with a timestamp suffix (e.g., hms-mirror-keys_20250210_143022.p12) in the same directory
  3. Creates a new keystore encrypted with the new password
  4. Transfers all encryption keys from the old keystore into the new one
  5. Saves the new keystore file and applies restrictive file permissions (0400)
  6. Updates the in-memory password reference for the remainder of the current session

All encryption keys remain unchanged — only the keystore's password protection is updated. Existing encrypted data (connection passwords, keytabs, etc.) is not affected.

docs.title: After Changing: Restart Required
docs.text:
After a successful password change, a prominent Restart Required alert appears with instructions:

  1. Before stopping the application, update your password configuration:

    If using an environment variable: 

    export HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="your-new-password"

    If using a system property: 

    # Add to your startup command:
-Dhms-mirror.keystore.password="your-new-password"

  2. Restart HMS-Mirror for the new password to take effect on the next startup

Warning

If HMS-Mirror is restarted without the correct password configured, it will not be able to open the keystore or decrypt any stored credentials. The application will fail to start properly.

Note

The current running session continues to work after the password change because the server updates its in-memory reference. The restart is required so that the new password is loaded from the environment on next startup.

docs.title: Complete Walkthrough: Moving to Production
docs.text:
This example shows the full process of replacing the default development password with a production password:

1. Start HMS-Mirror with the default password (no env var set):

java -jar hmsm-server-*.jar
# Uses default: hms-mirror-dev-keystore-password

2. Change the password in the UI:

  • Navigate to Preferences > Master Password
  • Current Password: hms-mirror-dev-keystore-password
  • New Password: My$ecureProductionP@ss2025
  • Confirm: My$ecureProductionP@ss2025
  • Click Change Master Password

3. Configure the new password in your environment:

# Add to your deployment scripts, systemd service file, or secrets manager:
export HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="My$ecureProductionP@ss2025"

4. Restart HMS-Mirror:

# Stop the running instance, then restart with the new password available
java -jar hmsm-server-*.jar

5. Verify — Log in and confirm connections and credentials are accessible.

docs.title: Keystore Backups
docs.text:
Each password change creates a timestamped backup in the keystore directory:

~/.hms-mirror/security/
  hms-mirror-keys.p12                       # Current keystore
  hms-mirror-keys_20250115_143022.p12       # Backup from Jan 15
  hms-mirror-keys_20250210_091500.p12       # Backup from Feb 10

Backups are protected with the previous password (the one that was active when the backup was created). These backups allow recovery if the new password is lost — you can restore a backup file over the current keystore and restart with the corresponding password.

Tip
