  • GitHub Issues - Report bugs or request features

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This guide helps you diagnose and resolve common issues with HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: Cannot Connect to HiveServer2
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Could not establish connection to jdbc:hive2://...
java.sql.SQLException: Unable to connect

Possible Causes and Solutions

Cause Solution Service not running Verify HiveServer2 is running on the cluster Wrong host/port Check hostname and port (default: 10000 binary, 10001 HTTP) Firewall blocking Check firewall rules, security groups Wrong transport mode Match transportMode (binary vs http) to server config SSL mismatch Enable/disable SSL to match server

Diagnostic Steps

  1. Test basic connectivity: 

    telnet your-host.example.com 10000

  2. Test with beeline: 

    beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://your-host:10000/default"

  3. Check server logs on the cluster

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication Failures
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: GSS initiate failed
javax.security.auth.login.LoginException: Unable to obtain password

Possible Causes and Solutions

Cause Solution No valid ticket Run kinit to obtain a ticket Expired ticket Run kinit to renew Wrong principal Verify the service principal Clock skew Sync time with KDC krb5.conf issues Verify /etc/krb5.conf configuration

Diagnostic Steps

  1. Check for valid ticket: 

    klist

  2. Obtain new ticket: 

    kinit -kt /path/to/keytab principal@REALM
# or interactively
kinit principal@REALM

  3. Test principals: 

    kvno hive/hostname@REALM

docs.title: Knox Gateway Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: HTTP 401 Unauthorized
Error: HTTP 403 Forbidden
Error: SSL handshake failed

Possible Causes and Solutions

Cause Solution Wrong credentials Verify Knox username/password Wrong httpPath Check Knox topology path Certificate issues Configure trust store correctly Knox service down Verify Knox is running

Example Knox URI 

jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive

docs.title: Metastore Direct Connection Failures
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Cannot connect to metastore database
Error: Access denied for user 'hive'@'host'

Possible Causes and Solutions

Cause Solution Wrong JDBC URL Verify database host, port, name Wrong credentials Check username and password Missing driver Ensure JDBC driver is in aux_libs Network blocked Check database firewall rules Permissions Grant SELECT on metastore tables

Required Drivers

Place in ~/.hms-mirror/aux_libs/: - MySQL: mysql-connector-java-8.0.x.jar - PostgreSQL: postgresql-42.x.x.jar - Oracle: ojdbc8.jar

docs.title: Table Creation Failures
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Error creating table
Error: Table already exists
Error: Permission denied

Solutions by Error

Table Already Exists - Enable table overwrite in configuration - Drop existing table manually - Use a different target database name

Permission Denied - Verify user has CREATE TABLE permission - Check database permissions - Verify warehouse directory permissions

Invalid Location - Ensure storage path exists - Check path permissions - Verify namespace is accessible

docs.title: Data Movement Failures
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Insert failed
Error: EXPORT failed
Error: File not found

SQL Strategy Failures

Error Cause Solution Out of memory Table too large Switch to EXPORT_IMPORT Timeout Query running too long Increase timeout, use EXPORT Type conversion Incompatible types Check column types

EXPORT_IMPORT Strategy Failures

Error Cause Solution No space Insufficient storage Clear space, use different path Permission denied Can't write to export path Check directory permissions Import failed Incompatible Hive versions Check version compatibility

docs.title: ACID Table Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Cannot export transactional table
Error: ACID operations not supported
Error: Compaction required

Solutions

Issue Solution Export fails Use EXPORT_IMPORT strategy Wrong Hive version Ensure compatible Hive 3.x versions Compaction needed Run compaction before migration Bucket mismatch Adjust bucket threshold settings

ACID Configuration

If ACID tables are problematic: 

acid:
  downgradeAcid: true  # Convert to external

docs.title: Partition Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Too many partitions
Error: Partition not found
Error: Dynamic partition strict mode

Solutions

Issue Solution Partition limit exceeded Increase limit or use EXPORT_IMPORT Missing partitions Verify source partitions exist Strict mode Set hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode=nonstrict

Configuration for Large Partitioned Tables

hybrid:
  sqlPartitionLimit: 500
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 1000

optimizations:
  overrides:
    - key: hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode
      rightValue: nonstrict
    - key: hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions
      rightValue: "10000"

docs.title: Slow Migrations
docs.text:
Symptoms - Jobs taking much longer than expected - Timeouts during migration - Resource exhaustion

Diagnostic Questions

  1. How large are the tables?
  2. How many partitions?
  3. What strategy is being used?
  4. What are cluster resources like?

Solutions by Cause

Large Tables with SQL Strategy - Switch to EXPORT_IMPORT - Use HYBRID with appropriate thresholds - Increase parallelism

Many Small Tables - Increase parallelism - Ensure metadata operations are fast - Check Metastore performance

Network Bottleneck - Check network bandwidth - Run HMS-Mirror closer to clusters - Use DISTCP for large data movement

docs.title: Memory Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Java heap space
Error: OutOfMemoryError
Error: GC overhead limit exceeded

Solutions

Increase HMS-Mirror memory: 

java -Xmx8g -Xms4g -jar hms-mirror-server.jar

Reduce job scope: - Process fewer databases per job - Split large datasets - Lower parallelism settings

docs.title: Query Timeouts
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Query timed out
Error: SocketTimeoutException

Solutions

Increase timeouts: 

hiveServer2:
  connectionTimeout: 300000  # 5 minutes
  queryTimeout: 3600000      # 1 hour

Or switch strategy: - Use EXPORT_IMPORT instead of SQL - Break large tables into smaller jobs

docs.title: Database Corruption
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: RocksDB corruption detected
Error: Cannot open database

Solution

  1. Stop HMS-Mirror
  2. Backup the RocksDB directory: 
    mv ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb.backup
  3. Restart HMS-Mirror (creates fresh database)
  4. Re-import configurations from backups

docs.title: Disk Space
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: No space left on device
Error: Cannot write to RocksDB

Solutions

  1. Clear old data: 

    # Check disk usage
du -sh ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb

  2. Move to larger disk: 

    mv ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb /larger/disk/rocksdb
# Update application.properties
hms-mirror.rocksdb.path=/larger/disk/rocksdb

docs.title: Permission Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error: Permission denied: rocksdb
Error: Cannot create lock file

Solution 

chmod -R 755 ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb
chown -R $USER ~/.hms-mirror/rocksdb

docs.title: UI Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptoms - Blank page - Connection refused - 404 errors

Solutions

Issue Solution Server not running Start HMS-Mirror server Wrong port Check configured port (default: 8090) Context path wrong Use /hms-mirror path Browser cache Clear browser cache, hard refresh

Verify Server Running 

curl http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/health

docs.title: API Errors
docs.text:
Symptoms 

Error 500: Internal Server Error
Error 400: Bad Request
Error 404: Not Found

Diagnostic Steps

  1. Check server logs: 

    tail -f ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log

  2. Enable debug logging: 

    logging.level.com.cloudera.utils.hms=DEBUG

  3. Test API directly: 

    curl -X GET http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/connections

docs.title: Log Locations
docs.text: Log Location Application ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log Reports ~/.hms-mirror/reports/<job-id>/ Console Standard output/error

docs.title: Enabling Debug Logging
docs.text:
Edit application.properties: 

logging.level.com.cloudera.utils.hms=DEBUG
logging.level.org.apache.hive=DEBUG

Or at startup: 

java -jar hms-mirror-server.jar --logging.level.com.cloudera.utils.hms=DEBUG

docs.title: Common Log Patterns
docs.text:
Connection Success 

INFO - Connected to HiveServer2: jdbc:hive2://...
INFO - Metastore connection established

Connection Failure 

ERROR - Failed to connect: java.sql.SQLException
WARN - Retrying connection...

Migration Progress 

INFO - Processing database: sales_db
INFO - Migrating table: orders (1/45)
INFO - Table orders: SUCCESS

Migration Error 

ERROR - Failed to migrate table: orders
ERROR - Cause: Partition limit exceeded

docs.title: Information to Collect
docs.text:
When reporting issues, gather:

  1. HMS-Mirror version 

    java -jar hms-mirror-server.jar --version

  2. Java version 

    java -version

  3. Configuration (sanitized - remove passwords)

  4. Log files with debug enabled

  5. Error messages (complete stack trace)

  6. Environment details

  7. Source cluster type and version
  8. Target cluster type and version
  9. Network topology

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Documentation: This guide and related docs
  • GitHub Issues: Report bugs and request features
  • Logs: Check application logs for detailed errors

docs.title: Self-Help Checklist
docs.text:
Before seeking help:

  • Checked logs for detailed error messages
  • Verified network connectivity to clusters
  • Tested connections individually
  • Ran validation on job
  • Tried with a minimal test case
  • Searched documentation for error message
  • Checked for common issues in this guide

docs.title: Common Fixes
docs.text: Problem Quick Fix Connection refused Check host/port/firewall Authentication failed Verify credentials/kinit Table exists Enable overwrite or use new name Out of memory Increase -Xmx or split job Too many partitions Increase limit or use EXPORT ACID errors Enable downgrade or use EXPORT Timeout Increase timeout or use different strategy Permission denied Check user permissions

docs.title: Useful Commands
docs.text:
# Check HMS-Mirror is running
curl http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/health

# Test HiveServer2 connectivity
beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default"

# Check Kerberos ticket
klist

# Get new Kerberos ticket
kinit -kt keytab principal@REALM

# Check disk space
df -h ~/.hms-mirror

# View recent logs
tail -100 ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log

# Find errors in logs
grep -i error ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log

docs.title: Quick Start Tutorial
docs.text: This tutorial walks you through your first HMS-Mirror migration in about 15 minutes. You'll perform a simple SCHEMA_ONLY migration to understand the workflow.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • HMS-Mirror installed and running (see Installation Guide)
  • Network access to at least one DataLake environment with HiveServer2
  • Valid credentials for the cluster (Kerberos ticket or username/password)

docs.title: What We'll Do
docs.text:
1. Create a connection to a cluster
  2. Create a basic configuration
  3. Create a dataset with a few tables
  4. Build and run a job
  5. Review the results

docs.title: Step 1: Access the Web UI
docs.text:
Open your browser and navigate to:

http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror

You'll see the HMS-Mirror dashboard with navigation options for Connections, Configurations, Datasets, and Jobs.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Connection
docs.text: First, we need to tell HMS-Mirror how to connect to your cluster.

docs.title: 2.1 Navigate to Connections
docs.text: Click Connections in the navigation menu.

docs.title: 2.2 Create New Connection
docs.text: Click the New Connection button (or use the wizard).

docs.title: 2.3 Fill in Basic Settings
docs.text:
Enter the following information:

Field Example Value Description Name my-cluster A friendly name for this connection Legacy No Check if this is HDP/CDH (pre-CDP)

docs.title: 2.4 Configure HiveServer2
docs.text:
In the HiveServer2 section:

Field Example Value Description URI jdbc:hive2://your-host:10000/default Your HiveServer2 JDBC URL Driver Class org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver Usually auto-detected

For Connection Properties, add any required settings: - transportMode → binary (or http for Knox) - ssl → true (if using SSL)

docs.title: 2.5 Configure Authentication
docs.text:
Select your authentication method:

No Authentication (development clusters) - Leave default settings

Kerberos (enterprise clusters) - Ensure you have a valid Kerberos ticket (klist to verify) - Add connection property: principal → hive/_HOST@REALM

LDAP (username/password) - Add connection property: user → your-username - Add connection property: password → your-password

docs.title: 2.6 Test the Connection
docs.text:
Click Test Connection to verify everything works.

Expected result: 

✓ Connection successful
  Connected to: Hive 3.1.3000
  Databases found: 15

docs.title: 2.7 Save the Connection
docs.text: Click Save to store your connection.

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Configuration
docs.text: Configurations define how the migration behaves. For this tutorial, we'll use minimal settings.

docs.title: 3.1 Navigate to Configurations
docs.text: Click Configurations in the navigation menu.

docs.title: 3.2 Create New Configuration
docs.text: Click New Configuration to open the configuration wizard.

docs.title: 3.3 Configuration Details
docs.text:
On the Details page:

Field Value Name quickstart-config Description Basic configuration for quick start tutorial

Click Next.

docs.title: 3.4 Migration Settings
docs.text:
For this tutorial, accept the defaults: - Copy Views: No (leave unchecked) - Evaluate Partition Location: No

Click Next.

docs.title: 3.5 Optimizations
docs.text: Accept the defaults and click Next.

docs.title: 3.6 Transfer Settings
docs.text: Accept the defaults and click Next.

docs.title: 3.7 ACID Settings
docs.text: Accept the defaults and click Next.

docs.title: 3.8 Conversions
docs.text: Accept the defaults (no Iceberg conversion).

docs.title: 3.9 Save Configuration
docs.text: Click Save to create your configuration.

docs.title: Step 4: Create a Dataset
docs.text: Datasets define which databases and tables to migrate.

docs.title: 4.1 Navigate to Datasets
docs.text: Click Datasets in the navigation menu.

docs.title: 4.2 Create New Dataset
docs.text: Click New Dataset.

docs.title: 4.3 Dataset Details
docs.text: Field Value Name quickstart-dataset Description Small dataset for tutorial

docs.title: 4.4 Add a Database
docs.text:
Click Add Database and enter:

Field Value Description Database Name default Or any database you want to test Include Tables .* All tables (or a specific pattern) Exclude Tables (empty) Leave blank to include all

Tip

For your first test, choose a database with only a few small tables.

docs.title: 4.5 Save Dataset
docs.text: Click Save to create your dataset.

docs.title: Step 5: Build a Job
docs.text: Now we'll combine everything into a job.

docs.title: 5.1 Navigate to Jobs
docs.text: Click Jobs in the navigation menu, then click Build.

docs.title: 5.2 Create New Job
docs.text: Click New Job or Build Job to start the job wizard.

docs.title: 5.3 Job Details
docs.text:
Field Value Name quickstart-job Description My first HMS-Mirror migration

Click Next.

docs.title: 5.4 Select Data Strategy
docs.text:
Choose SCHEMA_ONLY for this tutorial.

Why SCHEMA_ONLY?

This strategy only creates table definitions on the target without moving data. It's safe, fast, and perfect for learning the workflow.

Click Next.

docs.title: 5.5 Select References
docs.text:
Field Selection Dataset quickstart-dataset Configuration quickstart-config LEFT Connection my-cluster RIGHT Connection my-cluster (or another cluster if available)

Note

For SCHEMA_ONLY with the same cluster, you can use the same connection for both LEFT and RIGHT. The tables will be created in a different database (with a prefix or suffix based on configuration).

Click Next.

docs.title: 5.6 Options
docs.text:
Accept the defaults for this tutorial.

Click Next.

docs.title: 5.7 Review Summary
docs.text:
Review your job configuration:

Job: quickstart-job
Strategy: SCHEMA_ONLY
Dataset: quickstart-dataset (1 database)
Configuration: quickstart-config
LEFT: my-cluster
RIGHT: my-cluster

docs.title: Step 6: Validate the Job
docs.text: Before running, validate the job to catch any issues.

docs.title: 6.1 Click Validate
docs.text: On the job card or summary page, click Validate.

docs.title: 6.2 Review Validation Results
docs.text:
You should see:

✓ Job validation passed
  - Connections: Valid
  - Configuration: Valid
  - Dataset: Valid
  - Strategy compatibility: Valid

If there are warnings or errors, address them before proceeding.

docs.title: Step 7: Run a Dry Run
docs.text: A dry run generates all the SQL scripts without actually executing them.

docs.title: 7.1 Click Run (Dry Run)
docs.text: Click Run and ensure Dry Run is selected (or toggle it on).

docs.title: 7.2 Monitor Progress
docs.text: Watch the progress indicator as HMS-Mirror: 1. Connects to clusters 2. Discovers tables 3. Analyzes each table 4. Generates DDL scripts

docs.title: 7.3 Wait for Completion
docs.text: The job should complete in a few seconds to a couple of minutes, depending on the number of tables.

docs.title: 8.1 View the Report
docs.text: Once complete, click View Report on the job.

docs.title: 8.2 Understand the Report
docs.text:
The report shows:

Summary Section 

Total Tables: 5
Successful: 5
Failed: 0
Skipped: 0

Table Details

Each table shows: - Status (SUCCESS, FAILED, SKIPPED) - Strategy used - SQL statements generated - Any issues or warnings

Generated SQL

Expand a table to see the DDL:

-- LEFT Cluster (Source)
USE default;
SHOW CREATE TABLE my_table;

-- RIGHT Cluster (Target)
USE default;
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS my_table (
  id INT,
  name STRING,
  created_date DATE
)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://...'
TBLPROPERTIES (...);

docs.title: 8.3 Download Scripts (Optional)
docs.text: Click Download to get the SQL scripts as files you can review or execute manually.

docs.title: Step 9: Execute (Optional)
docs.text: Once you're comfortable with what the dry run shows:

docs.title: 9.1 Run with Execute
docs.text:
Click Run again, but this time disable the Dry Run option.

Warning

This will actually create tables on your target cluster. Make sure you're ready.

docs.title: 9.2 Monitor and Review
docs.text: The process is the same as dry run, but now the SQL is actually executed on the clusters.

docs.title: What You've Learned
docs.text:
Congratulations! You've completed your first HMS-Mirror migration. You now know how to:

  • ✅ Create cluster connections
  • ✅ Configure migration settings
  • ✅ Define datasets with database/table selections
  • ✅ Build and validate jobs
  • ✅ Run dry runs to preview changes
  • ✅ Review migration reports

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Now that you understand the basics:

docs.title: Try Different Strategies
docs.text: Experiment with other data strategies: - LINKED - Create views pointing to source data - SQL - Move data using INSERT statements - EXPORT_IMPORT - Use Hive's native export/import

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text:
• Core Concepts - Understand LEFT/RIGHT, strategies, and components
  • Data Strategies - Deep dive into each migration strategy
  • Managing Connections - Advanced connection settings
  • Managing Configurations - Full configuration options

docs.title: Plan a Real Migration
docs.text:
When you're ready for a real migration:

  1. Inventory your databases and tables
  2. Choose the appropriate strategy
  3. Test with a small subset first
  4. Run dry runs to validate
  5. Execute with monitoring

Tip

Use the AI Analysis feature (sparkles icon) on configurations and jobs to get recommendations and identify potential issues before running.

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text: Learn HMS-Mirror through hands-on, step-by-step tutorials. Each tutorial walks you through a complete scenario from start to finish.

docs.title: Quick Start Tutorial
docs.text:
Time: ~15 minutes | Level: Beginner

Your first HMS-Mirror migration! This tutorial covers:

  • ✅ Creating a cluster connection
  • ✅ Building a basic configuration
  • ✅ Defining a dataset
  • ✅ Running a SCHEMA_ONLY migration
  • ✅ Reviewing the results

What you'll learn: - The complete workflow from setup to execution - How the web UI components work together - How to validate and dry-run before executing

Prerequisites: - HMS-Mirror installed and running - Network access to at least one cluster - Valid authentication credentials

docs.title: Tutorial Roadmap
docs.text: After completing the Quick Start, continue your learning with these scenarios (coming soon):

docs.title: Planned Tutorials
docs.text: Tutorial Description Level SQL Data Migration Move small tables using INSERT...SELECT Intermediate ACID Table Migration Handle transactional tables properly Intermediate HDP to CDP Migration Complete cluster upgrade scenario Advanced Storage Migration to S3 Move from HDFS to cloud storage Advanced Disaster Recovery Setup Configure ongoing replication Advanced Iceberg Conversion Modernize tables to Iceberg format Advanced

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
We recommend following this progression:

Beginner                    Intermediate                 Advanced
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►

┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────────────┐
│ Quick Start   │───►│ SQL Migration │───►│ Full Cluster Migration│
│ (SCHEMA_ONLY) │    │ ACID Handling │    │ Storage Migrations    │
└───────────────┘    └───────────────┘    └───────────────────────┘

      ▼                     ▼                        ▼
  Learn the UI         Handle data            Production scenarios
  Basic workflow       Complex tables         Enterprise patterns

docs.title: Environment Checklist
docs.text:
Before starting any tutorial, verify:

  • HMS-Mirror server is running (curl http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/health)
  • You can access the web UI in your browser
  • You have network connectivity to your clusters
  • Authentication is ready (Kerberos ticket obtained if needed)

docs.title: Recommended Reading
docs.text:
For best results, read these before diving into tutorials:

  1. Introduction - Understand what HMS-Mirror does
  2. Core Concepts - Learn the terminology
  3. Data Strategies - Understand migration options

docs.title: Start Small
docs.text:
• Begin with a single database containing a few small tables
  • Use SCHEMA_ONLY or dry-run mode to preview without risk
  • Validate your setup before attempting large migrations

docs.title: Use Dry Run
docs.text: Every job can run in dry-run mode first: - Generates all scripts without executing - Shows exactly what will happen - Catches issues before they affect your clusters

docs.title: Check Reports
docs.text: After each migration: - Review the summary for success/failure counts - Examine failed tables for specific errors - Use AI analysis (if enabled) for insights

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you get stuck during a tutorial:

  • Troubleshooting Guide - Common problems and solutions
  • FAQ - Frequently asked questions
  • GitHub Issues - Report problems or ask questions

Ready to start? Begin with the Quick Start Tutorial.

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Connections
docs.text: HMS-Mirror can discover and import Hadoop/Hive configurations directly from Cloudera Manager instances, automatically creating HCFS profiles and HiveServer2 connections.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Instead of manually configuring connections, you can point HMS-Mirror at a Cloudera Manager instance and import:

  • HCFS Profiles — Hadoop storage configurations (HDFS, S3, ADLS, etc.)
  • HS2 Connections — HiveServer2 endpoints with JDBC URIs

The import process automatically detects:

  • Kerberos requirements
  • ZooKeeper-based HS2 discovery
  • SSL/TLS configurations
  • Namespace and warehouse settings

docs.title: Creating a CM Connection
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Connectors > Cloudera Manager Connections
  2. Click Add Connection
  3. Fill in the connection details:
Field Description Connection Name Friendly name for this CM instance Description Optional description Cloudera Manager URL CM API endpoint (e.g., https://cm.example.com:7183) API Username CM API user account API Password CM API password Use TLS (HTTPS) Enable TLS for CM communication Skip TLS Verification Allow self-signed certificates (not for production)
  1. Click Create Connection
  2. Click Test to verify connectivity

docs.title: Step 1: Discovery
docs.text:
1. Click Import on a CM connection
  2. HMS-Mirror scans the CM instance for importable items
  3. Discovered items are displayed in a hierarchical view:
  4. HCFS profiles (parent items)
  5. HS2 connections (child items, grouped under their HCFS profile)

Each item shows status badges:

  • Green badge — already exists in HMS-Mirror (will be skipped)
  • Yellow lock — requires Kerberos authentication
  • ZK badge — uses ZooKeeper-based HS2 discovery

docs.title: Step 2: Selection and Credentials
docs.text:
1. Select the items you want to import
  2. For Kerberized items: select a pre-existing Kerberos credential from the dropdown
  3. For non-Kerberized HCFS profiles: optionally enter a simple auth username
  4. For non-Kerberized HS2 connections: enter username and password

Tip

Create Kerberos credentials under Connections > Kerberos Credentials before importing, so they're available in the dropdown during import.

docs.title: Step 3: Import
docs.text:
1. Click Import Selected (n)
  2. Monitor the import progress
  3. Review results showing created, skipped, and errored items
  4. Click Done to complete

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Cloudera Manager instance must be network-accessible from HMS-Mirror
  • Valid CM API credentials with read access to cluster configurations
  • CM API typically runs on port 7183 (HTTPS) or 7180 (HTTP)
  • For Kerberized clusters: pre-create Kerberos credentials in HMS-Mirror before import

docs.title: Managing Configurations
docs.text: Configurations define how HMS-Mirror performs migrations - what options to apply, how to handle special cases, and what optimizations to use. This guide covers creating and managing configurations through the web UI.

docs.title: What is a Configuration?
docs.text:
A configuration is a reusable set of migration settings that controls:

  • Migration behavior - How to handle views, non-native tables, and properties
  • Optimizations - Query tuning and performance settings
  • Transfer settings - Warehouse directories and storage paths
  • ACID handling - Transactional table options
  • Conversions - Format transformations like Iceberg

Configurations are separate from jobs, allowing you to reuse the same settings across multiple migrations.

docs.title: Configurations Page
docs.text:
Navigate to Configurations from the main menu to see all saved configurations.

Each configuration card displays: - Configuration name - Description (if provided) - Key settings overview - Quick action buttons (Edit, Copy, Delete, AI Analysis)

docs.title: AI Analysis
docs.text:
Click the sparkles icon (✨) on any configuration card to get AI-powered analysis:

  • Configuration review and recommendations
  • Potential issues identified
  • Best practices suggestions
  • Risk assessment

docs.title: Using the Configuration Wizard
docs.text:
The wizard walks you through each configuration section.

  1. Click New Configuration or the + button
  2. Complete each wizard page:
  3. Details
  4. Migration
  5. Optimizations
  6. Transfer
  7. ACID
  8. Conversions

docs.title: Page 1: Details
docs.text:
Basic information about the configuration.

Field Description Example Name Unique identifier hdp-to-cdp-standard Description Purpose and notes Standard settings for HDP to CDP migration

docs.title: Page 2: Migration
docs.text: Controls which tables are processed and how.

docs.title: Views
docs.text: Option Description Copy Views Migrate view definitions to target Views Only Only process views, skip tables

docs.title: Non-Native Tables
docs.text:
Tables backed by external systems (HBase, Kudu, etc.)

Option Description Skip Don't process non-native tables Include Attempt to migrate

docs.title: Partition Handling
docs.text: Option Description Evaluate Partition Location Check and handle partition-specific locations Partition Discovery How to find partitions

docs.title: Table Properties
docs.text: Option Description Property Filter Regex to exclude certain properties Reset Location Reset external table locations

docs.title: Page 3: Optimizations
docs.text: Query performance and tuning settings.

docs.title: Query Optimizations
docs.text: Option Description Default Sort Dynamic Partition Inserts Order data for better partitioning false Auto Tune Let HMS-Mirror optimize settings false

docs.title: Compression
docs.text: Option Description Text Compression Compression codec for text formats Codec GZIP, SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD

docs.title: Statistics
docs.text: Option Description Gather Statistics Collect table/column stats Shadow Statistics Use shadow approach for stats

docs.title: Property Overrides
docs.text:
Custom Hive session properties applied during migration.

Structure 

Property Key → LEFT Value, RIGHT Value

Common Overrides

Property Description hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode Partition mode (strict/nonstrict) hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions Max partitions per query mapreduce.job.reduces Number of reducers

Adding Overrides

  1. Enter the property key
  2. Set the value for LEFT cluster (optional)
  3. Set the value for RIGHT cluster (optional)
  4. Click Add to save the override

docs.title: Page 4: Transfer
docs.text: Where data goes and how paths are translated.

docs.title: Warehouse Directories
docs.text:
Define target locations for migrated tables.

Directory Description Example External Warehouse Location for external tables s3a://bucket/warehouse/external Managed Warehouse Location for managed tables s3a://bucket/warehouse/managed

docs.title: Storage Settings
docs.text: Option Description Common Storage Shared storage path for both clusters Intermediate Storage Temp location for EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Namespace Translation
docs.text:
For STORAGE_MIGRATION strategy:

Option Description Translation Type RELATIVE or ALIGNED Source Namespace Original storage namespace Target Namespace New storage namespace

docs.title: Page 5: ACID
docs.text: Settings for transactional (ACID) tables.

docs.title: Basic Options
docs.text: Option Description Include ACID Tables Process transactional tables ACID Only Only process ACID tables

docs.title: Migration Options
docs.text: Option Description Downgrade ACID Convert to external tables In-Place Migrate within same cluster Migrate Full ACID migration

docs.title: Limits
docs.text: Option Description Default Partition Limit Max partitions for ACID migration 0 (no limit) Bucket Threshold Min buckets for SQL strategy 0

docs.title: Page 6: Conversions
docs.text: Table format transformations.

docs.title: Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
Convert Hive tables to Apache Iceberg format.

Option Description Enable Iceberg Turn on Iceberg conversion Conversion Mode IN_PLACE or SHADOW Target Version Iceberg format version (1 or 2) File Format Output format (PARQUET, ORC, AVRO)

docs.title: Modify an Existing Configuration
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Configurations
  2. Click Edit on the configuration card
  3. The wizard opens with current values
  4. Modify settings as needed
  5. Click Save

docs.title: Copying Configurations
docs.text:
Create a new configuration based on an existing one:

  1. Click Copy on the configuration card
  2. Enter a new name
  3. Modify settings as needed
  4. Save the new configuration

docs.title: Basic Schema Migration
docs.text:
name: schema-only-basic
migration:
  copyViews: false
  evaluatePartitionLocation: false
optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: false
acid:
  includeAcidTables: false

docs.title: Full Data Migration with Optimization
docs.text:
name: full-migration-optimized
migration:
  copyViews: true
  evaluatePartitionLocation: true
optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true
  autoTune: true
  textCompression: GZIP
transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://data-lake/warehouse/external'
    managed: 's3a://data-lake/warehouse/managed'
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: true

docs.title: ACID Table Migration
docs.text:
name: acid-migration
migration:
  copyViews: false
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  acidOnly: true
  downgradeAcid: false
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2
  partitionLimit: 500

docs.title: Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
name: iceberg-conversion
migration:
  copyViews: false
iceberg:
  enabled: true
  conversionMode: SHADOW
  targetVersion: 2
  fileFormat: PARQUET

docs.title: Storage Migration (HDFS to S3)
docs.text:
name: hdfs-to-s3-migration
transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: 's3a://new-warehouse/external'
    managed: 's3a://new-warehouse/managed'
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED
optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true

docs.title: Configuration Validation
docs.text: HMS-Mirror validates configurations before they can be used.

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text:
• Required fields are populated
  • Paths are properly formatted
  • Settings are compatible with selected strategy
  • No conflicting options

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
Configuration: hdp-migration-config

✓ Passed
  - All required fields present
  - Paths properly formatted
  - Settings compatible

⚠ Warnings
  - ACID tables will be downgraded to external
  - Consider enabling sortDynamicPartitionInserts for large partitioned tables

✗ Errors (if any)
  - Invalid warehouse path format

docs.title: Export a Configuration
docs.text:
1. Click Export on the configuration card
  2. Save the YAML file

docs.title: Import a Configuration
docs.text:
1. Click Import Configuration
  2. Select the YAML file
  3. Review and save

docs.title: Export Format
docs.text:
name: example-config
description: Example configuration
migration:
  copyViews: true
  evaluatePartitionLocation: true
  nonNativeTableHandling: SKIP
optimizations:
  sortDynamicPartitionInserts: true
  autoTune: false
  overrides:
    - key: hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode
      leftValue: nonstrict
      rightValue: nonstrict
transfer:
  warehouse:
    external: s3a://warehouse/external
    managed: s3a://warehouse/managed
acid:
  includeAcidTables: true
  downgradeAcid: true
  partitionLimit: 100
iceberg:
  enabled: false

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Create descriptive names that indicate purpose: - schema-only-basic - Basic schema migration - hdp-cdp-full-migration - Full migration from HDP to CDP - acid-downgrade-prod - Production ACID downgrade - iceberg-conversion-test - Iceberg conversion testing

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text:
• Start simple: Begin with minimal settings, add complexity as needed
  • Test first: Use configurations in dry-run mode before executing
  • Document: Use descriptions to explain configuration purposes
  • Version: Create new configurations rather than modifying existing ones for major changes

docs.title: Common Patterns
docs.text:
Development Environment - Disable optimization for faster testing - Smaller partition limits - Skip ACID tables initially

Production Environment - Enable all optimizations - Proper warehouse paths - Include all table types - Appropriate partition limits

docs.title: Reusability
docs.text: Design configurations for reuse: - Keep environment-specific paths in separate configurations - Use consistent naming conventions - Document any assumptions in the description

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Path Format Errors 

Error: Invalid warehouse path
Ensure paths start with proper scheme (hdfs://, s3a://, etc.)

Missing Required Fields 

Error: External warehouse directory required for STORAGE_MIGRATION
Fill in all required fields for your chosen strategy

Incompatible Settings 

Error: Cannot use ACID downgrade with in-place migration
Review and resolve conflicting options

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Use AI Analysis for configuration recommendations
  • Check the troubleshooting guide for common errors
  • Review logs for detailed error messages

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Datasets - Define what to migrate
  • Managing Jobs - Combine configurations into jobs
  • ACID Table Migration - Detailed ACID handling

docs.title: Managing Connections
docs.text: Connections define how HMS-Mirror communicates with your DataLake environments powered by Hive and Hive Metastore. This guide covers creating, editing, and managing connections through the web UI.

docs.title: What is a Connection?
docs.text:
A connection contains all the information needed to connect to a cluster:

  • HiveServer2 settings - JDBC URL, driver, and connection properties
  • Metastore Direct settings - Direct database access (optional)
  • Authentication - Kerberos, LDAP, or no authentication
  • Session properties - Hive session settings

Connections are reusable - define them once and use them in multiple jobs.

docs.title: Connections Page
docs.text:
Navigate to Connections from the main menu to see all saved connections.

Each connection card displays: - Connection name - Cluster type (Legacy or Non-Legacy) - HiveServer2 URI (if configured) - Metastore type (if configured) - Quick action buttons

docs.title: Connection Details
docs.text:
Click on a connection card to view full details:

Connection: prod-cluster
├── Type: Non-Legacy (CDP)
├── HiveServer2
│   ├── URI: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default
│   └── Properties: transportMode=binary, ssl=true
├── Metastore Direct
│   ├── Type: MYSQL
│   └── URI: jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive
└── Partition Discovery
    └── Auto: true

docs.title: Using the Connection Wizard
docs.text:
The wizard guides you through connection setup step by step.

  1. Click New Connection or the + button
  2. Follow the wizard pages:
  3. Basic Information
  4. HiveServer2 Configuration
  5. Metastore Direct (Optional)
  6. Session Properties

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text:
Field Description Example Name Unique identifier for this connection prod-hdp-cluster Description Optional description Production HDP 3.1 cluster Legacy Check if HDP or CDH (pre-CDP) ☑ for HDP/CDH

Note

The Legacy flag affects how HMS-Mirror handles certain table types and properties.

docs.title: HiveServer2 Configuration
docs.text: Configure the primary connection method.

docs.title: URI Format
docs.text:
The HiveServer2 JDBC URI follows this pattern:

jdbc:hive2://<host>:<port>/<database>[;param1=value1;param2=value2]

Common URI patterns:

Standard Binary Transport 

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default

HTTP Transport 

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10001/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=cliservice

SSL Enabled 

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;ssl=true

Knox Gateway 

jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive

Zookeeper Discovery 

jdbc:hive2://zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2

docs.title: Driver Class
docs.text:
Usually auto-detected, but can be specified:

Driver Class Apache Hive org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver

docs.title: URI Parameters
docs.text:
All connection properties should be included directly in the JDBC URI string. Common parameters include:

Parameter Description Values transportMode Transport protocol binary, http httpPath HTTP endpoint path cliservice, gateway/... ssl Enable SSL/TLS true, false sslTrustStore Path to trust store /path/to/truststore.jks sslTrustStorePassword Trust store password password principal Kerberos service principal hive/_HOST@REALM user Username for LDAP auth username password Password for LDAP auth password

Important

Include all connection properties in the URI string rather than as separate connection properties. For example: jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default;ssl=true;transportMode=binary

docs.title: Metastore Direct Configuration
docs.text: Optional: Configure direct Metastore database access for advanced operations.

docs.title: Metastore Types
docs.text: Type Description MYSQL MySQL or MariaDB POSTGRESQL PostgreSQL ORACLE Oracle Database MSSQL Microsoft SQL Server

docs.title: URI Formats
docs.text:
MySQL/MariaDB 

jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive?useSSL=false

PostgreSQL 

jdbc:postgresql://metastore.example.com:5432/hive

Oracle 

jdbc:oracle:thin:@metastore.example.com:1521:hive

docs.title: Credentials
docs.text:
Field Description Username Database user with read access Password User's password

Security Note

Passwords are stored encrypted. Consider using a service account with minimal required permissions.

docs.title: Partition Discovery
docs.text:
Configure how HMS-Mirror discovers table partitions.

Option Description Auto Automatically discover partitions Manual Specify partition locations manually

docs.title: No Authentication
docs.text:
Use for development clusters without security enabled.

No additional configuration required in the URI.

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication
docs.text:
For clusters with Kerberos security, the service principal must be included in the URI:

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@YOUR.REALM

Prerequisites - Kerberos credentials configured in Utilities > Preferences > Kerberos - Access to KDC - Properly configured /etc/krb5.conf on the HMS-Mirror server

Important: The Kerberos service principal must be in the JDBC URI, not as a separate connection property. Your user credentials (keytab or password) are managed centrally through the Preferences > Kerberos tab.

See Kerberos Authentication for detailed configuration.

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text:
For clusters using username/password authentication, include credentials in the URI:

jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;user=your-username;password=your-password

docs.title: Knox Gateway Authentication
docs.text:
For clusters accessed through Apache Knox, include all parameters in the URI:

jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive;user=knox-username;password=knox-password

docs.title: Testing Connections
docs.text: Always test connections before saving.

docs.title: Running a Test
docs.text:
1. After configuring the connection, click Test Connection
  2. HMS-Mirror attempts to connect and run basic queries
  3. Review the results

docs.title: Successful Test
docs.text:
✓ Connection Successful

HiveServer2:
  ✓ Connected successfully
  ✓ Server version: 3.1.3000
  ✓ Databases found: 25

Metastore Direct (if configured):
  ✓ Connected successfully
  ✓ Metastore version: 3.1.0

docs.title: Failed Test
docs.text:
✗ Connection Failed

Error: Could not establish connection
Details: java.sql.SQLException: Cannot connect to jdbc:hive2://...
Cause: Connection refused

Suggestions:
- Verify the host and port are correct
- Check network connectivity
- Verify the service is running

docs.title: Troubleshooting Connection Failures
docs.text: Error Possible Cause Solution Connection refused Service not running or wrong port Verify HS2 is running, check port Host not found DNS or hostname issue Use IP address or fix DNS Authentication failed Wrong credentials Verify username/password or Kerberos ticket SSL handshake failed Certificate issue Check trust store configuration Timeout Network/firewall issue Check firewall rules and network path

docs.title: Modify an Existing Connection
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Connections
  2. Click the Edit button on the connection card
  3. Make your changes
  4. Click Test Connection to verify
  5. Click Save

docs.title: What Can Be Changed
docs.text:
• Connection name and description
  • HiveServer2 URI and properties
  • Metastore Direct settings
  • Authentication configuration
  • Session properties

docs.title: What Can't Be Changed
docs.text: Some operations require creating a new connection: - If jobs reference the connection and are running

docs.title: Remove a Connection
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Connections
  2. Click the Delete button on the connection card
  3. Confirm the deletion

docs.title: Deletion Restrictions
docs.text: You cannot delete a connection that is: - Currently in use by a running job - Referenced by saved jobs (you'll be warned)

docs.title: Handling Referenced Connections
docs.text:
If a connection is referenced by jobs: 1. Update or delete the referencing jobs first 2. Then delete the connection

Or force delete (will invalidate referencing jobs).

docs.title: Export a Connection
docs.text:
Export connection configuration for backup or sharing:

  1. Click the Export button on a connection
  2. Save the YAML file
name: prod-cluster
legacyHive: false
hiveServer2:
  uri: jdbc:hive2://hs2.example.com:10000/default;transportMode=binary;ssl=true
metastoreDirect:
  type: MYSQL
  uri: jdbc:mysql://metastore.example.com:3306/hive

docs.title: Import a Connection
docs.text:
Import a previously exported connection:

  1. Click Import Connection
  2. Select or drag the YAML file
  3. Review the imported settings
  4. Click Save

Note

Sensitive data (passwords) are not included in exports. You'll need to re-enter them after import.

docs.title: Session Properties
docs.text: Session properties are Hive configuration settings applied when HMS-Mirror connects.

docs.title: Common Session Properties
docs.text: Property Description Example hive.exec.dynamic.partition Enable dynamic partitioning true hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode Partitioning mode nonstrict hive.exec.compress.output Compress query output true hive.exec.parallel Enable parallel execution true

docs.title: Adding Session Properties
docs.text: Session properties are configured separately from connection properties and are applied after the connection is established.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Use clear, descriptive names: - Include environment: prod-, dev-, test- - Include cluster identifier: -hdp-cluster, -cdp-east - Example: prod-cdp-east-cluster

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Use service accounts with minimum required permissions
  • Rotate credentials regularly
  • Use keytab files instead of passwords where possible
  • Don't share connections with production credentials

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
• Create separate connections for each environment
  • Document connection purposes in descriptions
  • Group related connections by naming convention

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Always test connections after creation
  • Re-test after any infrastructure changes
  • Test periodically to catch configuration drift

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Configurations - Configure migration settings
  • Managing Datasets - Define what to migrate
  • Kerberos Authentication - Detailed Kerberos setup

docs.title: Managing Datasets
docs.text: Datasets define what databases and tables to migrate. This guide covers creating and managing datasets through the web UI.

docs.title: What is a Dataset?
docs.text:
A dataset specifies:

  • Which databases to include in a migration
  • Which tables within those databases (using filters)
  • Database-specific settings like renaming or custom locations

Datasets are reusable - define them once and use them in multiple jobs with different configurations or strategies.

docs.title: Datasets Page
docs.text:
Navigate to Datasets from the main menu to see all saved datasets.

Each dataset card displays: - Dataset name - Description (if provided) - Number of databases included - Quick action buttons

docs.title: Dataset Details
docs.text: Click on a dataset to see: - List of included databases - Table filter patterns for each database - Target database settings

docs.title: New Dataset Form
docs.text:
1. Click New Dataset or the + button
  2. Fill in the basic information
  3. Add databases with their settings
  4. Save the dataset

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text: Field Description Example Name Unique identifier sales-tables Description Purpose and scope All tables from sales databases

docs.title: Adding Databases
docs.text: Click Add Database to include a database in the dataset.

docs.title: Database Settings
docs.text: Field Description Required Database Name Source database name Yes Include Pattern Regex for tables to include No Exclude Pattern Regex for tables to exclude No Target Name Rename database on target No Target Warehouse Custom location on target No

docs.title: Table Filtering
docs.text: Use regex patterns to control which tables are included.

docs.title: Include Pattern
docs.text:
Tables matching this pattern are included. Leave empty to include all.

Pattern Description Matches .* All tables Everything fact_.* Tables starting with fact_ fact_sales, fact_orders .*_dim Tables ending with _dim customer_dim, product_dim fact_.*\|dim_.* Multiple patterns fact_, dim_ customer Exact match customer (exact) customer.* Starting with customer, customer_info, customers

docs.title: Exclude Pattern
docs.text:
Tables matching this pattern are excluded, even if they match the include pattern.

Pattern Description Excludes test_.* Test tables test_orders, test_data .*_backup Backup tables orders_backup, customers_backup tmp_.*\|temp_.* Temporary tables tmp_, temp_ .*_archive Archive tables sales_archive, logs_archive

docs.title: Filter Evaluation Order
docs.text:
1. Include pattern is applied first (defaults to all if empty)
  2. Exclude pattern removes matches from the included set
  3. Final set is processed

Example

Database: sales_db
Include: .*
Exclude: test_.*|.*_backup

Result:
✓ customers
✓ orders
✓ products
✗ test_orders (excluded by test_.*)
✗ customers_backup (excluded by .*_backup)

docs.title: Common Filter Patterns
docs.text:
All fact tables 

Include: fact_.*
Exclude: (empty)

All tables except test and temp 

Include: .*
Exclude: test_.*|temp_.*|tmp_.*

Specific tables only 

Include: customers|orders|products
Exclude: (empty)

Tables with specific suffix 

Include: .*_current|.*_latest
Exclude: (empty)

docs.title: Database Renaming
docs.text: Change the database name on the target cluster.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Environment prefixes: sales → prod_sales
  • Namespace changes: legacy_db → new_db
  • Version suffixes: analytics → analytics_v2

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Source Target Name Result sales sales_migrated Creates sales_migrated on target orders prod_orders Creates prod_orders on target

docs.title: Custom Warehouse Locations
docs.text: Override the default warehouse location for specific databases.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Different storage tiers for different databases
  • Regulatory requirements for data location
  • Performance optimization (different storage backends)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Database Target Warehouse pii_data s3a://secure-bucket/warehouse/pii analytics s3a://analytics-bucket/warehouse temp_data hdfs:///tmp/warehouse

docs.title: Simple Single Database
docs.text:
name: sales-all-tables
description: All tables from sales database
databases:
  - name: sales_db
    includePattern: .*

docs.title: Multiple Databases with Filters
docs.text:
name: production-data
description: Production fact and dimension tables
databases:
  - name: sales_db
    includePattern: fact_.*|dim_.*
    excludePattern: test_.*
  - name: inventory_db
    includePattern: .*
    excludePattern: .*_staging|.*_temp
  - name: customers_db
    includePattern: customer.*|account.*

docs.title: Database Renaming
docs.text:
name: hdp-to-cdp-migration
description: Migrate with renamed databases
databases:
  - name: sales_db
    targetName: cdp_sales_db
    includePattern: .*
  - name: inventory_db
    targetName: cdp_inventory_db
    includePattern: .*

docs.title: Custom Warehouse Locations
docs.text:
name: tiered-storage-migration
description: Different storage tiers per database
databases:
  - name: hot_data
    targetWarehouse: s3a://fast-storage/warehouse
    includePattern: .*
  - name: cold_data
    targetWarehouse: s3a://archive-storage/warehouse
    includePattern: .*
  - name: pii_data
    targetWarehouse: s3a://secure-storage/warehouse
    includePattern: .*

docs.title: Selective Table Migration
docs.text:
name: incremental-migration-batch-1
description: First batch of tables for phased migration
databases:
  - name: enterprise_db
    includePattern: customers|orders|products|inventory
  - name: analytics_db
    includePattern: daily_.*|weekly_.*
    excludePattern: .*_test

docs.title: Modify an Existing Dataset
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Datasets
  2. Click Edit on the dataset card
  3. Modify databases, filters, or settings
  4. Save changes

docs.title: Adding/Removing Databases
docs.text:
• Click Add Database to include new databases
  • Click the Remove button on a database to exclude it

docs.title: Updating Filters
docs.text:
Modify include/exclude patterns to change which tables are processed.

Note

Filter changes take effect on the next job run.

docs.title: Copying Datasets
docs.text:
Create a new dataset based on an existing one:

  1. Click Copy on the dataset card
  2. Enter a new name
  3. Modify as needed
  4. Save

docs.title: Export a Dataset
docs.text:
1. Click Export on the dataset card
  2. Save the YAML file

docs.title: Import a Dataset
docs.text:
1. Click Import Dataset
  2. Select the YAML file
  3. Review and save

docs.title: Export Format
docs.text:
name: example-dataset
description: Example dataset for documentation
databases:
  - name: sales_db
    includePattern: ".*"
    excludePattern: "test_.*"
    targetName: null
    targetWarehouse: null
  - name: analytics_db
    includePattern: "fact_.*|dim_.*"
    excludePattern: ".*_staging"
    targetName: analytics_prod_db
    targetWarehouse: "s3a://analytics/warehouse"

docs.title: Dataset Validation
docs.text: When used in a job, HMS-Mirror validates datasets:

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text:
• Database names are valid
  • Regex patterns are syntactically correct
  • Target names don't conflict
  • Databases exist on source cluster

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
Dataset: production-migration

✓ Passed
  - All databases found on source
  - Regex patterns valid

⚠ Warnings
  - Database 'legacy_db' has 0 tables matching filters

✗ Errors (if any)
  - Database 'missing_db' not found on source cluster
  - Invalid regex in exclude pattern: [unclosed

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Use descriptive names: - By purpose: financial-reports-tables - By environment: prod-to-dr-sync - By phase: migration-phase-1-batch-2 - By department: marketing-analytics

docs.title: Filter Design
docs.text:
Start broad, then narrow 1. Begin with .* to include all 2. Add exclude patterns for unwanted tables 3. Refine as needed

Test patterns first - Use a tool like regex101.com to test patterns - Verify matches before running migrations

Be specific - Avoid overly broad patterns that might include future tables - Document pattern intentions in descriptions

docs.title: Dataset Organization
docs.text:
By migration phase 

migration-phase-1-critical
migration-phase-2-secondary
migration-phase-3-archive

By data domain 

customer-data-tables
financial-data-tables
operational-data-tables

By frequency 

daily-sync-dataset
weekly-sync-dataset
one-time-migration

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Smaller datasets run faster than large ones
  • Split large migrations into multiple datasets
  • Consider table sizes when grouping

docs.title: No Tables Found
docs.text:
Warning: 0 tables matched for database 'sales_db'

Possible causes: - Include pattern too restrictive - Database name misspelled - Tables don't exist on source

Solutions: - Verify database and table names on source - Test regex pattern - Use .* to include all, then refine

docs.title: Too Many Tables
docs.text:
Warning: 10,000+ tables matched

Considerations: - Migration will take longer - Consider splitting into multiple datasets - Use more restrictive filters

docs.title: Pattern Errors
docs.text:
Error: Invalid regex pattern: [unclosed bracket

Solution: - Fix regex syntax - Escape special characters properly - Test pattern before saving

docs.title: Conflicting Targets
docs.text:
Error: Multiple databases targeting 'sales_db'

Solution: - Use unique target names for each database - Remove duplicate database entries

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Jobs - Combine datasets with configurations
  • Managing Connections - Set up cluster connections
  • Quick Start Tutorial - See datasets in action

docs.title: Managing Jobs
docs.text: Jobs are where everything comes together - combining datasets, configurations, and connections to execute migrations. This guide covers creating, validating, and running jobs through the web UI.

docs.title: What is a Job?
docs.text:
A job combines:

  • Dataset - What databases and tables to migrate
  • Configuration - How to perform the migration
  • Data Strategy - Which migration method to use
  • LEFT Connection - Source cluster connection
  • RIGHT Connection - Target cluster connection (if needed)
  • Options - Strategy-specific settings

Jobs can be saved, validated, run multiple times, and their results tracked.

docs.title: Jobs Page
docs.text:
Navigate to Jobs → Build from the main menu to see all saved jobs.

Each job card displays: - Job name and description - Data strategy - Referenced components - Status (ready, running, completed) - Quick action buttons

docs.title: Job Actions
docs.text: Icon Action Description ✏️ Edit Modify job settings ▶️ Run Execute the job ✓ Validate Check for errors ✨ AI Analysis Get AI-powered recommendations 📋 Summary View job summary 🗑️ Delete Remove the job

docs.title: Using the Job Wizard
docs.text:
The wizard guides you through job creation in 5 steps.

  1. Click New Job or Build Job
  2. Complete each wizard page:
  3. Job Details
  4. Data Strategy
  5. References
  6. Options
  7. Summary

docs.title: Step 1: Job Details
docs.text:
Basic information about the job.

Field Description Example Name Unique job identifier hdp-to-cdp-sales-migration Description Purpose and notes Migrate sales database from HDP to CDP

docs.title: Step 2: Data Strategy
docs.text:
Select the migration method.

Strategy Description When to Use SCHEMA_ONLY Schema without data Testing, schema sync LINKED Views to source data Quick access SQL Data via INSERT Small-medium tables EXPORT_IMPORT Hive export/import ACID tables HYBRID Automatic selection Mixed workloads STORAGE_MIGRATION Same-cluster move Storage changes COMMON Shared storage Linked clusters DUMP DDL extraction Documentation ICEBERG_CONVERSION Format conversion Modernization

Tip

Use the Strategy Selection Wizard if unsure which strategy to choose.

docs.title: Step 3: References
docs.text:
Select the components for this job.

Reference Description Required Dataset What to migrate Yes Configuration How to migrate Yes LEFT Connection Source cluster Yes RIGHT Connection Target cluster Depends on strategy

docs.title: Reference Selection
docs.text:
1. Click the dropdown for each reference
  2. Select from saved items
  3. Or click Create New to build on the fly

docs.title: Connection Requirements by Strategy
docs.text: Strategy LEFT RIGHT SCHEMA_ONLY Required Required LINKED Required Required SQL Required Required EXPORT_IMPORT Required Required HYBRID Required Required STORAGE_MIGRATION Required Optional COMMON Required Required DUMP Required Not needed ICEBERG_CONVERSION Required Optional

docs.title: Step 4: Options
docs.text: Strategy-specific settings.

docs.title: Storage/Namespace Options
docs.text:
For strategies that move data or change locations:

Option Description Reset to Default Reset to default warehouse location Global Location Map Namespace translation rules

docs.title: Hybrid Strategy Options
docs.text:
When using HYBRID strategy:

Option Description Default SQL Partition Limit Max partitions for SQL 100 SQL Size Limit Max table size for SQL 1GB Export Partition Limit Max partitions for EXPORT 500

docs.title: Step 5: Summary
docs.text:
Review the complete job configuration before saving.

Job Summary
───────────────────────────────
Name: hdp-to-cdp-sales-migration
Strategy: HYBRID

References:
  Dataset: sales-tables (3 databases)
  Configuration: hdp-migration-config
  LEFT: hdp-prod-cluster
  RIGHT: cdp-prod-cluster

Options:
  SQL Partition Limit: 100
  Export Partition Limit: 500

Warnings:
  ⚠ ACID tables will be downgraded
───────────────────────────────

Click Save to create the job.

docs.title: Validating Jobs
docs.text: Before running, validate jobs to catch configuration issues.

docs.title: Run Validation
docs.text:
1. Click Validate on the job card
  2. Wait for validation to complete
  3. Review results

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror validates:

  • Connections - Can connect to clusters
  • Dataset - Databases exist, patterns valid
  • Configuration - Settings compatible with strategy
  • Strategy compatibility - All pieces work together
  • Permissions - Access to required resources

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
All Passed 

✓ Job validation passed

Connections:
  ✓ LEFT: Connected successfully
  ✓ RIGHT: Connected successfully

Dataset:
  ✓ sales_db: 45 tables matched
  ✓ inventory_db: 23 tables matched

Configuration:
  ✓ All settings valid

Strategy:
  ✓ HYBRID compatible with configuration

With Warnings 

✓ Job validation passed with warnings

⚠ Warnings:
  - 3 ACID tables will be downgraded to external
  - Large table 'orders' (50GB) will use EXPORT_IMPORT
  - 2 views will be skipped (copyViews disabled)

With Errors 

✗ Job validation failed

✗ Errors:
  - Cannot connect to RIGHT cluster
  - Database 'missing_db' not found
  - Invalid warehouse path format

docs.title: Addressing Validation Issues
docs.text:
1. Review each error and warning
  2. Edit the job or referenced components
  3. Re-validate until all issues resolved

docs.title: Dry Run vs Execute
docs.text:
Mode Description Effect Dry Run Generate scripts only No changes to clusters Execute Run the migration Creates tables, moves data

Best Practice

Always run a dry run first to review planned operations.

docs.title: Starting a Job
docs.text:
1. Click Run on the job card
  2. Select mode:
  3. Dry Run (default and recommended first)
  4. Execute
  5. Click Start

docs.title: Monitoring Progress
docs.text:
While the job runs, you can monitor:

Progress Bar 

[████████░░░░░░░░░░░░] 40%
Processing: sales_db.orders
Tables: 18/45

Status Updates - Connecting to clusters - Discovering tables - Analyzing schemas - Generating DDL - Executing statements (Execute mode) - Generating reports

docs.title: Job States
docs.text: State Description Ready Job saved, not yet run Running Currently executing Completed Finished successfully Failed Encountered errors Cancelled Stopped by user

docs.title: Reports Page
docs.text: After completion, click View Report to see results.

docs.title: Report Contents
docs.text:
Summary 

Migration Summary
─────────────────
Total Tables: 68
Successful: 65
Failed: 2
Skipped: 1

Duration: 15m 32s
Strategy: HYBRID

Per-Table Details

Each table shows: - Status (SUCCESS, FAILED, SKIPPED) - Strategy used (for HYBRID) - Generated SQL - Execution time - Errors (if any)

Generated Scripts

View or download the SQL scripts: - DDL statements - Data movement queries - Cleanup scripts

docs.title: Error Analysis
docs.text:
For failed tables: 

Table: sales_db.large_orders
Status: FAILED
Error: Exceeded partition limit (1500 > 500)
Suggestion: Increase partitionLimit in configuration

docs.title: AI Report Analysis
docs.text: Click the sparkles icon to get AI-powered analysis: - Success rate assessment - Error categorization - Root cause analysis - Recommendations for failures - Best practices suggestions

docs.title: Editing Jobs
docs.text:
1. Click Edit on the job card
  2. Modify settings
  3. Save changes

Note

Running jobs cannot be edited.

docs.title: Copying Jobs
docs.text:
Create a new job based on an existing one:

  1. Click Copy on the job card
  2. Enter a new name
  3. Modify as needed
  4. Save

docs.title: Deleting Jobs
docs.text:
1. Click Delete on the job card
  2. Confirm deletion

Note

Deleting a job does not undo migrations or delete reports.

docs.title: Re-running Jobs
docs.text:
Jobs can be run multiple times:

  1. Click Run on the job card
  2. Select mode
  3. Start

Each run creates a new report with a timestamp.

docs.title: Schema Synchronization Job
docs.text:
name: nightly-schema-sync
strategy: SCHEMA_ONLY
dataset: all-databases
configuration: schema-only-config
leftConnection: prod-cluster
rightConnection: dr-cluster

docs.title: Full Migration Job
docs.text:
name: hdp-to-cdp-migration
strategy: HYBRID
dataset: production-tables
configuration: full-migration-optimized
leftConnection: hdp-cluster
rightConnection: cdp-cluster
options:
  hybrid:
    sqlPartitionLimit: 100
    exportPartitionLimit: 500

docs.title: Storage Migration Job
docs.text:
name: hdfs-to-s3-migration
strategy: STORAGE_MIGRATION
dataset: analytics-database
configuration: s3-migration-config
leftConnection: cluster-hdfs
options:
  storage:
    targetNamespace: s3a://data-lake

docs.title: Strategy Selection Wizard
docs.text: If you're unsure which strategy to use, the wizard helps you decide.

docs.title: Using the Wizard
docs.text:
1. On the Data Strategy page, click Help Me Choose
  2. Answer questions about your migration:
  3. Do you need to move data?
  4. Do you have ACID tables?
  5. What are your table sizes?
  6. Same cluster or different?
  7. Get a recommended strategy with explanation

docs.title: Wizard Questions
docs.text:
1. Data Movement
  2. Do tables need data copied to target?

  3. Or just schema definitions?

  4. Table Types

  5. Do you have ACID (transactional) tables?

  6. Any views to migrate?

  7. Scale

  8. Approximate table sizes?

  9. Number of partitions?

  10. Infrastructure

  11. Same cluster or different?
  12. Shared storage available?

docs.title: Wizard Result
docs.text:
Recommended Strategy: HYBRID

Reasoning:
- You have mixed table sizes → HYBRID handles this automatically
- ACID tables present → Will use EXPORT_IMPORT for those
- Small tables → Will use SQL for efficiency

Alternative: EXPORT_IMPORT
- If all tables are large
- If exact data fidelity is critical

docs.title: Job Organization
docs.text:
Naming Conventions - Include purpose: schema-sync-, full-migration- - Include source/target: hdp-to-cdp- - Include scope: -sales-db, -phase-1

Example: hdp-to-cdp-full-migration-sales-db-phase-1

docs.title: Execution Strategy
docs.text:
Start Small 1. Test with a single small database first 2. Verify results 3. Scale up gradually

Use Dry Runs 1. Always dry run before execute 2. Review generated SQL 3. Check for warnings

Phase Large Migrations 1. Split into multiple jobs by database or table group 2. Run sequentially or in parallel (different databases) 3. Verify each phase before proceeding

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Before Running - Validate thoroughly - Address all errors - Understand warnings

During Running - Monitor progress - Check for early failures - Be ready to cancel if needed

After Running - Review reports immediately - Investigate failures - Document issues and solutions

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Use job descriptions to document purpose
  • Keep notes on configuration choices
  • Track job history for auditing

docs.title: Job Won't Start
docs.text:
Connection Issues 

Error: Cannot connect to LEFT cluster
- Verify cluster is running - Check network connectivity - Validate credentials

Validation Errors 

Error: Job validation failed
- Run validation to see specific issues - Fix errors before running

docs.title: Job Fails During Execution
docs.text:
Permission Errors 

Error: Permission denied creating table
- Check user permissions on target - Verify service account access

Resource Errors 

Error: Out of memory
- Reduce parallelism - Increase HMS-Mirror memory - Split into smaller jobs

docs.title: Slow Performance
docs.text:
Large Tables - Consider EXPORT_IMPORT over SQL - Increase partition limits appropriately - Run during off-peak hours

Many Tables - Split into multiple jobs - Process databases in parallel - Use appropriate strategy for scale

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Reports and Results - Detailed report analysis
  • Data Strategies - Strategy deep dive
  • Troubleshooting - Common issues and solutions

docs.title: Web UI Overview
docs.text: This guide introduces the HMS-Mirror web interface, its navigation structure, and common UI patterns.

docs.title: Accessing the Web UI
docs.text:
After starting HMS-Mirror, open your browser to:

http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror

Replace localhost and 8090 with your server's hostname and configured port.

docs.title: Main Menu
docs.text:
The navigation menu provides access to all major sections.

Menu Item Description Path Dashboard Overview and status /hms-mirror Connections Cluster connections /hms-mirror/connections Configurations Migration settings /hms-mirror/config Datasets Database/table selections /hms-mirror/datasets Jobs Build and manage jobs /hms-mirror/jobs Reports View migration reports /hms-mirror/reports Settings Application settings /hms-mirror/settings

docs.title: Sub-Navigation
docs.text:
Some sections have sub-pages:

Jobs - Build - Create and manage jobs - Runtime - Running job status

Settings - Preferences - User preferences - LLM - AI integration settings - RocksDB - Storage management

docs.title: Responsive Design
docs.text:
The UI adapts to different screen sizes:

Desktop (>1024px) - Full sidebar navigation - Multi-column card layouts - Side-by-side form columns

Tablet (768px-1024px) - Collapsible navigation - Two-column layouts - Stacked form sections

Mobile (<768px) - Hamburger menu - Single-column layouts - Full-width forms

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror follows accessibility best practices:

  • Keyboard navigation - All features accessible via keyboard
  • Screen reader support - Proper ARIA labels
  • Color contrast - WCAG compliant colors
  • Focus indicators - Visible focus states

docs.title: Theme
docs.text: The UI uses a consistent color theme: - Primary: Blue (#3B82F6) - Success: Green (#10B981) - Warning: Yellow (#F59E0B) - Error: Red (#EF4444) - Neutral: Gray scale

docs.title: Preferences
docs.text: User preferences (Settings → Preferences): - Items per page - Default view (grid/list) - Notification settings

  • Managing Configurations - Set up migration options

docs.title: Reports and Results
docs.text: After running a migration job, HMS-Mirror generates detailed reports. This guide explains how to view, interpret, and use these reports.

docs.title: From Jobs
docs.text: After a job completes: 1. Navigate to Jobs → Build 2. Find the completed job 3. Click View Report or the report icon

docs.title: From Reports Page
docs.text: View all historical reports: 1. Navigate to Reports from the main menu 2. Browse or search for reports 3. Click to view details

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
High-level overview of the migration.

Executive Summary
═════════════════

Job:        hdp-to-cdp-migration
Strategy:   HYBRID
Mode:       Execute
Duration:   45m 23s

Result:     ✓ Completed with warnings

• 95 of 100 tables migrated successfully
• 3 tables failed
• 2 tables skipped

docs.text:
Issues that didn't fail but need attention.

════════

⚠ 5 ACID tables were downgraded to external
  Tables: t1, t2, t3, t4, t5
  Impact: Tables are no longer transactional

⚠ 2 views reference tables not in dataset
  Views: v_customer_summary, v_sales_report
  Impact: Views may fail if source tables not available

docs.title: Generated SQL
docs.text: View all generated SQL statements.

docs.title: SQL Sections
docs.text:
DDL Scripts 

-- RIGHT Cluster DDL

USE sales_db;

CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS customers (
  customer_id INT,
  name STRING,
  email STRING
)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 's3a://data-lake/warehouse/sales_db/customers';

DML Scripts (Data Movement) 

-- Data Movement

INSERT INTO sales_db.customers
SELECT * FROM left_sales_db.customers;

Cleanup Scripts 

-- Cleanup (if needed)

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_customers;

docs.title: Download Scripts
docs.text: Click Download SQL to get scripts as files: - left_cluster.sql - Scripts for source cluster - right_cluster.sql - Scripts for target cluster - cleanup.sql - Post-migration cleanup

docs.title: AI-Powered Analysis
docs.text: Get intelligent insights from the AI analysis feature.

docs.title: Accessing AI Analysis
docs.text: Click the ✨ (sparkles) icon to generate AI analysis.

docs.title: Analysis Content
docs.text:
AI Report Analysis
══════════════════

Executive Summary
─────────────────
The migration completed with a 95% success rate. Most tables
migrated successfully using the SQL strategy, with larger tables
automatically using EXPORT_IMPORT.

Key Findings
────────────
1. The 3 failed tables all have partition counts exceeding the
   configured limit of 500.

2. Average migration time was 27 seconds per table, with outliers
   being large partitioned tables.

3. ACID table downgrades were applied as configured, affecting
   5 tables.

Recommendations
───────────────
1. Increase partitionLimit to 2000 for the failed tables and
   re-run.

2. Consider using EXPORT_IMPORT for tables over 10GB to improve
   reliability.

3. Review downgraded ACID tables to ensure applications don't
   require transactional semantics.

Risk Assessment
───────────────
• Low: Most tables migrated successfully
• Medium: Failed tables need attention before cutover
• Action Required: Re-run failed tables with adjusted settings

docs.title: Web UI
docs.text: Interactive viewing with: - Expandable sections - Sortable tables - Search and filter - Click-through details

docs.title: Markdown Export
docs.text: Human-readable text format: - Good for documentation - Version control friendly - Email attachments

docs.title: YAML Export
docs.text: Machine-readable format: - Automation integration - Custom processing - Archival

docs.title: JSON Export
docs.text: API-friendly format: - Integration with other tools - Programmatic access - Dashboard feeds

docs.title: Viewing History
docs.text: All reports are saved and accessible: 1. Navigate to Reports 2. Filter by date, job, or status 3. Click to view any historical report

docs.title: Comparing Reports
docs.text: Compare runs of the same job: - View changes between runs - Track progress over time - Identify regressions

docs.title: Exporting Reports
docs.text:
1. Open a report
  2. Click Export
  3. Choose format (Markdown, YAML, JSON)
  4. Download the file

docs.title: Archiving Reports
docs.text: Reports are stored in RocksDB: - Automatically retained - No manual management needed - Backed up with RocksDB directory

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text: A table is successful when: - Schema created on target - Data moved (if applicable) - Partitions registered - No errors during execution

docs.title: Failure Reasons
docs.text:
Common failure categories:

Category Examples Permission Can't create table, can't write data Resource Timeout, out of memory Configuration Partition limit, size threshold Data Type mismatch, corrupt data Connectivity Network issues, service unavailable

docs.title: Skipped Tables
docs.text: Tables may be skipped due to: - Exclude patterns matching - Table type not supported - Configuration settings (e.g., skip ACID)

docs.title: Re-running Failed Tables
docs.text:
1. Note failed tables from report
  2. Adjust configuration or limits
  3. Create new dataset with only failed tables
  4. Run new job with adjusted settings

docs.title: Verifying Migration
docs.text: Use reports to verify: - [ ] All expected tables migrated - [ ] Row counts match (if available) - [ ] No critical errors - [ ] Warnings are acceptable

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Export reports for: - Migration documentation - Audit trails - Stakeholder communication - Post-migration review

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Troubleshooting - Resolve common errors
  • Managing Jobs - Re-run or modify jobs
  • LLM Integration - AI analysis features

docs.title: User Management
docs.text: HMS-Mirror provides user account management for administrators and self-service profile settings for all users.

docs.title: User Roles
docs.text: Role Capabilities ADMIN Full access: manage users, configure preferences, access all features USER Standard access: create and run migrations, manage own profile

docs.title: Profile Settings (All Users)
docs.text: All authenticated users can manage their own profile.

docs.title: Viewing Your Profile
docs.text:
Navigate to your Profile page (click your username or avatar in the top navigation).

Your profile displays:

  • Username
  • Email address
  • Assigned roles (ADMIN, USER)
  • Account status (active/inactive)
  • Account creation date
  • Last login timestamp

docs.title: Updating Your Email
docs.text:
1. Edit the email field
  2. Click Update Email

docs.title: Changing Your Password
docs.text:
1. Enter your current password
  2. Enter a new password (minimum 8 characters)
  3. Confirm the new password
  4. Click Change Password

docs.title: User Management (Admins Only)
docs.text: Administrators can manage all user accounts from Admin > User Management.

docs.title: Viewing Users
docs.text:
The management page shows a table of all users with:

  • Username
  • Email
  • Account status (active/inactive)
  • Roles
  • Last login time

Use the search/filter bar to find users by username or email.

docs.title: Resetting a User's Password
docs.text:
1. Click the key icon next to the user
  2. Confirm the action in the dialog
  3. A new random password is generated and displayed
  4. Copy the password and securely share it with the user

Warning

The generated password is shown only once. Make sure to copy it before closing the dialog.

docs.title: Granting or Revoking Admin Role
docs.text:
1. Click the shield icon next to the user
  2. Select grant or revoke
  3. Confirm in the dialog

Note

You cannot revoke the admin role from the last remaining admin account, and you cannot modify your own admin role.

docs.title: Enabling or Disabling Users
docs.text:
1. Click the toggle icon next to the user
  2. Confirm in the dialog

Disabled users cannot log in. You cannot disable your own account.

docs.title: User Creation
docs.text:
New user accounts are created through:

  • Registration at the login page (when local authentication is used)
  • Automatic creation on first login when LDAP or OIDC is configured with "Dynamically Create Users" enabled

Administrators cannot directly create user accounts from the management page — users must register or be auto-created via directory integration.

docs.title: Using the Web UI
docs.text: HMS-Mirror v4 introduces a modern web-based interface for managing migrations. This section covers everything you need to know about navigating and using the UI effectively.

docs.title: Accessing the UI
docs.text:
After starting HMS-Mirror, open your browser to:

http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror

Replace localhost:8090 with your server's hostname and port if different.

docs.title: Web UI Overview
