docs.text: Get familiar with the interface: - Dashboard navigation - Common UI patterns (cards, wizards, dialogs) - Status indicators and icons - Keyboard shortcuts - Accessibility features

docs.title: Managing Connections
docs.text: Create and manage cluster connections: - Creating new connections - Configuring HiveServer2 settings - Setting up authentication - Testing connections - Importing/exporting connection configs

docs.title: Managing Configurations
docs.text: Build migration configurations: - Using the configuration wizard - Migration settings (views, table properties) - Optimization settings - Transfer and warehouse settings - ACID table handling - Iceberg conversion options

docs.title: Managing Datasets
docs.text: Define what to migrate: - Creating datasets - Adding databases - Table filtering with regex patterns - Database renaming and prefixes - Custom warehouse locations

docs.title: Managing Jobs
docs.text: Build and execute migrations: - Creating jobs with the wizard - Selecting data strategies - Combining datasets, configs, and connections - Validating jobs - Running dry runs and executions - Monitoring job progress

docs.title: Viewing Reports
docs.text: Analyze migration results: - Understanding report structure - Table-level details - Viewing generated SQL - Identifying errors and warnings - Downloading reports - AI-powered analysis (if enabled)

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
A typical migration through the UI follows this pattern:

1. Connections     2. Configuration     3. Dataset     4. Job        5. Report
   ┌─────┐            ┌─────┐           ┌─────┐       ┌─────┐        ┌─────┐
   │ Add │──────────► │Build│─────────► │Define│────► │Build│──────► │View │
   │Clusters│         │Settings│        │Tables│      │& Run│        │Results│
   └─────┘            └─────┘           └─────┘       └─────┘        └─────┘
  1. Connections - Set up connections to your source and target clusters
  2. Configuration - Define how the migration should behave
  3. Dataset - Specify which databases and tables to include
  4. Job - Combine everything, validate, and execute
  5. Report - Review results and address any issues

docs.title: Quick Actions
docs.text: I want to... Where to go Connect to a new cluster Connections → New Connection Start a migration Jobs → Build → New Job Check migration results Reports → Select report Change LLM settings Settings → LLM Export my setup Each section has Export options

docs.title: Wizard-Driven Creation
docs.text: Complex tasks like creating configurations and jobs are guided by step-by-step wizards that help you: - Make informed choices - Avoid common mistakes - See summaries before committing

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Before running any job, the UI provides validation that checks: - Connection availability - Configuration compatibility - Dataset validity - Strategy requirements

docs.title: AI Analysis (Optional)
docs.text: If LLM integration is enabled, look for the ✨ sparkle icon to get: - Configuration recommendations - Risk assessment for jobs - Report insights and suggested actions

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• UI Overview - Learn the interface patterns
  • Quick Start Tutorial - Complete a hands-on migration
  • Managing Jobs - Execute your first job

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The SCHEMA_ONLY strategy transfers table and database metadata (DDL) from the source cluster to the target cluster without moving any data. The table locations are automatically translated from the source namespace to the target namespace.

This workflow is ideal when you plan to move data using external tools like Hadoop DistCp, cloud provider migration services, or when data is already accessible from both clusters.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• External data transfer: Data will be copied using DistCp, AWS DataSync, Azure Data Factory, or similar tools
  • Large datasets: When Hive-based data transfer is too slow or resource-intensive
  • Offline migrations: When source and target clusters cannot communicate directly
  • Pre-migration setup: Creating target schemas before data arrives
  • Format preservation: When you need exact file format/layout preservation (no rewriting)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Connections: Source (LEFT) and target (RIGHT) connections configured and tested
  2. Dataset: Tables to migrate defined in a Dataset with appropriate filters
  3. Network: Target cluster must have network access to the Hive metastore (for DDL execution)
  4. Permissions:
  5. READ access on source metastore
  6. CREATE DATABASE/TABLE permissions on target
  7. Storage Planning: Understand how source paths map to target paths

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Connection Definitions
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Connections in the sidebar
  2. Create a LEFT (source) connection:
  3. Configure HiveServer2 URI for the source cluster
  4. Set HCFS Namespace (e.g., hdfs://source-cluster:8020 or s3a://source-bucket)
  5. Select the appropriate Platform Type (HDP3, CDP7_2, etc.)
  6. Test the connection
  7. Create a RIGHT (target) connection:
  8. Configure HiveServer2 URI for the target cluster
  9. Set HCFS Namespace to target storage (e.g., hdfs://target-cluster:8020 or s3a://target-bucket)
  10. Test the connection

docs.title: Step 2: Define the Dataset
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Datasets and click Create New Dataset
  2. Configure the database selection:
  3. Add databases to include
  4. Set table filters (regex patterns for include/exclude)
  5. Configure partition limits if needed
  6. Save the Dataset

docs.title: Step 3: Create the Configuration
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Configurations and click Create New Configuration
  2. Set basic options:
  3. Create If Not Exists: Enable (recommended for idempotent migrations)
  4. Migrate Non-Native: Enable if you have HBase/Druid tables
  5. Configure ACID handling (if applicable):
  6. Migrate ACID: Enable if source has ACID tables
  7. Downgrade: Enable to convert ACID to EXTERNAL tables
  8. Set Transfer options:
  9. Warehouse Directories: Configure if target uses different paths
  10. Save the Configuration

docs.title: Step 4: Create and Validate the Job
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Jobs > Create New Job
  2. Job Details:
  3. Provide a descriptive name (e.g., prod-analytics-schema-only)
  4. Add description documenting the migration scope
  5. Data Strategy: Select SCHEMA_ONLY
  6. References:
  7. Select your Dataset
  8. Select your Configuration
  9. Select LEFT (source) connection
  10. Select RIGHT (target) connection
  11. Options: Configure as needed
  12. Review the summary and create the job

docs.title: Step 5: Validate the Job
docs.text:
1. From the Jobs list, click Validate on your job
  2. Review the validation report:
  3. Check for any schema incompatibilities
  4. Review location translations
  5. Verify table counts match expectations
  6. Address any validation errors before proceeding

docs.title: Step 6: Execute the Job (Dry Run)
docs.text:
1. Click Run on the job
  2. Select Dry Run mode
  3. Review the generated scripts:
  4. *_execute.sql - DDL statements for the target
  5. distcp_workbook.md - DistCp commands for data transfer
  6. Verify the location translations are correct

docs.title: Step 7: Execute the Job (Live)
docs.text:
1. Click Run on the job
  2. Select Execute mode
  3. Monitor the execution progress
  4. Review the execution report for any errors

docs.title: Step 8: Transfer Data Externally
docs.text:
After schema migration completes, transfer data using your preferred method:

Option A: Hadoop DistCp 

# Review generated distcp commands in the report
hadoop distcp \
  -Dmapreduce.job.hdfs-servers.token-renewal.exclude=target-cluster \
  hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table \
  hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table

Option B: Cloud Provider Tools 

# AWS DataSync, Azure ADF, GCP Transfer Service
# Configure source and target according to your cloud provider

docs.title: Step 9: Validate Migration
docs.text:
1. Connect to the target cluster
  2. Verify table schemas exist: 
    SHOW DATABASES;
USE your_database;
SHOW TABLES;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED your_table;
  3. After data transfer, verify data accessibility: 
    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM your_table;

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All databases created on target
  • All tables created with correct schemas
  • Table locations point to target namespace
  • Partition definitions preserved (for partitioned tables)
  • Table properties migrated correctly
  • Views migrated (if enabled)
  • Data accessible after external transfer

docs.title: Issue: "Table already exists"
docs.text: Solution: Enable createIfNotExists in configuration, or drop existing tables first

docs.title: Issue: Location translation incorrect
docs.text: Solution: Verify HCFS namespace settings in both connections. Check warehouse directory configuration.

docs.title: Issue: ACID tables failing
docs.text: Solution: Enable ACID migration and optionally ACID downgrade in configuration

docs.title: Issue: Views failing
docs.text: Solution: Ensure all base tables are migrated before views. Views are processed after tables.

docs.title: Issue: Permission denied on target
docs.text: Solution: Verify the HiveServer2 user has CREATE privileges on target databases

docs.title: Generated Artifacts
docs.text:
After execution, HMS-Mirror generates:

File Purpose *_execute.sql DDL statements executed on target *_clean.sql Cleanup scripts (if rollback needed) distcp_workbook.md DistCp commands for data transfer runStatus.yaml Execution status and statistics

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• SQL Workflow - When data needs transformation during migration
  • LINKED Workflow - When testing before full migration
  • Phased Migration - For large-scale incremental migrations

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The SQL strategy uses Hive SQL INSERT statements to migrate data from the source cluster to the target cluster. Data is read from source tables via HiveServer2 and written to newly created target tables using distributed SQL execution.

This approach leverages Hive's query engine for data transfer, enabling format conversion, schema transformation, and optimization during migration.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Format conversion needed: Converting between file formats (e.g., Text to Parquet, Avro to ORC)
  • Schema transformation: Adding/removing columns, changing data types
  • Data filtering: Migrating subset of data based on predicates
  • Compression changes: Applying different compression during migration
  • Linked clusters: Source and target clusters can communicate via network
  • Moderate data sizes: Tables that can be processed within Hive query timeouts

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Linked Clusters: Source and target must have network connectivity
  2. Connections: Both LEFT and RIGHT connections tested and working
  3. HCFS Access: Target cluster must be able to write to its storage namespace
  4. Compute Resources: Sufficient YARN/Tez resources for distributed queries
  5. Permissions:
  6. READ on source tables
  7. CREATE DATABASE/TABLE on target
  8. WRITE to target storage locations

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Connections
docs.text:
1. LEFT (Source) Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 URI with appropriate authentication
  3. HCFS Namespace of source storage
  4. Platform Type matching source cluster version

  5. Test connection to verify READ access

  6. RIGHT (Target) Connection:

  7. HiveServer2 URI with WRITE permissions
  8. HCFS Namespace for target storage
  9. Platform Type for target cluster
  10. Test connection to verify CREATE/WRITE access

docs.title: Step 2: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create a new Dataset
  2. Add databases to include
  3. Configure table filters:
  4. Use regex patterns for include/exclude
  5. Set partition limits to prevent resource exhaustion
  6. Consider table size limits for very large tables
  7. Save the Dataset

docs.title: Step 3: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Create a new Configuration
  2. Basic Settings:
  3. createIfNotExists: Enable for idempotent runs

  4. migrateNonNative: Enable if HBase tables exist

  5. ACID Migration (if source has ACID tables):

  6. migrateACID.on: Enable
  7. migrateACID.downgrade: Enable to convert to EXTERNAL

  8. migrateACID.partitionLimit: Set appropriate limit (default: 500)

  9. Optimization Settings:

  10. autoTune: Enable for automatic parameter tuning
  11. compressTextOutput: Enable if using text format

  12. Configure custom overrides if needed

  13. Statistics (optional):

  14. enableAutoTableStats: Enable for query optimizer

  15. enableAutoColumnStats: Enable for detailed statistics

  16. Transfer Settings:

  17. Configure warehouse directories if different from defaults
  18. Set intermediate storage if needed

docs.title: Step 4: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Jobs > Create New Job
  2. Job Details:
  3. Name: Descriptive name (e.g., analytics-sql-migration)

  4. Description: Document scope and purpose

  5. Data Strategy: Select SQL

  6. References:

  7. Dataset: Your defined dataset
  8. Configuration: Your configuration
  9. LEFT Connection: Source cluster

  10. RIGHT Connection: Target cluster

  11. Hybrid Settings (affects SQL partition limits):

  12. sqlPartitionLimit: Maximum partitions per table (default: 500)

  13. sqlSizeLimit: Maximum table size in bytes (default: 1GB)

  14. Review and create the job

docs.title: Step 5: Validate Job
docs.text:
1. Click Validate on the job
  2. Review validation results:
  3. Table compatibility check
  4. Location translation preview
  5. Partition count verification
  6. Size estimation (if available)
  7. Address any validation errors

docs.title: Step 6: Dry Run
docs.text:
1. Execute job in Dry Run mode
  2. Review generated SQL scripts: 
    -- Example generated SQL
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS target_db;
USE target_db;

CREATE TABLE target_table (
  col1 STRING,
  col2 INT
) STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/target_db.db/target_table';

INSERT INTO target_table
SELECT * FROM source_db.source_table;
  3. Verify:
  4. Table DDL is correct
  5. INSERT statements have proper column mappings
  6. Partition handling is appropriate

docs.title: Step 7: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute job in Execute mode
  2. Monitor progress:
  3. Per-table execution status
  4. Error messages if any

  5. Duration tracking

  6. Handle any failures:

  7. Review error logs
  8. Fix configuration issues
  9. Re-run failed tables only

docs.title: Step 8: Validate Results
docs.text:
1. Connect to target cluster

  2. Verify table creation: 

    SHOW DATABASES;
USE your_database;
SHOW TABLES;

  3. Verify data: 

    -- Row count comparison
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM target_table;

-- Sample data check
SELECT * FROM target_table LIMIT 10;

-- For partitioned tables
SHOW PARTITIONS target_table;

  4. Verify statistics (if enabled): 

    DESCRIBE FORMATTED target_table;
-- Check numRows, rawDataSize statistics

docs.title: For Large Tables
docs.text:
optimization:
  autoTune: true
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.exec.parallel:
        RIGHT: "true"
      hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode:
        RIGHT: "nonstrict"
      hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions:
        RIGHT: "10000"
      hive.tez.container.size:
        RIGHT: "8192"

docs.title: For ACID Tables
docs.text:
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true  # Convert to EXTERNAL
  partitionLimit: 500
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2  # Remove fake buckets

docs.title: For Format Conversion
docs.text: Set target table properties in the Configuration or use Iceberg conversion for modern formats.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All databases created on target
  • All tables created with correct schemas
  • Row counts match between source and target
  • Partition structures preserved
  • Data accessible via queries
  • Statistics collected (if enabled)
  • Performance acceptable for queries

docs.title: Issue: Query timeout on large tables
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase Hive query timeout settings - Use HYBRID strategy to switch large tables to EXPORT_IMPORT - Reduce table scope and migrate in batches

docs.title: Issue: Out of memory during INSERT
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase Tez container size in optimization overrides - Reduce concurrent operations - Use DISTRIBUTE BY for better partition handling

docs.title: Issue: Partition limit exceeded
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase sqlPartitionLimit in hybrid settings - Use EXPORT_IMPORT for partition-heavy tables - Split migration by partition ranges

docs.title: Issue: ACID table migration failing
docs.text: Solutions: - Ensure migrateACID.on is enabled - Enable downgrade to convert to EXTERNAL - Check source ACID table transaction status

docs.title: Issue: Slow performance
docs.text: Solutions: - Enable autoTune for automatic optimization - Set custom overrides for parallel execution - Consider time-of-day for reduced cluster load

docs.title: Generated Artifacts
docs.text: File Purpose *_execute.sql DDL and INSERT statements *_clean.sql Cleanup/rollback scripts runStatus.yaml Execution statistics

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• EXPORT_IMPORT Workflow - Alternative for large tables
  • HYBRID Workflow - Auto-select between SQL and EXPORT_IMPORT
  • ACID Migration - Detailed ACID handling

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The EXPORT_IMPORT strategy uses Hive's native EXPORT and IMPORT commands to migrate tables between clusters. Data is exported to a staging location on the source cluster (accessible by target), then imported to create the table on the target cluster.

This approach preserves exact file formats and layouts while providing atomic table migration with metadata integrity.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Exact format preservation: When you need bit-for-bit identical files
  • Atomic migrations: Need complete table transfer or rollback
  • Moderate partition counts: Tables with fewer than ~100 partitions
  • Linked clusters: Shared or accessible storage between clusters
  • Metadata preservation: EXPORT/IMPORT preserves all table properties

docs.title: When NOT to Use
docs.text:
• Tables with >100 partitions (use SQL or HYBRID instead)
  • When format conversion is required
  • When clusters cannot share storage access
  • Very large tables (EXPORT creates additional copies)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Shared Storage: Target cluster must be able to read from export location
  2. Export Directory: Accessible path for staging exported data
  3. Connections: Both clusters configured and tested
  4. Permissions:
  5. EXPORT permission on source tables
  6. WRITE to export staging directory
  7. IMPORT permission on target
  8. CREATE DATABASE/TABLE on target

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Export Location
docs.text:
The export location must be accessible from BOTH clusters. Options:

Option A: Source HDFS (if target can read) 

/apps/hive/warehouse/export_staging/

Option B: Shared Cloud Storage 

s3a://shared-bucket/hms-mirror-export/
abfs://container@account/hms-mirror-export/

Option C: Intermediate Storage 

# Configure in transferConfig.intermediateStorage
s3a://migration-staging/export/

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Connections
docs.text:
1. LEFT (Source) Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 with EXPORT permissions

  3. HCFS Namespace of source storage

  4. RIGHT (Target) Connection:

  5. HiveServer2 with IMPORT/CREATE permissions
  6. HCFS Namespace for target storage
  7. Must be able to read from export location

docs.title: Step 3: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create new Dataset
  2. Add databases and tables
  3. Important Filters:
  4. Set maxPartitions filter to avoid large partition tables
  5. Consider using HYBRID strategy for mixed workloads
  6. Save the Dataset

docs.title: Step 4: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Create new Configuration

  2. Transfer Settings: 

    transferConfig:
  exportBaseDirPrefix: "/apps/hive/warehouse/export_"
  # Or for cloud:
  # exportBaseDirPrefix: "s3a://bucket/hms-mirror-export/"

  3. Basic Settings:

  4. createIfNotExists: Enable

  5. saveWorkingTables: Disable (to clean up exports)

  6. ACID Handling (if applicable):

  7. migrateACID.on: Enable

  8. Note: ACID tables require special handling with EXPORT_IMPORT

  9. Warehouse Configuration:

  10. Set target warehouse directories if different from defaults

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Jobs > Create New Job

  2. Job Details:

  3. Name: prod-export-import-migration

  4. Description: Document scope

  5. Data Strategy: Select EXPORT_IMPORT

  6. References:

  7. Select Dataset, Configuration, Connections

  8. Hybrid Settings:

  9. exportImportPartitionLimit: Maximum partitions (default: 100)

docs.title: Step 6: Validate Job
docs.text:
1. Run validation
  2. Check for:
  3. Export directory accessibility
  4. Table partition counts within limits
  5. Storage capacity for export staging
  6. Review partition counts - tables exceeding limit will be skipped

docs.title: Step 7: Dry Run
docs.text:
Review generated commands:

-- Source cluster (LEFT)
EXPORT TABLE source_db.source_table
TO '/apps/hive/warehouse/export_source_db/source_table';

-- Target cluster (RIGHT)
IMPORT TABLE target_db.target_table
FROM '/apps/hive/warehouse/export_source_db/source_table'
LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/target_db.db/target_table';

docs.title: Step 8: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute in Execute mode
  2. Monitor stages:
  3. EXPORT phase: Data copied to staging directory
  4. IMPORT phase: Table created and data registered on target
  5. Handle failures and retry as needed

docs.title: Step 9: Validate Results
docs.text:
1. Verify tables on target: 

    SHOW TABLES IN target_db;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED target_table;

  2. Verify data integrity: 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM target_table;

  3. Compare file counts and sizes if needed

docs.title: Step 10: Cleanup
docs.text:
After successful validation:

  1. Remove export staging data: 

    hdfs dfs -rm -r /apps/hive/warehouse/export_*/

  2. Or use the cleanup scripts generated by HMS-Mirror

docs.title: Configuration Example
docs.text:
# Configuration for EXPORT_IMPORT
transferConfig:
  exportBaseDirPrefix: "s3a://migration-bucket/export/"
  remoteWorkingDirectory: "hms_mirror_working"

migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true

createIfNotExists: true
saveWorkingTables: false

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Export staging location accessible from both clusters
  • All tables exported successfully
  • All tables imported on target
  • Row counts match source
  • Table properties preserved
  • Partition structures intact
  • Export staging cleaned up

docs.title: Issue: "Export path not accessible from target"
docs.text: Solution: - Use shared cloud storage for export location - Configure intermediate storage - Ensure cross-cluster filesystem access is configured

docs.title: Issue: "Table too large to export"
docs.text: Solution: - EXPORT creates a copy, doubling storage temporarily - Use SQL strategy for very large tables - Consider HYBRID to auto-select

docs.title: Issue: "Too many partitions"
docs.text: Solution: - Reduce exportImportPartitionLimit to fail faster - Use SQL strategy for partition-heavy tables - Use HYBRID for automatic selection

docs.title: Issue: "ACID table export failed"
docs.text: Solution: - ACID tables require active transactions to complete - Run ACID compaction before export - Consider SQL strategy with ACID downgrade

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied on export path"
docs.text: Solution: - Ensure Hive user can write to export directory - Check HDFS/S3 permissions - Verify service accounts have cross-cluster access

docs.title: Storage Considerations
docs.text:
EXPORT_IMPORT temporarily requires extra storage:

Source Data:     100 GB
Export Staging:  100 GB  (temporary copy)
Target Data:     100 GB
------------------------
Peak Usage:      300 GB
Final Usage:     200 GB  (after cleanup)

Plan for 3x storage during migration, 2x after cleanup.

docs.title: Generated Artifacts
docs.text: File Purpose *_execute.sql EXPORT and IMPORT statements *_clean.sql Cleanup for export staging runStatus.yaml Execution statistics

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• SQL Workflow - When format conversion needed
  • HYBRID Workflow - Auto-select best strategy
  • Large Table Workflow - Handling big tables

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The HYBRID strategy intelligently combines EXPORT_IMPORT and SQL approaches based on each table's characteristics. HMS-Mirror analyzes partition counts and table sizes, then automatically selects the most efficient migration method for each table.

This provides the best of both strategies: fast EXPORT_IMPORT for smaller tables and scalable SQL for larger ones.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Mixed table sizes: Databases with both small and large tables
  • Automated optimization: Don't want to manually categorize tables
  • Performance optimization: Best throughput without manual tuning
  • Partition variety: Some tables have few partitions, others have many

docs.title: Decision Logic
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror applies these rules for each table:

IF partition_count <= exportImportPartitionLimit (default: 100)
   AND table_size <= sqlSizeLimit (default: 1GB)
THEN use EXPORT_IMPORT
ELSE IF partition_count <= sqlPartitionLimit (default: 500)
THEN use SQL
ELSE SKIP (table exceeds all limits)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Linked clusters: Network connectivity between source and target
  2. Shared storage access: For EXPORT_IMPORT portion
  3. Compute resources: For SQL portion
  4. Export staging: Accessible directory for EXPORT operations

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Connections
docs.text: Same as SQL/EXPORT_IMPORT workflows - ensure both clusters are accessible.

docs.title: Step 2: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create Dataset with target databases/tables
  2. Consider partition filters to pre-filter very large tables
  3. Tables exceeding all limits will be flagged for manual handling

docs.title: Step 3: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Basic Settings:
  2. createIfNotExists: Enable

  3. Standard ACID and optimization settings

  4. Transfer Settings:

  5. exportBaseDirPrefix: Location for EXPORT operations
  6. warehouse: Target warehouse directories

docs.title: Step 4: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select HYBRID

  2. Hybrid Settings (critical for this strategy):

Setting Default Description exportImportPartitionLimit 100 Max partitions for EXPORT_IMPORT sqlPartitionLimit 500 Max partitions for SQL sqlSizeLimit 1GB Max size for SQL strategy
  1. Adjust thresholds based on your environment:
  2. Larger clusters: Increase limits
  3. Resource-constrained: Decrease limits
  4. Partition-heavy workload: Increase exportImportPartitionLimit

docs.title: Step 5: Validate and Review Strategy Selection
docs.text:
1. Run validation

  2. Review the Strategy Assignment Report: 

    Database: analytics
├── fact_sales (500 partitions, 50GB) → SQL
├── dim_product (0 partitions, 100MB) → EXPORT_IMPORT
├── dim_customer (0 partitions, 500MB) → EXPORT_IMPORT
├── fact_inventory (1000 partitions, 100GB) → SKIPPED
└── summary_daily (365 partitions, 5GB) → SQL

  3. Adjust limits if too many tables are skipped

docs.title: Step 6: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute in Execute mode
  2. HMS-Mirror processes tables in optimized order:
  3. EXPORT_IMPORT tables grouped for efficiency
  4. SQL tables processed with appropriate parallelism
  5. Monitor per-table progress and strategy used

docs.title: Step 7: Validate Results
docs.text:
1. Verify all tables migrated
  2. Check tables that used each strategy
  3. Validate data integrity for both approaches

docs.title: For Partition-Heavy Environments
docs.text:
# Increase EXPORT_IMPORT threshold for environments with many partitions
hybrid:
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 200  # Up from 100
  sqlPartitionLimit: 1000          # Up from 500

docs.title: For Large Table Environments
docs.text:
# Increase size limits for clusters with ample resources
hybrid:
  sqlSizeLimit: 5368709120  # 5GB instead of 1GB

docs.title: For Resource-Constrained Environments
docs.text:
# Lower limits to prevent resource exhaustion
hybrid:
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 50
  sqlPartitionLimit: 200
  sqlSizeLimit: 536870912  # 512MB

docs.title: Strategy Selection Examples
docs.text: Table Profile Partitions Size Strategy Selected Small dimension 0 10 MB EXPORT_IMPORT Medium dimension 0 500 MB EXPORT_IMPORT Large dimension 0 2 GB SQL Partitioned fact (daily) 365 100 GB SQL Partitioned fact (hourly) 8760 500 GB SKIPPED Small partitioned 50 200 MB EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Handling Skipped Tables
docs.text:
Tables exceeding all limits need manual handling:

  1. Split by partition range: Migrate partition ranges separately
  2. Use distcp: Use SCHEMA_ONLY + external data copy
  3. Increase limits: If cluster can handle larger workloads
  4. Dedicated migration: Create separate job with higher limits
-- Example: Migrate partition ranges
-- Job 1: Historical partitions
-- Dataset filter: partition_date < '2023-01-01'

-- Job 2: Recent partitions
-- Dataset filter: partition_date >= '2023-01-01'

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Strategy assignment reviewed
  • EXPORT_IMPORT tables migrated
  • SQL tables migrated
  • Skipped tables identified and planned
  • Data integrity verified for all tables
  • Export staging cleaned up

docs.title: Issue: Too many tables using SQL (slow)
docs.text: Solution: Increase exportImportPartitionLimit to shift more tables to faster EXPORT_IMPORT

docs.title: Issue: Too many tables skipped
docs.text: Solution: - Increase sqlPartitionLimit - Handle skipped tables with dedicated jobs or SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp

docs.title: Issue: EXPORT_IMPORT tables failing
docs.text: Solution: Check export directory accessibility and permissions

docs.title: Issue: SQL tables timing out
docs.text: Solution: - Decrease sqlSizeLimit to shift large tables to EXPORT_IMPORT - Increase query timeouts in optimization overrides

docs.title: Issue: Inconsistent results between strategies
docs.text: Solution: Both strategies should produce identical data - verify source consistency

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start with defaults: Default thresholds work for most environments
  2. Review validation: Check strategy assignments before execution
  3. Monitor resources: Watch YARN queue utilization during execution
  4. Handle skipped tables: Plan for tables exceeding limits
  5. Test in dev first: Validate threshold settings in non-production

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• SQL Workflow - Understanding SQL strategy
  • EXPORT_IMPORT Workflow - Understanding EXPORT_IMPORT
  • Large Table Workflow - Handling tables that exceed limits
  • Partition-Heavy Workflow - Strategies for partition-heavy tables

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The LINKED strategy creates tables on the target cluster that point directly to data locations on the source cluster. No data is copied - the target cluster simply references the source storage through cross-cluster HDFS access or shared cloud storage.

This is ideal for testing and validation before committing to a full migration.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Pre-migration testing: Validate queries work on target cluster before moving data
  • Shared storage: Both clusters can access the same storage (HDFS federation, S3, ADLS)
  • Read-only access: Target cluster needs read access to source data
  • Proof of concept: Quick setup to demonstrate migration feasibility
  • Phased migrations: Step 1 of a multi-phase migration

docs.title: When NOT to Use
docs.text:
• When clusters cannot access each other's storage
  • When target needs write access to tables
  • For production workloads (source cluster dependency)
  • When source data will be deleted before migration completes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Cross-cluster access: Target cluster can read source storage
  2. Network connectivity: HDFS RPC or cloud storage access configured
  3. Authentication: Kerberos cross-realm trust or service account access
  4. Permissions: Target Hive user can read source HDFS locations

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Cross-Cluster Access
docs.text:
Before starting, verify target cluster can access source storage:

# From target cluster, test access to source HDFS
hdfs dfs -ls hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/

# For cloud storage
aws s3 ls s3://source-bucket/warehouse/
az storage blob list --container-name source-container

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Connections
docs.text:
1. LEFT (Source) Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 URI

  3. HCFS Namespace: hdfs://source-cluster:8020 or s3a://source-bucket

  4. RIGHT (Target) Connection:

  5. HiveServer2 URI
  6. HCFS Namespace: Keep as source namespace for LINKED tables
  7. Important: The target tables will point to source locations

docs.title: Step 3: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create Dataset with databases/tables to link
  2. All tables will be created as LINKED - no filtering needed for this strategy

docs.title: Step 4: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Basic Settings:

  2. createIfNotExists: Enable

  3. LINKED-specific considerations:

  4. Tables created will have EXTERNAL type
  5. Locations will be source cluster paths
  6. No data movement occurs

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select LINKED
  2. Configure references as usual
  3. No hybrid settings needed (no data transfer)

docs.title: Step 6: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute job
  2. HMS-Mirror creates tables on target pointing to source locations:
-- Generated DDL
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE target_db.linked_table (
  col1 STRING,
  col2 INT
)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/source_db.db/source_table';

docs.title: Step 7: Validate Linked Tables
docs.text:
1. Verify table creation: 

    USE target_db;
SHOW TABLES;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED linked_table;
-- Check LOCATION points to source cluster

  2. Verify data access: 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM linked_table;
SELECT * FROM linked_table LIMIT 10;

  3. Test your query workloads on target cluster

docs.title: Step 8: Plan Next Steps
docs.text:
After validation, decide on next steps:

Option A: Convert to SCHEMA_ONLY - Use CONVERT_LINKED strategy to update locations - Move data via distcp - Tables become independent

Option B: Migrate with SQL/HYBRID - Create new job with SQL or HYBRID strategy - Data copied to target storage - Drop linked tables or let HMS-Mirror replace them

Option C: Keep as LINKED - For read-only analytics workloads - Source cluster continues to own data - Suitable for data sharing scenarios

docs.title: Converting LINKED Tables
docs.text:
After data is copied externally, use CONVERT_LINKED to update table locations:

Source Cluster (LEFT):
  /warehouse/db.db/table  [DATA]

Target Cluster (RIGHT - current LINKED state):
  CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE table
  LOCATION 'hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table'

After distcp to target:
  hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table  [DATA COPY]

After CONVERT_LINKED:
  CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE table
  LOCATION 'hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table'

See CONVERT_LINKED Workflow for details.

docs.title: Use Case: Testing Migration Before Committing
docs.text:
Phase 1: LINKED
├── Create linked tables on target
├── Run validation queries
├── Verify application compatibility
└── Identify any issues

Phase 2: Data Copy
├── Run distcp to copy data to target storage
├── Verify data integrity
└── Compare source and target data

Phase 3: CONVERT_LINKED
├── Update table locations to target storage
├── Verify table accessibility
└── Tables now independent from source

Phase 4: Cutover
├── Switch applications to target cluster
├── Monitor for issues
└── Decommission source tables when ready

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Cross-cluster storage access verified
  • All tables created on target
  • Table locations point to source storage
  • Data accessible via target cluster queries
  • Query performance acceptable
  • Application compatibility verified

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied reading source location"
docs.text: Solutions: - Configure Kerberos cross-realm trust - Add target cluster's service principal to source HDFS ACLs - For cloud storage, configure IAM roles/policies

docs.title: Issue: "Cannot resolve source cluster nameservice"
docs.text: Solutions: - Add source cluster's HDFS configuration to target - Configure dfs.nameservices and dfs.ha.namenodes for source - Ensure network connectivity to source namenodes

docs.title: Issue: "Slow query performance on linked tables"
docs.text: Solutions: - Cross-cluster HDFS has higher latency than local - This is expected for LINKED - plan for full migration - Consider caching or materialized views for frequently accessed data

docs.title: Issue: "Source table changed after linking"
docs.text: Solutions: - LINKED tables see current source data (no snapshot) - Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE to refresh partitions - For consistency, coordinate with source cluster changes

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Fast setup: No data copying, just DDL execution
  • Zero storage: No additional storage on target
  • Live data: See current source data
  • Validation: Test target cluster with real data

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Read dependency: Target depends on source availability
  • Network latency: Cross-cluster queries are slower
  • No isolation: Changes to source affect target queries
  • Not for production: Source cluster is single point of failure

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• CONVERT_LINKED Workflow - Convert to independent tables
  • SCHEMA_ONLY Workflow - Alternative for offline migrations
  • Phased Migration - Multi-phase approach
  • Testing with LINKED - Detailed testing workflow

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The STORAGE_MIGRATION strategy relocates table data within a single cluster from one storage location to another. This is used for cloud migrations (HDFS to S3/ADLS), namespace changes, warehouse reorganization, or moving to different storage tiers.

Unlike other strategies that migrate between clusters, STORAGE_MIGRATION operates within the same metastore, changing only the physical data location.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Cloud adoption: Moving from HDFS to S3, ADLS, or GCS
  • Storage tiering: Moving data to different storage classes
  • Namespace changes: Reorganizing warehouse directory structure
  • Cluster consolidation: Moving data when changing HDFS namespace
  • Warehouse standardization: Aligning table locations with conventions

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Single cluster operation: Source and target are the same metastore
  2. Target storage: New storage location configured and accessible
  3. Permissions: Hive user can read source and write to target
  4. Storage capacity: Sufficient space in target location
  5. Connection: Only LEFT connection needed (same cluster)

docs.title: Translation Types
docs.text: Type Description Use Case RELATIVE Maintains relative path from source Moving warehouse root ALIGNED Aligns to target warehouse conventions Reorganizing structure

docs.title: Data Movement Strategies
docs.text: Strategy Description Performance SQL Uses INSERT to copy/transform data Slower, allows transformation DISTCP Direct file copy via Hadoop DistCp Faster, no transformation

docs.title: Step 1: Plan Storage Layout
docs.text:
Define your target storage structure:

Current (HDFS):
hdfs://cluster/warehouse/
├── external/
│   └── db.db/
│       └── table/
└── managed/
    └── db.db/
        └── table/

Target (S3):
s3a://datalake-bucket/warehouse/
├── external/hive/
│   └── db.db/
│       └── table/
└── managed/hive/
    └── db.db/
        └── table/

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Connection
docs.text:
Only LEFT connection is needed (no RIGHT for storage migration):

  1. LEFT Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 URI of the cluster
  3. HCFS Namespace: Current namespace (e.g., hdfs://cluster)
  4. Platform Type: Your cluster version

docs.title: Step 3: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create Dataset with databases/tables to migrate
  2. Consider filtering by database or table patterns
  3. Note: Large tables may need special handling

docs.title: Step 4: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Transfer Settings: 

    transferConfig:
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED  # or RELATIVE
    dataMovementStrategy: SQL  # or DISTCP
    dataFlow: PULL  # for DISTCP
    strict: true
    createArchive: false  # true to keep source copy
    skipDatabaseLocationAdjustments: false

  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "s3a://bucket/warehouse/external/hive"
    managedDirectory: "s3a://bucket/warehouse/managed/hive"

  2. ACID Handling (if applicable):

  3. ACID tables can be migrated but may need downgrade
  4. Consider converting to EXTERNAL if moving to cloud

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select STORAGE_MIGRATION

  2. Target Namespace:

  3. Set to new storage prefix (e.g., s3a://bucket)

  4. This overrides the connection's namespace

  5. References:

  6. Select LEFT connection only
  7. RIGHT connection not used

docs.title: Step 6: Validate Job
docs.text:
1. Run validation
  2. Review translation preview: 
    Current: hdfs://cluster/warehouse/db.db/table
Target:  s3a://bucket/warehouse/external/hive/db.db/table
  3. Verify warehouse directory calculations

docs.title: Step 7: Execute (SQL Strategy)
docs.text:
For SQL-based migration:

  1. HMS-Mirror creates transfer tables at target location
  2. Data is copied via INSERT statements
  3. Original tables are renamed (archived)
  4. Transfer tables are renamed to original names
-- Generated SQL (simplified)
CREATE TABLE db.table_hms_mirror_transfer
LOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/external/hive/db.db/table'
AS SELECT * FROM db.table;

ALTER TABLE db.table RENAME TO db.table_storage_migration_archive;
ALTER TABLE db.table_hms_mirror_transfer RENAME TO db.table;

docs.title: Step 7 (Alternative): Execute (DISTCP Strategy)
docs.text:
For DISTCP-based migration:

  1. HMS-Mirror generates distcp commands
  2. Execute distcp commands to copy files
  3. HMS-Mirror updates table locations
# Generated distcp command
hadoop distcp \
  -Dmapreduce.job.reduces=50 \
  -update -strategy dynamic \
  hdfs://cluster/warehouse/db.db/table \
  s3a://bucket/warehouse/external/hive/db.db/table

docs.title: Step 8: Update Database Locations
docs.text:
Unless skipDatabaseLocationAdjustments is true:

ALTER DATABASE db SET LOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/external/hive/db.db';
ALTER DATABASE db SET MANAGEDLOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/managed/hive/db.db';

docs.title: Step 9: Validate Migration
docs.text:
1. Verify table locations: 

    DESCRIBE FORMATTED db.table;
-- Check Location points to new storage

  2. Verify data accessibility: 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM db.table;

  3. Verify storage: 

    # Old location should have archive or be empty
hdfs dfs -ls hdfs://cluster/warehouse/db.db/

# New location should have data
aws s3 ls s3://bucket/warehouse/external/hive/db.db/table/

docs.title: Step 10: Cleanup Archives
docs.text:
After validation, remove archived tables:

-- Review archives first
SHOW TABLES LIKE '*_storage_migration_archive';

-- Drop when ready
DROP TABLE db.table_storage_migration_archive;

Or remove source data directly: 

hdfs dfs -rm -r hdfs://cluster/warehouse/db.db/table_old

docs.title: For Cloud Migration (HDFS to S3)
docs.text:
transferConfig:
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED
    dataMovementStrategy: DISTCP
    dataFlow: PULL
    strict: true

  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "s3a://datalake/warehouse/external"
    managedDirectory: "s3a://datalake/warehouse/managed"

docs.title: For Warehouse Reorganization
docs.text:
transferConfig:
  storageMigration:
    translationType: ALIGNED
    dataMovementStrategy: SQL
    createArchive: true  # Keep original as backup

  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "/new/warehouse/external"
    managedDirectory: "/new/warehouse/managed"

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Target storage locations created
  • Tables updated to new locations
  • Data accessible from new locations
  • Database LOCATION/MANAGEDLOCATION updated
  • Partitioned tables have all partitions updated
  • Archive tables created (if createArchive=true)
  • Source data ready for cleanup

docs.title: Issue: "Target location already exists"
docs.text: Solutions: - Enable createIfNotExists - Clear target location before migration - Use different directory naming

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied on target storage"
docs.text: Solutions: - Configure cloud storage credentials for Hive - Set up S3/ADLS IAM roles/service principals - Verify Hadoop credential provider configuration

docs.title: Issue: "ACID table migration failing"
docs.text: Solutions: - ACID tables require special handling - Consider downgrading to EXTERNAL first - Run compaction before migration

docs.title: Issue: "Large table causing timeouts"
docs.text: Solutions: - Use DISTCP instead of SQL - Migrate large tables separately - Increase query timeouts

docs.title: Issue: "Partitions not updating"
docs.text: Solutions: - Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE after migration - Check partition-level location handling - Verify all partition locations translated

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Test with small dataset first: Validate configuration before full migration
  2. Use DISTCP for large tables: Much faster than SQL for big data
  3. Keep archives initially: Easier rollback if issues found
  4. Monitor storage metrics: Track space usage during migration
  5. Schedule during low activity: Migration can impact cluster performance
  6. Validate incrementally: Check tables as they complete

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• On-Premises to Cloud - Complete cloud migration
  • Large Table Handling - Strategies for big tables
  • ACID Migration - ACID table considerations

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The COMMON strategy is used when both source and target clusters share the same underlying storage. Schema definitions are transferred between clusters while table locations remain unchanged, pointing to the shared storage.

This is common in cloud environments where multiple clusters (dev, test, prod, or multi-tenant) access the same data lake storage.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Shared data lake: Multiple clusters access same S3/ADLS/GCS storage
  • Multi-cluster environments: Dev/Test/Prod clusters sharing datasets
  • Cluster upgrades: New cluster version accessing existing data
  • DR scenarios: Standby cluster shares storage with primary
  • Data sharing: Read-only access to shared datasets

docs.title: When NOT to Use
docs.text:
• When clusters have separate storage namespaces
  • When data isolation is required between clusters
  • When you need to copy/transform data during migration

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Cluster A (Source)                    Shared Storage                Cluster B (Target)
+------------------+                +------------------+           +------------------+
| Hive Metastore A |                |                  |           | Hive Metastore B |
| - db.table       |--------------->| s3a://datalake/  |<----------| - db.table       |
|   LOCATION: s3a  |                | warehouse/       |           |   LOCATION: s3a  |
+------------------+                | db.db/table/     |           +------------------+
                                    +------------------+

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Shared storage: Both clusters can access the same storage location
  2. Storage permissions: Both clusters have appropriate read (and optionally write) permissions
  3. Compatible Hive versions: Schema compatibility between cluster versions
  4. Network access: Both clusters can reach the shared storage

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Shared Storage Access
docs.text:
Confirm both clusters can access the storage:

# From source cluster
hdfs dfs -ls s3a://datalake/warehouse/

# From target cluster
hdfs dfs -ls s3a://datalake/warehouse/

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Connections
docs.text:
1. LEFT (Source) Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 URI of source cluster

  3. HCFS Namespace: Shared storage (e.g., s3a://datalake)

  4. RIGHT (Target) Connection:

  5. HiveServer2 URI of target cluster
  6. HCFS Namespace: Same shared storage (e.g., s3a://datalake)

Important: Both connections should have the same HCFS Namespace since storage is shared.

docs.title: Step 3: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create Dataset with databases/tables to share
  2. Tables will be registered on target without data movement

docs.title: Step 4: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Basic Settings:

  2. createIfNotExists: Enable for idempotent operations

  3. Ownership Transfer (optional): 

    transferOwnership:
  database: false  # Usually keep original ownership
  table: false

  4. Views (if needed):

  5. Enable view migration if views should be available on target

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select COMMON
  2. References: Select Dataset, Configuration, both Connections

docs.title: Step 6: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute job
  2. HMS-Mirror creates tables on target cluster:
-- Generated DDL (location unchanged)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE target_db.shared_table (
  col1 STRING,
  col2 INT
)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 's3a://datalake/warehouse/db.db/shared_table';

docs.title: Step 7: Validate
docs.text:
1. Verify tables on target: 

    USE target_db;
SHOW TABLES;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED shared_table;
-- Verify LOCATION is same as source

  2. Verify data access: 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM shared_table;

  3. Verify both clusters see same data: 

    -- Run on both clusters, should match
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM db.table;

docs.title: Use Case 1: Cluster Upgrade (Side-by-Side)
docs.text:
Upgrade from HDP to CDP while sharing storage:

HDP 3.1 Cluster          S3 Data Lake           CDP 7.2 Cluster
+---------------+       +---------------+       +---------------+
| Metastore     |       |               |       | Metastore     |
| db.fact_sales |------>| s3://bucket/  |<------| db.fact_sales |
|               |       | warehouse/    |       |               |
+---------------+       +---------------+       +---------------+

1. Set up CDP cluster
2. Run COMMON migration to register tables
3. Test applications on CDP
4. Cut over when ready
5. Decommission HDP cluster

docs.title: Use Case 3: Disaster Recovery
docs.text:
Standby cluster with shared storage:

Primary Cluster                     Standby Cluster
+---------------+                   +---------------+
| Active        |                   | Passive       |
| Metastore     |                   | Metastore     |
| (read/write)  |                   | (read only)   |
+---------------+                   +---------------+
       |                                   |
       v                                   v
+------------------------------------------+
| s3://datalake/warehouse/                 |
| (shared storage with replication)        |
+------------------------------------------+

Normal: Primary handles all workloads
Failover: Switch to standby, promote to primary

docs.title: Data Ownership
docs.text:
With COMMON storage, only one cluster should "own" the data:

  • Owner cluster: Can CREATE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
  • Consumer clusters: Should be read-only
-- On consumer cluster, consider read-only views
CREATE VIEW readonly_sales AS
SELECT * FROM shared_db.sales;
-- Application uses view instead of base table

docs.title: Partition Management
docs.text:
Partitions created on one cluster need to be visible on others:

-- After new partitions added on source
-- Run on target cluster to discover them
MSCK REPAIR TABLE db.partitioned_table;

docs.title: Schema Changes
docs.text:
Schema changes on source must be propagated:

  1. Option A: Re-run COMMON migration
  2. Option B: Manually sync schema changes
  3. Option C: Use schema registry for coordination

docs.title: Concurrency
docs.text:
With shared storage, concurrent writes can cause issues:

  • Use table-level locking if both clusters write
  • Prefer single-writer, multiple-reader model
  • Consider ACID tables for transactional consistency

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Storage accessible from both clusters
  • Tables registered on target cluster
  • Locations match source tables
  • Data query results match between clusters
  • Partition visibility correct
  • Permission model defined (read-only vs read-write)

docs.title: Issue: "Table data not visible on target"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify storage credentials on target cluster - Check storage bucket policies/ACLs - Run MSCK REPAIR for partitioned tables

docs.title: Issue: "Schema mismatch between clusters"
docs.text: Solutions: - Hive versions may have different defaults - Re-run migration with updated configuration - Manually adjust schema on target

docs.title: Issue: "Partition not visible on target"
docs.text: Solutions: - Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE tablename - Partitions created after migration need sync

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied on target writes"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify IAM roles/policies allow target cluster writes - Check if table is meant to be read-only on target - Configure appropriate storage permissions

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• Disaster Recovery Setup - DR with shared storage
  • Cross-Environment - Dev/Test/Prod sharing
  • LINKED Workflow - Alternative for testing without shared storage

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The DUMP strategy extracts schema definitions (DDL) from a cluster and generates SQL scripts without requiring a target cluster connection. This is useful for documentation, offline planning, backup, and scenarios where you need to capture metadata for later use.

No data is moved and no changes are made to any cluster.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Documentation: Capture current schema state for documentation
  • Offline planning: Generate scripts for review before migration
  • Backup: Create schema backups for disaster recovery
  • Audit: Record schema definitions for compliance
  • Script generation: Create DDL scripts for manual execution
  • No target available: When target cluster isn't ready yet

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Source connection only: Only LEFT connection needed
  2. Read permissions: Access to source metastore
  3. No target required: RIGHT connection not used

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Source Connection
docs.text:
1. Create LEFT Connection:
  2. HiveServer2 URI of source cluster
  3. HCFS Namespace of source storage
  4. Platform Type for correct DDL syntax

docs.title: Step 2: Define Dataset
docs.text:
1. Create Dataset with databases/tables to dump
  2. Configure filters as needed
  3. All matching tables will be included in dump

docs.title: Step 3: Create Configuration
docs.text:
1. Basic Settings:

  2. createIfNotExists: Enable (adds IF NOT EXISTS to DDL)

  3. Optional Settings:

  4. View migration: Enable to include views
  5. ACID settings: Configure for ACID table handling

docs.title: Step 4: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select DUMP
  2. References:
  3. Dataset: Your dataset
  4. Configuration: Your configuration
  5. LEFT Connection: Source cluster
  6. RIGHT Connection: Not required (will be ignored)

docs.title: Step 5: Execute
docs.text:
1. Execute the job
  2. HMS-Mirror generates scripts without connecting to target

docs.title: Step 6: Review Output
docs.text:
Output files generated:

reports/
└── job-id/
    ├── db1_execute.sql        # CREATE statements
    ├── db1_clean.sql          # DROP statements
    ├── db2_execute.sql
    ├── db2_clean.sql
    ├── runStatus.yaml         # Execution summary
    └── distcp_workbook.md     # Data location info

Example execute.sql content: 

-- Database: analytics
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS analytics
COMMENT 'Analytics data warehouse'
LOCATION 'hdfs://cluster/warehouse/analytics.db';

-- Table: analytics.fact_sales
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS analytics.fact_sales (
    sale_id BIGINT COMMENT 'Unique sale identifier',
    product_id INT,
    quantity INT,
    amount DECIMAL(10,2),
    sale_date DATE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 'hdfs://cluster/warehouse/analytics.db/fact_sales'
TBLPROPERTIES (
    'transient_lastDdlTime'='1234567890'
);

-- Add partitions
ALTER TABLE analytics.fact_sales ADD IF NOT EXISTS
    PARTITION (year=2024, month=1)
    LOCATION 'hdfs://cluster/warehouse/analytics.db/fact_sales/year=2024/month=1';

docs.title: Use Case 1: Pre-Migration Planning
docs.text:
Generate scripts to review before actual migration:

1. Run DUMP on source cluster
2. Review generated DDL
3. Identify potential issues:
   - Unsupported table types
   - Complex views
   - Special characters in names
4. Modify scripts if needed
5. Plan migration strategy

docs.title: Use Case 2: Schema Backup
docs.text:
Create regular schema backups:

# Scheduled job to dump schemas weekly
hms-mirror \
  --strategy DUMP \
  --output-dir /backups/schema/$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Store in version control
git add /backups/schema/*
git commit -m "Weekly schema backup $(date)"

docs.title: Use Case 3: Cross-Environment Script Generation
docs.text:
Generate scripts for different environments:

1. DUMP from production
2. Modify scripts:
   - Change database names (add _dev prefix)
   - Update locations for dev storage
   - Remove production-specific settings
3. Execute on development cluster

docs.title: Use Case 4: Compliance Documentation
docs.text:
Generate schema documentation for auditors:

1. Run DUMP on all databases
2. Include output in compliance package
3. Store with timestamp for audit trail
4. Use for data lineage documentation

docs.title: Include Everything
docs.text:
# Dump all table types and views
migrateNonNative: true
migrateVIEW:
  on: true
migrateACID:
  on: true

docs.title: Filter Specific Tables
docs.text:
# Dataset configuration
databases:
  - databaseName: analytics
    filter:
      includeRegEx: "fact_.*|dim_.*"
      excludeRegEx: ".*_temp|.*_bak"

docs.title: Output File Details
docs.text: File Contents Use *_execute.sql CREATE statements Schema creation *_clean.sql DROP statements Schema removal/cleanup runStatus.yaml Execution metadata Audit/tracking distcp_workbook.md Location information Data planning

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All databases included in output
  • All tables have DDL generated
  • Partition definitions included
  • Table properties preserved
  • Views included (if enabled)
  • Output files properly formatted

docs.title: Issue: "Missing tables in output"
docs.text: Solutions: - Check dataset filters aren't excluding tables - Verify connection permissions - Check table types (non-native may need explicit enable)

docs.title: Issue: "Views failing to dump"
docs.text: Solutions: - Enable migrateVIEW.on in configuration - Views with missing base tables may fail

docs.title: Issue: "Partition information missing"
docs.text: Solutions: - Very large partition counts may be truncated - Check partition limit settings

docs.title: Issue: "Special characters causing issues"
docs.text: Solutions: - Table/column names with special characters may need escaping - Review and manually fix generated scripts

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Regular backups: Schedule DUMP jobs for schema backup
  2. Version control: Store generated scripts in git
  3. Review before use: Always review scripts before execution
  4. Environment adaptation: Modify scripts for different environments
  5. Include metadata: Use runStatus.yaml for audit trails

docs.title: Execute on Target
docs.text:
# Connect to target cluster and run scripts
beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://target:10000" \
  -f /path/to/db_execute.sql

docs.title: Modify for Different Environment
docs.text:
# Change locations for different cluster
sed 's|hdfs://source-cluster|hdfs://target-cluster|g' \
  db_execute.sql > db_execute_target.sql

# Add prefix to database names
sed 's|CREATE DATABASE|CREATE DATABASE dev_|g' \
  db_execute.sql > db_execute_dev.sql

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• SCHEMA_ONLY Workflow - When target is available
  • Offline Planning - Multi-phase migrations

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The CONVERT_LINKED strategy converts previously created LINKED tables (tables pointing to source cluster storage) into regular tables pointing to target cluster storage. This is used after data has been copied to the target via external means (distcp, cloud sync, etc.).

This completes the second phase of a two-phase migration: first LINKED for testing, then CONVERT_LINKED after data copy.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• After LINKED migration: Tables were created using LINKED strategy
  • After data copy: Data has been copied to target storage externally
  • Testing complete: Validation on linked tables is finished
  • Ready for cutover: Ready to make target tables independent

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Existing LINKED tables: Tables on target pointing to source storage
  2. Data copied: Target storage contains the data files
  3. Both connections: Source and target clusters accessible
  4. Permissions: ALTER TABLE permissions on target

docs.title: Two-Phase Migration Overview
docs.text:
Phase 1: LINKED
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│  Source Cluster │     │ Target Cluster  │     │                 │
│                 │     │                 │     │  Applications   │
│  db.table       │────>│  db.table       │<────│  (testing)      │
│  LOCATION: src  │     │  LOCATION: src  │     │                 │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
         │
         │ Tables point to source, apps test on target
         v

Phase 2: Data Copy (External - distcp/sync)
┌─────────────────┐                          ┌─────────────────┐
│  Source Storage │ ──────distcp───────────> │ Target Storage  │
│  hdfs://src/... │                          │ hdfs://tgt/...  │
└─────────────────┘                          └─────────────────┘

Phase 3: CONVERT_LINKED
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│  Source Cluster │     │ Target Cluster  │     │                 │
│                 │     │                 │     │  Applications   │
│  db.table       │     │  db.table       │<────│  (production)   │
│  LOCATION: src  │     │  LOCATION: tgt  │     │                 │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
                               │
                               │ Tables now point to target storage
                               v

docs.title: Step 1: Verify LINKED Tables Exist
docs.text:
1. Connect to target cluster
  2. Verify tables are LINKED (point to source): 
    DESCRIBE FORMATTED db.table;
-- Location should show source cluster path
-- e.g., hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table

docs.title: Step 2: Copy Data to Target Storage
docs.text:
Use external tools to copy data:

Option A: Hadoop DistCp 

# Use distcp workbook from LINKED job
hadoop distcp \
  -update \
  hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table \
  hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table

Option B: Cloud Provider Tools 

# AWS
aws s3 sync s3://source-bucket/warehouse/ s3://target-bucket/warehouse/

# Azure
azcopy sync "source-container" "target-container"

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Data Copy
docs.text:
# Compare file counts
hdfs dfs -count hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table
hdfs dfs -count hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table

# Compare sizes
hdfs dfs -du -s hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table
hdfs dfs -du -s hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Connections
docs.text:
Same connections used for LINKED migration:

  1. LEFT Connection: Source cluster
  2. RIGHT Connection: Target cluster with correct HCFS namespace

docs.title: Step 5: Use Same Dataset
docs.text: Use the same Dataset from LINKED migration (or create matching one).

docs.title: Step 6: Create Configuration
docs.text:
# Configuration for CONVERT_LINKED
createIfNotExists: true

# Warehouse should match target storage layout
transferConfig:
  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/external"
    managedDirectory: "hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/managed"

docs.title: Step 7: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Select CONVERT_LINKED
  2. References: Same dataset, configuration, connections as LINKED job

docs.title: Step 8: Validate Job
docs.text:
1. Run validation
  2. Review location translations: 
    Current (LINKED): hdfs://source-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table
Target (CONVERTED): hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table
  3. Verify all linked tables are identified

docs.title: Step 9: Execute Conversion
docs.text:
1. Execute job
  2. HMS-Mirror runs ALTER TABLE commands: 
    ALTER TABLE db.table SET LOCATION 'hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table';

-- For partitioned tables, each partition is also updated
ALTER TABLE db.table PARTITION (year=2024, month=1)
SET LOCATION 'hdfs://target-cluster/warehouse/db.db/table/year=2024/month=1';

docs.title: Step 10: Validate Conversion
docs.text:
1. Verify new locations: 

    DESCRIBE FORMATTED db.table;
-- Location should now show target cluster path

  2. Verify data access: 

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM db.table;
-- Should return same count as before

  3. Verify partitions updated: 

    SHOW PARTITIONS db.table;
-- Each partition should point to target storage

docs.title: Step 11: Update Purge Flag (if applicable)
docs.text:
Tables migrated from legacy managed to EXTERNAL may need purge flag:

-- If table was legacy managed and should delete data on drop
ALTER TABLE db.table SET TBLPROPERTIES ('external.table.purge'='true');

HMS-Mirror handles this automatically based on source table type.

docs.title: Handling Partitioned Tables
docs.text:
Partitioned tables require updating each partition location:

-- HMS-Mirror generates for each partition
ALTER TABLE db.partitioned_table
PARTITION (year=2024, month=1)
SET LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/table/year=2024/month=1';

ALTER TABLE db.partitioned_table
PARTITION (year=2024, month=2)
SET LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/table/year=2024/month=2';
-- ... for all partitions

For tables with many partitions, this can take significant time.

docs.title: Legacy Managed Table Handling
docs.text:
Tables that were "legacy managed" (pre-Hive 3 managed without ACID) are converted to EXTERNAL during LINKED. CONVERT_LINKED sets the appropriate purge behavior:

Original Type After LINKED After CONVERT_LINKED Legacy Managed EXTERNAL (no purge) EXTERNAL with purge=true EXTERNAL EXTERNAL EXTERNAL (unchanged) ACID Handled separately Handled separately

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All linked tables identified
  • Data copy to target verified
  • Table locations updated to target
  • Partition locations updated (for partitioned tables)
  • Data accessible via target queries
  • Purge flag set correctly for legacy tables
  • Row counts match expected values

docs.title: Issue: "Data not found at new location"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify data copy completed successfully - Check target path matches expected location - Ensure distcp preserved directory structure

docs.title: Issue: "Partition locations not updating"
docs.text: Solutions: - Very large partition counts take time - Check for partition copy completeness - Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE to sync partitions

docs.title: Issue: "Table still shows source location"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify ALTER TABLE executed successfully - Check for permission issues on target - Re-run conversion for failed tables

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied on ALTER"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify Hive user has ALTER permissions - Check table ownership on target - Ensure target metastore allows modifications

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Complete data copy first: Ensure all data is on target before converting
  2. Verify file integrity: Compare checksums if critical
  3. Test before cutover: Run queries against both locations
  4. Keep source temporarily: Don't delete source until conversion verified
  5. Handle large tables separately: Partition-heavy tables may need extra time

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• LINKED Workflow - First phase of this process
  • Testing with LINKED - Detailed testing approach
  • Phased Migration - Complete multi-phase process

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This workflow covers the complete migration from Hortonworks Data Platform (HDP 2.x or 3.x) to Cloudera Data Platform (CDP). It addresses the key differences between platforms including Hive version changes, ACID table handling, and warehouse directory conventions.

docs.title: Migration Challenges
docs.text: Challenge HDP 2.x/3.x CDP 7.x HMS-Mirror Solution Hive Version Hive 1.x/2.x/3.x Hive 3.x/4.x Automatic DDL translation ACID Tables Optional/Limited Full support ACID migration/downgrade Managed Tables Legacy behavior Strict ACID Automatic conversion Warehouse Layout Single warehouse Separate managed/external Directory translation Table Properties HDP-specific CDP conventions Property conversion

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Source cluster (HDP):
  2. HDP 2.6+, 3.0, or 3.1
  3. HiveServer2 accessible

  4. HDFS accessible

  5. Target cluster (CDP):

  6. CDP 7.1+ (7.2+ recommended for Iceberg)
  7. HiveServer2 accessible

  8. Target storage configured (HDFS, S3, ADLS)

  9. Network:

  10. Connectivity between clusters (for SQL/HYBRID/EXPORT_IMPORT)
  11. Or shared storage access (for COMMON)

docs.title: Step 1.1: Inventory Source Environment
docs.text:
Connect to HDP cluster and gather information:

-- Count databases and tables
SHOW DATABASES;

-- For each database
USE database_name;
SHOW TABLES;

-- Identify ACID tables
SELECT TBL_NAME, TBL_TYPE
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
WHERE t.TBL_TYPE = 'MANAGED_TABLE';

-- Check for transactional tables
DESCRIBE FORMATTED table_name;
-- Look for: transactional=true

docs.title: Step 1.2: Identify Migration Scope
docs.text: Document: - Total databases and tables - ACID vs non-ACID table counts - Large tables (>100GB) - Partition-heavy tables (>500 partitions) - Views and their dependencies - Non-native tables (HBase, etc.)

docs.title: Step 2.1: Configure HDP (LEFT) Connection
docs.text:
1. HiveServer2 URI: 

    jdbc:hive2://hdp-hiveserver2:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM

  2. HCFS Namespace: 

    hdfs://hdp-nameservice

  3. Platform Type: Select HDP2 or HDP3

  4. Test connection: Verify READ access

docs.title: Step 2.2: Configure CDP (RIGHT) Connection
docs.text:
1. HiveServer2 URI: 

    jdbc:hive2://cdp-hiveserver2:10000/default;ssl=true;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM

  2. HCFS Namespace (choose based on storage):

  3. HDFS: hdfs://cdp-nameservice
  4. S3: s3a://cdp-datalake-bucket

  5. ADLS: abfs://container@account.dfs.core.windows.net

  6. Platform Type: Select CDP7_1, CDP7_2, or CDP7_3

  7. Test connection: Verify WRITE access

docs.title: Step 3.1: Create Migration Configuration
docs.text:
# HDP to CDP Migration Configuration

# Basic Settings
createIfNotExists: true
migrateNonNative: false  # Enable if HBase tables needed

# ACID Table Handling (Critical for HDP->CDP)
migrateACID:
  on: true
  only: false
  downgrade: true  # Convert to EXTERNAL (recommended)
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2  # Remove fake buckets from HDP
  partitionLimit: 500

# Views
migrateVIEW:
  on: true

# Transfer Settings
transferConfig:
  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "/warehouse/tablespace/external/hive"
    managedDirectory: "/warehouse/tablespace/managed/hive"

# Optimization
optimization:
  autoTune: true
  skipStatsCollection: false

docs.title: Step 3.2: Handle HDP-Specific Issues
docs.text:
Issue: Artificial Buckets HDP required bucket clauses for ACID. Set artificialBucketThreshold to remove:

migrateACID:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2  # Remove CLUSTERED BY with <=2 buckets

Issue: Legacy Managed Tables Pre-Hive 3 "managed" tables without ACID need conversion:

# These become EXTERNAL with purge flag in CDP
migrateACID:
  downgrade: true

docs.title: Step 4.1: Create Dataset
docs.text:
1. Add databases to migrate
  2. Configure filters:
  3. Exclude temporary tables: excludeRegEx: ".*_temp|.*_staging"
  4. Focus on specific schemas if phasing

docs.title: Step 4.2: Consider Phased Approach
docs.text:
For large environments, phase the migration:

Phase A: Non-ACID tables (simpler) 

# Dataset A: Non-ACID only
filter:
  tableTypes: ["EXTERNAL_TABLE"]

Phase B: ACID tables (requires downgrade) 

# Dataset B: ACID tables
migrateACID:
  on: true
  only: true  # Only ACID tables
  downgrade: true

docs.title: Step 5.1: Select Strategy
docs.text: Scenario Recommended Strategy Clusters connected, format OK HYBRID Clusters connected, format conversion SQL Clusters not connected SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp Testing first LINKED Shared storage COMMON

docs.title: Step 5.2: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Based on scenario
  2. References: Select your Dataset, Configuration, Connections
  3. Options:
  4. Enable Disaster Recovery if setting up DR
  5. Configure hybrid thresholds for mixed tables

docs.title: Step 5.3: Validate
docs.text:
1. Run validation
  2. Review:
  3. Table compatibility
  4. ACID table handling
  5. Location translations
  6. Partition counts

docs.title: Step 5.4: Dry Run
docs.text:
1. Execute dry run
  2. Review generated scripts
  3. Verify DDL translations

docs.title: Step 5.5: Execute Migration
docs.text:
1. Execute job
  2. Monitor progress
  3. Handle errors as they occur

docs.title: Step 6.1: Schema Validation
docs.text:
-- On CDP cluster
SHOW DATABASES;

-- Compare table counts
USE migrated_db;
SHOW TABLES;

-- Verify table structures
DESCRIBE FORMATTED table_name;

docs.title: Step 6.2: Data Validation
docs.text:
-- Row count comparison
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table_name;

-- Sample data check
SELECT * FROM table_name LIMIT 100;

-- For partitioned tables
SHOW PARTITIONS table_name;

docs.title: Step 6.3: Application Testing
docs.text:
1. Connect applications to CDP
  2. Run test workloads
  3. Verify query results match HDP

docs.title: Step 7.1: Final Sync (if needed)
docs.text: For tables that changed during migration: 1. Identify changed tables 2. Re-run migration for deltas 3. Or use sync mode for DR setup

docs.title: Step 7.2: Switch Applications
docs.text:
1. Update connection strings to CDP
  2. Verify applications work
  3. Monitor for errors

docs.title: Step 7.3: Decommission HDP
docs.text: After validation period: 1. Stop HDP workloads 2. Archive HDP data (optional) 3. Decommission HDP cluster

docs.title: Option A: Downgrade to EXTERNAL (Recommended)
docs.text:
migrateACID:
  downgrade: true

Benefits: - Simpler migration - Works with all strategies - No transactional overhead on target

docs.title: Option B: Preserve ACID
docs.text:
migrateACID:
  downgrade: false

Requirements: - CDP supports full ACID - Use SQL strategy - More complex, longer migration

docs.title: Issue: "Bucket clause mismatch"
docs.text:
HDP ACID required buckets, CDP doesn't: 

migrateACID:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2

docs.title: Issue: "Managed table location outside warehouse"
docs.text:
CDP enforces warehouse paths for managed tables: 

migrateACID:
  downgrade: true  # Convert to EXTERNAL

docs.title: Issue: "Statistics missing after migration"
docs.text:
optimization:
  skipStatsCollection: false
enableAutoTableStats: true

docs.title: Issue: "View dependencies failing"
docs.text: Migrate base tables before views. Views are processed last.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All databases created on CDP
  • All tables created with correct schemas
  • ACID tables properly converted
  • Data accessible and queryable
  • Views functional
  • Statistics collected
  • Applications tested
  • Performance acceptable

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• ACID Migration - Detailed ACID handling
  • ACID Downgrade - ACID to EXTERNAL conversion
  • Phased Migration - Multi-phase approach
  • Large Table Handling - Big table strategies

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This workflow covers migrating Hive data from on-premises Hadoop clusters to cloud-based data platforms. This includes migrations to AWS (S3/EMR), Azure (ADLS/HDInsight/Synapse), and Google Cloud (GCS/Dataproc).

docs.title: Migration Scenarios
docs.text: Scenario Source Target Strategy Connected On-prem HDFS Cloud Storage SQL/HYBRID via VPN Disconnected On-prem HDFS Cloud Storage SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp Intermediate On-prem HDFS Cloud Storage Multi-hop via staging

docs.title: AWS
docs.text:
Target Storage: s3a://bucket-name/warehouse/
Target Compute: EMR, CDP on AWS
Connection: Direct Connect or VPN

docs.title: Azure
docs.text:
Target Storage: abfs://container@account.dfs.core.windows.net/warehouse/
Target Compute: HDInsight, CDP on Azure, Synapse
Connection: ExpressRoute or VPN

docs.title: Google Cloud
docs.text:
Target Storage: gs://bucket-name/warehouse/
Target Compute: Dataproc, CDP on GCP
Connection: Cloud Interconnect or VPN

docs.title: Step 1.1: Provision Cloud Resources
docs.text:
1. Storage:
  2. Create storage bucket/container
  3. Configure appropriate IAM/ACLs

  4. Set up encryption (at-rest and in-transit)

  5. Compute:

  6. Provision Hive cluster (EMR/HDInsight/Dataproc/CDP)
  7. Configure networking

  8. Set up HiveServer2 with appropriate authentication

  9. Networking:

  10. Establish connectivity (VPN/Direct Connect/ExpressRoute)
  11. Configure security groups/firewall rules
  12. Test connectivity

docs.title: Step 1.2: Configure Cloud Credentials
docs.text:
On-prem cluster needs cloud storage access:

For AWS S3: 

<!-- core-site.xml -->
<property>
  <name>fs.s3a.access.key</name>
  <value>YOUR_ACCESS_KEY</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>fs.s3a.secret.key</name>
  <value>YOUR_SECRET_KEY</value>
</property>

Or use IAM roles with instance profiles.

For Azure ADLS Gen2: 

<!-- core-site.xml -->
<property>
  <name>fs.azure.account.auth.type</name>
  <value>OAuth</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>fs.azure.account.oauth.provider.type</name>
  <value>org.apache.hadoop.fs.azurebfs.oauth2.ClientCredsTokenProvider</value>
</property>

docs.title: Step 2.1: On-Premises (LEFT) Connection
docs.text:
name: "on-prem-production"
hcfsNamespace: "hdfs://nameservice1"
hs2Uri: "jdbc:hive2://hiveserver:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM"
platformType: "HDP3"  # or CDH6, CDP7_x

docs.title: Step 2.2: Cloud (RIGHT) Connection
docs.text:
AWS Example: 

name: "aws-datalake"
hcfsNamespace: "s3a://company-datalake"
hs2Uri: "jdbc:hive2://emr-master:10000/default"
platformType: "EMR6"

Azure Example: 

name: "azure-datalake"
hcfsNamespace: "abfs://datalake@storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net"
hs2Uri: "jdbc:hive2://hdinsight.azurehdinsight.net:443/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=/hive2"
platformType: "CDP7_2"

docs.title: Strategy A: Direct Connected (VPN/Direct Connect)
docs.text:
When clusters can communicate directly:

  1. Strategy: SQL, HYBRID, or EXPORT_IMPORT
  2. Data Flow: On-prem Hive → Cloud Storage
  3. Best For: Moderate data volumes with good bandwidth
strategy: HYBRID
hybrid:
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 100
  sqlPartitionLimit: 500
  sqlSizeLimit: 1073741824

docs.title: Strategy B: Intermediate Storage
docs.text:
When direct connectivity is limited:
