  1. Set up staging bucket accessible from both environments
  2. Phase 1: On-prem to staging
  3. Phase 2: Staging to cloud destination
transferConfig:
  intermediateStorage: "s3a://migration-staging-bucket/"

docs.title: Strategy C: Offline Migration
docs.text:
When no direct connectivity:

  1. SCHEMA_ONLY: Create tables on cloud
  2. Export data: Use distcp to staging or ship
  3. Import data: Copy to cloud storage
  4. Update locations: Or let SCHEMA_ONLY set correct locations

docs.title: Step 4.1: Create Migration Configuration
docs.text:
# On-Prem to Cloud Configuration

createIfNotExists: true

# ACID handling - typically downgrade for cloud
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true

# Cloud warehouse directories
transferConfig:
  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "s3a://bucket/warehouse/external"
    managedDirectory: "s3a://bucket/warehouse/managed"

# Optimization for long-distance transfer
optimization:
  autoTune: true
  compressTextOutput: true

docs.title: For Connected Migration (SQL/HYBRID)
docs.text:
1. Create and validate job
  2. Execute migration
  3. Data transfers directly to cloud storage

docs.title: For Disconnected Migration (SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp)
docs.text:
Step 5.1: Run SCHEMA_ONLY migration

# Creates tables on cloud pointing to cloud locations
hms-mirror --strategy SCHEMA_ONLY ...

Step 5.2: Copy data via distcp

# From on-prem cluster with cloud credentials
hadoop distcp \
  -Dmapreduce.map.memory.mb=4096 \
  -Dmapreduce.job.queuename=migration \
  -update -delete \
  hdfs://nameservice1/warehouse/db.db/ \
  s3a://bucket/warehouse/external/db.db/

Step 5.3: Verify and repair partitions

-- On cloud cluster
MSCK REPAIR TABLE db.table;

docs.title: AWS S3 Optimization
docs.text:
optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      fs.s3a.fast.upload:
        RIGHT: "true"
      fs.s3a.fast.upload.buffer:
        RIGHT: "bytebuffer"
      fs.s3a.multipart.size:
        RIGHT: "104857600"  # 100MB parts

docs.title: Azure ADLS Optimization
docs.text:
optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      fs.azure.write.request.size:
        RIGHT: "67108864"  # 64MB

docs.title: Step 7.1: Schema Validation
docs.text:
-- On cloud cluster
SHOW DATABASES;
USE migrated_db;
SHOW TABLES;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED table_name;

docs.title: Step 7.2: Data Validation
docs.text:
-- Compare row counts
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table_name;

-- Sample data verification
SELECT * FROM table_name LIMIT 100;

docs.title: Step 7.3: Performance Testing
docs.text:
1. Run representative queries
  2. Compare execution times
  3. Tune cloud configurations

docs.title: Phase 8: Cutover
docs.text:
1. Freeze source: Stop writes to on-prem
  2. Final sync: Migrate any remaining changes
  3. Validate: Confirm all data current
  4. Switch applications: Update connection strings
  5. Monitor: Watch for issues
  6. Decommission: Remove on-prem when ready

docs.title: Bandwidth Planning
docs.text: Data Volume 100 Mbps 1 Gbps 10 Gbps 1 TB ~24 hours ~2.5 hours ~15 min 10 TB ~10 days ~24 hours ~2.5 hours 100 TB ~100 days ~10 days ~24 hours

docs.title: Optimizing Transfer
docs.text:
1. Parallel distcp: Use multiple mappers
  2. Compression: Compress if not already
  3. Off-peak hours: Schedule during low usage
  4. Staging approach: For very large datasets

docs.title: Cost Considerations
docs.text: Cost Type Consideration Egress Cloud charges for data out Storage Cloud storage costs Compute Query processing costs Network VPN/Direct Connect fees

docs.title: Issue: "Slow transfer speeds"
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase distcp parallelism - Check network bandwidth - Use staging approach for large datasets

docs.title: Issue: "Authentication failures to cloud"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify cloud credentials configuration - Check IAM roles/policies - Validate service principals

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied on cloud storage"
docs.text: Solutions: - Check bucket/container policies - Verify IAM permissions - Validate cross-account access if applicable

docs.title: Issue: "Table data not visible after migration"
docs.text: Solutions: - Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE - Verify file permissions on cloud storage - Check partition locations

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start small: Migrate test databases first
  2. Monitor costs: Track cloud spending during migration
  3. Plan bandwidth: Size network appropriately
  4. Use staging: For large datasets or unreliable connections
  5. Validate incrementally: Check data as you migrate
  6. Keep source: Don't delete on-prem until validated

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Cloud resources provisioned
  • Connectivity established
  • Credentials configured
  • Test migration successful
  • Full migration completed
  • Data validated
  • Applications tested
  • Performance acceptable
  • Cutover completed

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• STORAGE_MIGRATION - Within-cluster cloud migration
  • SCHEMA_ONLY - Offline migration approach
  • HYBRID - Connected migration
  • Multi-Hop - Intermediate staging

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) tables in Hive provide transactional semantics including INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and MERGE operations. Migrating ACID tables requires special consideration due to their internal file structure and transactional nature.

This workflow covers strategies for migrating ACID tables between clusters.

docs.title: ACID Table Characteristics
docs.text: Property Value Migration Impact Table Type MANAGED_TABLE Must handle ownership transactional true Requires special handling Format ORC (typically) Format preserved or converted Bucketing Optional (required in older Hive) May need adjustment Delete Deltas Present Must be compacted or converted

docs.title: ACID File Structure
docs.text:
table_name/
├── base_0000001/          # Base files
│   ├── bucket_00000
│   └── bucket_00001
├── delta_0000002_0000002/ # Insert deltas
│   ├── bucket_00000
│   └── bucket_00001
└── delete_delta_0000003/  # Delete deltas (if any)
    ├── bucket_00000
    └── bucket_00001

docs.title: Option 1: ACID Downgrade (Recommended)
docs.text:
Convert ACID tables to EXTERNAL tables with purge during migration.

Benefits: - Simplest migration path - Works with all strategies (SQL, EXPORT_IMPORT, HYBRID) - No transactional overhead on target - Compatible with Iceberg conversion

Configuration: 

migrateACID:
  on: true
  only: false
  downgrade: true
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2
  partitionLimit: 500

docs.title: Option 2: ACID Preservation
docs.text:
Migrate ACID tables as ACID on target.

Benefits: - Preserves full transactional capabilities - No application changes needed

Limitations: - Requires SQL strategy - Target must support full ACID - More complex, longer migration

Configuration: 

migrateACID:
  on: true
  only: false
  downgrade: false

docs.title: Option 3: In-Place Operations
docs.text:
Perform ACID operations within the source cluster.

Use Cases: - ACID compaction before migration - ACID downgrade in place - Storage migration with ACID

Configuration: 

migrateACID:
  on: true
  inplace: true

docs.title: Step 1: Identify ACID Tables
docs.text:
-- Find all ACID tables
SELECT d.NAME as db_name, t.TBL_NAME, t.TBL_TYPE
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
JOIN metastore.TABLE_PARAMS p ON t.TBL_ID = p.TBL_ID
WHERE p.PARAM_KEY = 'transactional'
  AND p.PARAM_VALUE = 'true';

docs.title: Step 2: Check for Pending Transactions
docs.text:
-- Verify no open transactions
SHOW TRANSACTIONS;

-- Check compaction status
SHOW COMPACTIONS;

docs.title: Step 3: Run Compaction (Recommended)
docs.text:
Compact ACID tables before migration for cleaner data:

-- Major compaction for each ACID table
ALTER TABLE db.acid_table COMPACT 'major';

-- Monitor compaction
SHOW COMPACTIONS;

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Migration
docs.text:
# Configuration
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true  # Convert to EXTERNAL
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2
  partitionLimit: 500

# For explicit tables-only migration
# only: true

docs.title: Step 5: Create Dataset
docs.text:
Include ACID tables in your dataset:

databases:
  - databaseName: your_database
    filter:
      # Optional: filter specific tables
      includeRegEx: ".*"

docs.title: Step 6: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: SQL (required for ACID with downgrade)
  2. Configure hybrid settings for partition limits

docs.title: Step 7: Execute Migration
docs.text:
The migration process:

  1. Read: HMS-Mirror reads ACID table via HiveServer2
  2. Transform: Data extracted, transactions materialized
  3. Write: Data written as standard ORC/Parquet files
  4. Create: EXTERNAL table created on target with purge flag

Generated DDL Example: 

-- Original ACID table becomes:
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE target_db.former_acid_table (
  id BIGINT,
  name STRING,
  value DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/external/db.db/former_acid_table'
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'external.table.purge'='true'  -- Data deleted on DROP
);

-- Data migrated via:
INSERT INTO target_db.former_acid_table
SELECT * FROM source_db.acid_table;

docs.title: Step 8: Validate
docs.text:
-- On target cluster
DESCRIBE FORMATTED former_acid_table;
-- Verify: Table Type = EXTERNAL_TABLE
-- Verify: transactional = null or false
-- Verify: external.table.purge = true

-- Verify data
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM former_acid_table;

docs.title: Handling Artificial Buckets
docs.text:
Older Hive versions required bucket clauses for ACID. These "artificial" buckets should be removed:

migrateACID:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2

How it works: - Tables with CLUSTERED BY and <= threshold buckets have bucketing removed - Tables with > threshold buckets keep their bucketing (assumed intentional)

Example: 

-- Source (HDP with artificial bucket)
CREATE TABLE acid_table (id INT, name STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 2 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('transactional'='true');

-- Target (bucket removed, threshold=2)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE acid_table (id INT, name STRING)
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('external.table.purge'='true');

docs.title: Partition Limits
docs.text:
ACID tables with many partitions require careful handling:

migrateACID:
  partitionLimit: 500  # Tables exceeding this are skipped

For partition-heavy ACID tables: 1. Increase limit if cluster resources allow 2. Migrate in partition ranges 3. Use SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp with compaction

docs.title: In-Place ACID Operations
docs.text: For operations within the same cluster:

docs.title: In-Place Compaction
docs.text:
migrateACID:
  on: true
  inplace: true
# Run compaction without migration

docs.title: In-Place Downgrade
docs.text:
Convert ACID to EXTERNAL without moving between clusters:

migrateACID:
  on: true
  inplace: true
  downgrade: true

This creates a new EXTERNAL table from the ACID table data.

docs.title: ACID to Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
Convert ACID tables directly to Iceberg format:

migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true  # First downgrade from ACID

icebergConversion:
  enable: true
  fileTypeTranslation: PARQUET
  version: 2

Process: 1. ACID table read via SQL 2. Transactions materialized 3. Data written as Iceberg table 4. Full Iceberg capabilities available

docs.title: Issue: "Timeout reading ACID table"
docs.text: Solutions: - Run major compaction first - Reduce partition scope - Increase query timeout in overrides

docs.title: Issue: "Bucket count mismatch"
docs.text: Solutions: - Set artificialBucketThreshold appropriately - Verify source table bucketing intent

docs.title: Issue: "Open transactions preventing read"
docs.text: Solutions: - Wait for transactions to complete - Abort orphaned transactions (carefully) - Run compaction to finalize

docs.title: Issue: "Out of memory during migration"
docs.text: Solutions: - Reduce concurrent table migrations - Increase Tez container size - Migrate large tables separately

docs.title: Issue: "Delete deltas not handled"
docs.text: Solutions: - Run major compaction before migration - SQL strategy materializes deletes automatically

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Compact before migrating: Major compaction creates cleaner files
  2. Downgrade when possible: Simpler and faster migration
  3. Monitor transactions: Ensure no open transactions
  4. Test with sample first: Validate approach on subset
  5. Plan for large tables: ACID tables often contain more data

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All ACID tables identified
  • Compaction completed (if run)
  • Tables migrated to target
  • Data counts match source
  • External/purge flags set correctly
  • Bucketing handled appropriately
  • Applications tested with new tables

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• ACID Downgrade - Focused downgrade workflow
  • HDP to CDP - Platform migration with ACID
  • Iceberg Conversion - ACID to Iceberg
  • SQL Workflow - Primary strategy for ACID

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: ACID downgrade converts transactional (ACID) tables to non-transactional EXTERNAL tables. This is often the simplest migration path when full ACID capabilities aren't needed on the target cluster, or when migrating to platforms with limited ACID support.

docs.title: When to Downgrade
docs.text: Scenario Recommendation Migration to cloud storage Downgrade (cloud may have ACID limitations) Iceberg conversion planned Downgrade (Iceberg provides better ACID) Read-heavy workloads Downgrade (no transactional overhead) Mixed platform targets Downgrade (broader compatibility) Full ACID needed Do NOT downgrade

docs.title: What Changes During Downgrade
docs.text: Attribute ACID Table Downgraded Table Table Type MANAGED_TABLE EXTERNAL_TABLE transactional true false/null Purge Behavior Always purge external.table.purge=true File Format ORC (typically) Same or converted Bucket Clause Often required Removed if artificial DELETE/UPDATE Supported Not supported

docs.title: Step 1: Identify ACID Tables
docs.text:
-- Find all ACID tables
SELECT
  d.NAME as db_name,
  t.TBL_NAME,
  t.TBL_TYPE
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
JOIN metastore.TABLE_PARAMS p ON t.TBL_ID = p.TBL_ID
WHERE p.PARAM_KEY = 'transactional'
  AND p.PARAM_VALUE = 'true';

docs.title: Step 2: Verify Application Compatibility
docs.text:
Before downgrading, confirm applications don't need: - UPDATE statements - DELETE statements - MERGE statements - Snapshot isolation

If applications use these, either: - Modify applications to use INSERT-only patterns - Keep tables as ACID - Convert to Iceberg for modern ACID

docs.title: Run Compaction (Recommended)
docs.text:
Compact ACID tables for clean files:

-- Major compaction for each ACID table
ALTER TABLE db.acid_table COMPACT 'major' AND WAIT;

-- Or initiate and check later
ALTER TABLE db.acid_table COMPACT 'major';

-- Monitor compaction
SHOW COMPACTIONS;

docs.title: Verify No Open Transactions
docs.text:
-- Check for open transactions
SHOW TRANSACTIONS;

-- Verify no locks
SHOW LOCKS;

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Downgrade
docs.text:
# Configuration for ACID downgrade
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true
  only: false  # true to migrate ONLY ACID tables
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2  # Remove fake buckets
  partitionLimit: 500

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: SQL (required for downgrade)
  2. Enable ACID migration with downgrade
  3. Select appropriate datasets

docs.title: Step 6: Execute Downgrade Migration
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror executes:

  1. Reads ACID table via HiveServer2
  2. Materializes transactions: Applies all deltas
  3. Creates EXTERNAL table on target
  4. Writes clean data: Standard ORC/Parquet files
  5. Sets purge flag: For proper cleanup behavior

Generated DDL: 

-- Target table definition
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE db.formerly_acid_table (
  id BIGINT,
  name STRING,
  value DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION 'hdfs://target/warehouse/external/db.db/formerly_acid_table'
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'external.table.purge'='true',
  'hms_mirror_original_type'='MANAGED_TABLE'
);

-- Data migration
INSERT INTO db.formerly_acid_table
SELECT * FROM source_db.acid_table;

docs.title: Step 7: Validate Downgrade
docs.text:
-- On target cluster
DESCRIBE FORMATTED db.formerly_acid_table;

-- Verify:
-- Table Type: EXTERNAL_TABLE
-- transactional: null or absent
-- external.table.purge: true

-- Verify data
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM db.formerly_acid_table;

docs.title: Artificial Buckets (Remove)
docs.text:
HDP ACID tables often have artificial bucket clauses:

-- Source (HDP with artificial bucket)
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id INT,
  customer_id INT
)
CLUSTERED BY (order_id) INTO 2 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('transactional'='true');

Configuration to remove: 

migrateACID:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2

Result: 

-- Target (bucket removed)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE orders (
  order_id INT,
  customer_id INT
)
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('external.table.purge'='true');

docs.title: Intentional Buckets (Preserve)
docs.text:
For tables with intentional bucketing:

-- Source (intentional bucketing for joins)
CREATE TABLE customer_orders (
  order_id INT,
  customer_id INT
)
CLUSTERED BY (customer_id) INTO 32 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('transactional'='true');

Set threshold appropriately: 

migrateACID:
  artificialBucketThreshold: 2  # Only remove <= 2 buckets

Result: 

-- Target (bucketing preserved)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE customer_orders (
  order_id INT,
  customer_id INT
)
CLUSTERED BY (customer_id) INTO 32 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('external.table.purge'='true');

docs.title: With external.table.purge=true
docs.text:
-- Dropping table DELETES the data
DROP TABLE db.table;
-- Data at location is removed

docs.title: Without Purge Flag (or false)
docs.text:
-- Dropping table keeps the data
DROP TABLE db.table;
-- Data at location remains

HMS-Mirror sets external.table.purge=true for downgraded tables to maintain the original MANAGED table's cleanup behavior.

docs.title: In-Place Downgrade
docs.text:
For downgrading within the same cluster:

migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true
  inplace: true

Process: 1. Creates new EXTERNAL table from ACID data 2. Data rewritten to new location 3. Original ACID table optionally renamed/archived 4. New table renamed to original name

docs.title: ACID-Only Migration
docs.text:
To migrate only ACID tables (skip non-ACID):

migrateACID:
  on: true
  only: true  # Only process ACID tables
  downgrade: true

Use case: Separate jobs for ACID and non-ACID tables.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• All ACID tables identified
  • Compaction completed (if run)
  • Tables converted to EXTERNAL
  • transactional property removed
  • external.table.purge set to true
  • Bucketing handled correctly
  • Data counts match source
  • Applications tested with new tables

docs.title: Issue: "Compaction not completing"
docs.text: Solutions: - Check for blocking transactions - Increase compaction resources - Skip compaction (HMS-Mirror handles delta reads)

docs.title: Issue: "Bucket mismatch errors"
docs.text: Solutions: - Adjust artificialBucketThreshold - Verify bucketing intent in source tables

docs.title: Issue: "Data mismatch after downgrade"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify compaction completed - Check for in-flight transactions during migration - Re-run with fresh source state

docs.title: Issue: "Applications failing after downgrade"
docs.text: Solutions: - Applications may use UPDATE/DELETE - Modify applications or keep as ACID - Consider Iceberg for modern ACID needs

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Compact before migration: Cleaner source files
  2. Freeze writes: Prevent changes during migration
  3. Test applications: Verify with downgraded tables
  4. Document changes: Track which tables were downgraded
  5. Consider Iceberg: For modern ACID needs

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• ACID Migration - Complete ACID handling
  • Iceberg Conversion - ACID to Iceberg
  • HDP to CDP - Platform migration

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Apache Iceberg is an open table format designed for large analytic datasets. This workflow covers converting existing Hive tables to Iceberg format during migration, enabling features like hidden partitioning, schema evolution, time travel, and ACID semantics.

docs.title: Iceberg Benefits
docs.text: Feature Hive Tables Iceberg Tables Schema Evolution Limited Full support Partition Evolution Requires rewrite Dynamic Time Travel Not available Built-in ACID ORC-only Format-agnostic Hidden Partitioning No Yes Snapshot Isolation Limited Full

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Target platform support: CDP 7.2+, Spark 3.x, or compatible platform
  2. Source table format: ORC, Parquet, or Avro (or will be converted)
  3. SQL strategy: Iceberg conversion requires SQL-based migration
  4. Compute resources: Sufficient resources for data rewriting

docs.title: File Format Translation
docs.text: Option Description SAME Preserve source format if compatible PARQUET Convert all to Parquet ORC Convert all to ORC AVRO Convert all to Avro STANDARD Convert to Parquet (recommended)

docs.title: Iceberg Version
docs.text: Version Features 1 Original format, wider compatibility 2 Row-level deletes, improved metadata (recommended)

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Target Platform
docs.text:
Confirm Iceberg support:

-- On target cluster
CREATE TABLE test_iceberg (id INT)
STORED BY ICEBERG;

-- If this works, Iceberg is available
DROP TABLE test_iceberg;

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Connections
docs.text: Standard connection setup with Iceberg-capable target.

docs.title: Step 3: Create Configuration
docs.text:
# Iceberg Conversion Configuration

createIfNotExists: true

# Iceberg Conversion Settings
icebergConversion:
  enable: true
  fileTypeTranslation: PARQUET  # or SAME, ORC, AVRO, STANDARD
  version: 2  # Iceberg format version
  inplace: false  # true for same-cluster conversion
  tableProperties:
    write.format.default: parquet
    write.parquet.compression-codec: zstd
    write.metadata.compression-codec: gzip
    commit.retry.num-retries: "4"

# ACID tables should be downgraded first
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true

# Required: SQL strategy for conversion
# (Set via Job, not Configuration)

docs.title: Step 4: Create Dataset
docs.text:
1. Include tables to convert
  2. Consider excluding:
  3. Very large tables (migrate separately)
  4. Tables not benefiting from Iceberg
  5. Temporary/staging tables

docs.title: Step 5: Create Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Must be SQL for Iceberg conversion
  2. References: Select configuration with Iceberg enabled
  3. Note: EXPORT_IMPORT and HYBRID do not support Iceberg conversion

docs.title: Step 6: Validate
docs.text:
1. Run validation
  2. Review:
  3. Table format compatibility
  4. Partition structure translation
  5. Schema compatibility

docs.title: Step 7: Execute Conversion
docs.text:
1. Execute the job
  2. For each table:
  3. Iceberg table created on target
  4. Data written in specified format
  5. Iceberg metadata generated

Generated DDL Example: 

-- Original Hive table becomes:
CREATE TABLE target_db.converted_table (
  id BIGINT,
  name STRING,
  created_date DATE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
STORED BY ICEBERG
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 's3a://bucket/warehouse/target_db.db/converted_table'
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'format-version'='2',
  'write.format.default'='parquet'
);

docs.title: Step 8: Verify Iceberg Tables
docs.text:
-- Verify table is Iceberg
DESCRIBE FORMATTED converted_table;
-- Look for: STORED BY ICEBERG

-- Check Iceberg metadata
SELECT * FROM converted_table.snapshots;

-- Time travel query
SELECT * FROM converted_table FOR SYSTEM_VERSION AS OF 1;

docs.title: Scenario 1: Simple Table Conversion
docs.text:
Source: 

CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE events (
  event_id BIGINT,
  event_type STRING,
  event_time TIMESTAMP
)
STORED AS PARQUET;

Target Iceberg: 

CREATE TABLE events (
  event_id BIGINT,
  event_type STRING,
  event_time TIMESTAMP
)
STORED BY ICEBERG
STORED AS PARQUET;

docs.title: Scenario 2: Partitioned Table Conversion
docs.text:
Source: 

CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE sales (
  sale_id BIGINT,
  amount DECIMAL(10,2),
  sale_date DATE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
STORED AS PARQUET;

Target Iceberg: 

CREATE TABLE sales (
  sale_id BIGINT,
  amount DECIMAL(10,2),
  sale_date DATE,
  year INT,
  month INT
)
PARTITIONED BY (year, month)
STORED BY ICEBERG
STORED AS PARQUET;

Note: Iceberg uses partition transforms, partitions become regular columns.

docs.title: Scenario 3: ACID Table to Iceberg
docs.text:
Source (ACID): 

CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id BIGINT,
  customer_id INT,
  total DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('transactional'='true');

Conversion process: 1. ACID table read via SQL (materializes transactions) 2. Data written as Iceberg Parquet 3. Full Iceberg ACID capabilities available

docs.title: Scenario 4: Format Conversion
docs.text:
Source (Text): 

CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE legacy_data (
  col1 STRING,
  col2 STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

Target Iceberg (with format conversion): 

-- Configured with fileTypeTranslation: PARQUET
CREATE TABLE legacy_data (
  col1 STRING,
  col2 STRING
)
STORED BY ICEBERG
STORED AS PARQUET;

docs.title: In-Place Iceberg Conversion
docs.text:
For converting tables within the same cluster:

icebergConversion:
  enable: true
  inplace: true

In-Place Process: 1. Source files must already be Iceberg-compatible (Parquet/ORC/Avro) 2. Uses Iceberg's native in-place migration procedure 3. No data copying - only metadata transformation 4. Much faster than SQL-based conversion

Requirements: - Source cluster supports Iceberg - Files already in compatible format - Table structure supports Iceberg

docs.title: Table Properties
docs.text:
Common Iceberg table properties:

icebergConversion:
  tableProperties:
    # File format
    write.format.default: parquet
    write.parquet.compression-codec: zstd  # snappy, gzip, lz4

    # Metadata
    write.metadata.compression-codec: gzip
    write.metadata.delete-after-commit.enabled: "true"

    # Compaction
    write.target-file-size-bytes: "536870912"  # 512MB

    # History
    history.expire.max-snapshot-age-ms: "432000000"  # 5 days

docs.title: Partition Evolution
docs.text:
One of Iceberg's key features - partitioning can change without rewriting data:

-- After migration, evolve partitions
ALTER TABLE converted_table ADD PARTITION FIELD bucket(16, customer_id);

-- Future writes use new partitioning
-- Old data remains readable

docs.title: Issue: "Iceberg not available on target"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify Iceberg is installed on target cluster - Check Hive/Spark Iceberg integration - Verify platform version (CDP 7.2+ required)

docs.title: Issue: "Partition structure incompatible"
docs.text: Solutions: - Iceberg handles most partition types - Complex partition expressions may need adjustment - Consider partition evolution after migration

docs.title: Issue: "Conversion very slow"
docs.text: Solutions: - Iceberg conversion rewrites all data - Consider smaller batches - Increase compute resources - Use in-place conversion if possible

docs.title: Issue: "ACID tables not converting"
docs.text: Solutions: - Enable ACID migration with downgrade - SQL strategy is required - Ensure transactions are complete

docs.title: Issue: "Schema mismatch after conversion"
docs.text: Solutions: - Iceberg may handle types slightly differently - Review column type mappings - Adjust source schema if needed

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start with non-critical tables: Test conversion process
  2. Use version 2: Unless compatibility requires version 1
  3. Choose Parquet: Best balance of features and compatibility
  4. Configure compression: Use zstd for good compression/speed
  5. Set table properties: Configure for your workload
  6. Test time travel: Verify snapshot capabilities work
  7. Plan for large tables: May need separate handling

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Target supports Iceberg
  • Tables created as Iceberg format
  • Data accessible via queries
  • Partition structure correct
  • Time travel queries work
  • Schema matches expectations
  • Performance acceptable

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• SQL Workflow - Required strategy for Iceberg
  • ACID Migration - ACID to Iceberg
  • HDP to CDP - Platform migration with Iceberg

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This workflow covers setting up a Disaster Recovery (DR) replica of your Hive data warehouse using HMS-Mirror. DR mode creates tables with safe defaults (no data deletion on drop) and enables incremental sync for keeping the replica current.

docs.title: Option 1: Shared Storage DR
docs.text:
Primary Cluster                     DR Cluster
+---------------+                   +---------------+
| Active        |                   | Standby       |
| Metastore     |                   | Metastore     |
+---------------+                   +---------------+
       |                                   |
       v                                   v
+------------------------------------------+
|     Shared Storage (S3/ADLS/GCS)         |
|     with cross-region replication        |
+------------------------------------------+

Best for: Cloud environments with storage replication

docs.title: Option 2: Separate Storage DR
docs.text:
Primary Cluster                     DR Cluster
+---------------+                   +---------------+
| Active        |                   | Standby       |
| Metastore     |                   | Metastore     |
+---------------+                   +---------------+
       |                                   |
       v                                   v
+---------------+                   +---------------+
| Primary       | ====distcp====>  | DR Storage    |
| Storage       |                   |               |
+---------------+                   +---------------+

Best for: On-premises or isolated environments

docs.title: Key DR Features
docs.text: Feature Purpose disasterRecovery: true Enables DR mode external.table.purge: false Prevents accidental data deletion sync: true Enables incremental sync Read-only tables DR tables are read-only by default

docs.title: Step 1.1: Configure Connections
docs.text:
1. LEFT (Primary) Connection:
  2. Production cluster HiveServer2

  3. Production storage namespace

  4. RIGHT (DR) Connection:

  5. DR cluster HiveServer2
  6. DR storage namespace

docs.title: Step 1.2: Create DR Configuration
docs.text:
# Disaster Recovery Configuration
createIfNotExists: true

# DR-specific settings
# (Note: These are set via Job options, not Configuration)

# Transfer settings
transferConfig:
  warehouse:
    externalDirectory: "/dr/warehouse/external"
    managedDirectory: "/dr/warehouse/managed"

# ACID handling
migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true  # Recommended for DR

# Statistics
enableAutoTableStats: true

docs.title: Step 1.3: Create Dataset
docs.text:
1. Include all databases for DR
  2. Consider excluding temporary/staging tables: 
    filter:
  excludeRegEx: ".*_temp|.*_staging|.*_backup"

docs.title: Step 1.4: Create DR Job
docs.text:
1. Data Strategy: Choose based on connectivity
  2. Connected: SQL, HYBRID, or EXPORT_IMPORT
  3. Shared storage: COMMON

  4. Separate storage: SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp

  5. Enable Disaster Recovery:

  6. Check "Disaster Recovery Job" option

  7. Sync Mode: Leave disabled for initial load

docs.title: Step 1.5: Execute Initial DR Load
docs.text:
1. Execute the job
  2. Monitor progress
  3. This creates DR tables with:
  4. external.table.purge = false
  5. Read-only semantics
  6. DR-appropriate locations

docs.title: Step 2.1: Enable Sync Mode
docs.text:
After initial load, create a sync job:

  1. Create new job (or modify existing)
  2. Enable Disaster Recovery option
  3. Enable Sync option

docs.title: Step 2.2: Configure Sync Schedule
docs.text:
Run sync jobs on a schedule:

Option A: Scheduled via HMS-Mirror API 

# Cron job to trigger sync
0 */4 * * * curl -X POST http://hms-mirror:8080/api/v1/jobs/{job-key}/run

Option B: External Scheduler 

# Airflow, Control-M, or similar
hms-mirror-cli --job dr-sync-job --execute

docs.title: Step 2.3: Sync Behavior
docs.text: When sync is enabled: - Only new/modified tables are processed - Partitions are compared and updated - Deleted source objects are NOT auto-removed (safety)

docs.title: Step 3.1: Regular Validation
docs.text:
Schedule periodic validation:

-- Compare table counts
-- Run on both clusters
SELECT database_name, COUNT(*) as table_count
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
GROUP BY database_name;

-- Compare row counts (sample)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM critical_table;

docs.title: Step 3.2: DR Drill
docs.text:
Periodically test DR readiness:

  1. Connect test applications to DR cluster
  2. Run read-only workloads
  3. Verify query results match production
  4. Document any discrepancies

docs.title: Step 4.1: Decision to Failover
docs.text: Criteria for failover: - Primary cluster unavailable - Storage corruption detected - Extended outage expected

docs.title: Step 4.2: Execute Failover
docs.text:
1. Stop sync jobs: Prevent partial updates
  2. Verify DR state: Run validation queries
  3. Update table properties (if write needed): 
    -- Enable purge for specific tables if needed
ALTER TABLE db.table SET TBLPROPERTIES ('external.table.purge'='true');
  4. Switch applications: Update connection strings
  5. Verify application function: Test critical workflows

docs.title: Step 4.3: Post-Failover
docs.text:
1. Investigate primary failure
  2. Plan recovery/failback
  3. Document incident

docs.title: Phase 5: Failback Procedure
docs.text: After primary is restored:

docs.title: Step 5.1: Sync Changes to Primary
docs.text:
If writes occurred on DR:

  1. Create reverse sync job (DR as source)
  2. Sync changed tables back to primary
  3. Validate primary state

docs.title: Step 5.2: Resume Normal Operations
docs.text:
1. Switch applications back to primary
  2. Resume DR sync jobs
  3. Validate DR is current

docs.title: Initial Load Job
docs.text:
# Job settings
name: "dr-initial-load"
description: "Initial DR replica creation"
strategy: HYBRID  # or SQL, EXPORT_IMPORT
disasterRecovery: true
sync: false  # Initial load

docs.title: Sync Job
docs.text:
# Job settings
name: "dr-sync"
description: "Incremental DR sync"
strategy: HYBRID
disasterRecovery: true
sync: true  # Incremental sync

docs.title: Table Properties in DR Mode
docs.text: Property Value Purpose external.table.purge false Prevent data deletion on DROP EXTERNAL true All tables become EXTERNAL hms_mirror_dr_table true Marks as DR-managed

docs.title: Key Metrics
docs.text:
• Time since last sync
  • Table count comparison
  • Failed sync tables
  • Storage lag (for replicated storage)

docs.title: Alerts
docs.text: Configure alerts for: - Sync job failures - Large table count discrepancies - Extended sync duration - Storage replication lag

docs.title: Issue: "Sync taking too long"
docs.text: Solutions: - Review changed table volume - Consider more frequent syncs - Optimize large table handling

docs.title: Issue: "Tables missing in DR"
docs.text: Solutions: - Check sync job logs - Verify dataset includes all databases - Run full sync to catch up

docs.title: Issue: "Stale data in DR"
docs.text: Solutions: - Verify sync schedule running - Check for sync failures - Run manual sync if needed

docs.title: Issue: "Cannot write to DR tables"
docs.text: Solutions: - DR tables are read-only by design - Modify table properties if failover required - Consider if write is actually needed

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Test failover regularly: Don't wait for disaster
  2. Monitor sync lag: Know your RPO
  3. Document procedures: Clear runbooks for failover
  4. Automate validation: Regular automated checks
  5. Separate credentials: DR cluster should have independent auth
  6. Consider RTO: Test how long failover takes

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• DR cluster accessible
  • All tables replicated
  • Table counts match
  • Sample data verified
  • Sync job scheduled
  • Failover procedure documented
  • DR drill completed

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• COMMON Workflow - Shared storage DR
  • HYBRID Workflow - Separate storage DR
  • Phased Migration - Similar multi-phase approach

docs.title: Database Rename/Prefix Workflow
docs.text:
Coming Soon

This workflow documentation is under development. Check back soon for detailed step-by-step instructions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Large-scale migrations benefit from a phased approach that reduces risk, allows validation at each stage, and provides rollback points. This workflow covers planning and executing multi-phase migrations.

docs.title: Why Phased Migration?
docs.text: Approach Risk Rollback Validation Duration Big Bang High Difficult Post-migration Short Phased Low Easy Continuous Longer

docs.title: Strategy 1: By Database Priority
docs.text:
Phase 1: Non-critical databases (dev, test, sandbox)
Phase 2: Supporting databases (reference data, dimensions)
Phase 3: Core databases (transactions, facts)
Phase 4: Critical databases (real-time, customer-facing)

docs.title: Strategy 2: By Table Type
docs.text:
Phase 1: External tables (simplest)
Phase 2: Non-ACID managed tables
Phase 3: ACID tables
Phase 4: Views (depend on tables)

docs.title: Strategy 3: By Application
docs.text:
Phase 1: Batch analytics (overnight processing)
Phase 2: Reporting (business hours)
Phase 3: Ad-hoc queries (interactive)
Phase 4: Real-time (streaming)

docs.title: Strategy 4: By Data Freshness
docs.text:
Phase 1: Historical/archive data (cold)
Phase 2: Recent data (warm)
Phase 3: Current data (hot)
Phase 4: Real-time sync (active)

docs.title: Step 0.1: Inventory and Categorize
docs.text:
Create inventory of all databases and tables:

-- Get database inventory
SELECT
  d.NAME as database_name,
  COUNT(t.TBL_ID) as table_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN p.PARAM_VALUE = 'true' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as acid_count
FROM metastore.DBS d
LEFT JOIN metastore.TBLS t ON d.DB_ID = t.DB_ID
LEFT JOIN metastore.TABLE_PARAMS p ON t.TBL_ID = p.TBL_ID
  AND p.PARAM_KEY = 'transactional'
GROUP BY d.NAME;

docs.title: Step 0.2: Identify Dependencies
docs.text:
Map dependencies between databases/tables:

fact_sales → dim_product, dim_customer, dim_date
reporting_views → fact_sales, fact_inventory

docs.title: Step 0.3: Define Phases
docs.text:
Create phase plan:

Phase Databases Dependencies Duration Rollback Plan 1 dev_, test_ None 1 week Delete and retry 2 dim_* None 1 week Delete and retry 3 fact_* dim_* 2 weeks Restore from source 4 reporting_* fact_, dim_ 1 week Recreate views

docs.title: Step 0.4: Create Datasets for Each Phase
docs.text:
Phase 1 Dataset: 

name: "phase-1-dev-test"
databases:
  - databaseName: dev_analytics
  - databaseName: test_analytics
  - databaseName: sandbox

Phase 2 Dataset: 

name: "phase-2-dimensions"
databases:
  - databaseName: dimension_data
    filter:
      includeRegEx: "dim_.*"

Continue for each phase...

docs.title: Step 1.1: Create Phase 1 Job
docs.text:
1. Dataset: phase-1-dev-test
  2. Strategy: HYBRID (or appropriate)
  3. Configuration: Standard settings

docs.title: Step 1.2: Validate Phase 1
docs.text:
1. Run job validation
  2. Review table counts
  3. Check for issues

docs.title: Step 1.3: Execute Phase 1
docs.text:
1. Execute migration
  2. Monitor progress
  3. Handle errors

docs.title: Step 1.4: Verify Phase 1
docs.text:
1. Verify tables on target
  2. Run sample queries
  3. Connect test applications
  4. Validate for 1-2 weeks

docs.title: Step 1.5: Sign-off Phase 1
docs.text:
• All tables migrated
  • Data verified
  • Applications tested
  • Performance acceptable
  • Stakeholder approval

docs.title: Phase 2-N: Repeat Pattern
docs.text:
For each subsequent phase:

  1. Pre-requisites: Verify previous phase complete
  2. Create Job: For phase dataset
  3. Validate: Pre-execution checks
  4. Execute: Run migration
  5. Verify: Post-migration validation
  6. Sign-off: Stakeholder approval

docs.title: Final Phase: Views and Dependencies
docs.text:
Views should be migrated last:

# Views configuration
migrateVIEW:
  on: true

# Dataset for views
databases:
  - databaseName: reporting
    filter:
      tableTypes: ["VIRTUAL_VIEW"]

Views are automatically created after all base tables.

docs.title: Handling Source Changes
docs.text:
During extended migrations, source tables may change:

Option A: Freeze and Migrate 1. Freeze source changes during phase 2. Migrate tables 3. Unfreeze after verification

Option B: Delta Sync 1. Enable DR mode with sync 2. Run sync jobs to catch changes 3. Final sync before cutover

docs.title: Rollback Procedures
docs.text:
For each phase, document rollback:

Phase Rollback Procedure:
1. Identify affected tables
2. Drop target tables
3. Verify source still intact
4. Re-run migration when ready
5. Notify stakeholders

docs.title: Communication Plan
docs.text: Event Notify Method Phase start All stakeholders Email Phase complete All stakeholders Email Issues found Technical team Slack/Teams Rollback required All stakeholders Email + meeting

docs.title: Phase Tracking Template
docs.text:
## Phase X: [Name]

### Scope
- Databases: db1, db2, db3
- Table count: XXX
- Data volume: XX TB

### Schedule
- Start: YYYY-MM-DD
- Execute: YYYY-MM-DD
- Validate: YYYY-MM-DD to YYYY-MM-DD
- Sign-off: YYYY-MM-DD

### Pre-requisites
- [ ] Phase X-1 complete
- [ ] Dependencies migrated
- [ ] Resources available

### Execution
- [ ] Job created
- [ ] Validation passed
- [ ] Migration executed
- [ ] No errors

### Validation
- [ ] Table counts match
- [ ] Sample data verified
- [ ] Applications tested
- [ ] Performance checked

### Sign-off
- [ ] Technical approval: ________
- [ ] Business approval: ________
- [ ] Date: ________

### Issues
| Issue | Resolution | Date |
|-------|------------|------|
|       |            |      |

docs.title: Parallel Phase Execution
docs.text:
For independent databases, phases can run in parallel:

Week 1: Phase 1A (dev) + Phase 1B (test)
Week 2: Phase 2A (dim) + Phase 2B (reference)
Week 3: Phase 3 (facts - sequential, dependent)
Week 4: Phase 4 (views - sequential, dependent)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start small: First phase should be low-risk
  2. Allow validation time: Don't rush between phases
  3. Document everything: Issues, decisions, sign-offs
  4. Communicate regularly: Status updates to stakeholders
  5. Have rollback ready: Test rollback procedures
  6. Learn and adapt: Apply learnings to later phases

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
Per Phase: - [ ] All tables migrated - [ ] Data counts verified - [ ] Sample queries successful - [ ] Application connectivity tested - [ ] Performance acceptable - [ ] Dependencies satisfied - [ ] Stakeholder sign-off

Overall: - [ ] All phases complete - [ ] All applications migrated - [ ] Final validation complete - [ ] Source decommissioning planned

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• LINKED Workflow - Testing phase approach
  • Disaster Recovery - Sync between phases
  • HDP to CDP - Platform-specific phasing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Tables with very large data volumes (100GB+) or extreme partition counts (1000+) require special handling during migration. This workflow covers strategies for efficiently migrating large tables without overwhelming cluster resources.

docs.title: By Data Volume
docs.text:
-- Find tables by estimated size
SELECT
  d.NAME as db_name,
  t.TBL_NAME,
  p.PARAM_VALUE as total_size
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
JOIN metastore.TABLE_PARAMS p ON t.TBL_ID = p.TBL_ID
WHERE p.PARAM_KEY = 'totalSize'
  AND CAST(p.PARAM_VALUE AS BIGINT) > 107374182400  -- 100GB
ORDER BY CAST(p.PARAM_VALUE AS BIGINT) DESC;

docs.title: By Partition Count
docs.text:
-- Find tables by partition count
SELECT
  d.NAME as db_name,
  t.TBL_NAME,
  COUNT(p.PART_ID) as partition_count
FROM metastore.TBLS t
JOIN metastore.DBS d ON t.DB_ID = d.DB_ID
LEFT JOIN metastore.PARTITIONS p ON t.TBL_ID = p.TBL_ID
GROUP BY d.NAME, t.TBL_NAME
HAVING COUNT(p.PART_ID) > 500
ORDER BY partition_count DESC;

docs.title: Size Categories
docs.text: Category Data Size Partition Count Recommended Approach Small < 10 GB < 100 Standard migration Medium 10-100 GB 100-500 HYBRID or SQL Large 100 GB - 1 TB 500-2000 Dedicated handling Very Large > 1 TB > 2000 Partition ranges or distcp

docs.title: Option 1: Dedicated Large Table Jobs
docs.text:
Create separate jobs for large tables:

# Large table dataset
name: "large-tables-only"
databases:
  - databaseName: warehouse
    tables:
      - "fact_transactions"
      - "event_logs"

With tuned configuration: 

optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.tez.container.size:
        RIGHT: "16384"  # Larger containers
      hive.exec.parallel:
        RIGHT: "true"
      hive.exec.parallel.thread.number:
        RIGHT: "16"

hybrid:
  sqlPartitionLimit: 2000  # Increased limit
  sqlSizeLimit: 10737418240  # 10GB limit

docs.title: Option 2: Partition Range Migration
docs.text:
Migrate partitions in ranges:

Dataset 1: Historical (2020-2022) 

databases:
  - databaseName: warehouse
    tables:
      - "fact_transactions"
    filter:
      # Add partition filter in table selection
      partitionFilter: "year <= 2022"

Dataset 2: Recent (2023-present) 

databases:
  - databaseName: warehouse
    tables:
      - "fact_transactions"
    filter:
      partitionFilter: "year >= 2023"

docs.title: Option 3: SCHEMA_ONLY + DistCp
docs.text:
For very large tables:

  1. Create schema: SCHEMA_ONLY migration
  2. Copy data: External distcp
  3. Repair partitions: MSCK REPAIR TABLE
# Step 1: Schema only migration
hms-mirror --strategy SCHEMA_ONLY ...

# Step 2: Parallel distcp
hadoop distcp \
  -Dmapreduce.job.maps=100 \
  -strategy dynamic \
  hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/large_table \
  hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/large_table

# Step 3: Repair partitions
beeline -e "MSCK REPAIR TABLE db.large_table"

docs.title: Option 4: Incremental Migration
docs.text:
For continuously growing tables:

Day 1: Migrate historical data (bulk)
Day 2-N: Migrate daily increments
Final: Last sync before cutover

docs.title: Memory Configuration
docs.text:
optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      # Increase container memory
      hive.tez.container.size:
        BOTH: "16384"  # 16GB
      hive.tez.java.opts:
        BOTH: "-Xmx13107m"

      # Increase AM memory for large DAGs
      tez.am.resource.memory.mb:
        BOTH: "8192"

docs.title: Parallelism
docs.text:
optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      # Enable parallel execution
      hive.exec.parallel:
        BOTH: "true"
      hive.exec.parallel.thread.number:
        BOTH: "8"

      # Dynamic partition handling
      hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode:
        RIGHT: "nonstrict"
      hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions:
        RIGHT: "10000"
      hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions.pernode:
        RIGHT: "2000"

docs.title: Queue Isolation
docs.text:
optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      # Use dedicated queue
      tez.queue.name:
        BOTH: "migration"
      mapreduce.job.queuename:
        BOTH: "migration"

docs.title: Basic DistCp
docs.text:
hadoop distcp \
  -update \
  -delete \
  -skipcrccheck \
  hdfs://source/path \
  hdfs://target/path

docs.title: Optimized DistCp
docs.text:
hadoop distcp \
  -Dmapreduce.job.maps=200 \
  -Dmapreduce.job.reduces=50 \
  -Dmapreduce.map.memory.mb=4096 \
  -strategy dynamic \
  -bandwidth 500 \
  -update \
  -skipcrccheck \
  -p \
  hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/large_table \
  hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/large_table

docs.title: Parallel DistCp (by partition)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Parallel distcp for partitions
for year in 2020 2021 2022 2023; do
  hadoop distcp \
    -Dmapreduce.job.maps=50 \
    -strategy dynamic \
    hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/table/year=$year \
    hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/table/year=$year &
done
wait

docs.title: Progress Tracking
docs.text:
-- Monitor partition counts during migration
SELECT COUNT(*) as migrated_partitions
FROM db.table
WHERE 1=1;  -- Target cluster

-- Compare with source
-- hdfs dfs -count -h hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/table

docs.title: Resource Monitoring
docs.text:
• YARN Resource Manager: Queue utilization
  • Application logs: Query progress
  • HDFS: Storage utilization

docs.title: Row Count Validation
docs.text:
-- For partitioned tables, compare by partition
SELECT year, month, COUNT(*) as row_count
FROM db.large_table
GROUP BY year, month
ORDER BY year, month;

docs.title: File Count Validation
docs.text:
# Source file count
hdfs dfs -count -h hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/table

# Target file count
hdfs dfs -count -h hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/table

docs.title: Size Validation
docs.text:
# Source size
hdfs dfs -du -s -h hdfs://source/warehouse/db.db/table

# Target size (may differ slightly due to replication/compression)
hdfs dfs -du -s -h hdfs://target/warehouse/db.db/table

docs.title: Issue: "Query timeout on large table"
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase query timeout settings - Migrate in partition ranges - Use SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp

docs.title: Issue: "Out of memory"
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase container memory - Reduce parallelism - Process fewer tables concurrently

docs.title: Issue: "Too many mappers/reducers"
docs.text: Solutions: - Limit parallelism in configuration - Use queue isolation - Spread across multiple days

docs.title: Issue: "Slow progress"
docs.text: Solutions: - Check for data skew in partitions - Monitor network bandwidth - Consider off-peak execution

docs.title: Issue: "Partition repair taking too long"
docs.text: Solutions: - For tables with many partitions, MSCK REPAIR is slow - Consider using RECOVER PARTITIONS - Or create partitions explicitly

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Isolate large tables: Separate from regular migration
  2. Test with subset: Validate approach on sample partitions
  3. Schedule strategically: Run during low-activity periods
  4. Monitor continuously: Watch resource utilization
  5. Validate incrementally: Check as partitions complete
  6. Have fallback plan: Be ready to switch strategies

docs.title: Example: 5TB Table Migration
docs.text:
Table: warehouse.fact_events (5TB, 10,000 partitions)

Day 1: Schema migration
- Run SCHEMA_ONLY to create table structure
- Verify table exists on target

Day 2-4: Historical data (distcp)
- Copy partitions year <= 2022 (~4TB)
- Run overnight with high parallelism
- Validate each day's progress

Day 5: Recent data (distcp)
- Copy partitions year = 2023 (~1TB)
- Faster due to less volume

Day 6: Partition repair
- Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE
- Validate partition counts

Day 7: Final validation
- Row count comparison
- Sample query testing
- Performance validation

Day 8: Cutover
- Switch applications
- Monitor for issues

docs.title: Related Workflows
docs.text:
• Partition-Heavy Tables - Focus on partition handling
  • SCHEMA_ONLY - Schema-only approach
  • HYBRID - Automatic large table handling

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide covers common issues encountered during HMS-Mirror migrations and their solutions. Issues are organized by category for quick reference.

docs.title: Unable to Connect to HiveServer2
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Connection refused" error - "Unable to establish connection" message

Solutions:

  1. Verify HiveServer2 is running: 

    beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://hostname:10000"

  2. Check connection string format: 

    # Standard
jdbc:hive2://hostname:10000/default

# With Kerberos
jdbc:hive2://hostname:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM

# With SSL
jdbc:hive2://hostname:10000/default;ssl=true;sslTrustStore=/path/truststore.jks

# With HTTP transport (common in cloud)
jdbc:hive2://hostname:443/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=/hive2

  3. Test network connectivity: 

    telnet hostname 10000
nc -zv hostname 10000

  4. Check firewall rules: Ensure ports 10000 (binary) or 443 (HTTP) are open

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication Failures
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "GSS initiate failed" - "Could not obtain Kerberos principal"

Solutions:

  1. Verify Kerberos ticket: 

    klist
kinit -kt /path/to/keytab principal@REALM

  2. Check principal in connection string: 

    jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default;principal=hive/hostname@REALM

  3. Verify cross-realm trust (for linked clusters): 

    # krb5.conf should have both realms
[realms]
REALM1.COM = { ... }
REALM2.COM = { ... }

[capaths]
REALM1.COM = {
  REALM2.COM = .
}

docs.title: HDFS/Cloud Storage Access Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Permission denied" on storage paths - "Unknown host" for HDFS nameservice

Solutions:

  1. Verify HDFS access: 

    hdfs dfs -ls hdfs://nameservice/path

  2. Check HA configuration: Ensure hdfs-site.xml has nameservice config

  3. Verify cloud credentials: 

    # S3
aws s3 ls s3://bucket/

# ADLS
az storage blob list --container-name container

  4. Check Hadoop credential provider for cloud secrets

docs.title: Query Timeout
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Query timeout exceeded" - Long-running queries not completing

Solutions:

  1. Increase timeout settings: 

    optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.server2.idle.operation.timeout:
        BOTH: "14400000"  # 4 hours
      hive.server2.idle.session.timeout:
        BOTH: "28800000"  # 8 hours

  2. Use HYBRID strategy: Large tables auto-select better approach

  3. Migrate large tables separately: Dedicated jobs with higher limits

docs.title: Out of Memory Errors
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Java heap space" errors - "Container killed by YARN for exceeding memory limits"

Solutions:

  1. Increase container memory: 

    optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.tez.container.size:
        BOTH: "16384"
      hive.tez.java.opts:
        BOTH: "-Xmx13107m"

  2. Reduce parallelism: 

    optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.exec.parallel.thread.number:
        BOTH: "4"

  3. Process fewer tables concurrently

docs.title: Too Many Partitions
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Exceeded partition limit" - Tables being skipped

Solutions:

  1. Increase partition limits: 

    hybrid:
  sqlPartitionLimit: 1000  # Increase from 500
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 200  # Increase from 100

  2. Migrate partition ranges: Split into multiple jobs by partition values

  3. Use SCHEMA_ONLY + distcp: For very partition-heavy tables

docs.title: ACID Table Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Cannot read ACID table" - "Transaction timeout"

Solutions:

  1. Run compaction first: 

    ALTER TABLE db.table COMPACT 'major' AND WAIT;

  2. Enable ACID migration: 

    migrateACID:
  on: true
  downgrade: true

  3. Check for open transactions: 

    SHOW TRANSACTIONS;
SHOW LOCKS DATABASE db;

docs.title: Table Already Exists
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Table already exists" error

Solutions:

  1. Enable createIfNotExists: 

    createIfNotExists: true

  2. Drop existing tables first (if re-running)

  3. Use different target database for testing

docs.title: Column Type Mismatch
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Type mismatch" errors - "Cannot cast" errors

Solutions:

  1. Review type mappings: Some types differ between Hive versions

  2. Use SQL strategy with CAST: Explicit type conversion

  3. Modify source schema: If feasible, align types

docs.title: View Dependencies
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "View references non-existent table"

Solutions:

  1. Ensure base tables migrate first: Views are processed last

  2. Check cross-database references: Views may reference tables in other databases

  3. Migrate dependent databases together

docs.title: Slow Migration
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Migration taking much longer than expected

Solutions:

  1. Enable auto-tuning: 

    optimization:
  autoTune: true

  2. Increase parallelism: 

    optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      hive.exec.parallel:
        BOTH: "true"
      hive.exec.parallel.thread.number:
        BOTH: "8"

  3. Use dedicated queue: 

    optimization:
  overrides:
    properties:
      tez.queue.name:
        BOTH: "migration"

  4. Schedule during off-peak hours

docs.title: Data Skew
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Some partitions/tables much slower than others - Uneven resource utilization

Solutions:

  1. Use DISTRIBUTE BY (HMS-Mirror adds automatically)

  2. Split skewed partitions: Migrate separately

  3. Increase reducer count for skewed tables

docs.title: Row Count Mismatch
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Source and target counts don't match

Solutions:

  1. Check for in-flight transactions: Wait for completion

  2. Verify partition coverage: All partitions migrated?

  3. Check for filtered data: Review dataset filters

  4. Re-run for specific tables: With fresh source state

docs.title: Missing Partitions
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Partitions exist on source but not target

Solutions:

  1. Run MSCK REPAIR TABLE: 

    MSCK REPAIR TABLE db.table;

  2. Verify data was copied: Check storage locations

  3. Check partition limits: May have been skipped

docs.title: Cannot Access HMS-Mirror UI
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Browser shows connection error - 404 or 500 errors

Solutions:

  1. Verify server is running: 

    ps aux | grep hms-mirror
curl http://localhost:8080/hms-mirror/health

  2. Check port availability: 

    netstat -tlnp | grep 8080

  3. Review server logs: Check for startup errors

docs.title: Connection Test Failing
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Test connection shows "Failed" in UI

Solutions:

  1. Use Diagnose feature: Click "Diagnose" for AI-powered analysis

  2. Check driver availability: Ensure JDBC drivers are installed

  3. Verify network from server: HMS-Mirror server needs access, not just your browser

docs.title: Recovering from Failed Migration
docs.text:
1. Identify failure point: Check execution logs

  2. Clean up partial data: Drop incomplete tables if needed

  3. Fix configuration: Address root cause

  4. Re-run failed tables only: Create dataset with just failed tables

  5. Validate results: Compare with source

docs.title: Rolling Back Migration
docs.text:
1. Drop migrated tables on target: 

    DROP TABLE db.table;

  2. Verify source intact: Confirm source wasn't modified

  3. Analyze failure: Determine what went wrong

  4. Re-plan migration: Adjust approach as needed

docs.title: Information to Gather
docs.text:
When seeking help, collect:

  1. HMS-Mirror version: Check About page or startup logs
  2. Error messages: Exact error text
  3. Configuration: Relevant settings (sanitized)
  4. Execution logs: Full log output
  5. Environment: Platform types, versions

docs.title: Using Diagnose Feature
docs.text: For connection errors: 1. Click "Test" on connection 2. When test fails, click "Diagnose" 3. AI provides analysis and suggestions 4. Follow recommended steps

docs.title: Log Locations
docs.text:
• Server logs: logs/hms-mirror.log
  • Execution reports: reports/<job-id>/
  • Console output: stdout during execution

docs.title: Common Error Messages
docs.text: Error Likely Cause Solution "Connection refused" HS2 not running Start HiveServer2 "GSS initiate failed" Kerberos issue Renew ticket, check principal "Permission denied" Access rights Check ACLs, IAM roles "Table already exists" Re-running job Enable createIfNotExists "Query timeout" Large table Increase timeout or use different strategy "Container killed" Memory exhaustion Increase container memory "Too many partitions" Partition limit Increase limit or split migration "Unknown host" DNS/network Check nameservice config

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide provides step-by-step workflows for common HMS-Mirror migration scenarios. Each workflow document covers a specific use case or data strategy, including prerequisites, configuration steps, execution, and validation.

docs.title: Strategy-Based Workflows
docs.text:
These workflows focus on specific data migration strategies and when to use them:

Workflow Description Use When SCHEMA_ONLY Transfer schema definitions only Data moved externally via distcp SQL Migrate data using Hive SQL Linked clusters, format conversion needed EXPORT_IMPORT Use Hive's native export/import Linked clusters, moderate data sizes HYBRID Auto-select SQL or EXPORT_IMPORT Mixed table sizes, optimal performance LINKED Create linked tables to source data Testing/validation before full migration STORAGE_MIGRATION Relocate data within a cluster Cloud migration, namespace changes COMMON Shared storage between clusters Clusters sharing same HDFS/S3 DUMP Extract schema DDL scripts Documentation, offline planning CONVERT_LINKED Convert linked tables to regular After data copy to target

docs.title: Use-Case Workflows
docs.text:
These workflows address common migration scenarios:

Workflow Description HDP to CDP Migration Complete HDP 2.x/3.x to CDP 7.x migration On-Premises to Cloud Migrate from on-prem to cloud storage ACID Table Migration Special handling for transactional tables ACID Downgrade Convert ACID tables to EXTERNAL Iceberg Conversion Convert Hive tables to Iceberg format Disaster Recovery Setup Set up DR replica with incremental sync Database Rename/Prefix Migrate with database naming changes Phased Migration Multi-phase migration for large environments Testing with LINKED Test target cluster before full migration

docs.title: Advanced Workflows
docs.text: Workflow Description Multi-Hop Migration Migrations via intermediate storage Cross-Environment Dev/Test/Prod environment handling Large Table Handling Strategies for very large tables Partition-Heavy Tables Handling tables with many partitions

docs.title: Quick Start Decision Tree
docs.text:
Use this decision tree to select the right workflow:

START
  |
  v
Are clusters network-connected (linked)?
  |
  +-- NO --> Can you set up intermediate storage?
  |            |
  |            +-- YES --> Multi-Hop Migration (19)
  |            +-- NO  --> SCHEMA_ONLY (01) + external distcp
  |
  +-- YES --> Do you need to migrate data?
               |
               +-- NO  --> Just schema? --> SCHEMA_ONLY (01)
               |           Need testing? --> LINKED (05)
               |
               +-- YES --> Is this within same cluster?
                            |
                            +-- YES --> STORAGE_MIGRATION (06)
                            +-- NO  --> Do tables share storage?
                                         |
                                         +-- YES --> COMMON (07)
                                         +-- NO  --> Need format conversion?
                                                      |
                                                      +-- YES --> SQL (02)
                                                      +-- NO  --> Mixed table sizes?
                                                                   |
                                                                   +-- YES --> HYBRID (04)
                                                                   +-- NO  --> EXPORT_IMPORT (03)

docs.title: Common Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting any workflow:

  1. Connections Configured: Both source (LEFT) and target (RIGHT) connections must be tested and working
  2. Dataset Defined: Tables to migrate must be identified in a Dataset
  3. Configuration Created: Migration settings must be defined in a Configuration
  4. Permissions Verified: User must have appropriate Hive and HDFS/S3 permissions on both clusters
  5. Network Connectivity: For linked strategies, clusters must be able to communicate
  6. Driver Availability: Required JDBC drivers must be installed

docs.title: Workflow Document Structure
docs.text:
Each workflow document follows this structure:

  1. Overview: What this workflow accomplishes
  2. When to Use: Scenarios where this approach is appropriate
  3. Prerequisites: What must be in place before starting
  4. Step-by-Step Instructions: Detailed configuration and execution steps
  5. Validation: How to verify successful migration
  6. Troubleshooting: Common issues and solutions
  7. Related Workflows: Links to complementary approaches

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• In-App Diagnostics: Use the "Diagnose" button on connection errors for AI-powered troubleshooting
  • Configuration Tooltips: Hover over info icons for field descriptions
  • LLM Analysis: Use the AI assistant for configuration optimization suggestions
